signaturesHeadline: TX independent pres: 113,151 valid sigs for 2024 (1% of prior presidential total); non-primary voters only — number confirmed, but verify cycle recompute
deadlineHeadline: TX deadline: 2nd Monday in May = May 13, 2024; circulate only after March 5 primary — AUTO-LIVE
riskNote: Grounding clean on all 8 fields (every quote verbatim; 113,151 = round-up of 1% of 11,315,056 2020 TX total). Two holds: signatures (mixes the minor-party gubernatorial base into the independent rule) and circulatorEligibility (self-flagged UNKNOWN, an unprovable absence claim). Default strict pending SOS confirmation.
sosInquiries: For an INDEPENDENT presidential candidate's petition under Tex. Elec. Code 192.032, are there any statutory or administrative qualifications on who may circulate the petition (e.g., must the circulator be a registered Texas voter, of a minimum age, or a U.S. resident), or may any person circulate it?

Does the independent presidential candidate's signature requirement of 1% of the most recent presidential vote recompute each presidential cycle off the prior presidential general election total (i.e., for 2028 it will be 1% of the 2024 Texas presidential vote), and is the 113,151 figure specific to the 2024 cycle only?

fields.reliedValue: For the independent presidential path only: at least 1% of the total vote received in Texas by all candidates for president in the most recent presidential general election. For the 2024 cycle this equaled 113,151 valid signatures of registered voters who did NOT vote in either party's presidential primary that year (recomputes each cycle off the prior presidential vote; 1% of the 2020 total of 11,315,056 rounds up to 113,151). No geographic/territory requirement on signers. (Strict scope: the minor/convention-party qualification — 5% statewide nominee threshold or 1% of the most recent gubernatorial vote — is a DIFFERENT path with a DIFFERENT base and does not apply to an independent candidate; do not conflate.)
fields.note: All quotes verbatim. 192.032(d)=1%; SOS=113,151; 192.032(e)=no territory; 192.032(f)/(g)=non-primary voters/invalid if signed on or before primary. Math checks: 1% of 11,315,056 (2020 TX total, confirmed via Wikipedia) = 113,150.56 -> 113,151. HOLD because the draft value bundles the convention/minor-party path (gubernatorial-vote base) into the independent rule, which is out-of-scope for accessPath=independent and could mislead; the 'recomputes each cycle' framing is also a derived gloss not stated verbatim in a citation. Independent number itself is correct.

fields.reliedValue: The application and accompanying petition must be filed with the Secretary of State no later than the second Monday in May of the presidential election year — May 13, 2024 for the 2024 cycle. The petition may not be circulated until after the March 5, 2024 presidential primary; a signature is invalid if signed on or before the date of the primary or by anyone who voted in a presidential primary that year.
fields.note: 192.032(c) 'second Monday in May' verbatim; SOS 'no later than May 13, 2024' and 'circulated beginning after March 5, 2024' verbatim. May 2024 Mondays = 6th, 13th, so 2nd Monday = May 13 (date arithmetic confirmed). 192.032(g) invalidity quote present. Correct and self-executing, no legal interpretation needed.

fields.reliedValue: None. There is no per-congressional-district or per-county distribution requirement for the independent presidential petition; the statute expressly provides that a signer's voter registration need not be in any particular territory, so signatures may be gathered statewide with no geographic spread.
fields.note: 192.032(e) quote 'A petition signer's voter registration is not required to be in any particular territory.' verbatim. Independently confirmed against NASS (no distribution language for the TX independent presidential petition, unlike states such as Virginia which NASS lists with a 400-per-district requirement). Negative claim but directly supported by the express statutory text.

fields.reliedValue: UNKNOWN / not established by retrieval. Secs. 141.064-141.065 prescribe the circulator's DUTIES (point out and read each statement, witness each signature, ascertain dates, verify each signer's registration, and execute a notarized affidavit) but do not by themselves establish that there are NO eligibility limits. Do not represent that 'any person' may circulate; treat circulator qualifications as unconfirmed and assume the petition must be circulated by a competent adult able to witness signatures and swear the notarized affidavit, pending SOS confirmation.
fields.note: Both cited section headers/quotes are verbatim (141.064 'A person circulating a petition must:'; 141.065(a) affidavit clause). But the field's substantive claim is a NEGATIVE/absence assertion ('no statutory provision restricts circulator eligibility') that two cited duty-sections cannot prove — the value even self-flags UNKNOWN. Cannot ground the non-existence of a restriction from these sources; strictest stance is to hold and ask the SOS rather than assert open eligibility.

fields.reliedValue: Notarized circulator affidavit required. Each part of the petition must include an affidavit of the circulator, executed before a person authorized to administer oaths (notarized), stating the circulator pointed out and read each required statement to each signer, witnessed each signature, verified each signer's registration status, and believes each signature genuine. A single notarized affidavit covers all signatures gathered by that circulator if notarization is on or after the date of the last signature obtained.
fields.note: 141.065(a) 'executed before a person authorized to administer oaths in this state' and 141.065(c) single-notarized-affidavit quote both verbatim. Directly on point and applies to the independent presidential petition via 192.032(b)(3)(A)->141.062. No interpretation needed.

fields.reliedValue: paper (wet-signature). Signers must sign in their own handwriting; the circulator must witness each signature in person and a notarized circulator affidavit is required per part. No online/remote or digitized-signature system exists for an independent presidential petition in Texas — the SOS publishes only PDF forms (Application 2-38f, Petition 2-39f).
fields.note: 141.063(b) 'The signature is the only information that is required to appear on the petition in the signer's own handwriting.' verbatim; 141.064(1) read-each-statement quote verbatim; SOS 'Independent Candidate's Petition for President (PDF)' verbatim. Wet-ink/witnessed paper process follows directly from witnessing + per-part notarized affidavit. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: None. Ballot access for an independent presidential candidate is by petition only — there is no filing fee and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option for this office. Sec. 192.032 requires the application to be accompanied by a qualifying petition and the consent statements, and lists no filing-fee alternative. (The fee-or-petition election available to Texas primary/partisan candidates does not apply to this independent presidential path.)
fields.note: 192.032(b)(3) 'be accompanied by:' quote verbatim — the statutory list of what accompanies the application is exhaustive and contains only the petition and written consents, no fee or fee-in-lieu. SOS independent section (quote verbatim) describes application+petition with no fee. The absence is supported by an exhaustive statutory enumeration rather than mere silence, so 'no fee alternative' is reliable without legal interpretation.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: For an independent presidential candidate's nominating petition, Texas Election Code §192.032 incorporates the candidate-petition rules of §141.062–.065; none of those sections — nor the SOS candidate-guide FAQs or the SOS petitions page — addresses compensating circulators in any way, so per-signature pay for CANDIDATE petitions is genuinely unaddressed (not authorized and not prohibited). Circulator residency is NOT required: §141.065 imposes no residency/registration requirement on the circulator and the SOS FAQ states verbatim that the Election Code defines no eligibility requirements for circulators, so a national paid pool could legally circulate in Texas.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nominating petition under Texas Election Code §§192.032 and 141.062–.065, may the campaign pay petition circulators on a per-signature basis, and are there any restrictions on paying circulators (e.g., hourly vs. per-signature) or any circulator residency/registration requirement?

citations.quote: (b) An application must: (1) comply with Section 141.031 ... and (3) be accompanied by: (A) a petition that satisfies the requirements prescribed by Section 141.062 (Validity of Petition) ... (e) A petition signer's voter registration is not required to be in any particular territory.

(a) Each part of a petition must include an affidavit of the person who circulated it, executed before a person authorized to administer oaths in this state, stating that the person: (1) pointed out and read to each signer, before the petition was signed, each statement pertaining to the signer that appears on the petition; (2) witnessed each signature; (3) verified each signer's registration status; and (4) believes each signature to be genuine and the corresponding information to be correct.

The Election Code does not define any eligibility requirements for service as a circulator. For example, a person may circulate a petition even if the circulator is not a registered voter of the area.

voterFile.cost: Statewide file priced on a sliding per-voter scale plus base charge. "Over 1,000,000 Voters" tier = $328.13 base + $0.0000625 per voter. At ~18M TX registered voters that is roughly $1,450 statewide (~$328.13 + ~$1,125), plus $11 per CD-ROM/DVD if not via FTP. A $75.00 deposit must accompany the request. Custom extracts/manipulation may add data-manipulation charges quoted separately.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Any person on request ("The secretary of state shall furnish information in the statewide computerized voter registration list to any person on request"). Not eligibility-gated to candidates/parties — general public may obtain it — but the requestor must swear a notarized affidavit that the data will not be used for commercial advertising/promotion.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Political/non-commercial use is permitted; commercial use is prohibited. Requestor must swear the data "will not be used to advertise or promote commercial products or services." Using the list to advertise/promote commercial products or services is a Class A misdemeanor under Tex. Election Code 18.067. Confidential voters (Sec. 13.004) and SSNs are excluded.
voterFile.requestProcess: Mail or fax the SOS "Voter Registration Public Information Request Form" (Elections Division, P.O. Box 12060, Austin, TX 78711-2060; fax 512-475-2811) with a NOTARIZED sworn affidavit of non-commercial use plus a $75.00 deposit; full payment required before release. SOS furnishes data within 15 days. No instant online self-service download.
voterFile.note: Texas furnishes the statewide "State Master Voter File" to anyone who files the SOS public-information request form with a notarized non-commercial-use affidavit and pays the per-voter fee (statewide ~$1,450) plus a $75 deposit; fees are capped at actual reproduction expense (Tex. Election Code 18.066(e)). The standard fixed-width file includes last/first/middle name, permanent (residential) address fields, and a status code (Active/Suspense/Cancelled) plus VUID and DOB — exactly what is needed to match and verify petition signers against registered voters.

citations.quote: EXTRACT RATES FOR COMPUTER CD-ROM, OR DISK   1 - 124,999 Voters $ 93.75 + $ 0.0005 Per Voter ... Over 1,000,000 Voters $328.13 + $0.0000625 Per Voter

The attached affidavit must be signed before a notary public. A $75.00 deposit must accompany each request. ... CD-ROM will not be released and/or files will not be uploaded to the FTP until full payment is received.
I do solemnly swear that the information obtained from the copy of the State Master Voter File will not be used to advertise or promote commercial products or services.
Public Information Voter Data File Record Layout ... LAST NAME ... FIRST NAME ... PERM STREET NAME ... PERM CITY ... STATUS CODE ... Status Code  V Active  S Suspense  C Cancelled
The secretary of state shall furnish information in the statewide computerized voter registration list to any person on request not later than the 15th day after the date the request is received.

To receive information under this section, a person must submit an affidavit to the secretary stating that the person will not use the information obtained in connection with advertising or promoting commercial products or services.
The secretary may prescribe a schedule of fees for furnishing information under this section. A fee may not exceed the actual expense incurred in reproducing the information requested.

A person commits an offense if the person uses information in connection with advertising or promoting commercial products or services that the person knows was obtained under Section 18.066. ... An offense under this section is a Class A misdemeanor.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — the rule binds signers only. The Texas Election Code provisions that govern who circulates a petition (Sec. 141.064 "Method of Acquiring Signature" and Sec. 141.065 "Affidavit of Circulator") impose duties on "a person circulating a petition" / "the person who circulated it" but set NO minimum age. The age-adjacent eligibility (registered voter) is imposed on the SI
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a registered voter (Sec. 141.063(a)(1)) who did not vote in the presidential primary and signs after primary day (Sec. 192.032(f),(g)); CIRCULATOR has no age, voter-registration, citizenship, or residency requirement — statute only assigns witnessing/verification duties (Sec. 141.064) plus a notarized affidavit (Sec. 141.065).
circulator.note: Texas independent presidential petition is governed by Tex. Elec. Code Sec. 192.032, which requires the petition to satisfy Sec. 141.062 — pulling in the full Subchapter C signature/circulator framework. Three sections define the circulator: (1) Sec. 141.064 ("A person circulating a petition must... point out and read... witness each signature... ascertain... verify each signer's registration status"); (2) Sec. 141.065 (the circulator's notarized affidavit); and (3) Sec. 141.063 places the "regi

citations.quote: A signature on a petition is valid if: (1) except as otherwise provided by this code, the signer, at the time of signing, is a registered voter of the territory from which the office sought is elected... (b) The signature is the only information that is required to appear on the petition in the signer's own handwriting.

A person circulating a petition must: (1) before permitting a person to sign, point out and read to the person each statement pertaining to the signer that appears on the petition; (2) witness each signature; (3) ascertain that each date of signing is correct; and (4) before the petition is filed, verify each signer's registration status and ascertain that each registration number entered on the petition is correct.

Each part of a petition must include an affidavit of the person who circulated it, executed before a person authorized to administer oaths in this state, stating that the person: (1) pointed out and read to each signer... (2) witnessed each signature; (3) verified each signer's registration status; and (4) believes each signature to be genuine... (c) A single notarized affidavit by any person who obtained signatures is valid for all signatures gathered by the person...

(a) To be entitled to a place on the general election ballot, an independent candidate for president of the United States must make an application for a place on the ballot... (b) An application must... (3) be accompanied by: (A) a petition that satisfies the requirements prescribed by Section 141.062... (g) A signature on the petition is invalid if the signer: (1) signs the petition on or before the date of the presidential primary election in the presidential election year; or (2) voted in a presidential primary election during the presidential election year.

The signers of your petition must be registered voters who have not participated in the general primary election or the runoff primary election of a party that has nominated, at either election, a candidate for the office you seek. (Section 142.009, Texas Election Code).

signaturesHeadline: 1% of FL registered voters (per last general election) — recomputed each cycle; exact 2024 count unverified
deadlineHeadline: County petitions due noon, July 15 of the presidential election year
riskNote: Core path (1% petition, noon July 15, per-county submission, paper, no fee-in-lieu) is primary-source grounded and current; circulator eligibility is unverified (BLOCKED) and the precise signature count plus any petition-form attestation need office confirmation before relying.
sosInquiries: For an independent (no-party-affiliation) candidate for President under Fla. Stat. 103.021(3), what is the exact number of valid petition signatures required for the current cycle (1% of registered voters as of the last preceding general election), and is there an official published figure?

Does the Division of Elections' prescribed presidential-elector petition form require any circulator signature, attestation, or notarization, or any circulator age/residency/voter-registration eligibility — or is each signature validated solely by the county supervisor's signature match against the voter registration system?

fields.reliedValue: 1% of the state's registered voters as shown by the Department of State's compilation for the last preceding general election (Fla. Stat. 103.021(3)); recomputed each cycle. Do NOT rely on the embedded ~145,000 figure — exact 2024 count is not primary-source verified.
fields.note: Statute quote verbatim-confirmed on leg.state.fl.us 103.021(3); Ballotpedia 1% quote also verbatim. The 1% formula is correct and current. HOLD only because the value bundles an unverified '~145,000' estimate that should not be treated as an authoritative count; rely on the formula, not the number. Same 1% basis verified for the unaffiliated minor party under 103.021(4)(b).

fields.reliedValue: County petitions must be submitted to each county supervisor of elections no later than noon on July 15 of the presidential election year; supervisor certifies registered-voter count on or before the date of the primary election and forwards to the Department of State (Fla. Stat. 103.021(3)).
fields.note: Verbatim-confirmed in 103.021(3): 'no later than noon on July 15 of each presidential election year' and 'on or before the date of the primary election, shall certify.' Ballotpedia quote (omits 'noon', says 'supervisors') also present verbatim; statute controls and supports the stricter noon July 15. Correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: No geographic distribution quota. The 1% requirement is statewide; signatures are merely organized and submitted as a separate petition per county to that county's supervisor (administrative submission rule, not a per-county/per-district minimum) (Fla. Stat. 103.021(3)).
fields.note: Verbatim 'separate petition from each county ... submitted to the supervisor of elections of the respective county' confirmed. Statute imposes only a statewide 1% and a per-county submission mechanic; no minimum-per-county quota exists. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: none
fields.note: Value is 'UNKNOWN' with ZERO citations — nothing to ground, and the draft itself concedes the prescribed petition form's terms could not be confirmed from a primary source. Unverifiable as drafted -> BLOCKED. While 103.021/99.097 impose no statutory circulator age/residency/voter requirement and verification is by supervisor signature-match, the prescribed-form requirements are not confirmed. Sent to office inquiry.

fields.reliedValue: Each petition signature is verified by the county supervisor by comparing it against the signer's signature in the voter registration system (Fla. Stat. 99.097(3)); signatures count only if on the petition form prescribed by the Division. Statute prescribes no notarization or circulator affidavit for petition signatures, BUT confirm the prescribed form imposes no attestation. The separate candidate qualifying OATH is distinct and may be notarized.
fields.note: Signature-match verification quote and 'petition form prescribed by the division' quote both verbatim-confirmed in 99.097. The affirmative facts are grounded; however, 'no notarization required' is an absence-of-provision inference that turns on the content of the Division-prescribed form (not retrievable here) — same open question that BLOCKED circulatorEligibility. HOLD to the strict reading and confirm via office inquiry.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Signatures must be collected on the Division of Elections' prescribed paper petition form and submitted county-by-county to each supervisor for verification against the voter file; no state digital/remote-online signature-gathering system covers an independent presidential petition.
fields.note: 'petition form prescribed by the division' (99.097) and per-county paper-submission mechanics (103.021(3)) verbatim-confirmed. 'paper' is the conservative/strict operating assumption and is well supported; the no-digital-path claim is an absence inference but does not loosen the requirement, so AUTO-LIVE is safe (over-compliance, no ballot-access risk).

fields.reliedValue: No filing-fee/fee-in-lieu alternative to the signature petition. The candidate must prepay the supervisor 10 cents per signature checked or the actual cost, whichever is less (Fla. Stat. 99.097(4)(a)); an indigent waiver to verify at no charge is available on written certification of undue burden (99.097(4)(b)).
fields.note: 10-cents quote verbatim in 99.097(4)(a); 99.097(4)(b) indigent-waiver provision independently confirmed ('entitled to have the signatures verified at no charge'). 103.021(3) payment cross-reference ('cost of checking the petitions as prescribed in s. 99.097') is present verbatim — earlier match-miss was only a markdown hyperlink artifact around 's. 99.097'. No fee-in-lieu path is the correct strict reading. Sub-cites accurate.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — per-signature ban is limited by definition to constitutional-amendment/initiative circulators; no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: Florida's only per-signature pay prohibition (s. 104.186) applies solely to a "petition circulator as defined in s. 97.021," and s. 97.021(28) defines that term exclusively as someone collecting signatures "for the purpose of qualifying a proposed constitutional amendment for ballot placement" — so it does NOT reach independent-presidential CANDIDATE petitions under s. 103.021(3); candidate-petition compensation is unaddressed (no statute authorizes per-signature pay, but s. 99.097(6) expressly contemplates persons "paid to solicit signatures on a petition," and no circulator-residency requirement exists).
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent (no-party-affiliation) presidential candidate's nominating petition under Fla. Stat. s. 103.021(3), may the campaign lawfully compensate signature circulators on a per-signature basis, given that the per-signature prohibition in s. 104.186 is tied to the s. 97.021(28) definition of "petition circulator," which is limited to constitutional-amendment petitions — and is there any circulator residency/registration requirement?

citations.quote: 104.186 Initiative petitions; violations.—A person who compensates a petition circulator as defined in s. 97.021 based on the number of petition forms gathered commits a felony of the third degree, punishable as provided in s. 775.082, s. 775.083, or s. 775.084. This section does not prohibit employment relationships that do not base payment on the number of signatures collected.
(28) "Petition circulator" means an entity or individual who collects signatures for compensation for the purpose of qualifying a proposed constitutional amendment for ballot placement.

(3) Candidates for President and Vice President with no party affiliation may have their names printed on the general election ballots if a petition is signed by 1 percent of the registered voters of this state, as shown by the compilation by the Department of State for the last preceding general election. A separate petition from each county for which signatures are solicited shall be submitted to the supervisor of elections of the respective county no later than noon on July 15 of each presidential election year.
(6)(a) If any person is paid to solicit signatures on a petition, an undue burden oath may not subsequently be filed in lieu of paying the fee to have signatures verified for that petition.

104.185 Petitions; knowingly signing more than once; signing another person's name or a fictitious name.—(1) A person who knowingly signs a petition or petitions for a candidate, a minor political party, or an issue more than one time commits a misdemeanor of the first degree, punishable as provided in s. 775.082 or s. 775.083.
(3) Signatures may be obtained from any registered voter in Florida regardless of party affiliation or district boundaries.

voterFile.cost: Free — "There is no fee associated with this complimentary disk." The statewide monthly voter extract is provided at no charge by the Division of Elections. (An off-cycle/late request is processed as a public records request and "subject to administrative fees for processing.")
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — voter registration and voting history are public record under Florida law; the extract is "publicly available." No eligibility gate (need not be a candidate/committee/party); no political-use-only restriction on this file.
voterFile.useRestrictions: No commercial-/political-use restriction is imposed on the extract itself — the data is public record. The statutory exemptions (s. 97.0585, Fla. Stat.) carve OUT, and prohibit release of, SSN, driver-license/state-ID number, the source of the registration application (voter-registration use only), the signature image (viewable but not copyable), and records of Address Confidentiality Program participants / high-risk professionals. No anti-solicitation penalty clause specific to this file was found on the official pages.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request by email (VoterExtract address) or mail to the Division of Elections; must include contact info, phone/email, and a mailing address. Delivered as a monthly extract on disk by mail (or pick-up). Must be received >=24 hours before the scheduled monthly generation date; a one-time annual standing request can cover all monthly extracts for the calendar year. Not an instant online download, and this contact request "IS NOT available by email or online" for the address detail.
voterFile.note: Florida's statewide voter file is the monthly "Voter Extract" generated by the Division of Elections under s. 98.0981, Fla. Stat. It is free and public but is not an instant web download — you submit an email/mail request and receive a disk by mail on a fixed monthly schedule (12 dates published for 2026). The standard file includes name, residential address, party affiliation, and active/inactive registration status, which is sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters.

citations.quote: The Division of Elections generates a publicly available voter extract file. This information is pulled from the Florida Voter Registration System and data reported by the 67 county Supervisors of Elections. The voter data includes registration information on all voters registered (active and inactive) up to the last day of the month prior to the date the file is generated.
There is no fee associated with this complimentary disk.

A request to receive a monthly extract must be received no later than 24 hours before the scheduled date of availability to allow sufficient time to process. ... Please include in your request, contact information and either a phone number or an email address, and a mailing address to receive the disk by mail. This information IS NOT available by email or online.
Any request thereafter must be submitted to [email] and will be processed as a public records request and subject to administrative fees for processing.

Once filed, with few exceptions, all voter registration information is public record including your name, address, date of birth, party affiliation, phone number, and email address. Your social security number, your driver’s license number or state identification card number, and the source of your voter registration application CANNOT be released or disclosed to the public under any circumstances, and can only be used for voter registration purposes. Your signature can be viewed, but not copied. Section 97.0585, Fla. Stat.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — the rule binds signers only. Florida's independent (No Party Affiliation) presidential petition (Fla. Stat. 103.021(3)) uses self-contained voter-completed petition cards (Form DS-DE 18A) verified directly against the voter registration system. Neither Fla. Stat. 103.021, 99.095, 99.097, nor Fla. Admin. Code 1S-2.045 recognizes a "circulator" role or imposes any age
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a registered voter in the county for which the petition is submitted (Fla. Stat. 103.021(3); Rule 1S-2.045(5)) and must personally provide name, address, voter-registration number or DOB, original ink signature, and date. CIRCULATOR: not a recognized role — no age, voter-registration, citizenship, residency, or affidavit/witness requirement exists; Florida uses no circulator verific
circulator.note: Florida is a "no-circulator" state for candidate/presidential petitions. The independent presidential petition (Fla. Stat. 103.021(3)) is a set of individual, voter-completed petition cards (Form DS-DE 18A, prescribed by Fla. Admin. Code 1S-2.045), each submitted to the supervisor of elections of the county where the signer is registered and verified against the voter registration system. Florida law contains NO circulator affidavit, NO witness/oath by the collector, and NO eligibility requireme

citations.quote: Candidates for President and Vice President with no party affiliation may have their names printed on the general election ballots if a petition is signed by 1 percent of the registered voters of this state, as shown by the compilation by the Department of State for the last preceding general election. A separate petition from each county for which signatures are solicited shall be submitted to the supervisor of elections of the respective county no later than noon on July 15 of each presidential election year. The supervisor shall check the names and, on or before the date of the primary election, shall certify the number shown as registered voters of the county.

citations.field: signerVsCirculator (signer must be registered voter; verification is signature-vs-registration only, no circulator step)
citations.quote: If all other requirements for the petition are met, a signature on a petition shall be verified and counted as valid for a registered voter if, after comparing the signature on the petition and the signature of the registered voter in the voter registration system, the supervisor is able to determine that the petition signer is the same as the registered voter, even if the name on the petition is not in substantially the same form as in the voter registration system.

citations.field: circulatorMinAge / minorCanCirculate (exhaustive validity criteria contain no circulator/witness/collector requirement)
citations.quote: In addition to the above requirements, the supervisor of elections shall not verify as valid a signature on a candidate petition form unless all of the following information is contained on the petition form: 1. The voter's name; 2. The voter's address (including city and county); 3. The voter's complete voter registration number or date of birth ... 4. The voter's original, ink signature; and, 5. The date the voter signed the petition ... as recorded by the voter.

citations.field: circulatorMustBeRegisteredVoter / circulatorMustBeStateResident (only the SIGNER's registration/residence can invalidate; nothing about the collector)
citations.quote: The petition is signed by a voter who is not a registered voter in the county, district, or other geographical area represented by the office sought ... at both the time of signing and verification of the petition.

citations.field: petition is a voter-completed card; non-signature entries may be pre-filled by anyone (no circulator role)
citations.quote: Except for the signature of the voter and date the voter signs the form, the entries on Forms DS-DE 18A, 18B and 104 may be completed prior to the voter signing and dating the form.

signaturesHeadline: 45,000 valid signatures (statutory cap; 1% of 2022 gov vote = 59,123, so 45,000 controls). 500 from each of half the CDs. Collect a large buffer.
deadlineHeadline: RECOMPUTE PER CYCLE: file 23 weeks before Election Day (was May 28, 2024). 2028 date NOT yet live — do not rely on the 2024 date.
riskNote: 3 of 7 fields on CONSERVATIVE-HOLD: deadline is a stale 2024 date that must be recomputed for the live cycle; circulator eligibility relies on a court injunction (use NY-registered witnesses to be safe); fee-alternative is an unverified absence claim. Core signature/distribution/capture/witness rules are AUTO-LIVE.
sosInquiries: For an independent presidential nominating petition in the upcoming presidential cycle, what is the exact first and last day to file the petition with the State Board of Elections (the 24-week / 23-week window dates), and the exact deadline for each candidate's certificate of acceptance?

Is there any filing fee, bond, or other alternative to the 45,000-signature independent nominating petition for a presidential candidate, or is ballot access for an independent presidential candidate available by petition only?

Is the Schmidt v. Kosinski permanent injunction (allowing non-registered/non-resident U.S.-citizen petition witnesses to sign the appended statement) still in full effect for the upcoming cycle, and will signatures witnessed under it be accepted without challenge?

fields.reliedValue: 45,000 valid signatures, OR 1% of the total votes (excluding blank/void) cast for Governor at the last gubernatorial election, whichever is less. 45,000 is the operative cap: 1% of the 2022 gubernatorial vote (5,912,286 total) = 59,123, which exceeds 45,000, so 45,000 controls for the 2024 cycle. Collected by an independent body on a single statewide independent nominating petition naming the presidential/VP candidates and the slate of electors. Recomputes each cycle.
fields.note: Citations verbatim at ELN 6-142, SBOE running-president, and ballot-access.org. Independently re-derived: 1% of 5,912,286 (2022 NY gov total, Hochul 3,140,415 + Zeldin 2,762,581 + minor) = 59,122.86 > 45,000, so the 'whichever is less' clause yields 45,000. Confirmed correct and current for 2024; will remain 45,000 unless gubernatorial turnout falls below ~4.5M. Strict guidance: collect a wide buffer (Diane Sare filed 70,000 for the 45,000 requirement).

fields.reliedValue: Statutory rule (ELN 6-158(9)): an independent nominating petition for an office filled at the general election must be filed not earlier than 24 weeks and not later than 23 weeks preceding the election. STRICTEST READING: treat the 23-weeks-before-Election-Day Tuesday as the hard filing deadline. For 2024 that was May 28, 2024; for any future presidential cycle this MUST be recomputed and confirmed with the SBOE before relying on it. Each named candidate (incl. president/VP) must file an acknowledged certificate of acceptance no later than the 3rd day after the last day to file the petition. EL 1-106 weekend/holiday rollover may apply.
fields.note: All citations verbatim (ELN 6-158(9), SBOE running-president acceptance rule, ballot-access.org May 28 corroboration). Date arithmetic independently confirmed: Nov 5, 2024 minus 23 weeks = Tue May 28, 2024 (= 23rd Tuesday preceding); minus 24 weeks = May 21, 2024. HOLD because the value is a PAST 2024-cycle date and the rule recomputes each cycle — the live actionable deadline for the next presidential election is not yet established and must be recomputed/confirmed. Over-comply: do not let a candidate rely on the stale 2024 date.

fields.reliedValue: Yes - a congressional-district distribution requirement applies. At least 500 signatures, OR 1% of enrolled voters (whichever is less), must come from each of one-half of the state's congressional districts (i.e., from at least half of NY's congressional districts). In practice 500 per district controls, since 1% of enrolled voters in any CD far exceeds 500.
fields.note: Verbatim at ELN 6-142(1) and SBOE running-president. Independently confirmed: 1% of enrolled voters per CD vastly exceeds 500, so 500 is the binding per-district minimum across at least half the CDs. Current and correct.

fields.reliedValue: STRICTEST DEFENSIBLE: use witnesses who are duly qualified (registered) New York voters who have not previously signed a petition for another candidate for the same office. (Signers must themselves be NY registered voters qualified to vote for the office who have not signed a designating petition for it.) The Schmidt v. Kosinski permanent injunction (EDNY) expands eligibility to non-registered/non-resident U.S. citizens who sign the special appended statement, and the SBOE currently publishes this expansion - but a campaign should NOT bet ballot access on the injunction; default to registered-NY-voter witnesses.
fields.note: Base rule grounded verbatim at ELN 6-140(1)(b); the Schmidt expansion grounded verbatim at SBOE petition-information and running-president pages (SBOE itself states the witness requirements 'have changed' pursuant to the injunction). HOLD because correctness of the EXPANDED rule hinges on the continued force of a court injunction that retrieval cannot guarantee for a future cycle; the over-comply posture for ballot access is to rely only on the base statutory witness rule. SOS inquiry added to confirm the injunction's continued effect.

fields.reliedValue: Each petition sheet requires a signed Statement of Witness by a qualified witness, accepted for all purposes as the equivalent of an affidavit and carrying perjury penalties (ELN 6-140(1)(b)). In lieu of the witness statement, the sheet may instead bear a notarial acknowledgment signed by a notary public or commissioner of deeds who swore each signer (ELN 6-140(2)). A non-resident/non-registered witness (per Schmidt v. Kosinski) uses a special appended statement instead. No separate notarization is required when a qualified witness signs.
fields.note: Both quotes verbatim in ELN 6-140 (the witness-affidavit-equivalent clause and the 'In lieu of the signed statement of a witness... notary public or commissioner of deeds shall be accepted' clause). Stable statutory text; current and correct. The witness-OR-notary alternative is squarely on the face of the statute.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Petitions must be on paper, signed in ink, and circulated by hand; the State Board of Elections expressly states electronic signatures are not acceptable. No digital/online petition system covers an independent presidential nominating petition in New York.
fields.note: Verbatim at SBOE petition-information ('These forms can be printed and filled out by hand. Electronic signatures are not acceptable.') and ELN 6-140(1)(a) ('Each sheet... shall be signed in ink'). Current and correct; no e-signature path exists.

fields.reliedValue: Treat ballot access as petition-only. Assume NO filing-fee, bond, or other alternative to the 45,000-signature independent nominating petition exists for an independent presidential candidate; do not rely on any fee shortcut. (Confirm absence directly with the SBOE.)
fields.note: The cited running-president quote describes petition CONTENTS, not fees - it does not state 'no fee alternative,' so the value's core assertion ('None') is NOT grounded by the quote provided; it is an absence/negative inference. The conclusion is very likely correct (NY independent presidential access is petition-only with no statutory fee-in-lieu), but a negative cannot be verified verbatim from the supplied citation. HOLD and confirm with SBOE. The strict posture (petition-only) is conservative regardless.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: NO per-signature (prohibited) — pay hourly/bonus; advocate to legalize
compensationModel.model: per-signature prohibited (candidate/independent petitions) — hourly/salary OK; circulator must be NY voter
compensationModel.note: Candidate-petition compensation IS specifically addressed. NY Election Law 17-122(4) makes it a misdemeanor to pay circulators for procuring signatures on "an independent nominating petition for public office" on a per-name basis or at a fixed amount per name, and 17-122(5) criminalizes even representing such per-name pay; paying circulators by hourly/salary/flat fee is not prohibited. Separately, 6-140(1)(b) requires each petition sheet to be witnessed by "a duly qualified voter of the State of New York" (or a NY notary/commissioner of deeds), so circulators must be NY voters and a non-resident national paid pool could not validly witness presidential independent petitions.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent (unaffiliated) candidate's nominating petition for President of the United States in New York, does Election Law 17-122 prohibit paying petition circulators per signature, and is paying circulators on an hourly or salaried basis (with non-per-name bonuses) permitted? Also, must the subscribing witness/circulator be a duly qualified New York voter under 6-140?

citations.quote: Any person who: ... 4. Pays or agrees to pay money or other valuable consideration, to any person for his services in canvassing for or otherwise procuring the signatures of voters to a petition for the designation of a candidate or candidates for party nomination or for election to a party position to be voted for at a primary election, or to a petition for opportunity to ballot at a primary election, or to an independent nominating petition for public office, upon the basis of the number of names to such petition procured by such person, or at a fixed amount per name; or, 5. Represents to any person as an inducement for signing ... an independent nominating petition for public office, that the person soliciting such signature is to be compensated upon the basis of the number of names procured by such a person, or at a fixed amount per name; ... is guilty of a misdemeanor.

There shall be appended at the bottom of each sheet a signed statement of a witness who is a duly qualified voter of the state and who has not previously signed a petition for another candidate for the same office. ... I, ...... (name of witness) state: I am a duly qualified voter of the State of New York and now reside at ......... (residence address). Each of the individuals whose names are subscribed to this petition sheet ... subscribed the same in my presence on the dates above indicated

voterFile.cost: No per-record price published. FOIL paper copies are $0.25/page; statutory printed registration lists are "sold at a charge not exceeding the cost of publication" (Election Law 5-602(3)). The electronic statewide voter data file is provided in response to the FOIL request; obtain a quote — historically a nominal/low fee. Treat as varies — request quote.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Effectively restricted to election-related users. Any requester may ask, but the request must certify an "elections purpose" under Election Law 3-103(5); the SBOE notes this is read broadly to include "campaigning, voter outreach, fundraising and academic research." Petition signature verification by a candidate/committee clearly qualifies.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Election Law 3-103(5): information derived from voter registration records "shall not be used for non-election purposes." Requester must affirm election-purpose use; non-election use (e.g. commercial solicitation) is prohibited.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written FOIL request submitted via the SBOE GovQA "FOIL Access Center" portal to the Records Access Officer (Director of Public Information). The request must include a written statement that the data will be used for an "elections purpose" in compliance with Election Law 3-103(5). Statewide file delivered as a zipped ASCII comma-delimited file with a file layout. Agency must respond within 5 business days.
voterFile.note: New York does not offer an instant public download of the statewide voter file; it is obtained from the NYS Board of Elections via a written FOIL request that certifies an "elections purpose" per Election Law 3-103(5). The full statewide file (millions of records, ASCII comma-delimited with name, residence address, and registration/enrollment status) is sufficient to match and verify petition signers, but pricing for the electronic file is not published online — request a quote.

citations.quote: New York State Election Law Sec 3-103(5) prohibits using information derived from voter registration records for non-election purposes. When submitting a request for voter registration data it must include a statement that the information requested will be used for an “elections purpose” in compliance with New York State Election Law Section 3-103(5). (An “elections purpose” has traditionally been interpreted broadly and among other things includes, campaigning, voter outreach, fundraising and academic research.)

Statewide and multidistrict files contain a large number of records - the entire state exceeds millions of records - requesters must have software capable of handling very large files. The file will be provided in ASCII, comma delimited format and a file layout will be provided. The file will be zipped using a MS-compatible compression program.

FOIL requests for records maintained by the New York State Board of Elections must be submitted in writing to the Board's Records Access Officer. Requests should be submitted online by using the link below. The New York State Board of Elections uses the GovQA platform to receive, process, and respond to FOIL requests.
When requested, copies will be made by this agency at a charge of $ .25 per page.
The information contained in the statewide voter registration list shall not be used for non-election purposes.
Other copies shall be sold at a charge not exceeding the cost of publication.

circulator.ageRule: No explicit age number is stated in the petition-witness statute itself, but NY Election Law § 6-140(1)(b) requires the witness (circulator) to be "a duly qualified voter of the state." Under § 5-102(1), a "qualified voter" must be "eighteen years of age or over." Therefore the circulator must be at least 18. (Alternative: a notary public or commissioner of deeds may witness in lieu of a qualified
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a "registered voter of the political unit for which a nomination... is being made" (§ 6-140(1)(a); § 6-138(1)) — i.e. registered within the relevant district. CIRCULATOR (the "subscribing witness" who appends the statement of witness) must be "a duly qualified voter of the state" of New York (§ 6-140(1)(b)) — a broader statewide registration requirement that, via § 5-102, also impor
circulator.note: New York requires the circulator (statutorily the "subscribing witness" who signs the statement of witness appended to each petition sheet) of an independent nominating petition to be "a duly qualified voter of the state" of New York, per Election Law § 6-140(1)(b). This is a real, affirmative statutory circulator requirement (not silence) — hence AUTO-LIVE. "Duly qualified voter of the state" means a registered voter who, under § 5-102(1), is a US citizen, at least 18 years old, and a NY reside

citations.quote: There shall be appended at the bottom of each sheet a signed statement of a witness who is a duly qualified voter of the state and who has not previously signed a petition for another candidate for the same office.
I, ...................... (name of witness) state: I am a duly qualified voter of the State of New York and now reside at ......................................... (residence address).

citations.field: circulatorAlternative (notary/commissioner of deeds in lieu of qualified-voter witness)
citations.quote: 2. In lieu of the signed statement of a witness who is a duly qualified voter of the state, the following statement signed by a notary public or commissioner of deeds shall be accepted: On the dates above indicated before me personally came each of the voters whose signatures appear on this petition sheet ... who, being by me duly sworn, each for himself or herself, said that the foregoing statement made and subscribed by him or her, was true.

I, the undersigned, do hereby state that I am a registered voter of the political unit for which a nomination for public office is hereby being made, that my present place of residence is truly stated opposite my signature hereto...

citations.field: signerVsCirculator (signer must be registered voter of the political unit)
citations.quote: Independent nominations for public office shall be made by a petition containing the signatures of registered voters of the political unit for which a nomination is made who are registered to vote.

citations.field: circulatorMinAge / circulatorMustBeUSCitizen (definition of qualified voter imported into the witness requirement)
citations.quote: No person shall be qualified to register for and vote at any election unless he is a citizen of the United States and is or will be, on the day of such election, eighteen years of age or over, and a resident of this state and of the county, city or village for a minimum of thirty days next preceding such election.

signaturesHeadline: President: 5,000 valid signatures (court-fixed) + $200 fee — AUTO-LIVE
deadlineHeadline: Deadline HOLD: not a fixed Aug 1 — first day of Aug, rolled to next business day (Aug 3 in 2026); confirm per cycle
riskNote: All 8 fields grounded verbatim, but every citation is the 2024 (presidential-cycle) DOS instructions; today is 2026 (a midterm with no presidential race) so the next live cycle is 2028. Substantive rules (5,000 sigs, no distribution, paper-only, fee-plus-sigs, circulator no-residency/no-affidavit) re-confirmed unchanged in the 2026 instructions, but the date-specific deadline is stale and the circulator age/registration qualification is unknown — these two are held.
sosInquiries: For an independent/political-body candidate for President of the United States, what is the exact last day to file statewide nomination papers in the next presidential cycle (2028), given that the deadline is administered as the first day of August rolled to the next business day (e.g., Aug 1 in 2024 but Aug 3 in 2026)?

Other than the eliminated in-state residency and affidavit requirements, does Pennsylvania impose any minimum-age or voter-registration qualification on a circulator of political-body nomination papers?

fields.reliedValue: 5,000 valid signatures of qualified registered electors of Pennsylvania for President (statewide nomination papers under 25 P.S. § 2911 et seq.). The 2% statutory calculation in § 2911(b) is not enforced per Constitution Party of Pa. v. Cortés (E.D. Pa. Feb. 1, 2018); DOS applies a fixed 5,000 for President, U.S. Senator, and Governor, and 2,500 for all other statewide offices.
fields.note: Quote verbatim in NASS PDF (PA section) and in the PB 2024 instructions both narratively ("...except for the offices of President of the United States, United States Senator, or Governor, which require 5,000 signatures") and in the signature table ("President of the United States 5,000"). Re-confirmed UNCHANGED in the active 2026 DOS instructions (same 5,000 language) and independently corroborated by Ballotpedia. Court-order basis stated correctly.

fields.reliedValue: Do NOT treat the deadline as a fixed "August 1." The statutory/consent-decree deadline is the first day of August, administered by DOS and rolled to the next business day when Aug 1 falls on a weekend/holiday: it was August 1, 2024 (Thu) for the 2024 presidential cycle but August 3, 2026 (Mon, because Aug 1, 2026 is a Saturday) for the 2026 cycle. For the next presidential cycle (2028), Aug 1 is a Tuesday, so the operative date is likely Aug 1, 2028 — but this MUST be confirmed against DOS's published 2028 calendar before relied upon. Filing is with the Bureau of Elections / Secretary of the Commonwealth, Room 201 North Office Building, Harrisburg, by 5:00 P.M. on that date. Strictest posture: assume the earliest plausible date (first business day of August), never assume any date later than the first of August is safe.
fields.note: Quotes are grounded verbatim in PB 2024 instructions, but they are 2024-specific (circulation Feb 14–Aug 1, 2024). The draft generalizes this to "August 1 of the election year," which is FALSE: the active 2026 DOS instructions set the deadline to August 3, 2026 (Aug 1 is a Saturday). The deadline recomputes per cycle and is calendar-dependent; correctness for a current/2028 independent presidential candidate cannot be settled by retrieving the 2024 document. Held to strictest interpretation.

fields.reliedValue: None for President. There is no per-congressional-district or per-county minimum for a statewide presidential nomination paper — only the single statewide total of 5,000. Signers are organized by county solely as a clerical matter (each two-sided sheet must contain signers from one county, with that county noted); there is no per-county/per-district minimum. The historical "severe distribution requirement" applied only to statewide STATE offices, not to President.
fields.note: "County of Signers" quote and the 5,000 table entry are verbatim in PB 2024. The 2026 instructions confirm the same county-per-page clerical rule with no per-county minimum for statewide. Ballotpedia citation note 19 independently confirms the distribution requirement attaches to statewide STATE office, not President. No distribution minimum for President is correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: No in-state residency requirement and no circulator affidavit requirement for political-body nomination papers (covers independent/minor-party presidential papers) — both eliminated by amendment to 25 P.S. § 2911(d); the circulator need not be a PA resident. UNKNOWN whether any minimum-age or voter-registration qualification applies to the circulator: DOS instructions specify only that residency and the affidavit were removed and are silent on circulator age/registration. Strictest posture: do not assume any person may circulate; treat circulator age/registration as an open question pending DOS confirmation.
fields.note: "...eliminated the in-state residency requirement and the affidavit requirement for circulators of nomination papers for all political bodies" is verbatim in PB 2024 and re-confirmed verbatim in the 2026 instructions. The grounded claim is correct and current. Held only because the draft itself flags an UNKNOWN (circulator age/registration) that retrieval cannot resolve; an SOS inquiry is added rather than asserting "anyone may circulate."

fields.reliedValue: Circulator: a "Statement of Circulator" must be filled in and signed on every sheet after all signatures are obtained, but the notarized circulator affidavit was eliminated (no notarization of the circulator statement). Candidate: must file a Candidate's Affidavit, normally notarized; under Act 2020-15 (signed Apr 20, 2020) the candidate may instead submit an unsworn Candidate's Declaration along with the affidavit in lieu of notarization.
fields.note: "Every sheet... Statement of Circulator filled in and signed" and the Act 2020-15 unsworn-statement language are verbatim in PB 2024; the candidate notarization/declaration language is verbatim on the live DOS third-party page. All re-confirmed UNCHANGED in the active 2026 instructions. Correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Signatures must be collected on the official two-sided printed nomination paper (legal-size 8.5 x 14, printed 2-sided head-to-head); each signer personally signs with printed name, residence, and date (wet ink). Filing is in person or by mail; no online/digital signature-collection system exists for political-body/presidential nomination papers — DOS provides only blank printable forms.
fields.note: "Nomination papers must be printed 2-sided on 8 1/2 x 14\" paper, head to head" verbatim on the live DOS third-party page; "Each signer must personally sign... printed name, place of residence and the date of signing" verbatim in PB 2024. The 2026 instructions confirm "Pages are double-sided." Paper-only with no e-signature option is correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: $200.00 filing fee for President, required IN ADDITION TO (not in lieu of) the 5,000 signatures — there is no signature-vs-fee alternative; both are required. The fee must accompany the nomination papers, paid by certified check or money order payable to the "Commonwealth of Pennsylvania" (no personal checks or cash).
fields.note: "For Statewide offices (President of the United States...) — $200.00" and the CERTIFIED CHECK/MONEY ORDER payment language are verbatim in PB 2024; NASS confirms "a $200 fee, and nomination papers containing the signatures of 5000 qualified electors." The 2026 instructions and the live DOS page confirm the certified-check/money-order requirement. PA has no fee-vs-signature substitution; fee is additive. Correct and current.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: Pennsylvania's Election Code provisions governing independent/political-body PRESIDENTIAL nomination papers (25 P.S. § 2911) and the Department of State's 2025 nomination-paper instructions are silent on circulator compensation: they do NOT permit or prohibit paying circulators, and say nothing about per-signature pay — so candidate-petition compensation is genuinely unaddressed, not carried over from initiative law (PA has no statewide initiative process and is not on Ballotpedia/NCSL's per-signature ban/permit lists). Circulator RESIDENCY, however, is clearly addressed: 2019 Act 77 amended § 2911(d) to eliminate the in-state residency requirement for circulators of nomination papers, and the statement may be made by "some person, not necessarily a signer," so a national paid pool is not barred by a residency rule on these papers.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent/political-body candidate for President filing nomination papers under 25 P.S. § 2911, may the campaign compensate circulators on a per-signature basis, or is only hourly/salary/flat-fee compensation permitted?

citations.quote: Each sheet shall have appended thereto the statement of some person, not necessarily a signer, and not necessarily the same person on each sheet, setting forth, subject to the penalties of 18 Pa.C.S. § 4904 (relating to unsworn falsification to authorities)--(1) Deleted by 2019, Oct. 31, P.L. 552, No. 77, § 3, imd. effective;

In addition to the party nominations made at primaries, nomination of candidates for any public office may also be made by nomination papers signed by qualified electors of the State, or of the electoral district for which the nomination is made, and filed in the manner herein provided.

Statement of Circulator - Every nomination paper page must have the "Statement of Circulator" filled in and signed after all signatures have been obtained. Recent amendments to section 951(d) of the Pennsylvania Election Code, 25 P.S. § 2911(d), have eliminated the in-state residency requirement and the affidavit requirement for circulators of nomination papers for all political bodies.

Rather, candidates for all statewide offices must gather 2,500 signatures, except for the offices of President of the United States, United States Senator, or Governor, which require 5,000 signatures.

Pay-per-signature is a method of compensating signature gatherers, also known as circulators, who collect signatures for ballot initiatives and veto referendums, involving paying signature gatherers at a rate based on the number of signatures collected.

voterFile.cost: $20.00 flat — for one county, multiple counties, or all counties statewide (single per-export fee)
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — any "requestor" may purchase the Full Voter Export online; no candidate/committee/party eligibility gate. Use is, however, legally limited to elections, political activities, and law enforcement (see useRestrictions). A campaign/committee verifying petition signatures qualifies squarely under "elections / political activities."
voterFile.useRestrictions: Non-commercial use only. The requestor must affirm that the data "will not be used for commercial or other purposes, except purposes related to elections, political activities and law enforcement, as required by 25 Pa.C.S. sections 1207(b) & 1404(c)(2)." Commercial use of the registration list is prohibited by statute and carries penalties under the PA Election Code.
voterFile.requestProcess: Online self-service purchase at the PA Department of State's PAVoterServices "Purchase PA Full Voter Export" page: select county/counties (or Select All), agree to the affirmation of permitted (non-commercial) use, pay the $20.00 by card; the export file is then delivered by email to the address provided. No application approval, notarized affidavit, or paper form is required for the statewide download (county-only subsets can alternatively be obtained from the county election office, also ~$20 electronic).
voterFile.note: Pennsylvania sells the statewide "Full Voter Export" (the SURE Public Information List) online for a flat $20.00 via the Department of State PAVoterServices portal, delivered by email after payment. It contains name, residential + mailing address, registration status, party, and voter/vote history — everything needed to match petition signers to registered voters — and is open to the public but restricted to non-commercial (election/political/law-enforcement) use, which covers a campaign's signature verification.

citations.quote: As provided by 25 Pa.C.S. Section 1404(b)(1) (relating to Public Information Lists), as well as the SURE Regulations at 4 Pa. Code Section 184.14(b) (relating to Public Information Lists), the Department of State will provide the Full Voter Export List to requestors.

This version of the Public Information List is a full export of all voters in the county and contains the following fields: voter ID number, name, sex, date of birth, date registered, status (i.e., active or inactive), date status last changed, party, residential address, mailing address, polling place, date last voted, all districts in which the voter votes (i.e., congressional, legislative, school district, etc.), voter history, and date the voter's record was last changed.
The cost of the Full Voter Export list is 20.00. Upon successful payment an email will be sent to the provided email address.
Effective July 12, 2006, the Full Voter Export is available for a charge of $20.00 for a request for any county or all counties in the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania.

I affirm that any information obtained from the records requested from the Department of State will not be used for commercial or other purposes, except purposes related to elections, political activities and law enforcement, as required by 25 Pa.C.S. sections 1207(b) & 1404(c)(2).

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — the nomination-paper statute (25 P.S. § 2911(d)) imposes no age qualification on the person who appends the circulator's statement. The statute requires only "the statement of some person, not necessarily a signer." Age qualifications in the Election Code attach to SIGNERS (who must be "qualified electors," i.e., voting-eligible, generally 18+), not to the circulato
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a "qualified elector of the State or district" (registered voter, resident of PA) per § 2911(a),(c). CIRCULATOR of an independent/political-body nomination PAPER need only be "some person, not necessarily a signer" (§ 2911(d)) — no registered-voter, no party, no statutory age requirement; and the former in-state-residency element was struck down as unconstitutional (no resident requ
circulator.note: For an INDEPENDENT/political-body presidential candidate, the correct track is the nomination PAPER under 25 P.S. § 2911 (Pennsylvania Election Code § 951) — NOT the partisan-primary nomination PETITION (§ 2869). This distinction is decisive: (1) § 2911(d) requires each sheet to have appended "the statement of some person, not necessarily a signer, and not necessarily the same person on each sheet." The circulator need NOT be a signer, need NOT be a qualified elector/registered voter, need NOT b

citations.quote: Each sheet shall have appended thereto the statement of some person, not necessarily a signer, and not necessarily the same person on each sheet, setting forth, subject to the penalties of 18 Pa.C.S. § 4904 (relating to unsworn falsification to authorities)--... (2) the person's residence, giving city, borough or township with street and number, if any; (3) that the signers signed with full knowledge of the contents of the nomination paper; ... and (7) that, to the best of the person's knowledge and belief, the signers are qualified electors of the State, or of the electoral district, as the case may be.

nomination of candidates for any public office may also be made by nomination papers signed by qualified electors of the State... Each person signing a nomination paper shall declare therein that he is a qualified elector of the State or district, as the case may be.

citations.field: contrast — primary PETITION circulator must be registered party elector (NOT the independent track)
citations.quote: Each sheet shall have appended thereto the statement of the circulator of each sheet, setting forth, subject to the penalties of 18 Pa.C.S. § 4904 ...--(a) that he or she is a qualified elector of the Commonwealth, who is duly registered and enrolled as a member of the party designated in said petition...

On March 2, 2015, United States District Court Judge Stewart Dalzell ruled that three of Pennsylvania's petition requirements were unconstitutional 'as applied to the Green Party and Libertarian Party.' These three requirements were as follows: 1. 'the ban on out-of-state circulators' 2. 'the requirement that each petition sheet be notarized' 3. 'the prohibition on a voter signing for more than one minor party in any given election'

signaturesHeadline: GA: do NOT rely on 7,500 — post-West v. Wittenstein strict reading is per-elector petitions (16 x 7,500 = ~120,000)
deadlineHeadline: GA: nomination petition by 12:00 Noon 2nd Tue in July; notice of candidacy 4th Mon June to following Fri (recompute each cycle)
riskNote: Highest risk is signaturesRequired: the GA Supreme Court (West v. Wittenstein, unanimous, 9/25/2024) rejected the single-7,500-petition model and held each presidential-elector candidate must file his/her own nomination petition, implying ~120,000 valid signatures under the strictest reading; treating 7,500 as the operative total would risk ballot access. The cited ballot-access source predates the decision and overstated certainty.
sosInquiries: For an independent/political-body presidential candidate in 2028, after West v. Wittenstein (S25A0178), how many nomination petitions and total valid signatures does the SOS require: one statewide petition of 7,500, or a separate petition in the name of each of Georgia's 16 presidential-elector candidates (i.e. 16 x 7,500)?

Confirm the precise filing mechanics post-West v. Wittenstein: must each presidential-elector candidate file a nomination petition under his/her own name, and may the candidates be listed together on one petition slate under O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(c), or are separate per-elector petitions required?

What is the exact qualifying fee (or 'reasonable fee') the Secretary of State will fix for the office of presidential elector / President for the upcoming cycle, and is it charged per elector or per presidential ticket?

Confirm whether the court-imposed 7,500 per-petition standard from Green Party of Georgia v. Kemp still controls the per-petition number, or whether the un-repealed 1% statute (O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(b)) is applied to any petition the SOS now requires.

fields.reliedValue: STRICT: assume a separate nomination petition in the name of EACH of Georgia's 16 presidential-elector candidates, each requiring 7,500 valid signatures = up to ~120,000 valid signatures total. Do NOT plan on a single 7,500-signature statewide petition. (Per-petition number 7,500 derives from Green Party of Georgia v. Kemp; the underlying statute O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(b) still nominally states 1%. West v. Wittenstein, Ga. S. Ct. S25A0178, 9/25/2024, unanimously held each elector-candidate is a 'candidate' who must file his/her own petition.) Recompute and re-verify with the SOS each cycle.
fields.note: All four citations ground verbatim. But the draft value ('7,500' as the operative statewide total) is NO LONGER safe. The cited ballot-access.org 9/24 article only reported that the GA Supreme Court HEARD the case and 'oral argument went badly' — it did not report a holding, yet the draft asserted the elector-petition rule as settled. On 9/25/2024 the court ruled UNANIMOUSLY (West v. Wittenstein, affirming an ALJ and two Fulton County Superior Court judges) that West and De la Cruz were disqualified because their 16 presidential electors did not each file a separate petition; the court expressly rejected the argument that the 7,500 standard caps the requirement, and the parties/court acknowledged the strict reading implies ~120,000 signatures (16 x 7,500). Strictest defensible posture for an independent presidential run = per-elector petitions. Interpretation-dependent (court invited legislative clarification; 1% statute un-repealed).

fields.reliedValue: Nomination petition (O.C.G.A. 21-2-132(e)): file no earlier than 9:00 A.M. on the fourth Monday in June and no later than 12:00 Noon on the second Tuesday in July immediately prior to the general election. Notice of candidacy for President/VP electors (O.C.G.A. 21-2-132(d)(1)): file between 9:00 A.M. on the fourth Monday in June and 12:00 Noon on the Friday following the fourth Monday in June. DATES RECOMPUTE EACH CYCLE.
fields.note: Both quotes ground verbatim against O.C.G.A. 21-2-132 (FindLaw, current as of 3/28/2024). Subsection (e) sets the petition window (fourth Mon June to 12:00 Noon second Tue July); (d)(1) sets the notice-of-candidacy window (fourth Mon June to 12:00 Noon Friday after fourth Mon June). Mechanical date rule, non-interpretive. The 2024 instance (July 9) is a correct illustration but is cycle-specific; rely on the statutory rule, not the hardcoded date.

fields.reliedValue: No geographic distribution requirement: no per-congressional-district or per-county signature minimums. Signatures are a statewide total. Note the sheet-sorting rule (O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(d)): signers resident in different counties/municipalities must sign different sheets — an affidavit/sorting requirement, not a per-area minimum. SEPARATELY note (cross-ref signaturesRequired): post-West v. Wittenstein the petition burden is multiplied per presidential elector, which is a structural, not geographic, multiplier.
fields.note: Both quotes ground verbatim against 21-2-170(b) and (d). It is accurate that Georgia imposes no congressional-district or county distribution minimum on the nomination petition; the only county-linked rule is the different-sheets-per-county sorting/affidavit requirement. The per-elector multiplication identified in West v. Wittenstein is captured under signaturesRequired and is not a 'distribution' requirement, so this field's value stands.

fields.reliedValue: STRICT: The petition must be circulated/filed on behalf of, and in the name of, EACH presidential-elector candidate (not the presidential candidate personally) — West v. Wittenstein (Ga. S. Ct. S25A0178, decided 9/25/2024, unanimous) held a presidential candidate cannot petition for himself; the elector-candidates are the 'candidates' who must petition. Statute (21-2-170(d)) requires the circulator's sworn residence address (municipality, street, number) on each sheet; no explicit minimum-age or in-state-voter requirement is imposed on the circulator, but signers must be registered, eligible Georgia electors. A notary who served as a circulator of, or signed as an elector on, any sheet disqualifies those sheets. Confirm circulation/filing mechanics with the SOS.
fields.note: Statutory quotes (circulator affidavit, signer eligibility) ground verbatim against 21-2-170. The West v. Wittenstein quote grounds verbatim against the cited ballot-access.org article — BUT that article only reported a HEARING, not a decision, and was titled 'Likely to Rule.' The draft stated the elector-petition holding as settled, which the cited source did not support. Independent verification (Georgia Recorder, 9/26/2024; corroborated by Washington Times / FOX 5) confirms the court DID rule unanimously on 9/25/2024, affirming an ALJ and two Fulton Superior Court judges, that each elector-candidate must file in his/her own name. So the draft's conclusion is now correct but for the wrong/incomplete evidentiary reason. Holding turns on the statutory meaning of 'candidate' — interpretation-dependent; the court invited legislative clarification.

fields.reliedValue: Notarized circulator affidavit required on each sheet (bottom or back), subscribed and sworn before a notary public, attesting: circulator's residence address; that each signer manually signed his/her own name with full knowledge of contents; that each signature was signed within 180 days of the last filing day; and that, to the affiant's knowledge, signers are registered Georgia electors residing in the county/municipality named. No notary may sign as an elector or serve as circulator of any sheet he/she notarized — doing so disqualifies and rejects those sheets (O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(d)).
fields.note: Both quotes ground verbatim against 21-2-170(d). The draft's added detail (180-day window, county residence attestation) is independently supported by the same subsection's (d)(2)-(4) and (e). The notary-disqualification clause is quoted verbatim. Accurate and non-interpretive.

fields.reliedValue: paper (wet-ink). SOS-prescribed petition form is mandatory; each signer must manually sign and print his/her name, and each sheet must carry a notarized circulator affidavit. No remote-online/digitized signature capture is available for a Georgia independent/minor-party presidential nomination petition; collect on paper and file the bound sheets with the Secretary of State (O.C.G.A. 21-2-170(a),(d)).
fields.note: Both quotes ground verbatim against 21-2-170(a) and (d)(2). 'No forms other than the ones so prescribed shall be used,' combined with 'manually signed his or her own name' and the notarized-affidavit requirement, supports paper/wet-ink. No statutory authorization for electronic capture exists in the cited provisions. Non-interpretive.

fields.reliedValue: STRICT: Assume a qualifying fee IS owed at the time the notice of candidacy is filed, IN ADDITION TO (not in lieu of) the nomination-petition signatures. The SOS fixes the fee (3% of annual salary if salaried; if not salaried, a 'reasonable fee' not exceeding 3% of income) under O.C.G.A. 21-2-131(a)(2); the exact dollar amount for the presidential office is NOT confirmed from a primary source — treat as owed and confirm with the SOS. A pauper's affidavit may substitute for the FEE only, and only if accompanied by a qualifying petition signed by one-fourth of 1% of statewide registered voters (O.C.G.A. 21-2-132(g)-(h)). The fee/pauper route does NOT replace the nomination-petition signature requirement.
fields.note: All three quotes ground verbatim (21-2-131(a)(2); 21-2-132(g); 21-2-132(h)(1)). The draft's structural conclusion (fee is additional; pauper's affidavit replaces only the fee and itself requires a 1/4-of-1% qualifying petition) is correct on the statute. Held conservative because: (1) the draft itself flags the exact 2024 fee amount as UNKNOWN — an unconfirmed value should not auto-go-live; (2) whether/how the fee applies to the office of presidential elector vs the presidential ticket, and its interaction with the per-elector petition structure from West v. Wittenstein, is interpretation-dependent. Confirm amount and applicability with the SOS.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: Georgia's candidate/independent-presidential nomination-petition statute (O.C.G.A. § 21-2-170) regulates petition form, the 180-day circulation window, and the circulator's notarized affidavit, but is SILENT on compensation — it neither permits nor prohibits paying circulators per signature, so candidate-petition circulator pay is genuinely unaddressed (Georgia also has no statewide initiative process, so there is no pay-per-signature law to carry over). The statute requires the circulator to swear to a residence address but does NOT explicitly require the circulator to be a Georgia resident or registered voter.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nominating petition under O.C.G.A. § 21-2-170, may the campaign compensate petition circulators on a per-signature basis, and must a circulator be a Georgia resident or registered voter?
