citations.quote: Each sheet shall bear on the bottom or back thereof the affidavit of the circulator of such sheet, which affidavit must be subscribed and sworn to by such circulator before a notary public and shall set forth: (1) His or her residence address, giving municipality with street and number, if any; (2) That each signer manually signed his or her own name with full knowledge of the contents of the nomination petition; (3) That each signature on such sheet was signed within 180 days of the last day on which such petition may be filed; and (4) That, to the best of the affiant's knowledge and belief, the signers are registered electors of the state qualified to sign the petition, that their respective residences are correctly stated in the petition, and that they all reside in the county or municipality named in the affidavit.

No notary public may sign the petition as an elector or serve as a circulator of any petition which he or she notarized. Any and all sheets of a petition that have the circulator's affidavit notarized by a notary public who also served as a circulator of one or more sheets of the petition or who signed one of the sheets of the petition as an elector shall be disqualified and rejected.

No nomination petition shall be circulated prior to 180 days before the last day on which such petition may be filed, and no signature shall be counted unless it was signed within 180 days of the last day for filing the same.

Each sheet must also contain, either on the bottom or the back, an affidavit of the circulator of the sheet signed before a notary public. This affidavit must state the following: the residence address of the circulator ... No notary public is allowed to sign a petition or act as the circulator of a petition that he or she notarized.

Of the 26 states that provide for an initiative or referendum process, 16 states allow campaigns to make payments to signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected, while 10 states prohibit campaigns from paying signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected.

voterFile.cost: $485.00 flat for the statewide file (Statewide Voter List). Sub-state lists also available: County Voter List $70, US Congressional District $125, State Senate District $95, State House District $75, etc. Non-refundable; no custom orders.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — "By law, voter registration lists are available to the public." No candidate/committee/party gating and no eligibility application; anyone may purchase. (Use is restricted to non-commercial purposes, but obtaining is not eligibility-gated.)
voterFile.useRestrictions: Commercial use is prohibited. Per O.C.G.A. § 21-2-225(c) the data "may not be used by any person for commercial purposes," and under O.C.G.A. § 21-2-601 "any person who uses the list of electors ... for commercial purposes shall be guilty of a misdemeanor." Verifying petition signatures (a political, non-commercial use) is permitted. No notarized affidavit is required; the restriction is statutory rather than enforced via a sworn-statement form.
voterFile.requestProcess: Online purchase via the Georgia SOS online store (georgiasecretaryofstate.net) by credit/debit card, OR a fillable PDF "Voter List Order Form" mailed with check/money order to the Secretary of State's Office, 2 MLK Jr. Drive, Suite 802 West Tower, Atlanta, GA 30334. Not an instant download: "Current processing time is 2 weeks"; the file is delivered electronically by email as a password-protected link.
voterFile.note: Georgia sells a public Statewide Voter List for a flat $485.00 through the SOS online store; the file includes voter name, residential address, registration date and status (Active and Inactive voters) — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters. Delivery is electronic (email link) within ~1-2 weeks, not an instant download, and commercial use of the data is a misdemeanor.

citations.quote: | Statewide Voter List | $485.00 |

By law, voter registration lists are available to the public and contain the following information: voter name, residential address, mailing address if different, race, gender, registration date and last voting date. The Statewide Voter List does not include telephone numbers, date of birth, Social Security number or Drivers License number. The Statewide Voter List includes Active and Inactive Voters.

This data may not be used by any person for commercial purposes. O.C.G.A. § 21-2-225 ( c ) In accordance with O.C.G.A. § 21-2-601, any person who uses the list of electors provided for in O.C.G.A. § 21-2-225 for commercial purposes shall be guilty of a misdemeanor.
Current processing time is 2 weeks from the date the order was received by the Georgia Secretary of State's Office. Requests for expedited service will not be processed.

Prior to the acceptance and processing of any order, the purchaser/customer must remit payment for all charges in the form of a valid check, money order, or credit card (online system or in person pickup).
The file will contain a password protected link.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — rule binds signers only. O.C.G.A. § 21-2-170(d) enumerates the circulator's duties (swear the affidavit before a notary, state own residence, attest to the signatures) and imposes NO minimum age on the circulator. The only age-adjacent requirement in the statute is on the SIGNER, who must be a registered/eligible elector (effectively 18+). Statutory silence on circu
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER (O.C.G.A. § 21-2-170(b),(c)) must be a registered, eligible Georgia elector residing in the relevant county/municipality and entitled to vote in the next election; the CIRCULATOR (§ 21-2-170(d)) faces none of these — they need only swear the affidavit before a notary, disclose their own residence address, and attest to the signatures. No registered-voter, citizenship, residency, or age requ
circulator.note: Controlling source is O.C.G.A. § 21-2-170, governing nomination petitions (the vehicle by which a political body/independent slate of presidential electors gets on the GA ballot — see § 21-2-170(c) referencing "presidential electors"). The statute draws a sharp signer/circulator line. SIGNERS must be registered, eligible GA electors resident in the named county/municipality (subsec. (b) and (c)). The CIRCULATOR's duties are exhaustively listed in subsec. (d): subscribe and swear the affidavit be

citations.quote: Each sheet shall bear on the bottom or back thereof the affidavit of the circulator of such sheet, which affidavit must be subscribed and sworn to by such circulator before a notary public and shall set forth: (1) His or her residence address, giving municipality with street and number, if any; (2) That each signer manually signed his or her own name with full knowledge of the contents of the nomination petition; (3) That each signature on such sheet was signed within 180 days of the last day on which such petition may be filed; and (4) That, to the best of the affiant's knowledge and belief, the signers are registered electors of the state qualified to sign the petition, that their respective residences are correctly stated in the petition, and that they all reside in the county or municipality named in the affidavit.

A nomination petition of a candidate seeking an office which is voted upon state wide shall be signed by a number of voters equal to 1 percent of the total number of registered voters eligible to vote in the last election ... and the signers of such petition shall be registered and eligible to vote in the election at which such candidate seeks to be elected.

Each person signing a nomination petition shall declare therein that he or she is a duly qualified and registered elector of the state, county, or municipality entitled to vote in the next election for the filling of the office sought by the candidate supported by the petition and shall add to his or her signature his or her residence address ...

citations.field: circulator notary restriction (only express circulator-specific limit)
citations.quote: No notary public may sign the petition as an elector or serve as a circulator of any petition which he or she notarized. Any and all sheets of a petition that have the circulator's affidavit notarized by a notary public who also served as a circulator of one or more sheets of the petition or who signed one of the sheets of the petition as an elector shall be disqualified and rejected.

citations.field: no statutory definition of 'circulator' with eligibility criteria
citations.quote: As used in this chapter, the term: ... (7) “Elector” means any person who shall possess all of the qualifications for voting now or hereafter prescribed by the laws of this state, including applicable charter provisions, and shall have registered in accordance with this chapter.

signaturesHeadline: 5,000 valid (max 15,000); fixed number, no per-district minimum
deadlineHeadline: 90th day before general (Aug 7, 2024); recomputes each cycle
riskNote: Core petition rule (5,000 sigs, 90-day, paper wet-ink, no notary) is grounded and AUTO-LIVE; two CONSERVATIVE-HOLDs: circulator residency depends on current injunction status, and the "no fee alternative" rests on a statutory gap (president absent from the 3513.10 fee schedule) that needs SOS confirmation.
sosInquiries: For an independent presidential (president/vice-president) nominating petition filed under R.C. 3513.257, is a petition circulator currently required to be an Ohio resident, or does R.C. 3503.06(C)(1)(b) (age 18 only) plus the Citizens in Charge v. Husted injunction mean no residency requirement is enforced for the 2024/2028 cycle?

For an independent candidate for president/vice-president, is any filing fee owed at the time of filing the nominating petition under R.C. 3513.261? The 3513.10(A)/(B) fee schedule and the 3513.10(G) 'statewide office' definition do not list president/vice-president — please confirm the fee amount (if any) and that there is no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures alternative.

fields.reliedValue: 5,000 valid signatures of qualified electors (statewide minimum), with petitions rejected if they purport to contain more than 15,000 signatures. Fixed statutory number, not a percentage; does not recompute by cycle.
fields.note: R.C. 3513.257(A) quote confirmed verbatim on FindLaw ('no less than five thousand qualified electors ... no petition shall be accepted ... more than fifteen thousand signatures'). Ballotpedia quote confirmed verbatim (line 234: 'at least 5,000 qualified voters'). NASS report independently corroborates 'at least 5,000 qualified electors.' Independently re-derived and correct for an independent presidential candidate. Minor caveat: the parenthetical extending the same 5,000 threshold to a 'new/minor-party presidential candidate' is loose (minor-party ballot status runs through a separate party-formation/petition mechanism), but it does not affect the independent-candidate value relied upon.

fields.reliedValue: Not later than 4 p.m. of the 90th day before the general election. For 2024 (general election Nov. 5, 2024) this was August 7, 2024. Statement of candidacy + nominating petition + a sufficient slate of presidential electors must be filed with the Secretary of State by that time. The 90-day rule recomputes each cycle. HB2 (2024 special session) moved only the major-party certification deadline to the 65th day; it did not change the independent-candidate petition deadline.
fields.note: R.C. 3513.257 quote confirmed verbatim ('not later than four p.m. of the ninetieth day before the day of the general election'). NASS report quote confirmed verbatim (lines 846-848) and independently corroborates the 90th-day independent-candidate deadline AND the HB2 note that the 65th-day change applies only to MAJOR-PARTY certification (NASS lines 835-839), exactly as the draft claims. Date arithmetic independently verified: 90 days before Nov 5, 2024 = Aug 7, 2024 (Wednesday). Statute also confirms the presidential-elector slate must be submitted at filing. Value is current and correct; the specific date is cycle-specific and recomputes.

fields.reliedValue: None. The 5,000-signature requirement is a single statewide total; no minimum number of signatures per congressional district or per county. Mechanically, each petition paper must contain signatures of electors of only one county and separate papers are used per county, but no geographic distribution minimum is imposed.
fields.note: R.C. 3513.257(A) and R.C. 3513.261 quotes both confirmed verbatim on FindLaw. Independently re-derived: 3513.257 sets one statewide aggregate (5,000) with no district/county allocation; 3513.261's one-county-per-paper rule is a sorting/validity mechanic, not a distribution quota. Adversarial note worth flagging to staff (does not change the gate): 3513.261 further provides that if a single petition paper mixes counties, the board counts only signatures from the majority county and invalidates the rest — a paper-integrity rule, still not a geographic distribution minimum.

fields.reliedValue: A circulator of a nominating petition for presidential electors must be at least 18 years of age (R.C. 3503.06(C)(1)(b)). STRICTEST POSTURE: also assume the circulator may need to be an Ohio resident — the general residency requirement in R.C. 3503.06(C)(1)(a) is on the books and is reported as non-enforced only by virtue of the Citizens in Charge v. Husted injunction, an enforcement-status fact that retrieval cannot durably confirm as current. Plan for resident, 18+ circulators. Circulators need not be registered voters. Signers must be registered Ohio electors qualified to vote on the candidacy (R.C. 3501.38(A)).
fields.note: R.C. 3503.06(C)(1)(b) quote (age 18, presidential-elector petition) confirmed verbatim; Hamilton County 2024 guide quote confirmed verbatim (lines 47-50, including the Citizens in Charge v. Husted citations); R.C. 3501.38(A) signer-qualification quote confirmed verbatim. The age-18 and signer requirements are AUTO-LIVE-grade. HOLD is driven solely by the residency claim: whether the statutory Ohio-residency requirement is currently unenforced depends on the live status of a federal injunction, which is a currency/legal-interpretation question retrieval cannot settle to the strict standard. Defaulting to the over-comply posture (assume residency may apply) avoids any ballot-access risk.

fields.reliedValue: No notarization required. Each petition paper requires a circulator statement made UNDER PENALTY OF ELECTION FALSIFICATION (not before a notary) attesting to the number of signatures, that the circulator witnessed the affixing of every signature, that all signers were to the best of the circulator's knowledge and belief qualified to sign, and that every signature is genuine. Each petition must also carry the boldface warning that election falsification is a fifth-degree felony.
fields.note: R.C. 3501.38(E)(1) circulator-statement quote and 3501.38(J) boldface-felony-warning quote both confirmed verbatim on FindLaw. R.C. 3513.261's prescribed petition form independently shows the same circulator declaration ('under penalty of election falsification ... witnessed the affixing of every signature') with no notary jurat. Statute uses 'penalty of election falsification' rather than notarization throughout. Value is current and correct; no notary/witness requirement exists.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Signatures must be collected on the official paper nominating petition with signatures affixed in ink or indelible pencil. Ohio provides no remote-online or in-person digital signature system for an independent presidential nominating petition; the petition is wet-ink paper filed with the Secretary of State.
fields.note: R.C. 3513.261 quote ('All signatures on nominating petitions shall be written in ink or indelible pencil') and R.C. 3501.38(B) quote ('Signatures shall be affixed in ink') both confirmed verbatim on FindLaw. Independently re-derived: ink/indelible-pencil wet-signature paper petition, no statutory e-signature alternative for nominating petitions. The specific form name 'Form 3-O' is non-load-bearing color not appearing in the cited statutory quotes, but the substantive paper/wet-ink mode is fully grounded and correct.

fields.reliedValue: Treat ballot access as obtainable only via the 5,000-signature nominating petition; there is no money-payment alternative to gathering signatures for an independent presidential candidate. STRICTEST POSTURE: do not assume a fee can substitute for signatures, and confirm with the SOS whether any nominal filing fee is nonetheless owed at filing under R.C. 3513.261. The 3513.10(A)/(B) fee tables and the 3513.10(G) 'statewide office' definition do not list president/vice-president, creating an interpretive gap as to what (if any) fee 3513.261's cross-reference produces.
fields.note: R.C. 3513.261 fee-cross-reference quote and R.C. 3513.10(G) 'statewide office' definition quote both confirmed verbatim on FindLaw. Adversarial finding: the draft's CONCLUSION (no fee alternative; president absent from the fee schedule) is correct as far as the texts go, but bridging '3513.261 says pay the 3513.10(A)/(B) fee for the office' with '3513.10 lists no fee for president' requires a legal interpretation (does the office owe $0, or is the fee provision simply inapplicable?) that retrieval cannot definitively settle. Because the load-bearing point for ballot access is that signatures are the route (which is safe regardless), HOLD with the over-comply posture and route the fee-amount question to the SOS.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: NO per-signature (prohibited) — pay hourly/bonus; advocate to legalize
compensationModel.model: per-signature prohibited by statute (ORC 3599.111, expressly covers candidate/nominating petitions) but that statute was struck down for initiatives in Citizens for Tax Reform v. Deters; status as applied to candidate petitions is unsettled
compensationModel.note: Ohio's per-signature ban (ORC 3599.111) is candidate-petition-specific: it expressly names "any declaration of candidacy, nominating petition" and makes per-signature/per-volume pay a 5th-degree felony, requiring payment "except on the basis of time worked." However, the Sixth Circuit struck that statute as unconstitutional in Citizens for Tax Reform v. Deters, 518 F.3d 375 (6th Cir. 2008) (cert. denied) — but in an INITIATIVE-petition posture, so its reach to a candidate-petition prosecution is an extrapolation, not a clean authorization; the statute is still on the books. Paying circulators by time worked is clearly lawful, and circulators need not be Ohio residents/electors (residency requirement enjoined in Citizens in Charge v. Husted).
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nominating petition, does the Ohio Secretary of State / Elections Division consider ORC 3599.111's per-signature/per-volume compensation prohibition enforceable against candidate-petition circulators after Citizens for Tax Reform v. Deters, 518 F.3d 375 (6th Cir. 2008), or may circulators be compensated on a per-signature basis?

citations.quote: No person shall receive compensation on a fee per signature or fee per volume basis for circulating any declaration of candidacy, nominating petition, initiative petition, referendum petition, recall petition, or any other election-related petition that is filed with or transmitted to a board of elections, the office of the secretary of state, or other appropriate public office. No person shall pay any other person for collecting signatures on election-related petitions or for registering voters except on the basis of time worked.
Whoever violates division (B) of this section is guilty of receiving improper compensation for circulating a petition, a felony of the fifth degree.
Citizens for Tax Reform v. Deters, 518 F.3d 375 (6th Cir. 2008), was a decision that overturned an Ohio statute that made it a felony to pay petitioners by the signature.

On March 5, 2008, a three judge panel of the Sixth Circuit Court of Appeals unanimously upheld the district court ruling to strike Ohio's law banning per-signature payments.... On November 17, 2008, the United States Supreme Court announced that it was declining to hear Ohio's appeal.
The case arose out of an attempt of Citizens for Tax Reform (CTR), an Ohio political advocacy group, to quality a citizen initiative for the 2005 general election ballot in that state.

the Ohio residency requirement is not currently in place based on the preliminary injunction in Citizens in Charge v. Husted, Case: 2:13-CV-00935 (S.D. Ohio 11/13/13); See also, Citizens in Charge v. Husted, 810 F. 3d 437 (6th Cir 2016). ... a circulator does not have to be an Ohio elector or Ohio resident.

voterFile.cost: Free. The entire statewide voter registration file is downloadable at no charge from the Ohio Secretary of State's Voter Files Download Page (no fee, no account, no form).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — anyone. The statewide voter file is treated as a public record and posted online for open download with no eligibility gate (no candidate/committee/registered-user restriction).
voterFile.useRestrictions: No use-restriction gate is imposed at the point of download (no affidavit, click-through use agreement, or commercial-use prohibition on the SoS download page itself). General Ohio public-records/voter-registration law applies; the SoS states the files are published "in accordance with Ohio law." No purpose attestation is required to obtain the file, which is ideal for petition-signature matching.
voterFile.requestProcess: Instant online download. Go to the SoS Voter Files Download Page, select "Statewide Voter Files," and click each of the four files to download directly. No request, form, approval, or payment.
voterFile.note: Ohio publishes the full statewide voter registration file for free, instant, gate-free download via the Secretary of State's Voter Files Download Page. The statewide file is split into four compressed (gzip) CSV files by county range (SWVF_1_22 Adams-Erie, SWVF_23_44 Fairfield-Lawrence, SWVF_45_66 Licking-Pike, SWVF_67_88 Portage-Wyandot), updated weekly, and a downloadable "Voter File Layout" documents columns including voter name, residential address, and voter (registration) status — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters.

citations.quote: Welcome to the Ohio Secretary of State's Voter Files Download Page. At this page you can download files of all registered voters by county, state senate district, state representative district, congressional district, or the entire state of Ohio. The statewide file is divided into four .zip files SWVF_1_22 (Adams-Erie), SWVF_23_44 (Fairfield-Lawrence), SWVF_45_66 (Licking-Pike) and SWVF_67_88 (Portage-Wyandot). The voter files available on this page are updated every week.
Note: These statewide voter files are available as compressed (gzip) files. Please use a compression tool to uncompress these files after downloading.
The Ohio Secretary of State’s office publishes the voter registration files on this page in accordance with Ohio law.

The following links allow you to download files of all registered voters by county, state senate district, state representative district, congressional district, or the entire state of Ohio. The voter files available at this page are updates every week. The data is in CSV format that can be imported into Microsoft Excel ot other spreadsheet database programs.

circulator.ageRule: For an independent presidential nominating petition (a "nominating petition for presidential electors" under ORC 3513.257), the circulator must be at least 18 years of age. ORC 3503.06(C)(1)(b): "No person shall be entitled to circulate a nominating petition for presidential electors unless the person is at least eighteen years of age." This is the ONLY eligibility requirement Ohio imposes on circ
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: must be a registered Ohio elector qualified to vote on the candidacy (ORC 3501.38(A) + 3503.06(B)). CIRCULATOR of a presidential-elector petition: only requirement is being at least 18 years old (ORC 3503.06(C)(1)(b)) plus signing the witnessing affidavit (3501.38(E)(1)) — NO residency, NO voter-registration, and NO citizenship requirement. The strict resident/registered requirements bind
circulator.note: Ohio statute is explicit and draws a deliberate circulator-vs-signer and presidential-vs-other distinction. ORC 3503.06(C)(1) sets two different circulator rules: (a) for petitions GENERALLY, the circulator "must be a resident of this state and is at least eighteen years of age," BUT this is expressly "Except for a nominating petition for presidential electors"; and (b) for "a nominating petition for presidential electors," the circulator need only be "at least eighteen years of age." An indepen

citations.quote: (C)(1)(a) Except for a nominating petition for presidential electors, no person shall be entitled to circulate any petition unless the person is a resident of this state and is at least eighteen years of age. (b) No person shall be entitled to circulate a nominating petition for presidential electors unless the person is at least eighteen years of age.

(B) No person shall be entitled to sign any petition, unless the person is registered as an elector and resides in a precinct in which the candidacy or issue that is the subject of the petition will appear on the ballot.

(A) Only electors qualified to vote on the candidacy or issue which is the subject of the petition shall sign a petition. Each signer shall be a registered elector pursuant to section 3503.01 of the Revised Code. The facts of qualification shall be determined as of the date when the petition is filed.

citations.field: circulator duties (no residency/registration imposed — only a witnessing affidavit)
citations.quote: (E)(1) On each petition paper, the circulator shall indicate the number of signatures contained on it, and shall sign a statement made under penalty of election falsification that the circulator witnessed the affixing of every signature, that all signers were to the best of the circulator's knowledge and belief qualified to sign, and that every signature is to the best of the circulator's knowledge and belief the signature of the person whose signature it purports to be... On the circulator's statement for a declaration of candidacy or nominating petition for a person seeking to become a statewide candidate or for a statewide initiative or a statewide referendum petition, the circulator shall identify the circulator's name, the address of the circulator's permanent residence, and the name and address of the person employing the circulator...

citations.field: address-disclosure carve-out does not reach presidential petitions
citations.quote: (2) As used in division (E) of this section, "statewide candidate" means the joint candidates for the offices of governor and lieutenant governor or a candidate for the office of secretary of state, auditor of state, treasurer of state, or attorney general.

citations.field: independent presidential candidate qualifies by nominating petition with slate of presidential electors
citations.quote: there is submitted to the secretary of state, at the time of filing the petition, a slate of presidential electors sufficient in number to satisfy the requirement of the United States Constitution.

citations.field: signature count / signers are qualified electors (signer requirement)
citations.quote: ... shall be signed by no less than five thousand qualified electors, provided that no petition shall be accepted for filing if it purports to contain more than fifteen thousand signatures.

citations.field: 5,000 qualified-voter signatures for independent presidential candidate (corroboration)
citations.quote: An independent presidential candidate seeking placement on the general election ballot must file a petition and a statement of candidacy with the secretary of state. The petition must contain the signatures of at least 5,000 qualified voters and be submitted no later than 90 days before the general election.

signaturesHeadline: NC: 1.5% of last gubernatorial turnout = 83,874 (2024 cycle, NCSBE-confirmed) + 200 from each of 3 districts. AUTO-LIVE.
deadlineHeadline: NC deadline DISPUTED: statute+NCSBE say noon, day of primary (Mar 2024/2026); Ballotpedia says last Fri June. HOLD on earliest.
riskNote: Two unresolved legal-interpretation conflicts (presidential petition deadline; whether any filing fee applies to an independent presidential candidate) force CONSERVATIVE-HOLD on deadline and feeAlternative; treat the earliest deadline (noon, day of primary) and a possible fee as binding until NCSBE confirms. All citations grounded verbatim; nothing BLOCKED.
sosInquiries: For an unaffiliated/independent candidate for President of the United States under G.S. 163-122, is the petition-filing deadline noon on the day of the primary election (per the NCSBE petitions table and G.S. 163-122(a)(1)), or is there a separate presidential-specific deadline (e.g., the last Friday in June, with presidential-elector certification by the first Friday in August)? Please confirm the controlling date for the current cycle.

Does an unaffiliated/independent candidate for President of the United States owe any filing fee under G.S. 163-122(e)/G.S. 163-107 given that the G.S. 163-107 fee schedule lists no line item for President or presidential electors, or is the office fee-free? If a fee applies, what is the amount and does the G.S. 163-107.1 petition-in-lieu-of-fee alternative apply?

fields.reliedValue: Formula-based: signatures equal to 1.5% of the total number of voters who voted in the most recent general election for Governor, plus at least 200 registered voters from each of three different NC congressional districts. For the 2024 gubernatorial base this equals 83,874 (per NCSBE 2026 Unaffiliated Candidate Fact Sheet). Statewide/presidential path runs through G.S. 163-122(a)(1), with G.S. 163-1(c) confirming an unaffiliated presidential candidate qualifies under 163-122 and nominates electors.
fields.note: All three citations ground verbatim (FindLaw 163-122, NCSBE unaffiliated-petitions table, FindLaw 163-1(c)). Formula is current and correct. Adversarial note: the draft labels the 2024 integer UNKNOWN, but the NCSBE 2026 official fact sheet explicitly states 83,874 (1.5% of 2024 gubernatorial turnout) for the statewide path — the figure IS confirmable from a primary source, so I relied on it. Minor wording variance (statute 'voters who voted' vs NCSBE 'registered voters who voted'; Ballotpedia 'votes cast for governor') is immaterial in practice. Value recomputes each cycle.

fields.reliedValue: STRICTEST: petition signature pages due to the county board of elections by 12:00 noon on the DAY OF THE PRIMARY ELECTION (e.g., noon March 5, 2024; noon March 3, 2026 per NCSBE fact sheet) — the earliest of the conflicting candidate deadlines. County-board verification no later than 15 business days after canvass of the primary. Do NOT rely on any later 'last Friday in June' date until NCSBE confirms in writing.
fields.note: Both citations ground verbatim. UNRESOLVED CONFLICT for the PRESIDENTIAL contest: statute 163-122(a)(1) + NCSBE petitions table + NCSBE 2026 fact sheet all say 'noon on the day of the primary'; Ballotpedia (citing FEC) says the independent-presidential petition is due 'no later than the last Friday in June' with electors certified by 'the first Friday in August.' Reconciling these requires legal interpretation retrieval cannot settle (whether a presidential-specific timeline supersedes 163-122). Strictest = earliest deadline (day of primary), which is what the draft adopts; held rather than auto-lived because missing the true deadline is catastrophic. Note the draft omits the possible first-Friday-in-August presidential-elector certification step.

fields.reliedValue: Geographic distribution required: at least 200 registered voters from EACH of three NC congressional districts (minimum 200 from each of three different districts). Petition pages must additionally be divided into sections by the county in which signatures were obtained, for county-level verification.
fields.note: Citation grounds verbatim in 163-122(a)(1): 'the petition must be signed by at least 200 registered voters from each of three congressional districts in North Carolina. The petitions shall be divided into sections based on the county in which the signatures were obtained.' NCSBE 2026 fact sheet independently confirms '200 registered voters from three different congressional districts.' Adversarial check cleared a stale Ballotpedia phrase ('each of the state's congressional districts') — the statute and the fact sheet both say THREE, so the draft is correct.

fields.reliedValue: NC law regulates SIGNERS, not circulators: each signer must be a qualified registered NC voter residing in the applicable county, and no one may sign on behalf of another. No separate statutory circulator (age/residency/voter-status) requirement found for the unaffiliated petition; circulator-specific eligibility is UNKNOWN (no primary source establishes one).
fields.note: All three citations ground verbatim (NCSBE petitions page signer language; 'No one may sign a petition on behalf of another person'; statute signer requirement). The value is itself conservative — it asserts the signer rules and explicitly marks circulator eligibility UNKNOWN rather than claiming none exists. That is the strict posture, so no stricter reading is needed. Field rests on absence of a circulator statute, which is consistent with the primary sources reviewed.

fields.reliedValue: None / not required. Verification is performed by election officials (Executive Director for statewide petitions, or the county board chair), who check signatures against registration records and attach a signed certificate. No notarized affidavit or circulator certification by the petition gatherer is required by G.S. 163-122 or NCSBE petition instructions.
fields.note: Citation grounds verbatim in 163-122(a)(1)(a): the Executive Director examines names, check-marks qualified/registered signers, and attaches a signed certificate stating signatures were checked against registration records. No circulator affidavit/notary appears anywhere in 163-122, the NCSBE petitions page, or the 2026 fact sheet. Correct and not interpretation-dependent.

fields.reliedValue: paper only. Signatures must be collected on official paper petition sheets, in ink, with original (wet) signatures; electronic signatures are not counted and signatures cannot be collected electronically. No digital/remote-online capture exists for the NC unaffiliated presidential petition.
fields.note: Both quotes ground verbatim on the NCSBE petitions page: 'Signatures cannot be collected electronically.' and 'Signatures must be originals (please use INK); no electronic signatures will be counted. (Photocopies of signed forms are not accepted by county or state elections offices.)' Paper-only is correct and unambiguous.

fields.reliedValue: STRICTEST: do NOT assume the office is fee-free. G.S. 163-122(e) requires an unaffiliated candidate to pay the G.S. 163-107 filing fee for the office OR use the G.S. 163-107.1 petition-in-lieu-of-fee alternative; the NCSBE 2026 fact sheet answers 'Is there a filing fee to run as an unaffiliated candidate? Yes.' Treat a filing fee as potentially due for an independent presidential candidate and budget for the G.S. 163-107.1 fee-waiver petition as a hedge until NCSBE confirms in writing whether President carries a $0 fee.
fields.note: Both citations ground verbatim (163-122(e) fee-or-alternative language; 163-107 fee-schedule preamble). BUT the draft's CONCLUSION ('no fee for President') is an inference — the 163-107 table lists no 'President/presidential electors' line, so the draft reasons the fee is $0. This conflicts with the NCSBE fact sheet's plain 'Yes' that unaffiliated candidates owe a filing fee, and resolving it requires legal interpretation (does the missing line mean $0, or does the Board apply another basis?). Underpaying a required fee can void the filing, so strict posture = assume a fee may apply and confirm. Held, not auto-lived.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found (general secondary indication the practice is currently legal, but not enacted/candidate-specific)
compensationModel.note: The controlling unaffiliated-candidate-petition statute (N.C.G.S. 163-122, which governs independent presidential petitions) is entirely SILENT on circulator compensation: it does not authorize, prohibit, or mention paying circulators per signature or otherwise, and it imposes NO residency or voter-registration requirement on who may circulate/collect the signatures (only the signers must be qualified registered NC voters). NCSBE confirms "State law provides minimal guidelines for conducting petitions," and the proposed 2025 Chapter 22 petition rules (Sections .0100/.0300/.0400) contain no compensation provision at all. So candidate-petition compensation is UNADDRESSED in primary NC authority. A WRAL news report indicates per-signature pay for ballot-access petitions is currently legal in NC and that the Board is only "expected to recommend that lawmakers outlaw" it — but that is a legislative recommendation, not enacted law, and is not candidate-petition-specific primary authority, so this stays CONSERVATIVE-HOLD.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an UNAFFILIATED presidential candidate's nominating petition under N.C.G.S. 163-122, does any North Carolina statute or rule (a) permit or prohibit paying signature circulators per signature, (b) restrict paying circulators at all (e.g., hourly/salary), or (c) require circulators to be North Carolina residents or registered voters?

citations.quote: (a) Procedure for Having Name Printed on Ballot as Unaffiliated Candidate.--Any qualified voter who seeks to have the voter's name printed on the general election ballot as an unaffiliated candidate shall: (1) If the office is a statewide office, file written petitions with the State Board of Elections supporting the voter's candidacy for a specified office. These petitions must be filed with the State Board of Elections on or before 12:00 noon on the day of the primary election and must be signed by qualified voters of the State equal in number to one and a half percent (1.5%) of the total number of voters who voted in the most recent general election for Governor.

State law provides minimal guidelines for conducting petitions. The State Board has proposed rules for petitions that are designed to ensure consistency and transparency in the process for petitioners conducting petition drives and election officials tasked with reviewing and certifying petitions.

Like a majority of states, North Carolina allows political parties to pay collectors by the signature... The practice is legal, but critics say it raises the risk of fraud. Before the next legislative session, the board is expected to recommend that lawmakers outlaw the practice of paying collectors by the signature.

...in the contest for President of the U.S. | 1.5% of all registered N.C. voters who voted in the most recent election for N.C. Governor. | Noon on the day of the primary election to CBE. | N.C.G.S. §163-122(a)(1), N.C.G.S. §163-209

voterFile.cost: Free. The statewide voter registration file is a no-cost, publicly posted ZIP download with no request form, fee, or eligibility gate.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — anyone. The NC State Board of Elections (NCSBE) posts the file openly on its public website/FTP; no registration, application, or political-use eligibility is required to download.
voterFile.useRestrictions: No use-based gate or political-use limit is stated on the data page itself — the file is publicly published. The file already excludes data made confidential by state law (birthdate, SSN, driver's license number are withheld; certain protected/confidential voter records are masked with "#" or "xx/xx/xxxx"). Note that other NC statutes (e.g., G.S. 132 / election-records law) govern downstream misuse, but there is no contractual click-through restriction on the download itself.
voterFile.requestProcess: Instant online download. Click the "Statewide Voter Registration (ZIP)" link on the NCSBE "Voter Registration Data" page (or pull it directly from the dl.ncsbe.gov S3/FTP bucket). No form, approval, written request, or affidavit. Files are refreshed weekly on Saturday mornings.
voterFile.note: North Carolina is the easiest case for petition-signature verification: the full statewide voter file is a free, instant, ungated ZIP download from NCSBE, refreshed weekly. It contains voter name, residential street address, and registration status (Active/Inactive/Removed/Denied/Temporary) plus party and precinct — all the fields needed to match petition signers to registered voters. Confidential PII (birthdate, SSN, DL number) and protected-record details are excluded by state law.

citations.quote: These voter registration files contain the most up-to-date publicly available information for individuals registered or formerly registered to vote in North Carolina
What's included? Voter registration status (e.g., active, removed) and reason for status voter demographics, party affiliation, and address; voter county, precinct, and district information
What's not included? Voter birthdate, Social Security number, and driver's license number are confidential under state law and are not included.
Statewide Voter Registration (ZIP)
The files are updated weekly on Saturday mornings.
