A ACTIVE D DENIED I INACTIVE R REMOVED S TEMPORARY (APPLICABLE TO MILITARY AND OVERSEAS)

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — the rule binds signers only. N.C. Gen. Stat. § 163-122 (the unaffiliated-candidate nominating-petition statute, which governs unaffiliated presidential candidates via § 163-1(c)) imposes requirements only on the SIGNERS ("signed by qualified voters of the State"). The statute contains no circulator affidavit, oath, witness, or age provision. The NC State Board of El
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNERS must be qualified registered voters of the State (per § 163-122(a)(1): "signed by qualified voters of the State"; certified only if "qualified and registered to vote in the designated county"). The CIRCULATOR (collector) has NO statutory requirements at all — North Carolina has no circulator affidavit/witness mechanism; the State/county boards verify signers directly against the registrati
circulator.note: North Carolina's unaffiliated presidential nominating petition is governed by N.C. Gen. Stat. § 163-1(c) (which routes an unaffiliated presidential candidate to qualify "under G.S. 163-122") and § 163-122 itself (statewide office: 1.5% of votes cast in the most recent gubernatorial general election, plus at least 200 registered voters from each of three congressional districts). § 163-122 and the NCSBE Petitions guidance impose strict eligibility ONLY on the SIGNERS (must be qualified, registere

citations.quote: Any qualified voter who seeks to have the voter's name printed on the general election ballot as an unaffiliated candidate shall: (1) If the office is a statewide office, file written petitions with the State Board of Elections supporting the voter's candidacy for a specified office. These petitions ... must be signed by qualified voters of the State equal in number to one and a half percent (1.5%) of the total number of voters who voted in the most recent general election for Governor. Also, the petition must be signed by at least 200 registered voters from each of three congressional districts in North Carolina.

The Executive Director shall examine the names on the petition and place a check mark on the petition by the name of each signer who is qualified and registered to vote in the designated county and shall attach to the petition a signed certificate. Said certificates shall state that the signatures on the petition have been checked against the registration records and shall indicate the number of signers to be qualified and registered to vote in each county.

provided, that in the case of a candidate for President of the United States who has qualified to have his name printed on the general election ballot as an unaffiliated candidate under G.S. 163-122, that candidate shall nominate presidential electors.

citations.field: official guidance imposes rules on signers/collection only, none on the circulator
citations.quote: Collect signatures: Use the recommended signature collection forms appropriate to your petition ... You will be gathering the signatures of qualified voters who reside in the applicable county or counties. Remember: You must separate signature pages by county ... No one may sign a petition on behalf of another person. Signatures must be originals (please use INK); no electronic signatures will be counted.

signaturesHeadline: Signatures DISPUTED: draft says 30,000; statute (1% of 2022 gov vote) = ~44,620 -- HOLD high
deadlineHeadline: Deadline LIVE: 4:00 p.m. 110th day before general = Thu Jul 18, 2024
riskNote: Only the signature count is unsafe: the draft's flat 30,000 conflicts with its own 1%-formula citation (MCL 168.590b(2)), which on the 2022 gubernatorial total of 4,461,972 yields ~44,620, and Graveline v. Benson (6th Cir. 2021) separately struck the 30,000+distribution+deadline combination for statewide independents -- collect to the highest plausible number (~44,620) and confirm the exact per-cycle figure and Graveline's effect with the Bureau of Elections; all other fields verified live.
sosInquiries: For the 2024 presidential cycle, what is the exact number of valid signatures required for an independent (no-party) presidential qualifying petition under MCL 168.590b -- is it 1% of the 2022 total gubernatorial vote (~44,620), the older 30,000 figure, the Graveline v. Benson interim figure, or another administratively set number?

Does the holding of Graveline v. Benson (6th Cir. 2021) lowering/striking the statewide-independent signature and distribution requirements apply to the independent PRESIDENTIAL qualifying petition, or does the full MCL 168.590b(2)/(4) requirement (1% plus 100 signatures in 1/2 of congressional districts) still govern an independent presidential candidate for 2024?

fields.reliedValue: At least 1% of the total votes cast for all gubernatorial candidates at the last gubernatorial election (MCL 168.590b(2)). For the 2024 cycle the 2022 governor's race totaled 4,461,972 votes, so the strict statutory floor is approximately 44,620 valid signatures -- NOT the flat 30,000 the draft headlines. Plus the distribution requirement (>=100 in >=1/2 of congressional districts). Collect to the higher number and confirm the exact operative figure and Graveline's applicability with the Bureau of Elections before relying.
fields.note: Citations ground verbatim, but the value is self-contradictory and likely understated. The draft headlines 30,000 while its own MCL 168.590b(2) citation states a 1%-of-gubernatorial-vote FORMULA. Independent re-derivation: the 2022 MI governor general election totaled 4,461,972 votes (Whitmer 2,430,505 + Dixon 1,960,635 + others); 1% = 44,620 -- materially above 30,000. The flat 30,000 is the pre-amendment number; the Ballotpedia '30,000' text appears stale. Separately, Graveline v. Benson (6th Cir. 2021) struck the 30,000+distribution+deadline combination for statewide INDEPENDENT candidates (district court set 12,000 interim). The true operative 2024 number (44,620 statutory vs. 30,000 stale vs. 12,000 Graveline interim vs. an administrative figure) is a legal-interpretation question retrieval cannot settle. Strictest defensible posture: collect to the highest statutory floor (~44,620); over-collecting cannot jeopardize ballot access.

fields.reliedValue: 4:00 p.m. on the 110th day before the general November election. For 2024 that is Thursday, July 18, 2024 (110 days before Nov 5, 2024). MCL 168.590c(2). Signatures must be collected within the 180 days immediately before filing (MCL 168.590b(3)).
fields.note: MCL 168.590c(2) text grounded verbatim ('not later than 4 p.m. of the one hundred-tenth day before the general election'); 180-day window grounded in 590b(3); Ballotpedia corroborates. Date arithmetic independently confirmed: Nov 5, 2024 minus 110 days = Thursday, July 18, 2024. Correct, current, no interpretation needed.

fields.reliedValue: Yes. At least 100 registered electors must sign in each of at least one-half (1/2) of the state's congressional districts (MCL 168.590b(4), which expressly lists 'the office of president of the United States').
fields.note: MCL 168.590b(4) grounded verbatim and expressly names president; Ballotpedia corroborates. Adversarial caveat: Graveline v. Benson found the geographic-distribution requirement part of the unconstitutional combination for statewide STATE offices -- whether it survives as applied to independent PRESIDENTIAL is unsettled. But the rule is on the books and complying with it cannot harm ballot access (over-compliance), so it is gated live rather than held. The Graveline question is flagged in the SOS inquiry attached to signaturesRequired.

fields.reliedValue: Circulator must be at least 18 and a U.S. citizen; no Michigan residency required, but a non-resident circulator must mark the designated box on the certificate to accept Michigan jurisdiction (MCL 168.544c(3); HB 5152, 2014, removed residency requirements). Petition signers must be registered/qualified electors of the city/township in the petition heading.
fields.note: MCL 168.544c certificate text grounded verbatim (18+, U.S. citizen, signed in presence), as is the non-resident-box language and the 544c(3) reference to a 'qualifying petition for an office named in section 590b(4)' (which lists president). Ballotpedia confirms HB 5152 removed all circulator residency requirements. Correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: No notarization of the petition. Each sheet carries a CERTIFICATE OF CIRCULATOR self-attestation (false statement = misdemeanor); the circulator must sign and date it before filing (MCL 168.544c(5)); it is not sworn before a notary. (The candidate's separate Affidavit of Identity includes a campaign-finance statement signed before a notary -- a distinct document, not the petition.)
fields.note: MCL 168.544c(5) sign-and-date-before-filing rule and the misdemeanor warning grounded verbatim; Ballotpedia confirms the AOI campaign-finance statement is the notarized component (a separate document). No petition notarization required. Correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Wet-ink paper petition sheets signed in the circulator's physical presence; no remote/online or digitized signature path for an independent presidential qualifying petition in Michigan.
fields.note: MCL 168.544c 'each signature on the petition was signed in his or her presence' grounded verbatim; Ballotpedia signer-address requirement grounded. No statutory authorization for electronic collection located. Paper-only is correct.

fields.reliedValue: None for the independent (no-party) presidential qualifying-petition path -- access is by petition only; no filing fee may substitute. (The $100 fee-in-lieu-of-petition exists only for major-party state LEGISLATIVE nominating petitions.)
fields.note: Ballotpedia grounded verbatim: '$100 in lieu of filing a nominating petition' applies to a major party state legislative candidate only; the independent path requires an affidavit of identity plus a qualifying petition with no fee alternative. Sore-loser provision separately confirms an independent cannot use a partisan path/fee. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: Materially different. A new party qualifies under MCL 168.685: petitions equal to not less than 1% of the total votes cast for all gubernatorial candidates at the last gubernatorial election, same distribution rule (>=100 in >=1/2 of congressional districts), same 4 p.m. 110th-day deadline, signatures within 180 days; chairperson and secretary file a signed certificate with the petitions, and the party nominates its presidential candidate by convention. Same circulator-certificate/paper mechanics (685(4) incorporates the 544c form).
fields.note: MCL 168.685(1) long quote grounded verbatim (1% formula, 100-in-1/2-districts distribution, 110-day deadline, 180-day window, certificate filing) and 685(4) 'balance...as set forth in section 544c' grounded verbatim. Correct and current. Note: 685's use of the SAME 1% formula corroborates that the independent statewide signature requirement is the 1% formula (~44,620), reinforcing the doubt on the signaturesRequired '30,000' figure.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found (per-signature pay not prohibited by current law)
compensationModel.note: Michigan's candidate nominating-petition statute (MCL 168.544c), which independent/no-party presidential candidates use, does NOT address circulator compensation at all — paid vs. volunteer and per-signature pay are unaddressed in the candidate-petition election code. Paid circulators are plainly permitted (2022 GOP gubernatorial candidates used them) and circulator residency is NOT required (non-resident circulators are expressly contemplated); no current Michigan statute prohibits per-signature pay, but a bill to ban it (SB 529-531) is pending as of June 2026, so a national paid pool paid per signature is not affirmatively authorized by any primary source for candidate petitions.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nominating petition under MCL 168.544c, does Michigan law permit a campaign to pay circulators on a per-signature basis, and must a circulator be a Michigan resident or registered voter?

citations.quote: The undersigned circulator of the above petition asserts that he or she is 18 years of age or older and a United States citizen; that each signature on the petition was signed in his or her presence ... (16) The provisions of this section except as otherwise expressly provided apply to all petitions circulated under authority of the election law.

_____ If the circulator is not a resident of Michigan, the circulator shall make a cross or check mark on the line provided ... the undersigned circulator asserts that he or she is not a resident of Michigan and agrees to accept the jurisdiction of this state for the purpose of any legal proceeding or hearing that concerns a petition sheet executed by the circulator
A Michigan Senate committee moved forward bills Wednesday to outlaw political petition circulators being paid by the signature as part of a package of election fraud and disinformation deterrents.
In 2022, five Republican candidates for governor were disqualified from the Michigan primary ballot because of fraudulent petition signatures gathered by paid circulators.
The bills now go to the Senate floor. Similar bills cleared the Senate last year, but were never taken up by the House before the session ended in December.

voterFile.cost: No published flat statewide price; varies — request quote. By statute the requester pays "the cost of making, certifying, and delivering the tape, disk, or listing" (MCL 168.522); when obtained from the state via FOIA, the Dept. of State "may charge a fee to cover the cost of complying" (labor + media). Note: the separate $15/record "Record sales" program is for DRIVER/VEHICLE records only, NOT voter data.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Broadly available — the statute requires the file be delivered "to any person, upon request" (MCL 168.522). Not gated to candidates/committees/parties; the statewide Qualified Voter File data is obtained from the Bureau of Elections via written/FOIA request (it is NOT sold through the SOS commercial "Record sales / List sales" program, which covers only driver and vehicle records).
voterFile.useRestrictions: Statutorily protected/exempt fields are stripped from any released list — no driver-license/state-ID number, no month/day of birth, no phone number, no last-4 SSN, and no digitized signature (MCL 168.509gg; MCL 168.522(2) bars these from the list, though it may include year of birth). For the SOS's separate bulk data programs the state warns data "cannot be used for marketing or solicitation purposes... violations can be prosecuted as felony infractions."
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request to the Michigan Department of State / Bureau of Elections (election-data FOIA request). FOIA requests "must be submitted in writing" — online via MiLogin, or by mail/email to the MDOS FOIA Coordinator. (Local lists may alternatively be requested from a city/township/village clerk under MCL 168.522.) No instant online public download of the statewide file.
voterFile.note: Michigan does not offer a free instant download of the statewide voter file. Name + residential address (plus year of birth) of every registered elector are disclosable and sufficient to match petition signers to the rolls; sensitive fields (DL#, full DOB, phone, SSN-4, signature image) are FOIA-exempt. Obtain it by written/FOIA request to the Bureau of Elections, paying the cost of producing the file — no published flat statewide price, so treat as PAID / request a quote.

citations.quote: A clerk of a city, township, or village who maintains a computerized file of registered voters and who does not have direct access to the qualified voter files shall make, certify, and deliver to any person, upon request, a computer tape, disk, or listing, as specified by the person, of the names and addresses of the registered electors ... upon the payment to the clerk of the cost of making, certifying, and delivering the tape, disk, or listing.

A computer tape, disk, or listing provided under subsection (1) shall include, upon request, the year of birth of an elector but shall not include the month and day of birth of an elector. A computer tape, disk, or listing provided under subsection (1) shall not include a person's driver's license or state personal identification card number or any other information that is exempt from disclosure under section 509gg or other section of this chapter.

The secretary of state, a designated voter registration agency, or a county, city, township, or village clerk shall not release a copy of that portion of a registration record that contains any of the following: ... (c) A registered elector's driver license or state personal identification card number. (d) The month and day of birth of a registered elector. (e) The telephone number provided by a registered elector. (f) The digitized signature of an elector ...

Michigan's Freedom of Information Act (FOIA), MCL 15.231 et seq, provides that certain persons can receive copies or make inspections of most public records of public bodies upon written request. ... The department may charge a fee to cover the cost of complying with a person's FOIA request.
All records are $15 each; $16 if certified.

Bulk information sales don't include customer phone numbers or email addresses, and data cannot be used for marketing or solicitation purposes. MDOS checks compliance on all sales and violations can be prosecuted as felony infractions.

circulator.ageRule: Circulators must be 18 or older. The statutory Certificate of Circulator that every petition circulator must sign (MCL 168.544c(1)) requires the circulator to assert "that he or she is 18 years of age or older and a United States citizen." This is an affirmative requirement on the circulator (collector), distinct from the signer requirements. Under MCL 168.544c(16) these provisions "apply to all p
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a "qualified and registered elector" of the relevant district (MCL 168.590b(1)-(2)) and, for president, the petition must include at least 100 registered electors in each of at least half the congressional districts (168.590b(4)); each signer must be a registered elector of the city/township in the petition heading (168.544c(6)). CIRCULATOR (collector) need only be 18+ and a U.S. ci
circulator.note: Primary source is MCL 168.544c, whose statutorily-prescribed Certificate of Circulator is the controlling text for who may circulate. It affirmatively states the circulator "is 18 years of age or older and a United States citizen." Critically, the same certificate (1) lets a non-resident circulate (a check-box line: if "the circulator is not a resident of Michigan, the circulator shall make a cross or check mark on the line provided" and accepts Michigan jurisdiction), and (2) provides a line fo

citations.quote: CERTIFICATE OF CIRCULATOR The undersigned circulator of the above petition asserts that he or she is 18 years of age or older and a United States citizen; that each signature on the petition was signed in his or her presence...

If the circulator is not a resident of Michigan, the circulator shall make a cross or check mark on the line provided, otherwise each signature on this petition sheet is invalid and the signatures will not be counted by a filing official. By making a cross or check mark on the line provided, the undersigned circulator asserts that he or she is not a resident of Michigan and agrees to accept the jurisdiction of this state...
(County of Registration, if Registered to Vote, of a Circulator who is not a Resident of Michigan)

citations.field: applies to independent presidential qualifying petition
citations.quote: (3) If the circulator of a petition under section 482, a qualifying petition for an office named in section 590b(4), or a petition to form a new political party under section 685 is not a resident of this state, the circulator shall indicate where provided on the certificate of circulator that he or she agrees to accept the jurisdiction of this state... (16) The provisions of this section except as otherwise expressly provided apply to all petitions circulated under authority of the election law.

citations.field: signerVsCirculator (signer must be qualified registered elector; president named)
citations.quote: a qualifying petition for an office shall be signed by a number of qualified and registered electors of the district... (4) ...a qualifying petition for the office of president of the United States... shall be signed by at least 100 registered electors in each of at least 1/2 of the congressional districts of the state.

citations.field: signer must be registered elector of city/township in heading
citations.quote: (6) Except as provided in section 544d, a petition sheet must not be circulated in more than 1 city or township and each signer of a petition sheet must be a registered elector of the city or township indicated in the heading of the petition sheet.

signaturesHeadline: HOLD: packet self-contradicts (3,409 narrative vs 3,614 dates page) — rely on the higher 3,614
deadlineHeadline: HOLD: rule is 90th day before general; controlling 2028 packet date = Aug 16, 2028 (draft mis-anchored on expired Aug 7, 2024)
riskNote: Same official packet states two different signature counts (3,409 vs 3,614) and the draft's headline deadline is an expired 2024 date; both need DOE confirmation, so default to strictest (collect to 3,614, treat Aug 16, 2028 as the deadline) until resolved.
sosInquiries: For the 2028 cycle, the B05P presidential candidate packet states two different signature minimums for an independent presidential petition: the narrative text says 'at least 3,409' (1% of the 340,981 ballots cast for President in 2024), while the Election Law Dates page says 'at least 3,614 signatures.' Which figure is controlling, and what total of valid signatures must an independent presidential candidate collect to be certified for the November 14, 2028 ballot?

Please confirm the exact filing deadline for an independent presidential candidate petition for the 2028 general election: is it 5:00 p.m. prevailing time on August 16, 2028 (the 90th day before the November 14, 2028 general election), and does registered/certified mail postmarked on or before that date satisfy the deadline if received within 15 days?

fields.reliedValue: At least 3,614 valid signatures of qualified Alaska voters (the higher of the two conflicting figures the official B05P packet publishes). The statutory formula is 1% of the ballots cast for President at the last presidential general election; 1% of the certified 2024 total (340,981) computes to 3,409.81, which the packet narrative rounds to 'at least 3,409.' However, the same packet's Election Law Dates page states the petition must have 'at least 3,614 signatures.' Until the Division resolves the contradiction, collect to the higher 3,614 floor to avoid any risk of falling short.
fields.note: Both quotes verbatim-confirmed in B05P.pdf (downloaded and text-extracted; narrative at lines 67-72, dates page at lines 119-123). 340,981 confirmed as the certified 2024 AK presidential total. The draft picks 3,409 as the value and labels 3,614 a mere 'packet inconsistency' to be overridden by the formula. Adversarially, 3,614 implies a base of ~361,400, not 2024's 340,981, so it looks erroneous/stale — BUT it is published by the same controlling official source. Strict, never-risk-ballot-access posture requires relying on the HIGHER figure (3,614) pending DOE confirmation, not silently overriding it down to 3,409. Note also the 'at least one percent' floor arguably rounds UP to 3,410, not down to 3,409. Interpretation (which figure governs) cannot be settled by retrieval.

fields.reliedValue: File not earlier than January 1 of the presidential election year and not later than the 90th day before the presidential general election (AS 15.30.026(a)); by 6 AAC 25.074(a) the cutoff is 5:00 p.m. prevailing time on that 90th day, in person or by registered/certified mail (return receipt requested) postmarked on or before the 90th day and received within 15 days. For the upcoming (2028) cycle, the controlling B05P packet states this date is August 16, 2028 (90 days before the November 14, 2028 general election). Treat August 16, 2028, 5:00 p.m. prevailing time as the operative deadline.
fields.note: Statute + regulation quotes verbatim-confirmed (B05P lines 64-67/482-487; Cornell LII 6 AAC 25.074(a)). The statutory rule is correct. BUT the draft's headline value anchors on 'August 7, 2024' — the 90th day before the Nov 5, 2024 general, an EXPIRED date for the wrong (already-completed) cycle — while burying the actual controlling date (August 16, 2028, stated twice in the packet, lines 44/67/113/119) as a parenthetical 'for reference.' Arithmetic verified: Nov 14, 2028 minus 90 days = Aug 16, 2028. For a forward-looking 2028 ballot rule, relying on a past 2024 date is unsafe; relied value corrected to the 2028 packet date. Which cycle/date governs is an interpretation retrieval alone shouldn't decide.

fields.reliedValue: None. The statute requires only a statewide signature total (1% of the last presidential vote); there is no per-congressional-district or per-county distribution requirement. Alaska has a single at-large U.S. House district.
fields.note: Quote verbatim-confirmed in B05P.pdf (line 68). Independently re-derived: AS 15.30.026(a) and 6 AAC 25.074 impose a single statewide 1% requirement with no geographic distribution language anywhere in the statute, regulation, or subscriber pages. Alaska is a single at-large district, so a distribution requirement would be structurally meaningless. No adversarial counter found.

fields.reliedValue: No separate circulator requirement is found in AS 15.30.026, AS 15.30.025, or 6 AAC 25.074. The only confirmed person-eligibility requirement is that each SIGNER (subscriber) must be a qualified voter of the State of Alaska and must provide a printed name, Alaska residence address, an identifier (last 4 SSN, voter #, DOB, ADL #, or AK State ID #), signature, and date. Treat the existence/absence of a distinct circulator rule as UNCONFIRMED and verify with the Division before relying on it.
fields.note: Both quotes verbatim-confirmed in B05P.pdf (subscriber petition text line 366-367; subscriber-provide line 92-94). The signer requirement is solidly grounded. However, the field's core claim is a NEGATIVE/absence inference ('no separate circulator rule exists anywhere'), which two cited sources cannot conclusively establish — a separate signature-verification or petition-circulation regulation could exist outside these two documents. Under strict posture this absence is interpretation-required and should be confirmed, not asserted as settled. Strictest defensible framing: rely only on the affirmatively-grounded signer requirement; treat circulator rules as unconfirmed.

fields.reliedValue: No notarized affidavit and no per-signature circulator/witness certification is required. The only required certification is a written statement signed by the submitting candidate or party, filed at submission, acknowledging the number of petition booklets or subscriber pages included (6 AAC 25.074(b)). Individual subscriber signatures are not separately notarized or witnessed.
fields.note: Both quotes verbatim-confirmed (B05P signed-statement text line 97-99; Cornell LII 6 AAC 25.074(b)). Independently re-derived from the regulation: 25.074(b) requires only the single 'written statement signed by the submitting candidate or party.' The subscriber pages and statute contain no notary or per-signature witness/circulator-affidavit field. The receipt form (B05P lines 221-222/346-347) likewise asks only for the candidate/party signature. No adversarial counter found. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: paper. The petition is circulated on paper subscriber pages/booklets and filed as a single paper instrument, delivered either in person (by 5:00 p.m. prevailing time on the deadline) or by registered/certified mail (return receipt requested) postmarked on or before the 90th-day deadline and received within 15 days. Assume paper-only for this independent presidential petition.
fields.note: Core paper claim is grounded: Cornell LII 6 AAC 25.074(a) (registered/certified mail) and B05P 'mailed to the Division of Elections Director's Office' both verbatim-confirmed. 6 AAC 25.074 plus the physical subscriber pages clearly contemplate a paper instrument. HOWEVER, the draft's added NEGATIVE assertion — 'there is NO digital/remote-online system' and 'Alaska's e-signature system is limited to initiative/referendum measures, not candidate petitions' — is NOT supported by either cited quote and is an interpretive inference about scope. Strict posture: rely only on the grounded paper requirement (which is the safe operational answer); flag the no-digital-system claim as unverified by the citations.

fields.reliedValue: None. There is no filing fee and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures for ballot access via the independent presidential petition; signatures are the sole qualification mechanism under AS 15.30.026. The separate 'appropriate fee' in the Division's calendar relates only to placement in the Official Election Pamphlet under AS 15.58.030(a), not to getting on the ballot.
fields.note: Pamphlet-fee quote verbatim-confirmed in B05P.pdf (Election Law Dates page, lines 125-129). Independently re-derived: AS 15.30.026 conditions ballot access solely on the petition signatures; no filing fee or fee-in-lieu appears anywhere in the statute, regulation, or packet for the petition itself. The packet's only fee reference is expressly tied to the Official Election Pamphlet (AS 15.58.030(a)), a distinct downstream step. The value correctly distinguishes the two. No adversarial counter found.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: Alaska's independent-presidential petition lives in AS 15.30.026 (National Elections), which sets only the signer/qualified-voter requirement and a resident "state campaign chair" — it is entirely SILENT on petition circulators: no compensation rule (no per-signature cap and no prohibition), and no circulator residency/registration requirement. The familiar "$1 a signature" cap and pay-to-sign prohibition sit in a different chapter (AS 15.45.110, Initiative/Referendum/Recall) and do not by their terms reach candidate petitions, so candidate-petition circulator compensation is unaddressed.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent U.S. presidential candidate's nominating petition under AS 15.30.026, does any Alaska statute or regulation restrict how petition circulators may be compensated (e.g., may a campaign pay circulators per signature), and must a circulator be an Alaska resident or registered voter?

citations.quote: A person who desires to be an independent candidate for President of the United States must file with the director ... a petition signed by qualified voters of the state equal in number to at least one percent of the number of voters who cast ballots in an election under this chapter for President of the United States at the last presidential general election. ... (b)(3) the name, Alaska mailing address, and signature of the candidate's state campaign chair, who must be a state resident.

(c) A circulator may not receive payment or agree to receive payment that is greater than $1 a signature, and a person or an organization may not pay or agree to pay an amount that is greater than $1 a signature, for the collection of signatures on a petition. ... (e) A person or organization that violates (c) or (d) of this section is guilty of a class B misdemeanor.
Chapter 30. National Elections ... Chapter 45. Initiative, Referendum, and Recall

voterFile.cost: ~$20 for the statewide voter list (price estimate per the U.S. EAC compilation; the Alaska Division of Elections does not publish a per-record fee schedule online and quotes/processes orders through a divisional office). Treat $20 as an estimate, not a guaranteed published figure.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — Alaska voter registration records are an "Open" file. Names, addresses, and party affiliation are public and available to anyone who purchases the list (candidates, parties, committees, and the public alike). A voter may individually elect to keep their residential address confidential by providing a separate mailing address (AS 15.07.195(b)).
voterFile.useRestrictions: No statutory eligibility gate, but per the EAC compilation, "even the least restrictive states prohibit the commercial use of voter file information." The file excludes confidential PII (DOB/age, SSN, DL number, voter ID number, place of birth, signature) under AS 15.07.195(a). Specific penalty/affidavit language for commercial-use prohibition was not located on the primary Division of Elections page during this research.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written/in-person request to a Division of Elections divisional office — NOT an instant online download. The Division's Research page states lists are "available for purchase" and to "contact any divisional office for assistance." No public self-serve download portal for the bulk statewide file was found.
voterFile.note: Alaska's statewide voter list is an "Open" public record at a low cost (~$20 per the U.S. EAC), obtained by request to a Division of Elections divisional office rather than instant download. The standard file includes voter name, residential address, and party affiliation — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters — while DOB, SSN, DL number, voter ID number, place of birth, and signature are statutorily confidential (AS 15.07.195).

citations.quote: Lists and ReportsVoter lists are available for purchase. To purchase a list, or for other requests, contact any divisional office for assistance.
Alaska           Open          $20         Names, addresses, party affiliation.          Age or DOB, any part of SSN, DL number, voter ID number, place of birth, signature.

In general, even the least restrictive states prohibit the commercial use of voter file information, however the amount of PII (such as DOB or SSN) included in the voter file may remain significant and valuable to a variety of malicious actors.
[Alaska](https://www.elections.alaska.gov/research/#akcl) | Open | $20 | Name, address, political party | DOB, SSN, DL number, voter number, place of birth, signature

(a) The following information set out in state voter registration records is confidential and is not open to public inspection: (1) the voter's age or date of birth; (2) the voter's social security number, or any part of that number; (3) the voter's driver's license number; (4) the voter's voter identification number; (5) the voter's place of birth; (6) the voter's signature.

(b) In addition to the information in (a) of this section, a voter may elect in writing to keep the voter's residential address confidential and not open to public inspection if the voter provides a separate mailing address.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement for the independent presidential petition. The independent presidential petition is governed solely by AS 15.30.026 (Chapter 30, National Elections), which imposes requirements only on the SIGNERS ("qualified voters of the state") and is entirely silent as to who may circulate/collect signatures. There is no circulator-qualification statute, no circulator affidavit, a
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: must be a "qualified voter[] of the state" / registered Alaska voter (AS 15.30.026(a); form B05P "We, the undersigned qualified voters of the State of Alaska"). CIRCULATOR: no statutory requirement at all — Chapter 30 has no circulator-qualification provision, and the petition form has no circulator affidavit. (Contrast: AS 15.45.105 imposes U.S.-citizen / 18+ / state-resident on circulato
circulator.note: For Alaska's INDEPENDENT PRESIDENTIAL nominating petition (AS 15.30.026), there is NO circulator-eligibility statute. The full text of Chapter 30 (AS 15.30.010-.100), reproduced verbatim in the Division of Elections' current 2028 Presidential Candidate Packet (form B05P, Rev. 1/07/2025), contains no circulator provision; the official Subscribers' Page for Independent Candidates has only signer columns (printed name, Alaska residence address, identifier, signature, date) and NO circulator affidav

citations.field: circulatorAgeRule / minorCanCirculate / circulatorMustBeRegisteredVoter (independent presidential petition statute — binds SIGNERS only, silent on circulators)
citations.quote: (a) A person who desires to be an independent candidate for President of the United States must file with the director ... a petition signed by qualified voters of the state equal in number to at least one percent of the number of voters who cast ballots ... The petition must state that the signers desire the named candidate ... to appear on the ballot as an independent candidate for president ... (b) In order to appear on the ballot, a candidate who has qualified for ballot status under (a) of this section shall certify the following information to the director ... (1) the names of the electors ...; (2) the name of a candidate for Vice-President ...; and (3) the name, Alaska mailing address, and signature of the candidate's state campaign chair, who must be a state resident.

citations.field: signerVsCirculator (official petition form has signer columns only — NO circulator affidavit/certification block)
citations.quote: STATE OF ALASKA SUBSCRIBERS' PAGE — PETITION FOR INDEPENDENT CANDIDATES. We, the undersigned qualified voters of the State of Alaska, hereby petition that [name] appear on the ballot as an independent candidate for President of the United States at the next succeeding presidential general election under AS 15.30.026. PRINTED NAME | ALASKA RESIDENCE ADDRESS | IDENTIFIER (Last 4 SSN, Voter #, DOB, ADL # or AK State ID #) | SIGNATURE | DATE SIGNED

citations.field: circulatorMustBeUSCitizen / circulatorMustBeStateResident / circulatorMinAge (contrast: Alaska's ONLY statutory circulator-qualification rule is in Chapter 45 and applies to INITIATIVE/REFERENDUM booklets, not candidate/presidential petitions)
citations.quote: Sec. 15.45.105. Qualifications of circulator. To circulate a petition booklet, a person shall be (1) a citizen of the United States; (2) 18 years of age or older; and (3) a resident of the state as determined under AS 15.05.020.

citations.field: note (full Chapter 30 text reproduced in official 2028 packet contains NO circulator provision)

Number and manner of selecting candidates 25. Qualifications for limited political parties 30. Qualifications of electors 40. Requirements of party pledge 50. Interpretation of votes cast ... 60.
