signaturesHeadline: AR: 5,000 valid signatures (statewide, no distribution) — AUTO-LIVE
deadlineHeadline: AR: petition due noon, first Monday of August (2024 = Aug 5); electors certified by Sep 15 — AUTO-LIVE
riskNote: Core burden (5,000 sigs / first-Monday-of-August / no distribution / paper / no fee) is fully grounded and AUTO-LIVE. Open risk is procedural: circulator qualifications and notary/affidavit for the PRESIDENTIAL petition are not settled by primary sources (the standard SoS affidavit is expressly carved out for President/VP) — held conservative pending SoS confirmation; over-comply (qualified-elector circulators + notarize) until answered.
sosInquiries: For an INDEPENDENT (and political-group) presidential/VP ballot-access petition under Ark. Code Ann. 7-8-302(5)-(6): does the Secretary of State impose any qualifications on the person who CIRCULATES the petition (e.g., must the circulator be a registered Arkansas voter, an Arkansas resident, or at least 18)?

Does the SoS-prescribed presidential/VP petition form under 7-8-302(5)-(6) require a notarized circulator affidavit or any sworn/witnessed verification of the signatures, given that the standard independent-candidate signature-collection affidavit is carved out 'except President/Vice President'?

fields.reliedValue: 5,000 valid signatures of qualified electors (registered Arkansas voters), statewide. Flat statutory number under Ark. Code Ann. 7-8-302(6)(A) (independent) and 7-8-302(5)(B) (political group); does not recompute per cycle. Raised from 1,000 to 5,000 by HB1338 (signed Mar 8, 2021).
fields.note: All 3 citations verbatim-confirmed: FindLaw 7-8-302(6)(A) (current as of Mar 28, 2024), ballot-access.org HB1338 post, and Ballotpedia. Independently re-derived 5,000 for the independent presidential petition; the 10,000 figure correctly distinguished as the new-PARTY petition (7-7-205 / Amendment), not the independent/group presidential petition. No litigation or post-2024 change found.

fields.reliedValue: Petition filed with the Secretary of State by noon on the first Monday of August of the election year (2024 cycle = August 5, 2024; recomputes per cycle). SoS verifies sufficiency within 10 days. Separately, qualifying independent candidate must certify the elector slate to the SoS by September 15 (7-8-302(6)(C)).
fields.note: Statute quote verbatim in FindLaw 7-8-302(6)(A) and (6)(C); NASS independent-candidate line verbatim-confirmed (lines 117-119 of the 07/29/24 NASS summary). Verified independently that first Monday of August 2024 = Aug 5, 2024. Sep 15 elector-certification correctly sourced to (6)(C).

fields.reliedValue: None. 5,000 signatures collected statewide ('qualified electors of the state'); no per-congressional-district or per-county distribution requirement in Ark. Code Ann. 7-8-302.
fields.note: Statute quote verbatim in FindLaw; NASS describes a single statewide petition. Re-read of full 7-8-302 text confirms no geographic-distribution clause for the independent or group presidential petition.

fields.reliedValue: Treat circulators of the presidential petition as required to be qualified Arkansas electors: U.S. citizen, Arkansas resident, at least 18, and lawfully registered to vote — the strictest generally-applicable standard — UNTIL the SoS confirms whether any (or no) circulator qualifications apply to the President/VP petition specifically.
fields.note: Both SoS-handbook quotes confirmed verbatim (the 'except President/Vice President' carve-out at handbook p. re line 2244-2245, and the 'qualifications set out above, for purposes of signing the petition' qualified-elector list at lines 657-672). The draft's own value is UNKNOWN, which is honest: 7-8-302 sets no circulator qualifications and the standard affidavit is carved out for President/VP, so retrieval cannot settle what (if anything) the SoS-prescribed presidential form requires of a circulator. Over-comply with the strictest elector standard and confirm with SoS rather than rely on UNKNOWN.

fields.reliedValue: Assume the SoS-prescribed presidential/VP petition form MAY require a notarized circulator affidavit or sworn verification; notarize the petition (and have each circulator execute any verification the form provides) UNTIL the SoS confirms no such requirement applies to the President/VP petition.
fields.note: Citations verbatim-confirmed (SoS handbook 'except President/Vice President'; statute 7-8-302(5)(D) oath confirmed in FindLaw, applying to the GROUP certificate of choice, not the petition). But the draft's 'None statutorily required' is an inference from statutory silence plus an affidavit CARVE-OUT — and the thing carved out is precisely the notarized signature-collection affidavit. Whether the SoS form for the presidential petition nonetheless requires notarization is not settled by primary sources. Strictest defensible posture: notarize unless SoS says otherwise; never risk rejection.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Physical petition filed with the Secretary of State; no remote-online or in-person-digitized capture path exists for the independent or minor-party presidential petition.
fields.note: Statute 'file a petition ... at the time of filing' verbatim in FindLaw; SoS handbook group-presidential 'filing a petition with the Secretary of State' confirmed verbatim (lines 1200-1203). No statutory or handbook reference to any electronic/online signature system for this petition.

fields.reliedValue: None. No filing fee in lieu of or in addition to the 5,000-signature petition for an independent or minor-party presidential candidate; 7-8-302 provides only the petition route and specifies no fee. (Arkansas party filing fees apply only to candidates seeking a recognized party's primary nomination.)
fields.note: Statute quote verbatim in FindLaw (petition-only route, no fee provision in 7-8-302); Ballotpedia '5,000 signatures' / 'no write-in' quote confirmed verbatim. Re-read of full 7-8-302 confirms no fee alternative is created or required for the independent/group presidential path.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found; per-signature ban (§7-9-601) is initiative/referendum-only
compensationModel.note: For an INDEPENDENT PRESIDENTIAL candidate, the controlling petition statute is Ark. Code § 7-8-302 (Chapter 8, Presidential Electors), whose full text addresses only the filing deadline, the 5,000 qualified-elector signature requirement, SoS verification, and elector certification — it is SILENT on whether circulators may be paid, paid per signature, or be non-residents (it only requires that the 5,000 SIGNERS be qualified electors of the state). Arkansas's per-signature ban, paid-canvasser registration rules, and circulator residency/citizenship requirement all live in Chapter 9 (Initiatives, Referenda, and Constitutional Amendments) and are textually limited to "a statewide initiative petition or statewide referendum petition" (§ 7-9-601), so they do NOT carry over to candidate petitions. Candidate-petition circulator compensation is therefore UNADDRESSED in the Arkansas election code; treat conservatively.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an INDEPENDENT presidential nominating petition under Ark. Code § 7-8-302, may a campaign pay petition circulators on a per-signature basis, may it pay them at all (hourly/salary), and must a circulator be an Arkansas resident or registered voter? (Note: Ark. Code § 7-9-601's per-signature ban and the residency rule appear limited to statewide initiative/referendum petitions, not candidate petitions — please confirm whether any analogous restriction applies to candidate petitions.)

citations.quote: (6)(A) Persons desiring to have their names printed on the ballot as independent candidates for President of the United States and Vice President of the United States shall file a petition with the Secretary of State by noon on the first Monday of August of the year of the election. The petition shall contain at the time of filing the names of five thousand (5,000) qualified electors of the state declaring their desire to have printed on the ballot the names of the persons desiring their names to be printed on the ballot as independent candidates for President of the United States and Vice President of the United States.

(g)(1) It is unlawful for a person to pay or offer to pay a person, or receive payment or agree to receive payment, on a basis related to the number of signatures obtained on a statewide initiative petition or statewide referendum petition. (2) This subsection does not prohibit compensation for circulating petitions but only compensation for obtaining signatures when the compensation or compensation level is impacted by or related to the number of signatures obtained.

(a)(1) A person shall not provide money or anything of value to another person for obtaining signatures on a statewide initiative petition or statewide referendum petition unless the person receiving the money or item of value meets the requirements of this section.

(a)(1) A person desiring to have his or her name placed upon the ballot as an independent candidate without political party affiliation for any United States office other than President of the United States or Vice President of the United States or state, county, township, or district office in any general election in this state shall file ... (2) Each elector signing the petition shall be a registered voter ...

voterFile.cost: Up to $50 per county on electronic media (≤5,000 voters $10; 5,001–25,000 $25; >25,000 $50); printed lists ≤2¢/name. No statewide SoS file — assembled county-by-county across 75 county clerks (Ark. Code 7-5-109).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public. Ark. Code 7-5-109 says the county clerk "shall provide the list" upon request and imposes no eligibility limit (not restricted to candidates, committees, parties, or political use). The list is a public record subject only to redaction of "secure voter" addresses.
voterFile.useRestrictions: The statute imposes no commercial-use ban or political-use-only limit on the registered voter list itself. The one carve-out is "secure voters": county clerks "shall not reproduce any address of a secure voter in any format unless otherwise authorized by law," and all furnished lists exclude secure-voter addresses (Ark. Code 7-5-109(a)(2), (c)). No penalty for ordinary use is specified in this section.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written / in-person request to the county clerk in each county (the statewide list is assembled county-by-county; the statewide voter registration database is maintained centrally but the statutory access mechanism is the county clerk). No notarized affidavit or sworn statement of permitted use is required by statute. Delivered on compact disc or other electronic medium, or as a printed list.
voterFile.note: Arkansas voter registration lists are obtained from county clerks under Ark. Code 7-5-109 at low statutory fee caps (max $50 per county on electronic media), and the list includes "at least the names, addresses, and precinct numbers of the voters" - sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters. There is no free instant statewide download from the Secretary of State; a true statewide file means requesting from each of the 75 county clerks (the SOS maintains the central voter-registration database but the statutory list-access mechanism is the county clerk).

citations.quote: (c)(1)(A) Upon request every county clerk who maintains on computer the list of registered voters within the county shall provide the list on compact disc or other electronic medium, except the address of registered voters whose information is contained as a secure voter.
(B) The list of registered voters, excluding the addresses of registered voters listed as secure voters, shall include at least the names, addresses, and precinct numbers of the voters.

(2)(A) The fee for a list of registered voters ... of one (1) to five thousand (5,000) registered voters may be up to ten dollars ($10.00). (B) ... of five thousand one (5,001) to twenty-five thousand (25,000) registered voters may be up to twenty-five dollars ($25.00). (C) ... of more than twenty-five thousand (25,000) registered voters may be up to fifty dollars ($50.00).

(3) If a printed list of registered voters, excluding the addresses of registered voters listed as secure voters, is requested, the cost of the list may be no more than two cents (2¢) per name and address.
(2) The county clerks of the several counties of the state shall not reproduce any address of a secure voter in any format unless otherwise authorized by law.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — the rule binds signers only. The controlling statute for independent presidential candidates, Ark. Code § 7-8-302(6)(A), requires a petition signed by "five thousand (5,000) qualified electors of the state" but is entirely silent on who may circulate/collect the petition; it imposes no age, residency, registration, or citizenship requirement on the circulator. There
circulator.signerVsCirculator: The strict requirement — being a "qualified elector"/registered voter of Arkansas — binds the SIGNER only (§ 7-8-302(6)(A) requires "five thousand (5,000) qualified electors of the state"). No statute imposes any eligibility requirement (age, registration, residency, citizenship) on the CIRCULATOR who collects the signatures; the presidential statute does not even require a circulator affidavit/wi
circulator.note: Independent PRESIDENTIAL petitions in Arkansas are governed by Ark. Code § 7-8-302(6)(A), NOT the general independent-candidate statute § 7-7-103 (which expressly excludes "President of the United States or Vice President"). § 7-8-302(6)(A) requires 5,000 qualified electors to SIGN (due by noon on the first Monday of August), and the Secretary of State verifies within 10 days. The statute says NOTHING about who may circulate/collect — no circulator eligibility, no circulator affidavit, no age/re

citations.quote: (6)(A) Persons desiring to have their names printed on the ballot as independent candidates for President of the United States and Vice President of the United States shall file a petition with the Secretary of State by noon on the first Monday of August of the year of the election. The petition shall contain at the time of filing the names of five thousand (5,000) qualified electors of the state declaring their desire to have printed on the ballot the names of the persons desiring their names to be printed on the ballot as independent candidates for President of the United States and Vice President of the United States.

citations.field: general independent statute expressly EXCLUDES president (so § 7-7-103 does not govern)
citations.quote: A person desiring to have his or her name placed upon the ballot as an independent candidate without political party affiliation for any United States office other than President of the United States or Vice President of the United States or state, county, township, or district office ...

citations.field: signer requirement binds signer only (no circulator clause) under § 7-7-103
citations.quote: (2) Each elector signing the petition shall be a registered voter ... (C)(i) The person filing for office as an independent candidate shall submit with the petition an affidavit that swears: (a) The signatures were not collected for a period over ninety (90) days; (b) The beginning and ending dates for collection of signatures on the petition are those as indicated on the affidavit; and (c) The signatures were collected and the petition was executed and submitted in compliance with the law.

citations.field: strict circulator/canvasser regime limited to initiative/referendum, NOT candidate petitions
citations.quote: (a)(1) A person shall not provide money or anything of value to another person for obtaining signatures on a statewide initiative petition or statewide referendum petition unless the person receiving the money or item of value meets the requirements of this section. ... (c) As used in this section, "paid canvasser" means a person who is paid ... in exchange for soliciting a signature on a petition.

citations.field: secondary source confirming signer (qualified voter) requirement; no circulator requirement noted
citations.quote: An independent candidate can petition to have his or her name printed on the ballot as a candidate for the presidency. At least 5,000 qualified state voters must sign the petition, which must be submitted to the secretary of state by noon on the first Monday in August in the year of the election. The secretary of state must verify the petition within 10 days of filing.

signaturesHeadline: CA: 219,403 valid sigs (1% of statewide reg.); independent pres. qualifies via slate of 54 electors. AUTO-LIVE.
deadlineHeadline: CA: nomination papers due 5:00pm Aug 9, 2024 (E-88); circulate from Apr 26, 2024 (E-193). AUTO-LIVE.
riskNote: 6 of 7 fields verified live; only circulatorEligibility is held — draft's "circulators must be registered CA voters" is NOT supported by ELEC 102/8451 (age 18+ is the only requirement), so do not rely on a voter-registration screen for circulators.
sosInquiries: For the November 2024 independent presidential (presidential elector) nomination under Elections Code 8400/8451/102, must a petition circulator be a registered California voter, or is being 18 years of age or older the only circulator eligibility requirement?

fields.reliedValue: 219,403 valid signatures for the 2024 cycle, equal to 1% of statewide voter registration as of the last Report of Registration prior to the November 2022 General Election; recomputes each presidential cycle. No direct independent presidential line — the candidate qualifies by nominating a slate of 54 presidential electors (plus 54 alternates) via independent nomination, with the nomination papers carrying the signatures. Cal. Elections Code 8400, 8303, 8550; 2187(c)(6).
fields.note: All three quotes verbatim in sources. SoS PDF states '219,403... (equivalent to 1%... prior to the November 2022 General Election)' and the 54-elector qualification path. ELEC 8400 independently confirms 1% of registered voters for statewide office; Ballotpedia independently confirms 219,403 and '1% of the total number of registered voters in the state' for 2024 independent presidential. Path-via-electors framing is correct.

fields.reliedValue: For the Nov 5, 2024 General Election: nomination papers circulate from Apr 26, 2024 (E-193) and must be delivered to the county elections official no later than 5:00 p.m. on Aug 9, 2024 (E-88); electors' Declarations of Candidacy also due 5:00 p.m. Aug 9, 2024 (E-88). Statutory rule: 88 days before the general election; recomputes per cycle. Cal. Elections Code 8403(a)(2), 8550.
fields.note: SoS quotes verbatim. ELEC 8403(a)(2) independently confirms: for offices with no filing fee, papers circulated/delivered to county no earlier than 193 days and no later than 5 p.m. 88 days before the election. Ballotpedia confirms 8/9/2024 deadline and '5:00 p.m. 88 days prior to the election.'

fields.reliedValue: None. The 1% requirement is a single statewide total; there is no per-congressional-district or per-county distribution requirement for the independent presidential (presidential elector) nomination. Signatures are gathered and delivered county-by-county to the county where signers reside for verification, but no minimum-per-county quota applies.
fields.note: Quotes verbatim. ELEC 8400 sets a single statewide 1% number for statewide office with no geographic-distribution clause; ELEC 8403(c) addresses only a void-if-insufficient mechanism and per-county delivery for verification, not a per-county minimum. No distribution rule found in primary law.

fields.reliedValue: Circulators must be 18 years of age or older (Cal. Elections Code 102, 8451). The strictest reliable statement of eligibility is the age-18 requirement only. A registered voter who is a candidate may circulate and sign their own nomination papers (106(a)). Do NOT rely on a 'circulator must be a registered California voter' requirement — it is not supported by 102/8451; confirm any residency/registration condition with the SoS before screening circulators.
fields.note: The grounded SoS quote supports ONLY age 18+. The draft's added claim that circulators 'must be registered voters of California (per 102/8451)' is contradicted by the actual statutes: ELEC 102 reads 'A person shall not circulate... unless the person is 18 years of age or older' and ELEC 8451 says 'Circulators shall meet the requirements of Section 102' — neither imposes a voter-registration requirement (ELEC 104(b)(3) likewise lists only age 18+ for the circulator declaration). Holding because the value asserts an unsupported, stricter-than-law requirement; relied value corrected to the defensible age-18 floor.

fields.reliedValue: No notarization is required. Each section of the nomination paper must carry a circulator declaration signed in the circulator's own hand, certified under penalty of perjury (Cal. Elections Code 104); there is no notary/affidavit-before-officer requirement. All signers must be registered California voters (100, 8400), and signed papers are delivered to the county elections official where signers reside for verification before forwarding to the Secretary of State.
fields.note: Both quotes verbatim. SoS summary specifies no notary. ELEC 104 independently confirms the integrity mechanism is a circulator declaration 'under penalty of perjury under the laws of the State of California' — not notarization. Draft's core 'no notarization' conclusion is correct (draft does not name 104 but asserts nothing false).

fields.reliedValue: paper. Signatures are collected on physical nomination papers circulated to registered voters and delivered to county elections officials for verification. There is no remote-online or digitized signature system for the independent presidential (presidential elector) petition in California; the SoS Summary describes only paper nomination papers and Declarations of Candidacy filed by mail, in-person drop box, or at a county elections office.
fields.note: Both quotes verbatim in the SoS PDF. SoS process is entirely physical circulation/delivery; ELEC 8400/8403 contemplate paper nomination papers. No e-signature path exists for this petition.

fields.reliedValue: None. No filing fee is required to file the nomination papers, and California provides no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures alternative for the independent presidential (presidential elector) nomination — the signature petition is the sole path.
fields.note: Quote verbatim: SoS PDF 'No filing fee is required from any person in order to file nomination papers.' Ballotpedia 2024 table lists filing fee 'N/A' for CA independent presidential. The no-fee/no-fee-in-lieu framing is consistent (the no-fee status is precisely why the longer E-193 circulation window in 8403(a)(2) applies).

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found (per-signature legal only via initiative-law analogy; CA has no per-signature ban, all ban bills vetoed)
compensationModel.note: For CANDIDATE / independent-presidential nominating papers, California's Elections Code does NOT address circulator compensation at all — neither permitting nor prohibiting per-signature pay (the candidate-petition statutes Elec. Code 8400-8409 and the circulator chapter 8451-8454 are silent, as is the general petition chapter 100-108). The "pay-per-signature permitted" answer that exists in California comes exclusively from the INITIATIVE context (Div. 9, Elec. Code 9021), reinforced by Gov. Newsom's/Brown's repeated vetoes of bills (SB 660 2021, AB 1451 2019, AB 1947 2018) that would have banned per-signature pay for "initiative, referendum, or recall petitions" — none of which reached candidate petitions; do not carry that answer over. Paid circulation generally is not prohibited, and circulators of nominating papers need only be 18+ (Elec. Code 8451 -> 102), with NO residency or voter-registration requirement, so a national paid pool could lawfully circulate in CA. Because per-signature compensation is genuinely unaddressed for candidate petitions, hold conservative.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an INDEPENDENT candidate for President nominating paper under Elections Code sections 8400 and 8451, may a campaign compensate circulators on a per-valid-signature basis, or must circulators be paid only by hourly/salary/flat-bonus arrangements — and does any Elections Code provision, regulation, or Secretary of State guidance restrict how candidate-petition (as opposed to initiative/referendum/recall) circulators are paid?

citations.quote: CHAPTER 4. Circulators [8451 - 8454] ... 8451. Circulators shall meet the requirements of Section 102. ... 8452. A county elections official or a deputy county elections official may not circulate nomination papers, and circulators shall not obtain signatures within 100 feet of any election booth or polling place. ... 8454. (a) Circulators obtaining signatures to the nomination paper of any candidate may, at any time not more than 148 nor less than 88 days prior to the election, obtain signatures to the nomination paper of the candidate. (b) Circulators obtaining signatures to the nomination paper of any candidate for presidential elector may, at any time not more than 193 nor less than 88 days prior to the election, obtain signatures to the nomination paper of the candidate.
102. A person shall not circulate a state or local initiative, referendum, or recall petition or nominating paper unless the person is 18 years of age or older.

101. (a) ... (3) "THIS PETITION MAY BE CIRCULATED BY A PAID SIGNATURE GATHERER OR A VOLUNTEER. YOU HAVE THE RIGHT TO ASK." ... [This paid-gatherer disclosure requirement, like the Official Top Funders rules in Section 107, applies only to a "state or local initiative, referendum, or recall petition" and is not extended to candidate nominating papers.] ... 106. (b) Any person engaged in obtaining signatures to the nomination papers of a candidate for any office or to any recall, initiative or referendum petition, may, if otherwise qualified to sign the papers or petition, sign the papers or petition.

DIVISION 9. MEASURES SUBMITTED TO THE VOTERS [9000 - 9611] ... 9021. (a) A person who meets the requirements of Section 102 may circulate a statewide initiative or referendum petition anywhere within the state. ... [This is the initiative/referendum statute Ballotpedia cites for California's "pay-per-signature permitted" status; it governs ballot measures, not candidate nominating papers.]

California | Permitted | California Election Code, Section 9021 ... California Senate Bill 660 (Vetoed): The legislation would have banned paying signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected. Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) vetoed [it]. ... California Assembly Bill 1451 (Vetoed) ... California Assembly Bill 1947 (Vetoed): ... The legislation would have banned paying signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected.

voterFile.cost: $100 flat application fee for the statewide voter registration information file (one price for the whole state, regardless of record count). Single-voter records (≤10) are $30.00/record up to a $100 max. County-specific files vary by county.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Eligibility-gated, NOT general public. Per the SoS: "any candidate for federal, state, or local office, any committee for or against any initiative or referendum measure for which legal publication is made, any person for election, scholarly, journalistic, or political purposes, and any person for governmental purposes, as determined by the Secretary of State." A petition-signature-verification campaign/committee qualifies under the election/political-purpose and candidate/committee categories.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Strong. Use is limited to permitted (election/political/scholarly/journalistic/governmental) purposes only; commercial use is prohibited. Misuse is a crime: Elections Code 18109(a) makes it "a misdemeanor for a person in possession of information ... obtained pursuant to Article 5 ... knowingly to use or permit the use of all or any part of that information for any purpose other than as permitted by law," and 18109(b) makes obtaining/using the data without first complying with the application requirements of 2188 a misdemeanor. The application must state intended use and is certified "under penalty of perjury." Confidential-voter (Safe at Home, etc.) records are excluded entirely.
voterFile.requestProcess: Application form + approval (not an instant download). Submit the "California Voter Registration File Request" application (PDF) to the SoS. Elections Code 2188 requires a signed application stating the requestor's name, residence/business address, ID number, the specific information requested, and a statement of intended use, certified "under penalty of perjury." SoS reviews to confirm the use is legally permitted: "requests can take 7-14 business days to review and process." No refunds once sent.
voterFile.note: California does NOT offer a free public download of the statewide voter file; access is eligibility-gated to candidates, committees, and election/political/scholarly/journalistic/governmental users, requiring a sworn application and SoS approval. The statewide file is a low flat $100 fee and the standard Voter File includes name, residential and mailing address, registration date, party preference, and precinct (which carries registration status, including inactive voters on request) — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters. Misuse is a misdemeanor (Elec. Code 18109).

citations.quote: A qualified requestor can request the statewide voter registration information file from the Secretary of State. This file contains voter registration data for all California voters. ... The application fee for a statewide voter registration information file is $100.

State law allows the following individuals or groups of individuals to access, with some exceptions*, voter registration information: any candidate for federal, state, or local office, any committee for or against any initiative or referendum measure for which legal publication is made, any person for election, scholarly, journalistic, or political purposes, and any person for governmental purposes, as determined by the Secretary of State.

Voter Registration Data File (Voter File): The following information is contained in this file: the voter' name, date of birth, registration date, residential and mailing address, precinct, contact information, and political party preference. This file is always included for an approved request. Data on active voters is automatically included in this file. Data on inactive voters must be specifically requested on the application.

Once received, requests can take 7-14 business days to review and process. The Secretary of State will determine whether the application is complete and whether state law permits the proposed use of the voter registration information.

(b) The application shall set forth all of the following: ... (6) A statement of the intended use of the information requested. ... (e) The applicant shall certify to the truth and correctness of the content of the application, under penalty of perjury, with the signature of his or her name at length

(a) It is a misdemeanor for a person in possession of information ... obtained pursuant to Article 5 (commencing with Section 2183) of Chapter 2 of Division 2 of this code or Section 7924.000 of the Government Code, knowingly to use or permit the use of all or any part of that information for any purpose other than as permitted by law.

circulator.ageRule: The circulator must be 18 or older. The Circulator's Affidavit prescribed by Cal. Elec. Code § 8409 (the form for an independent nomination paper, which governs independent presidential candidates via the presidential-elector nomination process in § 8300/§ 8304) requires the circulator to "solemnly swear (or affirm) all of the following: 1. That I am 18 years of age or older." A person under 18 ma
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: must be a registered "voter of the County" (§ 8409 signer's statement); the 1% statewide signature threshold runs on registered voters (§ 8400). CIRCULATOR: only 18+, a stated residence address, and an eyewitness to the signing (§ 8409 circulator's affidavit) — no registration requirement.
circulator.note: Independent presidential candidates in California are nominated through a group of candidates for presidential electors under Elec. Code Division 8, Part 2, Chapter 3 (§ 8300 et seq.); § 8300 expressly covers "presidential elector," and § 8304 ties the elector group to the presidential/VP names on the ballot. The nomination paper and its Circulator's Affidavit are prescribed by § 8409. That affidavit requires the circulator to swear ONLY: (1) "That I am 18 years of age or older," (2) a residence

citations.quote: CIRCULATOR'S AFFIDAVIT — I, ________, solemnly swear (or affirm) all of the following: 1. That I am 18 years of age or older. 2. That my residence address, including street and number, is ___. ... 3. That I secured signatures in the County of ______ to the nomination paper of ________ as candidate for the office of ______; ... that I saw all the signatures on this section of the nomination paper being signed and that, to the best of my information and belief, each signature is the genuine signature of the person whose name it purports to be.

SIGNER'S STATEMENT — I, undersigned, am a voter of the County of ______, State of California. I hereby nominate __________ ... as a candidate for the office of _______ to be voted for at the election ... I have not signed the nomination paper of any other candidate for the same office.

citations.field: jurisdiction / applies to independent presidential candidates
citations.quote: A candidate for a partisan office, including that of presidential elector, may be nominated subsequent to, or by other means than, a primary election pursuant to this chapter.

citations.field: presidential-elector group ties to president/VP on ballot
citations.quote: When a group of candidates for presidential electors designates the presidential and vice presidential candidates for whom all of the group pledge themselves to vote, the names of the presidential candidate and vice presidential candidate designated by that group shall be printed on the ballot pursuant to Chapter 2 (commencing with Section 13100) of Division 13.

citations.field: signer requirement / signature threshold binds voters
citations.quote: Nomination papers for a statewide office for which the candidate is to be nominated shall be signed by voters of the state equal to not less in number than 1 percent of the entire number of registered voters of the state at the time of the close of registration prior to the preceding general election.
The affidavit of any circulator obtaining signatures under this chapter shall be verified free of charge by any officer authorized to administer oaths.

signaturesHeadline: 12,000 total (1,500 in EACH of 8 CDs) — fixed per-district, not 5,000
deadlineHeadline: 117th day before general election; time-of-day in conflict (SOS 3pm vs statute 5pm) — use 3pm
riskNote: 7 of 8 fields verify clean for an unaffiliated presidential candidate; only the deadline TIME-OF-DAY is in genuine source conflict (SOS page 3:00 p.m. vs statute effective 1/1/2025 5 p.m.) — treat 3:00 p.m. as the binding cutoff and confirm with SOS before any cycle.
sosInquiries: For an unaffiliated presidential nominating petition under C.R.S. 1-4-802(1)(f)(I), is the filing cutoff on the 117th day before the general election 3:00 p.m. (per your PresidentPetition.html guidance) or 5:00 p.m. (per the statutory text effective Jan 1, 2025)? Please confirm the exact wall-clock deadline currently enforced.

fields.reliedValue: 12,000 total — at least 1,500 valid signatures of registered Colorado electors in EACH of the state's 8 congressional districts (1,500 x 8 = 12,000). Fixed per-district number, not a percentage and not a flat statewide number. Signers must be registered Colorado voters who have not signed a petition for any other candidate for the same office.
fields.note: Both citations verbatim. SOS PresidentPetition.html: '1,500 signatures in each of the 8 congressional district (12,000 total)'; C.R.S. 1-4-802(1)(c)(I): 'At least one thousand five hundred in each congressional district for the office of president and vice president'. Independently confirmed: CO has 8 CDs post-2020 census, so 1,500x8=12,000. The draft's flag that Ballotpedia's stale '5,000' is incorrect is itself correct — Ballotpedia line 235 still says '5,000 eligible voters' which is the pre-redistricting flat figure superseded by the per-CD schedule. Current and correct.

fields.reliedValue: File the completed petition no later than 3:00 p.m. on the 117th day before the general election. The 117-day rule is confirmed by both the statute (C.R.S. 1-4-802(1)(f)(I)) and SOS guidance; the time-of-day is in conflict (SOS guidance says 3:00 p.m.; statutory text effective 1/1/2025 says 5 p.m.). Use the EARLIER 3:00 p.m. cutoff, which satisfies both. Circulation cannot begin earlier than the 173rd day before the general election (C.R.S. 1-4-802(1)(d)(I)). For 2024 the deadline fell on 7/11/24; recomputes each cycle off the November general-election date.
fields.note: All three citations verbatim. Findlaw shows the statute is split: text effective UNTIL 1/1/2025 = '3 p.m.', text effective 1/1/2025 = '5 p.m.'. The SOS web guidance still states 3:00 p.m. This is a genuine source conflict on time-of-day that retrieval cannot settle (which controls for a 2026+ cycle — updated statute or the not-yet-updated SOS page). Strictest defensible reading: file by 3:00 p.m., which complies under either rule. The 117-day count itself is not in dispute. Ballotpedia 7/11/24 / 8/7/24 dates match the footnote verbatim and are consistent with a 117-day computation off 11/5/2024.

fields.reliedValue: YES — distribution required: at least 1,500 signatures in EACH of the 8 congressional districts. Signers must be eligible electors residing within the district in which the office is to be elected. Per-CD minimum, not a statewide aggregate; signatures cannot be concentrated in one district.
fields.note: C.R.S. 1-4-802(1)(c) verbatim: 'Every petition for the office of president and vice president ... must be signed by eligible electors residing within the district or political subdivision in which the officer is to be elected', combined with 1-4-802(1)(c)(I)'s 'in each congressional district' establishes a binding per-CD minimum. SOS quote verbatim. Note: 'in each congressional district' is the operative distribution language; the residency clause and the per-CD minimum together fully support the value. Current and correct.

fields.reliedValue: A petition circulator must be a citizen of the United States and at least 18 years of age. No Colorado residency requirement and no voter-registration requirement is imposed on the circulator. Paid vs. volunteer circulators must wear an identifying badge (PAID CIRCULATOR / VOLUNTEER CIRCULATOR).
fields.note: C.R.S. 1-4-905(1) verbatim: 'A person shall not circulate a petition to nominate a candidate unless the person is a citizen of the United States and at least eighteen years of age.' SOS quote verbatim. Adversarial check: 1-4-905 imposes no residency/registration requirement on circulators (only US citizenship + 18+); the badge requirement in 1-4-905(6) confirms the parenthetical. Current and correct.

fields.reliedValue: BOTH a notarized circulator affidavit AND a notarized candidate acceptance are required. (1) Each petition SECTION must have attached a signed, dated, notarized circulator affidavit; the circulator must appear in the physical presence of the notary; an improperly notarized affidavit is invalid and the section cannot be accepted (C.R.S. 1-4-905(2)-(3)). (2) Before filing, the petition must have attached a notarized acceptance of nomination by the candidate (or by both joint president/VP candidates) (C.R.S. 1-4-906); SOS guidance requires the candidate/VP acceptance forms to be notarized.
fields.note: All three citations verbatim. 1-4-905(2)(a): 'Each petition section must have attached a signed, notarized, and dated affidavit executed by the person who circulated the petition section'; 1-4-905(2)(b)(I)(A) confirms physical-presence; 1-4-905(2)(b)(II) makes a violating affidavit invalid; 1-4-905(3) bars acceptance without the notarized affidavit. 1-4-906 verbatim on the notarized candidate acceptance. SOS notarization quote verbatim. Current and correct.

fields.reliedValue: paper. The unaffiliated presidential petition is paper-only: SOS must approve the petition format before circulation, the petition is circulated on paper, each section requires a wet-ink notarized circulator affidavit (circulator physically present before the notary), and the completed petition plus notarized acceptance forms are hand-delivered to the SOS Elections Division in Denver (1700 Broadway, Suite 550). No online/remote-signing or digitized-capture system covers an unaffiliated presidential nominating petition in Colorado.
fields.note: All three citations verbatim. SOS: 'Petition formats must be approved by the Secretary of State's office prior to circulation' and 'Hand deliver petition and acceptance forms to:' (Denver address printed on page). 1-4-905(2)(b)(I)(A) physical-presence notarization verbatim. The negative existence claim (no e-signing) is strongly supported by the wet-ink/physical-presence/hand-delivery requirements; no contrary source found. Current and correct for the unaffiliated presidential route.

fields.reliedValue: None for the unaffiliated presidential route. There is no filing-fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option for an unaffiliated presidential candidate; the nominating petition (1,500/CD, 12,000 total) is the only path onto the general-election ballot. The petition-or-fee CHOICE applies only to major-party presidential PRIMARY candidates (C.R.S. 1-4-1204), not to general-election access for an independent. Minor-party note: forming a NEW minor party requires a separate statewide petition of at least 10,000 registered electors filed by the 2nd Friday in January of the election year; a QPO candidate petitions under the same 1-4-802 schedule. An individual independent uses the 1-4-802 unaffiliated path and pays no fee.
fields.note: Citations verbatim: SOS 'Submit the notarized acceptance of petition nomination forms with the completed petition.' and QPO FAQ 'Be signed by at least 10,000 registered electors in Colorado; and' (also confirms '2nd Friday in January of the election year'). Adversarial check: the SOS PresidentPetition.html unaffiliated workflow lists no fee step and no fee-in-lieu option; 1-4-802 sets signatures as the sole requirement with no fee alternative. The 1-4-1204 primary fee/petition choice is a separate major-party primary mechanism not available to an independent. Current and correct. (The cited SOS quote supports the 'petition is the path' framing rather than a fee, which is the correct grounding for a negative-existence claim.)

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — candidate-petition statute permits paid circulators (licensed petition entity) but is silent on per-signature compensation
compensationModel.note: For Colorado CANDIDATE (independent presidential) nominating petitions, per-signature compensation of circulators is NOT addressed by the candidate-petition election code: CRS 1-4-905 and 1-4-905.5 clearly allow PAID circulators (via a Secretary-of-State-licensed "petition entity") and require paid/volunteer badges, but neither authorizes nor prohibits paying circulators on a per-signature basis. Colorado's per-signature case law (Meyer v. Grant) and per-signature-adjacent rules sit in the separate INITIATIVE code (Title 1, Article 40 / 1-40-135) and do not automatically govern candidate petitions. Circulators need only be U.S. citizens age 18+ (no Colorado residency required; the SoS expressly allows a circulator address outside Colorado), so a national paid pool can legally work there if the paying entity is licensed and each circulator is registered/trained.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an UNAFFILIATED candidate's nominating petition for President (Title 1, Article 4, Part 8), may a campaign or its licensed petition entity compensate circulators on a PER-SIGNATURE basis, or must circulators be paid only by the hour/salary/flat fee? Does any statute or election rule restrict per-signature pay for CANDIDATE (as opposed to initiative) petition circulators?

(b) A circulator who is to be paid for circulating a petition shall display an identification badge that includes the words “PAID CIRCULATOR” in bold-faced type that is clearly legible and the name and telephone number of the individual employing the circulator.

(c) “Petition entity” means any person or committee that directly or indirectly provides payment to a circulator to circulate a petition to nominate a candidate, to recall an elected officer in accordance with article 12 of this title 1, or to create a minor party in accordance with part 13 of this article 4.

voterFile.cost: $50.00 flat for the Statewide Registered Voter List (EX-003), delivered on data disc (per report). Alternatively, a full Election Cycle Subscription Service (weekly-updated EX-003 plus other extracts for the entire statewide election cycle) is $1,000.00. Free public online lookups exist only for individual voters, not the bulk file.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — no eligibility gate. Colorado voter registration records are statutorily "public records subject to examination by any person." The Elections Data Request Form asks only for requestor name/company and contact info; it is not limited to candidates, committees, or parties.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Social Security numbers, driver's license/state ID numbers, the SOS-assigned unique voter ID, and electronic copies of voter signatures are statutorily excluded from any release (C.R.S. 1-2-302(8)); preregistrant (under-18) information is kept confidential. Colorado law (C.R.S. 1-2-227 and related statutes) restricts use of voter registration data to election/political purposes and prohibits using it for commercial solicitation; the released file does not contain SSN or signature data, so it supports name/address matching but not signature-image comparison.
voterFile.requestProcess: Application form by mail. Complete the Colorado Department of State "Elections Data Request Form" (PDF), check the box for "Statewide Registered Voter List (EX-003)," enclose a check or money order payable to the Colorado Secretary of State, and mail it to the SOS Elections Division (1700 Broadway, Suite 550, Denver, CO 80290). Requests are returned via first-class mail within 72 hours; for expedited carriers the requestor must include a pre-paid mailer. Subscription service provides electronic download access.
voterFile.note: A campaign obtains Colorado's statewide voter file via the SOS Elections Data Request Form: the Statewide Registered Voter List (EX-003) costs $50 flat on disc, or $1,000 for the weekly-updated full-cycle subscription. The list includes voter name, residence address, and registration status — enough to match petition signers to registered voters — but excludes SSNs and signature images by statute, so it cannot be used for signature-image comparison.

Public Elections Data Request on Data Disc for $50.00 per report ... Statewide Registered Voter List (EX-003)

circulator.ageRule: C.R.S. § 1-4-905(1): "A person shall not circulate a petition to nominate a candidate unless the person is a citizen of the United States and at least eighteen years of age." The circulator must be at least 18. The affidavit (§ 1-4-905(2)(a)) further requires a sworn statement "that the affiant was a citizen of the United States and at least eighteen years of age at the time the section of the pet
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER (§ 1-4-904): must be an "eligible elector" (registered voter) and, for president, must reside within the congressional district where signatures are gathered (§ 1-4-802(1)(c)). CIRCULATOR (§ 1-4-905(1)): need only be a U.S. citizen and at least 18 — NOT required to be a registered voter or a Colorado resident.
circulator.note: Colorado's circulator-eligibility rule for candidate nominating petitions is set by C.R.S. § 1-4-905(1), which applies to the general petition procedures in Part 9 of Article 4. Unaffiliated presidential petitions are governed by § 1-4-802 (which expressly covers "president of the United States," requiring 1,500 signatures per congressional district) and use these same Part 9 circulation/affidavit procedures. The circulator statute imposes ONLY two affirmative requirements: (1) U.S. citizenship

Each petition section must have attached a signed, notarized, and dated affidavit executed by the person who circulated the petition section, which must include: The affiant's printed name, the address at which the affiant resides, including the street name and number, the city or town, the county, and the date of signature; ... a statement that the affiant was a citizen of the United States and at least eighteen years of age at the time the section of the petition was circulated and signed by the listed electors

(1) Every petition shall be signed only by eligible electors. ... (2)(b) Petitions to nominate candidates from a minor political party or unaffiliated candidates in a partisan election may be signed by any eligible elector who has not signed any other petition for any other candidate for the same office.

citations.field: applicability to unaffiliated presidential petition / signer residency in district

(I) At least one thousand five hundred in each congressional district for the office of president and vice president
