(b) A circulator who is to be paid for circulating a petition shall display an identification badge that includes the words “PAID CIRCULATOR” in bold-faced type that is clearly legible and the name and telephone number of the individual employing the circulator.

signaturesHeadline: 7,500 wet-ink signatures (lesser of 1% of last presidential vote or 7,500; 1% of 1,759,010 = ~17,590, so 7,500 governs)
deadlineHeadline: SOTS by 4:00pm Jul 29, 2026; town clerk by 4:00pm Aug 5, 2026 (90th day before Nov 3, 2026)
riskNote: All citations grounded verbatim against the 2026 ED-607, 2020 FAQ, candidate-ballot-access page, and Ballotpedia; the four hard operative rules (count, deadline, no distribution quota, witness/notary, paper-only) are AUTO-LIVE, but two fields assert legal NEGATIVES (no circulator residency requirement; no filing fee) that retrieval cannot conclusively settle, so they are held conservative pending SOS confirmation.
sosInquiries: Does Connecticut impose any in-state residency requirement on circulators of an independent presidential nominating petition, or may an out-of-state U.S. citizen (18+, not on felony parole) lawfully circulate and certify petition pages?

Is there any filing fee, deposit, or fee-based alternative associated with an independent presidential nominating petition, or is collecting the required signatures the sole path onto the general-election ballot?

fields.reliedValue: Lesser of (1) 1% of the votes cast for the same office at the last preceding election, or (2) 7,500. For an independent presidential candidate the operative number is 7,500, because 1% of Connecticut's 2024 presidential vote (1,759,010 total) is ~17,590, far above 7,500. The petition for the office of presidential elector must list seven candidates for Presidential Elector plus a candidate for President and a candidate for Vice-President. The Elections Services Division computes the exact figure at issuance, but the law prevails.
fields.note: Both ED-607 quotes (lesser-of-1%-or-7,500; seven electors + Pres + VP) found verbatim at lines 98-104 and 62-64; Ballotpedia quote found verbatim. Independently confirmed 7,500 is operative: CT 2024 presidential total = 1,759,010 (Wikipedia), 1% = 17,590 > 7,500. Operative number is statutory, not interpretation-dependent.

fields.reliedValue: Not later than 4:00 p.m. on the 90th day preceding the regular election. For the November 3, 2026 general election: submit to the Secretary of the State by 4:00 p.m. July 29, 2026; after July 29, pages may be submitted only to the town clerk of the signers' town of voting residence by 4:00 p.m. August 5, 2026. Withdrawals of signatures also run to Aug 5, 2026.
fields.note: ED-607 quote (Jul 29 SOTS / Aug 5 town clerk) found verbatim at lines 158-160; FAQ 90-day-rule quote found verbatim at line 66. Document states the specific 2026 dates authoritatively, consistent with the Nov 3, 2026 general election. Town-clerk Aug 5 date aligns with the 90-day rule; SOTS path closes a week earlier.

fields.reliedValue: None. Connecticut imposes no congressional-district or county signature-distribution quota. The only geographic constraints are (1) each signer must be an elector eligible to vote for all offices/candidates listed (statewide for president) and (2) no single page may be circulated among electors of more than one town (a per-page town-verification mechanic, not a distribution requirement).
fields.note: Both ED-607 quotes found verbatim (signer-eligibility at lines 109-111; one-town-per-page at lines 116-119). No district/county quota appears anywhere in the instructions; the one-town-per-page rule is correctly characterized as a town-clerk verification mechanic, not a geographic distribution requirement.

fields.reliedValue: Each circulator must be a United States citizen, at least 18 years of age, and not on parole for conviction of a felony; a candidate may circulate and sign his or her own petition. STRICTEST POSTURE pending SOS confirmation: treat in-state residency as potentially required and prefer Connecticut-resident circulators, because the asserted absence of any residency requirement is an inference from the form's silence (it asks circulators to state name/street/town/STATE of residence) rather than an affirmative statement of no-residency.
fields.note: Affirmative criteria (US citizen, 18+, not on felony parole) found verbatim in ED-607 (lines 90-91) and FAQ (lines 51-52); 'candidate may circulate own' grounded in FAQ. But 'no in-state residency requirement' is a NEGATIVE inferred from the form asking for state of residence; retrieval cannot conclusively confirm no residency rule exists. Held conservative to avoid ballot-access risk from out-of-state circulators; SOS inquiry added.

fields.reliedValue: Each page must carry the circulator's Statement of Residence and Eligibility and of the Authenticity of Signatures, signed under penalties of false statement, attesting each signer signed in the circulator's presence and was known to or identified to the circulator. That statement must be signed in the presence of an authorized officer (town clerk, Secretary of the State, notary public, judge of a court of record, family support magistrate, clerk/deputy clerk of a court with a seal, commissioner of deeds, Connecticut justice of the peace, or a Connecticut-admitted attorney), who must certify the circulator signed before him and was known/identified. Pages lacking the statement and certification are not accepted. This is a notarization-type attestation, not a self-notarized affidavit.
fields.note: Both ED-607 quotes found verbatim (attestation language at lines 138-141; list of authorized officers at lines 143-146). The officer-certification requirement and the rejection of pages lacking it (lines 149-151) confirm this is an officer-witnessed attestation, accurately characterized.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Independent (nominating-petition) presidential signatures must be wet-ink on the official Petition Signature Page distributed by the Secretary of the State, or on duplicates of pages issued by the SOTS; each page is town-specific and physically certified by the town clerk/registrars against the registry list. The SOTS DocuSign system applies only to primary petitions, certificates of party endorsement, 15% eligibility, and minor-party nominations with statewide ballot access, not to independent nominating-petition signature collection.
fields.note: ED-607 'official Petition Signature Page... or duplicates' quote found verbatim at lines 119-121; candidate-ballot-access DocuSign-scope quote found verbatim. Core operative fact (signatures = wet-ink paper) is solidly grounded and not interpretation-dependent. The secondary sub-claim that 'only ED-602b may be emailed' is not directly substantiated by the sources but does not affect the operative captureMode.

fields.reliedValue: Treat collecting the required 7,500 signatures as the sole and exclusive path onto the ballot for an independent/petitioning presidential candidate; assume NO filing fee and NO fee-based alternative, but confirm with the SOS before relying on the absence of any fee. No filing fee or fee alternative appears in the 2026 ED-607 instructions or the 2020/2026 FAQ, but the cited quote substantiates only the signature count, not the no-fee proposition.
fields.note: The attached citation quote (signature-count FAQ language) was found verbatim, so citation integrity holds, BUT that quote addresses signature counts, not fees, so it does not affirmatively substantiate 'no fee.' The 'None' claim is a NEGATIVE inferred from the sources' silence on fees. Substantively CT has no nominating-petition filing fee, but a negative cannot be conclusively settled by retrieval; held conservative and SOS inquiry added.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found; CT in-state residency required for circulators
compensationModel.note: Connecticut's candidate/independent nominating-petition statutes (Conn. Gen. Stat. 9-453a to 9-453u) and the Secretary of the State's official ED-607 instructions address only WHO may circulate (U.S. citizen, at least 18, a resident of a town in Connecticut, not on parole for a felony) and signature authenticity — they are entirely SILENT on compensating circulators, so per-signature pay for candidate petitions is unaddressed (not affirmatively permitted or prohibited). Connecticut's initiative/ballot-measure pay-per-signature framework does not apply because Connecticut has no statewide initiative process; the candidate-petition code controls and does not carry it over. Circulator in-state residency IS clearly required, which would bar a national/out-of-state paid circulator pool from validly circulating in Connecticut.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nominating petition under Conn. Gen. Stat. 9-453a et seq., may a campaign pay circulators on a per-signature basis, and may circulators be paid at all (e.g., hourly or salaried)? Also, does the 9-453j requirement that a circulator state residency "in this state" mean every circulator must be a Connecticut resident?

citations.quote: Each page of a nominating petition submitted to the town clerk or the Secretary of the State ... shall contain a statement as to the residency in this state and eligibility of the circulator and authenticity of the signatures thereon, signed under penalties of false statement, by the person who circulated the same. Such statement shall set forth (1) such circulator's residence address, including the town in this state in which such circulator is a resident, (2) the circulator's date of birth and that the circulator is at least eighteen years of age, (3) that the circulator is a United States citizen and not on parole for conviction of a felony, and (4) that each person whose name appears on such page signed the same in person in the presence of such circulator...

The town clerk or Secretary of the State shall not accept any page of a nominating petition unless the circulator thereof has signed before him or an appropriate person as provided in section 1-29, the statement as to the residency in this state and eligibility of the circulator and authenticity of the signatures thereon required by section 9-453j.
Who Can Be a Circulator: Each circulator of a nominating petition page must be a United States citizen, at least eighteen years of age, and shall not be on parole for conviction of a felony.

Circulator's Statement of Residence and Authenticity of Signatures: Boxes on the back of each petition page setting forth the circulator's name, street address and town of residence must be filled out before the circulator signs the Circulator's Statement of Residence and Eligibility and of the Authenticity of Signatures.

voterFile.cost: $300 flat for the full statewide voter registry file (CSV only). Paid by check, credit card, or cash; payment must be received before the file is released.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Open to any requester (no candidate/committee or registered-voter eligibility gate), BUT use is statutorily restricted to permitted purposes only: "election-related, scholarly, journalistic, political or governmental purposes." A petition-signature-verification use by a campaign/committee qualifies as an election-related/political purpose.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Strong statutory use limits under CGS 9-50d as amended by Public Act 26-1 §96: may NOT be used for any personal, private, or commercial purpose, including (I) harassment of any voter/household (per §53a-183), (II) advertising, solicitation, sale or marketing of products or services, or (III) reproduction/display in print, digital, or broadcast format. Violations are subject to a civil penalty imposed by the State Elections Enforcement Commission under §9-7b. Additionally, per PA 26-1 §96, the month and day of a voter's date of birth are exempt from disclosure (only year of birth is released to the public).
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request by mail or email to the Secretary of the State, LEAD, P.O. Box 150470, Hartford, CT 06115 / LEAD@ct.gov, using the "Connecticut Voter Registration Data File Request Form." Pay the $300 fee (check, credit card via the Credit Card Processing Form, or cash); the file is released only after payment is received. Questions: 860-509-6100 / LEAD@ct.gov.
voterFile.note: Connecticut sells the full statewide voter registry file (CSV only) for a $300 flat fee from the Secretary of the State's Legislation and Elections Administration Division (LEAD) via a written/emailed request; the file is generated from the statewide Centralized Voter Registration System and includes voter-application data (name, residential address), party enrollment, registration status, and election/participation history — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters. Classified RESTRICTED rather than PAID because, although anyone may purchase it, its use is legally confined to election-related/political/scholarly/journalistic/governmental purposes (no commercial, personal, or solicitation use) and is enforced by SEEC civil penalty.

citations.quote: To purchase a copy of the voter registry file you must make a request in writing either by mail or email at: Secretary of the State LEAD P.O. Box 150470 Hartford, Connecticut 06115 LEAD@ct.gov
The cost of the file is $300 and can be paid by check or with credit card; cash is also accepted. We must receive payment before releasing the voter registry file.

The Centralized Voter Registration System is utilized by all towns in Connecticut. It is the exclusive means by which a town produces an official voter registry list. The system includes information contained in voter registration applications, indicates whether eligible voters participated in past elections and primaries, and whether they voted in person or by absentee ballot.
Voter Registration Data File Request. Available only in CSV format ... Payment: $300 (If you pay with a credit card, please see the Credit Card Processing form)

Pursuant to CGS 9-50d as amended by Public Act 26-1 § 96, Voter registration information may only be used for election-related, scholarly, journalistic, political or governmental purposes, and shall not be used for any personal, private or commercial purpose, including, but not limited to, (I) harassment, as described in § 53a-183, of any voter or voter's household, (II) advertising, solicitation, sale or marketing of products or services to any voter or voter's household, and (III) reproduction in print, digital or broadcast visual or audio, or display in any other format, of such information.

(B) Any person who violates the provisions of subparagraph (A) of this subdivision shall be subject only to a civil penalty imposed by the State Elections Enforcement Commission pursuant to subsection (a) of § 9-7b, as amended by this act.

In accordance with Public Act 26-1, Sec. 96, the month and day of a voter's date of birth are exempt from public disclosure. Accordingly, only the voter's year of birth will be included in information released to the public.

circulator.ageRule: The circulator's affidavit (statement by circulator) under CGS § 9-453j must set forth "the circulator's date of birth and that the circulator is at least eighteen years of age." So circulators must be 18 or older. (This is a circulator-specific requirement, distinct from the signer's elector requirement.)
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: must be an elector (registered voter) — § 9-453a petition form warns "YOU MAY NOT SIGN THIS PETITION IF YOU ARE NOT AN ELECTOR." CIRCULATOR: must be a Connecticut resident, at least 18, a U.S. citizen, and not on parole for a felony (§ 9-453j) — but the circulator is NOT required to be a registered voter/elector.
circulator.note: CGS § 9-453j ("Statement by circulator") governs who may circulate a Connecticut nominating petition (the vehicle for independent/unaffiliated presidential candidates, §§ 9-453a–9-453s). The circulator must sign, under penalties of false statement, an affidavit setting forth: (1) residence address including the town IN THIS STATE in which the circulator resides → Connecticut residency required; (2) date of birth and that the circulator is AT LEAST EIGHTEEN years of age → 18+; (3) that the circul

citations.quote: Each page of a nominating petition submitted to the town clerk or the Secretary of the State and filed with the Secretary of the State under the provisions of sections 9-453a to 9-453s, inclusive, or section 9-216 shall contain a statement as to the residency in this state and eligibility of the circulator and authenticity of the signatures thereon, signed under penalties of false statement, by the person who circulated the same. Such statement shall set forth (1) such circulator's residence address, including the town in this state in which such circulator is a resident, (2) the circulator's date of birth and that the circulator is at least eighteen years of age, (3) that the circulator is a United States citizen and not on parole for conviction of a felony, and (4) that each person whose name appears on such page signed the same in person in the presence of such circulator and that either the circulator knows each such signer or that the signer satisfactorily identified himself to the circulator.

Such form shall include, at the top of the form and in bold print, the following: WARNING IT IS A CRIME TO SIGN THIS PETITION IN THE NAME OF ANOTHER PERSON WITHOUT LEGAL AUTHORITY TO DO SO AND YOU MAY NOT SIGN THIS PETITION IF YOU ARE NOT AN ELECTOR.

signaturesHeadline: DE: 1% of statewide registration (Dec 31 prior yr) — HOLD on the "~7,600" number; rely on the formula, confirm exact count w/ Commissioner
deadlineHeadline: DE: Petition + sworn non-affiliation declaration due by close of business Sept 1 of election year (circulate Jan 1–Jul 15) — LIVE
riskNote: Core legal rule (1% statewide, Sept 1 deadline, notarized circulator affidavit, paper-only, no fee) is fully grounded in 15 Del. C. 3002 + DE elections office; two holds — the specific ~7,600 signature count is unverified and possibly low (use the 1% formula, not a fixed number), and circulator eligibility is statutorily silent (assume strict: adult DE registered-voter circulators) pending Commissioner confirmation.
sosInquiries: For an independent/unaffiliated presidential candidate, exactly how many valid signatures does the 1% threshold equal for the current cycle (i.e., what was Delaware's total statewide registered-voter count as of December 31 of the year preceding the general election), and is there a published certified number we should rely on?

Does Delaware impose any eligibility requirements on the person who circulates an unaffiliated candidate's nominating petition (e.g., minimum age, Delaware residency, or being a registered Delaware voter), beyond the requirement that the circulator provide a sworn notarized witness statement under 15 Del. C. 3002(c)(4)?

fields.reliedValue: Not less than 1% of the total number of voters registered statewide in Delaware as of December 31 of the year immediately preceding the general election year (15 Del. C. 3002(b)). This is a recomputed percentage with no fixed statutory number; do NOT plan to a specific count such as ~7,600. Treat the required number as the 1% formula applied to the certified statewide registration total for the actual cycle, and confirm the exact figure with the State Election Commissioner before relying on it. Path: unaffiliated-candidate nominating petition; president is a statewide office.
fields.note: Legal rule fully grounded: statute quote (15 Del. C. 3002(b)) and DE elections-office quote both appear VERBATIM at the cited pages; Ballotpedia corroborates the 1% statewide basis. Adversarial re-derivation confirms the 1% formula is correct and current. HOLD is driven solely by the embedded, self-disclaimed estimate 'roughly 7,600 signatures (~760,000 registrants).' This number is NOT from a primary source and I could not independently verify it (web search was intermittently unavailable). Recent DE statewide registration has run well above 760k (plausibly ~770k-790k+), so 1% could be ~7,700-7,900 — meaning 7,600 may UNDERSTATE the requirement, a ballot-access risk. Stripped the hard number; relied value is the formula only.

fields.reliedValue: The nominating petition and the sworn unaffiliated-candidate declaration of non-affiliation must be filed on or before the close of the official business day on September 1 of the general election year, with the State Election Commissioner (president is a statewide office) per 15 Del. C. 3002(b). Separately, the petition may be circulated and executed only between January 1 and July 15 of the election year (15 Del. C. 3002(d)).
fields.note: Statute quote (3002(b)) appears VERBATIM at delcode.delaware.gov/title15/c030; Ballotpedia quote (Sept 1 filing; Jan 1-Jul 15 circulation) appears VERBATIM in the saved page (line 231). Independently re-derived: 3002(b) text confirms Sept 1 close-of-business filing; 3002(d) confirms the Jan 1-Jul 15 circulation window. Clean calendar facts, no interpretation. Added the circulation window to reliedValue because it is a hard companion constraint the draft mentioned only under distributionRule.

fields.reliedValue: None. A single statewide 1% threshold applies with no per-county or per-congressional-district minimum quota. Signatures are collected on county-segregated petition sheets — each signer must sign the petition prepared for the county in which that voter is registered, and each county's petition is filed with and verified by that county's Department of Elections (15 Del. C. 3002(d)). This is an organizational/filing rule, not a minimum-per-county distribution requirement.
fields.note: Statute quote (3002(d): 'Such petition shall be circulated and executed between January 1 and July 15... Each registered voter signing shall do so upon a petition prepared for the county in which that registered voter is registered, and the petition shall be filed with the Department. The signatures and other information thereon shall be verified by the department receiving the petition.') appears VERBATIM. Re-derivation confirms no per-county minimum exists in Chapter 30; the county-segregation is purely organizational. Characterization is accurate.

fields.reliedValue: Treat circulators conservatively: use circulators who are adults, Delaware residents, and themselves registered Delaware voters, and who can appear before a notary to swear they witnessed each signature (15 Del. C. 3002(c)(4)), pending written confirmation from the State Election Commissioner. The statute imposes no EXPRESS age/residency/voter-registration requirement on the circulator, but its silence does not rule out requirements in the Commissioner's prescribed petition form or regulations; the strict assumption avoids any risk of signature/petition rejection. Each SIGNER must be a registered Delaware voter on the date of signing (3002(e)(1)).
fields.note: Citation grounded: the quote '(1) The signer was not a registered voter on the date the signer signed the petition;' appears VERBATIM in 3002(e). The draft's value is 'UNKNOWN' for circulator qualifications, which matches the statute text (3002 has no circulator-eligibility clause; only the notarized witness statement in 3002(c)(4)). HOLD because correctness hinges on absence-of-evidence: retrieval cannot confirm that the Commissioner's prescribed form/regs impose no circulator requirements, and 'UNKNOWN' is not a safe operational value. Over-comply with the strictest plausible practice and ask the Commissioner directly (see sosInquiries).

fields.reliedValue: Yes. The nominating petition must contain a sworn notarized statement of the person circulating the petition that they witnessed the placing of each signature (15 Del. C. 3002(c)(4)). Separately, the candidate must file a sworn declaration (with the State Election Commissioner for a statewide/presidential candidate) stating non-affiliation with any political party for at least 90 days before filing (3002(b)). Each signer must also acknowledge that knowingly entering false information may subject the signer to perjury prosecution (3002(c)(3)).
fields.note: Both statute quotes appear VERBATIM at delcode.delaware.gov/title15/c030: 3002(c)(4) notarized circulator-witness statement, and 3002(b) the sworn non-affiliation declaration to the State Election Commissioner for statewide candidates. The perjury acknowledgment (3002(c)(3)) is also present in the retrieved text. Re-derivation confirms all three. Correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Wet-ink paper petition only. Each signer provides signature, printed name, registration address, Social Security number, and date (15 Del. C. 3002(c)(2)), on county-segregated paper petition sheets with a notarized circulator certification, filed with and verified against the permanent registration record by the county Department of Elections. The Department provides the unaffiliated petition as a downloadable PDF to be printed, circulated, and physically filed; there is no digital/online submission path for this petition.
fields.note: Statute quote ('Signature, printed name, address at which registered, social security number and date on which signed by signer;') appears VERBATIM in 3002(c)(2); elections-office quote ('Complete Petition for Unaffiliated Candidate to Secure Ballot Status by September 1st of a general election year.') appears VERBATIM. The operative value 'paper' is correct and is also the strict/safe answer. The 'NO digital system' claim is a negative I cannot exhaustively prove, but it is strongly supported (SSN + notarized wet-ink witness + downloadable PDF form), and it does not loosen any requirement, so AUTO-LIVE on the value 'paper'.

fields.reliedValue: None. There is no filing fee in lieu of, or in addition to, signatures for the unaffiliated (independent) path; the signature petition is the sole means of ballot qualification. Filing fees (no more than 1% of the office's total term salary, set by the party, 15 Del. C. 3103) apply only to major-party primary candidates, not to the unaffiliated petition route or to a presidential candidate using it.
fields.note: Both elections-office quotes appear VERBATIM: 'Set filing fees of no more than 1% of the total salary for the term of an office. ( 3103)' (listed under major political parties) and 'File An Unaffiliated Candidate Declaration' (under unaffiliated candidates, with no fee). Re-derivation confirms the unaffiliated path in Chapter 30 has no fee and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option. Correct.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: Delaware's candidate/unaffiliated-petition statute (15 Del. C. § 3002, which governs independent presidential candidates) does NOT address circulator compensation at all — neither permitting nor prohibiting per-signature pay, hourly pay, or any payment. There is no initiative/referendum process in Delaware, so no pay-per-signature law exists to even consider. Circulator pay is unaddressed; Delaware imposes no residency or voter-registration requirement on circulators (the code "does not specify any requirements for circulators"), but the criminal code's anti-bribery provisions target election officers and voters, not circulators.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent/unaffiliated presidential candidate's nominating petition under 15 Del. C. § 3002, may the campaign compensate petition circulators on a per-signature basis, and are there any restrictions on paying circulators (e.g., hourly only) or on circulator residency/voter-registration status?

citations.quote: The nominating petition shall contain such information and be on a form as prescribed by the State Election Commission. The following information shall, however, be contained in the petition: ... (4) A sworn notarized statement of the person circulating the petition that person witnessed the placing of each signature on the petition.

Such petition shall be circulated and executed between January 1 and July 15 of the year in which the general election is held. Each registered voter signing shall do so upon a petition prepared for the county in which that registered voter is registered, and the petition shall be filed with the Department.

Filed petitions must contain a sworn, notarized statement that the circulator witnessed the signing of each signature and that, to the best of the circulator's knowledge and belief, the signers are registered voters of the election district in which the candidate is running. The code does not specify any requirements for circulators.

An independent presidential candidate must petition for access to the general election ballot. The petition must contain signatures equaling at least 1 percent of the total number of registered voters in the state as of December 31 of the year preceding the election. This petition must be filed with the state election commissioner by September 1 in the year of the election. This petition must be circulated between January 1 and July 15 of the year in which the election is taking place.

Whoever bribes or attempts to bribe anyone expecting to hold any official position under the election or registration laws of this State, either as registrar, inspector, judge or otherwise, by giving money or the promise of money, office or the promise of office or position either under the state or federal government, to perform any service for any political party in this State or to favor any candidate for political office shall be fined not more than $1,000 and may be imprisoned not more than 6 months.

voterFile.cost: $25.00 flat per request for most requestors (incl. "Public"); free of charge ("No Cost") for qualified candidates per 15 Del. C. §304(e) and for major/minor political parties per §304(a)-(c). One file format/scope per request.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Anyone may request via the Voter Registration File Request form, which has four requestor types: Major/Minor Political Party; Candidate; Public; and General Assembly/State Agencies/County or Local Government. Free copies are reserved for ballot-qualified candidates (or a member of their committee) and political parties; the public and others pay the set fee. Not FOIA-able (Title 29 Ch. 100 expressly does not apply).
voterFile.useRestrictions: Strict, sworn under penalty of perjury: information "will not be used for commercial solicitation or for any other purpose not related to the electoral process," and the requestor may not make it available to the public/third parties or publish/republish it in a way that allows such use. A material misstatement on the form may constitute grounds for conviction of perjury.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written/PDF application form (Voter Registration File Request, VRFM006) submitted to the State Election Commissioner with payment (check/cash/money order) or "No Cost" box checked. Requires a signed affirmation/oath sworn under penalty of perjury. File is delivered via secure (encrypted) email; allow up to 10 business days; limit of 1 active request at a time. Form: https://elections.delaware.gov/public/forms/pdfs/statewide_vreg_order_form.pdf
voterFile.note: Delaware does not offer an instant public download of the statewide voter file; it is obtained by submitting the Voter Registration File Request form to the State Election Commissioner with a sworn political-use affirmation. It is free for ballot-qualified candidates (and their committee) and for political parties; otherwise the published cost is $25.00 per request. The standard file for candidates/parties includes voter name, residential address, party affiliation, voting history, legislative district, phone, and year of birth — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters.

citations.quote: NOTE: There is no charge for voter registration lists for filed or nominated candidates.
7. Payment Information (Cost is $25.00 per request. Select one payment option) ... No Cost (See Title 15, Chapter 3, §304(e))

I swear or affirm, under penalty of perjury, that: The Voter Registration information and any accompanying documents received as part of this request will not be used for commercial solicitation or for any other purpose not related to the electoral process, or make the information available to the public or third parties or publish or republish the information in a way that allows it to be used in that manner.

Files and lists provided to major and minor political parties pursuant to subsections (a) through (d) of this section and to candidates for elective office pursuant to subsection (e) of this section shall be limited to voter names, addresses, political party affiliations, voting history, legislative district information, telephone numbers, and years of birth.

(e) The State Election Commissioner shall, upon request, provide free of charge to any candidate for election who has qualified to appear on an upcoming primary or general election ballot a file and/or list of registered voters for the jurisdiction for which that person has qualified as a candidate.

(g) The State Election Commissioner, in collaboration with the Department, shall establish a reasonable cost for files and lists of registered voters requested by persons or organizations not eligible to receive lists in accordance with this section.

Files and lists provided to the public shall be limited to voter names, addresses, political party affiliations, voting history, legislative district information, and years of birth. The Delaware Freedom of Information Act, Chapter 100 of Title 29, shall not apply to information contained in voter lists and files created and maintained pursuant to this title.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — rule binds signers only. 15 Del. C. § 3002 imposes no minimum age (or any other personal qualification) on the circulator. The only requirement placed on the circulator is § 3002(c)(4): "A sworn notarized statement of the person circulating the petition that person witnessed the placing of each signature on the petition." Because the petition must be notarized, the
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a registered Delaware voter (§ 3002(c)(2), (d), (e)); CIRCULATOR need only witness signatures and provide a sworn notarized statement (§ 3002(c)(4)) — no registration/residency/citizenship/age requirement on the circulator.
circulator.note: For an unaffiliated (independent) PRESIDENTIAL candidate, Delaware ballot access runs through the statewide unaffiliated-candidate nominating petition in 15 Del. C. ch. 30 § 3002 (Chapter 43 on Presidential Electors references the "unaffiliated presidential candidate" but adds no separate petition/circulator rule). The circulator's role is defined narrowly and affirmatively in § 3002(c)(4): the circulator ("the person circulating the petition") must provide "a sworn notarized statement" that the

citations.quote: (4) A sworn notarized statement of the person circulating the petition that person witnessed the placing of each signature on the petition.

Each registered voter signing shall do so upon a petition prepared for the county in which that registered voter is registered, and the petition shall be filed with the Department. The signatures and other information thereon shall be verified by the department receiving the petition.

Signatures may be rejected for the following reasons: (1) The signer was not a registered voter on the date the signer signed the petition; (2) The signer's place of residence or the signer's Social Security number do not match those appearing in the permanent registration record; (3) The signer did not list that signer's own Social Security number;

fields.reliedValue: 5,223 signatures of currently registered Hawaii voters (1% of the 522,236 votes cast in the 2024 presidential election). Formula-based: not less than 1% of votes cast at the last presidential election; recomputes each cycle.
fields.note: Grounded: OoE page states verbatim '5,223 signatures of currently registered voters' and 'In the 2024 presidential election 522,236 votes were cast'; HRS 11-113(c)(2)(B) states 'not less than one per cent of the votes cast in the State at the last presidential election.' Independently re-derived: 1% of 522,236 = 5,222.36, rounded up to 5,223 — matches OoE. Current and correct for an independent/unqualified-group presidential petition.

fields.reliedValue: 4:30 p.m. on the 90th day prior to the general election (HRS 11-113(c)(2)); for the 2028 cycle, August 9, 2028, 4:30 pm per the Office of Elections.
fields.note: Grounded: HRS verbatim 'not later than 4:30 p.m. on the ninetieth day prior to the general election'; OoE page verbatim 'deadline to file a presidential petition is August 9, 2028, 4:30 pm.' Independently verified: 2028 general election is Tue Nov 7, 2028; 90 days prior = Wed Aug 9, 2028 — matches the OoE date exactly. Formula recomputes each cycle.

fields.reliedValue: None. Single statewide total (1% of statewide presidential votes); HRS 11-113 imposes no per-congressional-district or per-county distribution requirement.
fields.note: Grounded: HRS 11-113(c)(2)(B) requires only 'signatures... which constitute not less than one per cent of the votes cast in the State' — full statutory text contains no district/county language. Ballotpedia confirms a single statewide 1% threshold. Correct negative finding.

fields.reliedValue: Do not rely on 'no circulator requirements' as a planning assumption. Strictest defensible posture: signers must be currently registered Hawaii voters (confirmed by statute/OoE); for circulators, obtain and inspect the chief-election-officer-prescribed petition form before assuming no age/residency/voter-status requirement, since HRS 11-113(c)(2)(B) authorizes the CEO to add 'other information as determined.'
fields.note: Grounded: Ballotpedia (state/county petitions page) verbatim 'Hawaii Revised Statutes do not address requirements for petition circulators. Specifically, there are no residency requirements for circulators'; OoE confirms signers must be registered HI voters. Adversarial: the cited Ballotpedia text addresses party-formation/fee-waiver petitions generally, not specifically the 11-113 presidential petition, and 11-113(c)(2)(B) expressly lets the CEO prescribe additional form content. Statutory silence on circulators does not foreclose a form-level requirement; retrieval cannot settle this without the actual prescribed form. Held to avoid risking ballot access.

fields.reliedValue: Confirmed and required: a pre-issuance notarized Declaration of Intent from each prospective candidate, plus a sworn application filed at submission. Strictest posture on the petition itself: the statute's enumerated petition contents include no per-page witness/notary, but verify the CEO-prescribed petition form for any circulator affidavit/witness attestation before assuming none.
fields.note: Grounded: HRS verbatim requires 'a notarized statement from each prospective candidate of that prospective candidate's intent... Prior to being issued the petition form' and a 'sworn application'; OoE confirms the notarized Declaration of Intent. The candidate-side notarization claim is fully correct. Adversarial: the negative claim that the petition itself bears no witness/notary rests on the statute's enumerated contents (signatures, names, statement of support, address, date) — but 11-113(c)(2)(B) permits CEO-added form content, so the petition-level negative is not fully settleable by retrieval. Held pending inspection of the prescribed form.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Physical petition form prescribed and provided by the chief election officer, issued only after the notarized Declaration(s) of Intent and application are submitted. No remote_online or in_person_digitized path exists for the independent presidential petition.
fields.note: Grounded: HRS verbatim 'A petition that shall be upon the form prescribed and provided by the chief election officer'; OoE verbatim 'Upon receipt of the completed application form and notarized statements of intent, the Office of Elections will issue the petition.' No statutory or OoE basis for any electronic/online signature collection for this path. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: None. No filing fee for the HRS 11-113 presidential petition and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option for president. The HRS ch. 12 filing-fee / petition-in-lieu scheme applies to state/county nomination-paper candidates, not to the presidential petition.
fields.note: Grounded: OoE 'To request a presidential petition, submit the following' lists only the application and notarized Declaration — no fee; HRS 11-113 contains no fee provision. Ballotpedia's affidavit/petition-in-lieu-of-fee quote is on the state/county political-candidates page and is expressly tied to 'the district in which the candidate is seeking election' (HRS ch. 12), confirming it does not apply to president. Correct distinction; correct negative finding.

compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found; HRS silent on paying circulators
compensationModel.note: Hawaii's election code does NOT address compensation of nominating-petition circulators. The controlling statute for an independent/unqualified-party presidential petition, HRS §11-113, prescribes the petition form, the 1%-of-last-presidential-vote signature threshold, and required signer data, but says nothing about whether circulators may be paid per signature or at all. The election-fraud statute (HRS §19-3) prohibits bribing electors/voters, not paying petition circulators. Hawaii's only pay-per-signature law context is the initiative/referendum debate, which does not apply (Hawaii has no statewide initiative process and is not among the 10 states banning per-signature initiative pay). Because candidate-petition circulator compensation is unaddressed, treat per-signature pay as not clearly authorized.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent/unqualified-party presidential nominating petition under HRS §11-113, does Hawaii law permit compensating signature gatherers on a per-signature basis, and are there any residency or registration requirements for the persons who collect/witness those signatures?

citations.quote: In the case of candidates of parties or groups not qualified to place candidates on the primary or general election ballots, the person desiring to place the names on the general election ballot shall file with the chief election officer ... A petition that shall be upon the form prescribed and provided by the chief election officer containing the signatures of currently registered voters which constitute not less than one per cent of the votes cast in the State at the last presidential election.
The Hawaii Revised Statutes do not address requirements for petition circulators. Specifically, there are no residency requirements for circulators.

Every person who, directly or indirectly, personally or through another, gives, procures, or lends, or agrees or offers to give, procure, or lend, ... any money or office or place of employment or valuable consideration to or for any elector, person for an elector, person in order to induce any elector to vote or refrain from voting

Pay-per-signature is a method of compensating signature gatherers, also known as circulators, who collect signatures for ballot initiatives and veto referendums. ... There are 26 states that provide for an initiative or referendum process. Sixteen (16) of those states allowed campaigns to make payments to signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected. Ten (10) of those states prohibited campaigns from paying signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected.

voterFile.cost: Per-county flat fee. Verified: Maui County = $100.00 flat (plus $7.35 postage). Honolulu/City & County and the other counties (Hawaii, Kauai) charge a comparable flat fee per the same statewide form but do not publish the exact figure online — contact the county clerk for the current amount.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Restricted to election or governmental use. The applicant must swear (on the county-clerk-approved "Affidavit on Application for Statewide Voter Registration Data") that the data will be used "only for election or governmental purposes." In practice this covers candidates, candidate/noncandidate committees, political parties, and government agencies; it is not a general open-public download. The request is approved/signed off by the County Clerk(s).
voterFile.useRestrictions: Strict election/government-use-only restriction enforced by sworn affidavit. The applicant "affirms under penalty of law that the voter registration data shall be used only for election or governmental purposes and not for any other purposes unless specifically authorized by law." Commercial/non-election use is not permitted. The form carries a Chapter 19 HRS warning that knowingly providing false information may be a Class C felony (up to 5 years imprisonment and/or $10,000 fine). The data already excludes phone numbers, birth dates, and Social Security numbers; SSN/DOB are available only to government agencies showing justified need (HAR §3-172-31(c)). Voters may apply under HRS §11-14.5 to suppress their own residence address for safety/privacy reasons.
voterFile.requestProcess: Submit the county-clerk "Affidavit on Application for Statewide Voter Registration Data" (per HRS §11-97 and HAR §3-172-31(c)) with payment to the County Elections Division; staff review and provide the file on USB/CD for pickup or mail in ~1-2 weeks.
voterFile.note: Hawaii administers voter registration at the county level (Honolulu, Hawaii, Maui, Kauai); the statewide voter file is obtained from a County Elections Division by submitting a sworn "election or governmental purposes only" affidavit and paying a per-county flat fee (Maui = $100). The file includes name, district/precinct, voter status, and residential address (address is part of the register and released for election purposes under HRS §11-97), so it supports matching petition signers to registered voters, but it omits phone numbers, dates of birth, and SSNs.

citations.quote: A voter's full name, district/precinct designation, and voter status shall be public; but all other personal information, as provided on the voter registration affidavit, shall be confidential except for election or government purposes in accordance with rules adopted by the chief election officer, pursuant to chapter 91.
Hawaii's voter registration data is an electronic file available for purchase, for election purposes only. No phone numbers, birth dates or Social Security numbers are provided.
The cost to purchase voter registration data from Maui County is $100.00. Include an additional $7.35 for postage (USPS Priority Mail Flat Rate Envelope) or a pre-paid shipping label.

The undersigned fully understands and hereby affirms under penalty of law that the voter registration data shall be used only for election or governmental purposes and not for any other purposes unless specifically authorized by law.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — rule binds signers only. HRS § 11-113(c)(2)(B) requires the presidential petition to contain "the signatures of currently registered voters" and "a statement that the persons signing intend to support those candidates," but imposes NO requirement (and no affidavit) on the person who carries/circulates the petition or collects signatures. Hawaii's nomination-paper sc
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a currently registered Hawaii voter; CIRCULATOR has no requirements.
circulator.note: Hawaii imposes NO circulator eligibility requirements for an independent/non-qualified-party presidential nominating petition. HRS § 11-113(c)(2)(B) governs the petition and binds only the SIGNERS (must be currently registered Hawaii voters); it contains no circulator/witness affidavit or verification — unlike most states, Hawaii's nomination-paper system (cf. HRS § 12-3) has no circulator certification at all. The Office of Elections' detailed presidential-petition page (application, declaratio

The petition shall contain the names of the candidates, a statement that the persons signing intend to support those candidates, the address of each signatory, the date of the signer's signature, and other information as determined by the chief election officer.

(B) A petition that shall be upon the form prescribed and provided by the chief election officer containing the signatures of currently registered voters ... The petition shall contain the names of the candidates, a statement that the persons signing intend to support those candidates, the address of each signatory, the date of the signer's signature, and other information as determined by the chief election officer.

signaturesHeadline: 1,000 sigs statewide (flat statutory #, not %) — no distribution requirement

sosInquiries: For an independent presidential/VP petition under I.C. 34-708A (verified per 34-1807 on the recall-petition form), do the paid-circulator disclosure and 'paid petition circulator' badge requirements of 34-1807(2), and the circulator 18-years-or-older requirement, apply to this petition — given that 34-708A waives only the Idaho-residency requirement for circulators?

fields.reliedValue: 1,000 signatures of qualified electors — a flat statewide number fixed by I.C. 34-708A(1), not a percentage formula. One petition with 1,000 signatures covers both president and vice president (treated as one office). Does not recompute per cycle.
fields.note: All three quotes verified verbatim. I.C. 34-708A(1): 'a petition signed by one thousand (1,000) qualified electors'; 34-708A(2): president/VP are 'one (1) office, and only one (1) such petition need be filed.' Independently corroborated by I.C. 34-708(2)(a) ('One thousand (1,000) for any statewide office') and Ballotpedia ('At least 1,000 qualified voters'). Current as of Jan 1, 2024. No distribution/percentage element. Correct and unambiguous.

fields.reliedValue: Declaration of candidacy with attached 1,000-signature petition must be filed with the Secretary of State no later than August 1 of the election year (I.C. 34-708A(1)). Signatures verified no later than September 1, and SoS certifies candidate qualifications no later than September 1 (I.C. 34-708A(3)). Date is fixed in statute, not recomputed per cycle.
fields.note: Verbatim: 'must file declarations of candidacy as independent candidates no later than August 1' and 'Signatures on the petitions...shall be verified no later than September 1...The secretary of state shall certify candidate qualifications no later than September 1.' Ballotpedia agrees ('on or before August 1'). The Aug 1 filing date is the controlling, candidate-facing deadline; the two Sep 1 dates are administrative (verification + certification). Correct.

fields.reliedValue: None. The 1,000 signatures are a single statewide total with no geographic distribution requirement (no per-congressional-district or per-county minimums).
fields.note: I.C. 34-708A(1) requires only 'a petition signed by one thousand (1,000) qualified electors' with no distribution language; 34-708 (statewide office) likewise sets a flat 1,000 with no distribution. The county-clerk certificate in 34-1807(4) references 'Legislative District Number' only as a verification/sorting mechanic — it is not a per-district quota. Adversarial check found no distribution requirement in either statute. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: For the independent presidential petition, circulators are NOT required to be Idaho residents (explicit carve-out, I.C. 34-708A(3)). STRICTEST POSTURE pending SoS confirmation: comply with the full 34-1807 circulator regime EXCEPT residency — i.e., assume circulators must be at least 18 (the incorporated affidavit form recites 'a resident of the State of Idaho and at least eighteen (18) years of age', residency waived but 18+ assumed to remain), and assume paid circulators must give the 34-1807(2) verbal/printed payment disclosure AND wear a 'paid petition circulator' badge. Signers must each be a qualified elector of Idaho and a resident of the county shown on the sheet.
fields.note: Citations grounded verbatim. Adversarial finding: 34-1807's circulator-eligibility provisions are textually scoped to petitions 'for an initiative or referendum' — subsection (1) (18+/residency) and subsection (2) (paid-circulator disclosure + badge) both open with that phrase, and the void clauses (6),(8) reference 'initiative and referendum petitions.' 34-708A incorporates only the verification 'manner' and the affidavit 'form' (and explicitly waives residency). So whether the 18+ requirement and the paid-circulator badge/disclosure legally attach to a PRESIDENTIAL petition is a genuine interpretive question retrieval cannot settle. The draft asserts they do; that is plausible (the affidavit form itself recites 18+) but not certain for the badge/disclosure, which live outside the form. Held conservatively: comply with all of it (over-comply, zero ballot-access risk) and confirm with SoS.

fields.reliedValue: Notarized circulator affidavit required on each signature sheet. The verification form incorporated by I.C. 34-708A(3) (the 34-1807(3) recall-petition form) contains a sworn circulator affidavit 'Subscribed and sworn to before me...Notary Public' with a notary seal block. In addition, the county clerk examines the sheets and attaches a certificate to the SoS certifying the signers are qualified electors (34-1807(4)). Both notarized circulator certification and county-clerk verification apply.
fields.note: All quotes verified verbatim. The notary requirement flows directly from the affidavit FORM, which 34-708A(3) expressly incorporates ('on a form similar to that used for recall petitions...as prescribed by the secretary of state'), so the notarization is part of the incorporated form, not the initiative/referendum-only operative provisions. County-clerk examination (34-1807(4)) is part of the incorporated verification 'manner.' This is the stricter, ballot-access-safe reading; any residual ambiguity only adds verification rigor and does not risk the ballot line. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: paper — wet-ink signatures only. I.C. 34-1807(7): 'Any signature that is not a physical signature, including an electronic signature, is void.' No Idaho digital/remote/online petition system exists for an independent presidential petition; the prescribed recall-style paper form with notarized circulator affidavits and county-clerk verification is the only mechanism. No remote_online or in_person_digitized capture available.
fields.note: 34-1807(7) verified verbatim. Notably, subsection (7) is NOT textually limited to 'initiative or referendum' (unlike subs 1, 2, 6, 8), and 34-708A verifies 'in the manner prescribed in section 34-1807,' so the wet-ink void applies. Even on the narrowest reading, paper/wet-ink is the strict, ballot-access-safe floor and there is no online alternative for this path. Over-complying with paper carries zero risk. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: None. No filing fee and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option for the independent presidential/VP path. The route under I.C. 34-708A is entirely petition-based (declaration + 1,000-signature petition); the statute specifies no fee, and 34-705 (where declarations are filed with the SoS) prescribes no fee for this filing.
fields.note: Both quotes verified verbatim. 34-708A specifies no fee; 34-705 ('All candidates for district, state and federal offices shall file their declarations of candidacy with the secretary of state') prescribes no fee. This is an inference from statutory silence (proving a negative), but the petition path is the exclusive mechanism and no fee or fee-in-lieu option appears anywhere in 34-708A/34-708/34-705, consistent with Ballotpedia. The operationally safe posture is identical either way: complete the petition. Correct; minor residual uncertainty reflected in confidence.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: Paid circulation permitted with disclosure; per-signature pay unaddressed for candidate petitions; Idaho residency required for all circulators
compensationModel.note: Idaho's independent-candidate petition statute (Idaho Code 34-708, which covers "federal" office and is the path for an independent presidential candidate, 1,000 qualified-elector signatures) expressly directs that signatures "shall be verified in the manner prescribed in section 34-1807" — the same statute that governs and permits PAID signature gatherers (subject to disclosure/badge) and that mandates circulator residency. So paid circulation is permitted and Idaho residency is required, but neither 34-708 nor 34-1807 addresses PER-SIGNATURE compensation specifically for candidate petitions — it is neither authorized nor prohibited in the text, so it is unaddressed and a CONSERVATIVE-HOLD is warranted. (Note: 34-1807's own text refers to "initiative or referendum" petitions, so the extent to which its paid-circulator disclosure provisions transfer to candidate petitions is itself an interpretation question to confirm with the SoS.)
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an INDEPENDENT presidential candidate's nominating petition under Idaho Code 34-708 (which directs verification "in the manner prescribed in section 34-1807"), (a) may a campaign compensate petition circulators on a PER-SIGNATURE basis, or only by hourly/salary; (b) do the paid-circulator disclosure, badge, and paid-circulator affidavit requirements of 34-1807(2) apply to candidate petitions; and (c) must every circulator be an Idaho resident at least 18 years old, such that a non-resident's signature sheets would be void?

citations.quote: Any person who desires to offer himself as an independent candidate for federal, state, district, or county office may do so by complying strictly with the provisions of this section. ... Each such declaration must be accompanied by a petition containing the following number of signatures of qualified electors: (a) One thousand (1,000) for any statewide office;

Signatures on the petitions required in this section shall be verified in the manner prescribed in section 34-1807, Idaho Code, on a form similar to that used for recall petitions under chapter 17, title 34, Idaho Code, as prescribed by the secretary of state.
(1) Any person who circulates any petition for an initiative or referendum shall be a resident of the state of Idaho and at least eighteen (18) years of age.

(2)(a) Any person who accepts payment to circulate a petition for an initiative or referendum shall, prior to accepting a signature: (i) Verbally notify any potential signatory that such person is accepting payment to gather signatures and provide the name of the person or entity providing payment to such person

(6) Any petition on which signatures are obtained by a person not a resident of the state of Idaho and at least eighteen (18) years of age shall be void. The definition of resident in section 34-107, Idaho Code, shall apply to the circulators of initiative and referendum petitions.

A petition circulator must be at least 18 years old and a resident of Idaho. The circulator must verify the signatures contained within the petition by affidavit, which must be signed by a notary public. Any petition upon which signatures are obtained from non-eligible individuals will be void.

voterFile.cost: $20 flat per report for the statewide "Voter Registration Information" list (or $20 for "Voter Registration AND Voter History"); Absentee Ballot Reports are $10. Verbatim: "Voter Registration Information ($20/report)", "Voter Registration AND Voter History ($20/report)", "Absentee Ballot Report ($10/report)". A separate generic public-records copy fee of 25¢/page applies to non-voter records, not to these reports.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Effectively open to the public / any requesting party — the form has no candidate/committee/party eligibility gate. The requester must agree to the Idaho Code 34-437A(3) use restriction and certify the request under penalty (67-6624). The voter file may be used "for any political purpose," so campaigns/committees clearly qualify. The form itself notes the requester's submitted info "will become public record."
voterFile.useRestrictions: Anti-solicitation restriction applies. Per Idaho Code 34-437A(3) (quoted on the form): the list may not be used to mail/deliver any advertisement, offer, or solicitation for money/services/anything of value — i.e., no commercial mailing or telephone list use (also referenced via Idaho Code 74-120 on the public-records page). However, the statute expressly carves out political use: "any such list and label may be used for any political purpose," which covers a campaign verifying petition signatures against registered voters. Requester signs a certification under Section 67-6624, Idaho Code, making misuse a sworn-statement violation.
voterFile.requestProcess: Online request form (no instant download; an SOS staff-fulfilled report). Submit the "Voter Registration and Absentee Ballot Reports" form at forms.sos.idaho.gov: choose scope (Statewide / Congressional District / Legislative District), report type (Voter Registration Information, or Voter Registration AND Voter History), then provide a signed Certification ("Yes, I agree" under Section 67-6624, Idaho Code) plus a printed name/signature and acknowledge the 34-437A(3) use prohibition. The report is delivered as a .CSV download link emailed to the requester. ("Reports requested online will be provided in .CSV text format and made available as a download link sent to the email address you provide above.")
voterFile.note: Compiled from Idaho Secretary of State primary sources (Elections Division / Public Records Requests page and the official Voter Registration and Absentee Ballot Reports request form). The legislature.idaho.gov statute page (Idaho Code 34-437A) could not be fetched directly during this session, but the operative statutory text of 34-437A(3) is quoted verbatim from the SOS request form.

citations.quote: Voter Registration Information ($20/report)
Voter Registration AND Voter History ($20/report)
Absentee Ballot Report ($10/report)
Reports requested online will be provided in .CSV text format and made available as a download link sent to the email address you provide above.

Pursuant to Section 34-437A(3), Idaho Code: "No person to whom a list of statewide electors is furnished and no person who acquires a list of statewide electors prepared from such list shall use any information contained therein for the purpose of mailing or delivering any advertisement or offer for any property, establishment, organization, product, or service or for the purpose of mailing or delivering any solicitation for money, services, or anything of value. Provided however, that any such list and label may be used for any political purpose."
I hereby certify that the above information is true, complete and correct in accordance with Section 67-6624, Idaho Code.
Voter Registration & Absentee Ballot Reports ... Please use the Voter Registration & Absentee Ballot request form.
Information requested may not be used for purposes of a mailing or telephone list, as prohibited by section 74-120, Idaho Code.

circulator.ageRule: Minimum age 18. The presidential independent petition statute, Idaho Code 34-708A(2), directs that signatures "shall be verified ... in the manner prescribed in section 34-1807, Idaho Code ... except that the petition circulators are not required to be Idaho residents." Section 34-1807(1) provides that "Any person who circulates any petition ... shall be a resident of the state of Idaho and at lea
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: must be one of 1,000 "qualified electors" (i.e., registered Idaho voters) per 34-708A(1); the 34-1807 affidavit requires "each signer is a qualified elector of the State of Idaho and a resident of the county." CIRCULATOR: must be at least 18 (34-1807(1), incorporated by 34-708A(2)); residency is EXPRESSLY waived for the presidential petition ("the petition circulators are not required to b

citations.field: circulatorMustBeStateResident / circulatorAgeRule (governing presidential statute + residency carve-out)
citations.quote: (1) Any person who desires to be an independent candidate for the office of president must file a declaration of candidacy as an independent candidate no later than August 1. Such declaration must state that such person is an independent candidate with no political party affiliation and shall have attached thereto a petition signed by one thousand (1,000) qualified electors. (2) Signatures on the petition required in this section shall be verified no later than September 1 in the manner prescribed in section 34-1807 , Idaho Code, on a form similar to that used for recall petitions under chapter 17, title 34, Idaho Code, as prescribed by the secretary of state, except that the petition circulators are not required to be Idaho residents.

signaturesHeadline: 5,000 valid signatures (flat statewide, no distribution) — AUTO-LIVE
deadlineHeadline: 12:00 noon Monday before the Aug primary; recomputes each even year (2024: Aug 5, 2024) — AUTO-LIVE
riskNote: Signature count, deadline, paper capture, and no-fee-substitute are solid and AUTO-LIVE; the residual risk is in execution rules (per-sheet county collation under 25-303(f), whether the circulator "affidavit" must be notarized, and circulator qualifications) — all held conservatively pending SOS confirmation. Note 2026 is a midterm with no presidential race, so the live presidential deadline must be recomputed for the next presidential even year (e.g., 2028).
sosInquiries: For a statewide (state-at-large) independent presidential nomination petition under K.S.A. 25-303, does the per-sheet rule in 25-303(f) (all signers on one sheet must reside in the same county and election district of the office sought) require that petition sheets be collated/organized by county, and is there any minimum-per-county or per-congressional-district distribution of the 5,000 signatures?

For an independent presidential nomination petition, does the circulator/candidate 'affidavit' required by K.S.A. 25-303(f) have to be sworn and notarized, or does a signed verification without a notary satisfy the statute? Is there an official SOS petition form that prescribes the verification format?

Does Kansas impose any qualifications on a petition circulator for an independent nomination petition (e.g., age, Kansas residency, voter registration, or party-affiliation status), beyond the requirement that the circulator or candidate personally witness each signature under K.S.A. 25-303(f)?

fields.reliedValue: 5,000 signatures of qualified (registered) Kansas voters. President is a state-at-large office, so the flat statewide threshold under K.S.A. 25-303(c) applies. Fixed statutory number, not a percentage; does not recompute by cycle. No district/county distribution required.
fields.note: Grounded verbatim in K.S.A. 25-303(c) ('any office to be filled by the voters of the state at large... not less than 5,000 qualified voters') and Ballotpedia's 2024 presidential page ('At least 5,000 qualified voters must sign the petition'). Adversarial check: 25-303(d)'s 4% formula and the 25 / 5,000 floor-and-cap apply only to sub-state offices, not to a state-at-large office; the 5,000 figure is fixed in statute and does not recompute. Re-derived independently and could not refute.

fields.reliedValue: 12:00 noon on the Monday preceding the August primary (K.S.A. 25-305(b)); primary is the first Tuesday of August in even-numbered years (K.S.A. 25-203). For the most recent presidential cycle (2024): primary Tue Aug 6, 2024 → independent nomination petition deadline 12:00 noon Mon Aug 5, 2024. Recomputes each even year (SOS lists 12:00 p.m. Mon Aug 3, 2026 for the 2026 non-presidential cycle). Filed with the Secretary of State for national office.
fields.note: Grounded verbatim: 25-305(b) (independent nomination petitions due 'no later than 12:00 noon on the Monday preceding the date fixed for the holding of primary elections in accordance with K.S.A. 25-203'); 25-203 (primary 'first Tuesday of August in even-numbered years'); SOS page ('Independent Nominations: 12:00 p.m. Monday, August 3, 2026'). Adversarial check: President is a national office and 25-305(b) covers 'national... offices' with no presidential petition carve-out (25-305(d) carves out only WRITE-IN candidacy, not petitions), so the August deadline governs. Date arithmetic confirmed (Aug 2024 first Tuesday = Aug 6; Monday preceding = Aug 5). Value is cycle-dependent and the draft flags this; for the next presidential year (2028) the date must be recomputed. AUTO-LIVE for the cited 2024 value with the recompute caveat preserved.

fields.reliedValue: No per-district or per-county signature-distribution quota: the 5,000 signatures for a state-at-large office (25-303(c)) form a single statewide pool, and 25-303(d)'s per-division percentage applies only to sub-state offices. HOWEVER, K.S.A. 25-303(f) requires that all signers appearing on any single petition sheet reside in the same county and election district of the office sought. Strictest operational reading: organize/collate petition sheets by county so no sheet mixes signers from different counties. Confirm sheet-collation requirement with the SOS before circulating.
fields.note: Both quotes grounded verbatim in 25-303. The 'no distribution quota' conclusion is correct and well supported (25-303(c) is a flat statewide number). But 25-303(f)'s same-county/same-election-district per-sheet rule is a genuine operational constraint whose application to a state-at-large petition (the 'election district' being the whole state, yet the 'same county' limit still binding per sheet) is an interpretation retrieval cannot fully settle. Held conservatively (collate by county) to avoid a defective-petition / signature-invalidation risk. SOS inquiry filed.

fields.reliedValue: Statute (K.S.A. 25-303(f)) is silent on circulator age/residency/registration; it requires only that the candidate or 'petition circulator' personally witness each signature and append a signed verification. Because this is an unverifiable negative (an SOS rule or official form could impose qualifications not in the statute), do not rely on 'anyone may circulate.' Strictest defensible posture: have the candidate or a qualified, registered Kansas elector circulate and personally witness every signature, and confirm circulator qualifications with the SOS before deploying volunteers.
fields.note: Quote grounded verbatim in 25-303(f). The asserted value is the ABSENCE of a statutory circulator qualification — a negative that retrieval cannot conclusively establish (no SOS administrative rule was located confirming the absence). Defaulting to the strictest interpretation per the over-comply mandate; held pending SOS confirmation rather than published as AUTO-LIVE. SOS inquiry filed.

fields.reliedValue: A circulator/candidate verification must be appended to each set of petition documents (K.S.A. 25-303(f)) attesting the circulator/candidate personally witnessed every signature. The statute uses the word 'affidavit,' which ordinarily implies a sworn instrument. Strictest defensible reading: treat it as a sworn affidavit and have it notarized (or execute it on the official SOS petition form exactly as prescribed) rather than assume a bare unsworn signature suffices. Confirm notarization requirement and form with the SOS.
fields.note: Quote grounded verbatim. The draft concludes 'NOT notarization,' but 25-303(f) expressly calls it 'The affidavit of the candidate or a petition circulator' even while the operative clause requires only a signed verification. 'Affidavit' vs. 'signed verification' is precisely a legal-interpretation conflict retrieval cannot settle, and getting it wrong (unsworn where sworn is required) risks petition rejection. Over-comply: assume notarization may be required; hold and verify with SOS. SOS inquiry filed.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Independent nomination petitions are physical papers carried by circulators; each registered-voter signer must add place of residence and post office address, with a circulator/candidate verification appended to each set. No Kansas online/digital petition system covers an independent presidential nominating petition; petitions are filed in paper form with the Secretary of State.
fields.note: Grounded verbatim in 25-303(f) ('signatures... need not all be appended to one paper, but each registered voter signing... shall add... place of residence and post office address'). Statute plainly contemplates physical papers carried by circulators; no statutory or SOS digital-petition pathway exists for this petition type. Re-derived and could not refute. Note: this fixes COLLECTION mode as paper; it does not bear on the circulator-verification execution questions above.

fields.reliedValue: None. There is no filing fee that substitutes for signatures for an independent candidate; the valid nominating petition is the only route onto the general-election ballot, and the GEC/SOS fee is paid in addition to (not in lieu of) the petition. The party-primary 'petition OR filing fee' option does not extend to independent candidates.
fields.note: Both quotes grounded verbatim on the SOS candidate-information page ('Independent candidates do not run in the primary and may only file to run for office by petition'; footnote 1: 'Independent candidates may file for office only by petition. Upon filing a valid petition and paying the GEC/SOS fee, the candidate's name will be placed directly on the general ballot...'). Adversarial check: the page's general line 'Candidates must file by petition or filing fee' is the party-primary option; the independent-specific footnote confirms petition is mandatory and the fee is additive, not a substitute. Correct and current; AUTO-LIVE.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found; circulator residency NOT required
compensationModel.note: Kansas's candidate/independent-petition law (K.S.A. 25-303, 25-3608, 25-302a) and the Secretary of State's Candidate Petition Circulation Guide and Election Standards Chapter V address only circulator qualifications, the witness affidavit, residency, and signer rules — they are SILENT on compensation, so paying candidate-petition circulators per signature is UNADDRESSED (neither expressly authorized nor prohibited). Kansas has no statewide initiative process, so there is also no initiative pay-per-signature statute to import; circulators need NOT be Kansas residents, so a national paid pool is not blocked on residency grounds.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nominating petition under K.S.A. 25-303, may a campaign compensate petition circulators on a per-signature basis, and are there any restrictions on paying circulators (e.g., a per-signature ban, hourly/flat-fee requirement, or registration/disclosure obligations)?

citations.quote: Candidates and petition circulators may carry a petition through the election district if they meet the constitutional qualification to be an elector. However, they are not required by law to be registered voters in the election district. Each circulator must: 1.) witness every signature on every page; 2.) collect signatures from a single county on each page; 3.) sign, and have notarized, an affidavit that is attached to or printed on the back of the petition form.

According to Kansas law, circulators must: [1] be a United States citizen; [2] be at least 18 years of age; [3] have not been convicted of a felony or if convicted of a felony been pardoned or have had civil rights restored; and [4] agree to submit to the jurisdiction of the state for purposes of subpoena enforcement regarding the integrity and reliability of the petition process. [KSA 25-3608]

The U.S. Supreme Court has held that states may require petition circulators to possess the qualifications of electors in the states where they circulate petitions ... but they may not require actual registration or residency in the specific county or election district. Buckley vs. American Constitutional Law Foundation, Inc. Kansas law reflects this decision, as well as a similar Kansas federal case, that petition circulators need not be Kansas residents. [KSA 25-302a]; See Constitution Party of Kansas, et al. v. Biggs, No. 10-CV-4043 (D. Kan.)

The affidavit of the candidate or a petition circulator shall be appended to each petition and shall contain ... a verification, signed by the circulator or candidate, to the effect that such circulator or candidate personally witnessed the signing of the petition by each person whose name appears thereon.

voterFile.cost: Per official Kansas SOS "Voter Registration Data Request" (form CVR, Rev. 3/2/26): entire state = $300/CD-ROM; per U.S. Rep / State Senate / State Rep / State Judicial / State BOE district or per county = $100 per selection; per party affiliation = $50 per selection; advance-voting lists = $300 daily subscription, $150 weekly subscription, or $50 one-time; exclude inactive voters = $50 per selection. Data delivered via Email or CD-ROM. (County-level copies are available separately from each county election officer at "not more than the actual cost" per K.S.A. 25-2320.)
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — "Any person may make a written request." There is no candidate/committee eligibility gate. The only restriction is on USE (see useRestrictions), not on who may request. Driver's license number and last-4 SSN are removed before disclosure; all other registration info is public record.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Commercial use is prohibited and is a crime (class C misdemeanor) under K.S.A. 25-2320a — "Use of voter registration lists for commercial purposes is knowingly selling, giving or receiving the information... with the intent to use such list or information for any commercial purpose." Crucially, the statute expressly exempts political use: "compiling, using, giving, receiving, selling or purchasing the information on or derived from voter registration lists, solely for political campaign or election purposes, shall not constitute a commercial use." Signature verification for a candidate/committee petition is a political/election purpose and is permitted.
