The requester must sign the form certifying a right of access and acknowledging the commercial-use prohibition.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request via the SOS "Voter Registration Data Request" form (CVR): select database scope, delivery method (Email or CD-ROM), and any filters; total the per-selection fees; pay by pre-paid account or credit card; and SIGN the certification (right-of-access + commercial-use acknowledgment). Mail/submit to Kansas Secretary of State, Docking State Office Building, 915 SW Harrison St., Topeka, KS 66612 (contact: Kade Urban, kade.urban@ks.gov, 785-296-0080). No notarized affidavit required; no instant online download of the bulk file. (County election officers must provide requested copies within 10 days under K.S.A. 25-2320.)
voterFile.note: Kansas sells the statewide voter file through the SOS via form CVR; the standard product includes voter name, residential address, party affiliation, and active/inactive status (DL# and last-4 SSN are removed), which is sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters. Pricing is flat/tiered (e.g., $300 for the entire state on CD-ROM), and political/election use — including petition signature verification — is explicitly exempt from the commercial-use prohibition in K.S.A. 25-2320a.

citations.quote: 3. Database: o The entire state ($300/CD-ROM)  ...  4a. District: ... ($100 per selection)  ...  4b. Party affiliattion: ... ($50 per selection)  ...  5. Advance voting lists: o Daily subscription during advance voting period ($300) o Weekly subscription during advance voting period ($150 total) o One-time selection of advance voting period ($50)  6. Exclude: o Inactive voters ... ($50 per selection)

K.S.A. 25-2320a prohibits using information derived from voter registration records for commercial purposes. This includes using public records to sell property or services. ... Use of voter registration lists for commercial purposes is a crime. Sign below to request a record search and to indicate your understanding of the conditions outlined above.

Use of voter registration lists for commercial purposes is a class C misdemeanor. For purposes of this section, compiling, using, giving, receiving, selling or purchasing the information on or derived from voter registration lists, solely for political campaign or election purposes, shall not constitute a commercial use of voter registration lists.

Any person may make a written request for a copy of the registration books at any time except on any election day. ... The cost of copies shall be established by the county election officer at a price which is not more than the actual cost

All registered voters are listed on the state voter rolls, which are maintained in a centralized database. All information provided by the voter when registering to vote, except their driver's license number and last four social security, are public record.

circulator.ageRule: K.S.A. 25-3608(a)(2) requires a petition circulator to be "at least 18 years of age." This is a hard statutory floor that binds the circulator (the person who carries the petition and signs the verification), distinct from any requirement on signers.
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a "qualified voter"/"duly registered voter" residing in the relevant district (K.S.A. 25-303(c),(f); 25-205(b)). CIRCULATOR is defined separately in K.S.A. 25-3608: must be a U.S. citizen, at least 18, and a non-felon (or restored), but is expressly NOT required to be a registered voter or a Kansas resident (residents and nonresidents both permitted).
circulator.note: Independent presidential candidates in Kansas qualify by an independent nomination petition signed by not less than 5,000 qualified voters statewide (K.S.A. 25-303(c)); 2026 independent filing deadline is 12:00 p.m. Aug. 3, 2026 per the SoS. The circulator qualifications are governed by a dedicated defined-term statute, K.S.A. 25-3608 ("Petition circulator defined"), enacted in the same 2014 act (L. 2014, ch. 98) that amended 25-303 and 25-205. K.S.A. 25-205(d) expressly cross-references "a peti

citations.quote: 25-3608. Petition circulator defined. (a) For purposes of this act, "petition circulator" shall mean a person who is: (1) A United States citizen; (2) at least 18 years of age; and (3) has not been convicted of a felony or if convicted of a felony under the law of any state or of the United States, has been pardoned or restored to such person's civil rights.

(b) All petition circulators, whether residents or nonresidents of the state of Kansas, are required to agree to submit to the jurisdiction of the state, including its agencies, political subdivisions and election officials, for purposes of subpoena enforcement regarding the integrity and reliability of the petition process.

The affidavit described in this paragraph of a petition circulator as defined in K.S.A. 25-3608, and amendments thereto, or of the candidate shall be appended to each petition and shall contain, at the end of each set of documents carried by each circulator, a verification, signed by the circulator or the candidate, to the effect that such circulator or the candidate personally witnessed the signing of the petition by each person whose name appears thereon.

citations.field: signerVsCirculator (signer = qualified voter; circulator-vs-signer affidavit for independent petitions)
citations.quote: Independent nominations of candidates for any office to be filled by the voters of the state at large may be made by nomination petitions signed by not less than 5,000 qualified voters for each candidate ... The affidavit of the candidate or a petition circulator shall be appended to each petition and shall contain, at the end of each set of documents carried by each circulator or candidate, a verification, signed by the circulator or candidate, to the effect that such circulator or candidate personally witnessed the signing of the petition by each person whose name appears thereon.

citations.field: jurisdiction / independent presidential petition requirement (5,000 signatures, Aug 3 2026 deadline)
citations.quote: Independent Nominations: 12:00 p.m. Monday, August 3, 2026 ... Independent candidates may file for office only by petition. ... Percent requirements for petitions are based upon the total number of registered voters in the district as provided by KSA 25-205 and 25-303.

signaturesHeadline: 5,000 registered voters statewide (no distribution) + $500 fee, both required — KRS 118.315
deadlineHeadline: File by Fri after 1st Tue in Sept = Sep 8, 2028, 4:00 p.m. local (earliest affix/file Nov 3, 2027)
riskNote: All 8 fields grounded against primary sources (KRS 118.315, KRS 118.365, SOS 2028 President/Independent packet); quotes verbatim. Adversarial check cleared the KRS 118.591(1) cross-reference in current 118.315(2): 118.591 governs the presidential PREFERENCE PRIMARY petition (party path), NOT the independent general-election petition — no conflict. Two HOLDs are conservative-only (negative inferences about circulator rules / conditional notarization guideline), not refutations; substantive values stand.
sosInquiries: Does Kentucky impose any eligibility requirement (residency, age, KY voter registration) or attestation duty on a person who merely collects/circulates signatures on an independent presidential Nominating Petition (SBE 64/64-2), separate from each signer's own voter-registration requirement? Is there any State Board of Elections administrative regulation on circulators?

Confirm: for the independent/political-group presidential Nominating Petition (SBE 64/64-2), is NO notarization required for the petitioner signatures (i.e., the conditional 'if the filing form requires notarization' guideline does not apply to this form), and is there no separate circulator/witness affidavit?

fields.reliedValue: 5,000 registered voters statewide. Independent (or political group/organization) presidential ticket files a Nominating Petition (SBE 64 / SBE 64-2) under KRS 118.315, signed by the candidates for President and Vice President and a minimum of 5,000 registered voters (KRS 118.315(2): 5,000 petitioners for an office for which all electors of the state are entitled to vote). Each petitioner must include signature-affixation date, date of birth, and residence address (not a P.O. box); omission of any voids that signature. Signatures may not be affixed before the first Wednesday after the first Monday in November of the year preceding the election (Nov. 3, 2027 for the 2028 cycle).
fields.note: KRS 118.315(2) verbatim: '...shall contain five thousand (5,000) petitioners' for statewide office; SOS 2028 packet verbatim confirms '5,000 registered voters' and affix date Nov 3, 2027. Statute is current (eff. June 27, 2025). Adversarial: the new 118.315(2) carve-out 'excluding President of the United States in accordance with KRS 118.591(1)' refers ONLY to the presidential-preference-PRIMARY petition (party endorsement path under KRS 118.591) — it does not alter the independent general-election count. Verified by reading KRS 118.591 in full.

fields.reliedValue: Not later than the Friday following the first Tuesday in September preceding the election, by 4:00 p.m. local time. For 2028 = September 8, 2028, 4:00 p.m. local time. Earliest date to file (and to affix signatures) = first Wednesday after first Monday in November of the prior year = November 3, 2027. Statutory basis: KRS 118.365(6) (window) and KRS 118.365(8) (4 p.m. cutoff). Recomputes each cycle.
fields.note: KRS 118.365(6) and (8) verbatim-grounded. Independently re-derived 2028: first Tuesday in Sept 2028 = Sept 5; Friday following = Sept 8, 2028 — matches the SOS 2028 packet's stated 'September 8, 2028' (4:00 p.m. local). No interpretation; arithmetic, statute, and official packet all agree.

fields.reliedValue: None. The 5,000 signatures are a single statewide total with no geographic distribution (no per-congressional-district or per-county quota). Petition is for an office 'for whom all the electors of the state are entitled to vote'; signers are 'voters of the same district or jurisdiction from which the candidates seek nomination' (the whole state).
fields.note: KRS 118.315(2) sets one flat 5,000 statewide number for statewide office and lists no distribution requirement; SOS petition form's 'undersigned petitioners' clause verbatim confirms a single statewide pool. No distribution language anywhere in the statute or form.

fields.reliedValue: STRICT: Each signer must be a Kentucky registered voter from the jurisdiction (the state) and must personally list the signature date, date of birth, and a residential address that is NOT a P.O. box; omission of any of these voids that signature (KRS 118.315(2)). Candidates must not be registered members of a political party, and must not have been registered party members on January 1 immediately preceding the election. Do NOT rely on any assumption that a person who merely collects signatures is free of residency/age/registration constraints until confirmed with the State Board of Elections, since that is an absence-of-law inference not settled by retrieval.
fields.note: Core signer-eligibility and candidate-party-disaffiliation facts are verbatim-grounded (KRS 118.315(1)-(2)) and correct. HOLD is conservative-only: the draft's claim that 'no statutory age or residency rule is imposed on a person who merely collects signatures' is a negative inference (no circulator block on SBE 64/64-2, no statute found), which retrieval cannot conclusively settle vs. a possible SBE administrative regulation. Over-comply: do not promise an unrestricted out-of-state circulator without SBE confirmation. Inquiry filed.

fields.reliedValue: STRICT: No notary/witness affidavit and no per-petition circulator certification appears on the SBE 64 / SBE 64-2 petition; the only sworn element is the candidates' declaration that they possess the constitutional and statutory qualifications (KRS 118.315(2); declaration on the form). The SOS general guideline states notarization applies only 'If the filing form requires notarization' — treat as: confirm with SBE that the presidential Nominating Petition is NOT a notarized form before relying on the no-notary conclusion.
fields.note: The actual SBE 64/64-2 form (read in full) has no notarization line and no circulator/witness attestation on the petitioner pages — only the candidate declaration, verbatim-grounded. The draft's conclusion is supported by the primary form. HOLD is conservative because correctness rests on reading the SOS conditional guideline ('If the filing form requires notarization...') as not triggered by this form — an interpretation best confirmed with the office rather than assumed. Inquiry filed.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Independent presidential petition is collected on the State Board of Elections' prescribed paper Nominating Petition (SBE 64 and SBE 64-2, KRS 118.315) and filed with the Secretary of State's Election Division in person or by mail. No online/remote or in-person-digitized signature system exists for this petition; signatures must be physically affixed to the paper petition.
fields.note: SOS packet verbatim: 'Filing Form SBE 64 and SBE 64-2 ... Nominating Petition' and 'Candidates may file in person or by mail at the following address: Office of the Secretary of State, Election Division...'. No e-signature path appears in statute or packet. Strictest reading is paper-only, which the value already states.

fields.reliedValue: A $500.00 filing fee is required IN ADDITION to the 5,000 signatures (it is NOT a fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option). Independent/political-group/organization presidential candidates pay $500, payable to the Kentucky State Treasurer, filed with the Secretary of State together with the Nominating Petition. No fee alternative to signature-gathering exists.
fields.note: SOS 2028 packet lists BOTH 'Signatures Required: ... 5,000 registered voters' AND 'Filing Fee $500.00' for the same independent presidential filing, with 'Checks should be made payable to the Kentucky State Treasurer.' (verbatim) — confirming the fee is additive, not an alternative. No in-lieu provision anywhere.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found (per-signature ban exists only for ballot-question petitions)
compensationModel.note: For an independent PRESIDENTIAL candidate's nominating petition, Kentucky's controlling statute (KRS 118.315, the basis for the Secretary of State's SBE 64 Nominating Petition per the official filing packet) and the SOS filing instructions are entirely silent on petition circulators and on compensating them — candidate-petition compensation is UNADDRESSED, neither permitted nor prohibited. The only Kentucky statute criminalizing payment in connection with a petition, KRS 119.205(3), is expressly limited to "a petition to have a public question placed on the ballot" (ballot measures), so it does NOT carry over to candidate petitions; no candidate-petition per-signature rule exists. Circulators face no statutory residency/registration requirement (only signers must be registered KY voters), so a national paid pool could legally circulate, but because per-signature compensation for candidate petitions is unaddressed it should be treated conservatively.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent candidate for President filing a Nominating Petition (SBE 64) under KRS 118.315, may the campaign compensate the people who circulate/collect the petition signatures, and specifically may they be paid on a per-signature basis, or is only hourly/salary compensation permissible? Are there any residency or voter-registration requirements for circulators (as opposed to signers)?

citations.quote: The form of the petition shall be prescribed by the State Board of Elections. It shall be signed by the candidate and by registered voters from the district or jurisdiction from which the candidate seeks nomination. ... A petition of nomination for a state officer, or any officer for whom all the electors of the state are entitled to vote, shall contain five thousand (5,000) petitioners ... Each petitioner shall include the date he or she affixes the signature, address of residence, and date of birth.

The Nominating Petition shall be signed by the candidate for President and the candidate for Vice President and by a minimum of 5,000 registered voters from the district or jurisdiction from which the candidates seek nomination. Petitioners shall be registered voters and shall include the date the petition was signed, date of birth and residence address.

Any person who makes or offers to make an expenditure to any person to sign a petition to have a public question placed on the ballot, or any person who solicits, accepts, or receives any such expenditure as payment or consideration to sign a petition to have a public question placed on the ballot, shall be guilty of a Class B misdemeanor.
The state code does not specify petition circulator requirements. Specifically, there are no residency requirements for circulators.

voterFile.cost: Low flat statewide fee set by form SBE-84 (the fee schedule, formerly in 31 KAR 3:010, was last published as: CD-R minimum $25; $1.00 per 1,000 records up to 100,000, $0.50 per 1,000 over 100,000, "$450 statewide"; current exact figure must be confirmed on form SBE-84 itself).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Eligibility-gated. By statute (KRS 117.025(3)(i)) the State Board of Elections "shall furnish ... any and all precinct lists to duly qualified candidates, political party committees or officials thereof, or any committee that advocates or opposes an amendment or public question." It "may also furnish the precinct lists to other persons at the board's discretion." A petition/ballot-access candidate qualifies (a "duly qualified candidate" = anyone who has filed a letter of intent with the Registry of Election Finance or nomination papers with the SOS/county clerk).
voterFile.useRestrictions: Commercial use is prohibited by statute ("The board shall not furnish precinct lists to persons who intend to use the lists for commercial use"). 31 KAR 3:010 defines commercial use to include use/transfer for profit, solicitation of donations, or sale/advertisement of any good, service, or publication; political (including political fundraising), scholarly, journalistic, governmental, and news-media uses are exempted. The current regulation also bars furnishing data to requesters outside the U.S. or where the data would reasonably be expected to leave the U.S. Requester must affirm non-commercial use via sworn statement on form SBE-84.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written application: submit completed form SBE-84 ("Request for Voter Registration Data") to the State Board of Elections (140 West Walnut Street, Frankfort, KY 40601) with payment. The form requires a signed sworn statement that the requester has read KRS 117.025(3)(h)/(i) and 31 KAR 3:010, does not intend commercial use, and will not sell or give the lists for commercial use. A candidate's authorized representative must attach a candidate-signed statement on campaign letterhead. Not an instant online download.
voterFile.note: Kentucky does NOT sell its statewide voter file to the public; it is furnished only to candidates, parties, and ballot-question committees (others at the board's discretion) via form SBE-84 with a sworn non-commercial-use statement, at a low statewide fee. For petition-signature verification this is workable and lawful (political use is expressly permitted), and the standard list/CD-R includes voter name, residential address, county/precinct, party, gender, date of birth, and 5-year voting history. Note: as of the 31 KAR 3:010 version effective 5-5-2026 the fee schedule was moved out of the regulation and into form SBE-84, so the exact current price should be verified on the live SBE-84 form (the SBE site blocks automated fetching).

citations.quote: (i) Furnish at a reasonable price any and all precinct lists to duly qualified candidates, political party committees or officials thereof, or any committee that advocates or opposes an amendment or public question. The State Board of Elections may also furnish the precinct lists to other persons at the board's discretion, at a reasonable price to be determined by the board. The board shall not furnish precinct lists to persons who intend to use the lists for commercial use

Section 6. Requests for Voter Registration Lists. A request for voter registration lists shall be made by submitting a completed Request for Voter Registration Data, form SBE-84, to the State Board of Elections with payment of fees established in form SBE-84.

"Duly qualified candidate" means any person who has filed: (a) A letter of intent with the Kentucky Registry of Election Finance; or (b) Nomination papers with the Office of the Secretary of State or county clerk.

(7) The minimum charge for CD-R shall be twenty-five (25) dollars. (8) The charge for CD-R shall be one (1) dollar per 1,000 records up to 100,000 records; fifty (50) cents per thousand records over 100,000 records; $450 statewide.

"CD-R" means a compact computer disc with read-only memory containing the voter's name and address, county code, precinct code, gender, party, zip code, date of birth, date of registration, and five (5) year voting history generated from the statewide voter registration database.

Qualified candidates, political parties, and committees which advocate for or against an amendment or public question are eligible to request voter information. Every request must be submitted to the [State Board of Elections]

I further state that I have read KRS 117.025(3)(h) and 31 KAR 3:010, (quoted above), that I do not intend to use the voter registration lists for commercial use, that I will not sell or give the voter registration [lists for commercial use]

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — Kentucky's nominating-petition statutes impose NO age (or any other) requirement on the person who carries/collects the petition. KRS 118.315 and the related petition statutes regulate only the candidate (who must sign and swear a declaration of qualifications) and the signers/petitioners (who must be registered/qualified voters and must each write their own signatu
circulator.signerVsCirculator: The strict requirements bind the SIGNER only: each petitioner must be a registered voter ("electors qualified to vote") from the candidate's district/jurisdiction, and must personally record their signature date, residence address, and date of birth (omission voids that signature) — KRS 118.315(2). The CIRCULATOR (person who carries the petition and gathers signatures) is not mentioned anywhere; K
circulator.note: Independent presidential ballot access in Kentucky runs through the general nomination-by-petition statute, KRS 118.315 (5,000 valid registered-voter signatures statewide for a presidential/statewide office; signatures gatherable starting the first Wednesday after the first Monday in November of the year preceding the election). The statute's eligibility text is directed entirely at (a) the candidate and (b) the signers ("registered voters from the district or jurisdiction" / "electors qualified

citations.quote: (2) The form of the petition shall be prescribed by the State Board of Elections. It shall be signed by the candidate and by registered voters from the district or jurisdiction from which the candidate seeks nomination. ... Each petitioner shall include the date he or she affixes the signature, address of residence, and date of birth. Failure of a voter to include the signature affixation date, date of birth, and address of residence shall result in the signature not being counted.

(1) A candidate for any office to be voted for at any regular election may be nominated by a petition of electors qualified to vote for him or her, complying with the provisions of subsection (2) of this section.
The state code does not specify petition circulator requirements. Specifically, there are no residency requirements for circulators.
The signer of a petition must include the date he or she signs, his or her residence address, and his or her date of birth. ... Candidates are required to sign their own petitions.

citations.field: presidential petition (KRS 118.591 — requirements on signers/candidate, not circulator)
citations.quote: (1) Any person seeking the endorsement by a political party for the office of President of the United States ... may file with the Secretary of State certified petitions signed by five thousand (5,000) persons who, at the time they sign, are registered and qualified voters in the Commonwealth and are affiliated, by registration, with the same political party as the candidate for whom petitions are filed.

signaturesHeadline: 4,000-5,000 registered Maine voters (presidential-elector slate); any Maine voter may sign; no distribution rule — AUTO-LIVE
deadlineHeadline: CONSERVATIVE-HOLD: statute says July 25 cert / Aug 1 SOS for presidential electors, but Ballotpedia says May 25 / June 1 — treat EARLIER dates (May 25 / June 1) as the safe-harbor; signing not before Jan 1
riskNote: Two genuine interpretation risks need SOS confirmation before relying live: (1) the presidential-elector filing deadline (statute carve-out July 25/Aug 1 vs. Ballotpedia's May 25/June 1 for independents) — file by the earlier dates to be safe; (2) circulator eligibility (statute is silent, so do not assume non-resident/non-voter circulators are allowed — use Maine resident registered voters). All seven citations grounded verbatim; 2028 is the next presidential year (the 2026 SOS guide is a non-presidential midterm and omits the elector deadlines/figures, so the statute controls).
sosInquiries: For an INDEPENDENT/non-party candidate for President filing a slate of presidential electors by nomination petition under 21-A MRS sec 354, what are the exact 2028 deadlines to (a) deliver petitions to the municipal registrar for certification and (b) file with the Secretary of State — is it the July 25 / August 1 presidential-elector carve-out in sec 354(7)(B) and (8-A), and not the May 25 / June 1 dates that some summaries cite?

For a candidate nomination petition (not a ballot-question/initiative petition) under 21-A MRS sec 354, does the petition CIRCULATOR have to be a Maine resident and/or a registered Maine voter, or is the only requirement the notarized personal-witness oath in sec 354(7)(A)?

fields.reliedValue: At least 4,000 and not more than 5,000 registered Maine voters for the slate of presidential electors (21-A MRS sec 354(5)(A)); petitions for presidential electors may be signed by any Maine voter statewide (sec 354(2)). Flat statutory range, not a percentage of turnout.
fields.note: Grounded verbatim at sec 354(5)(A) ('For a slate of candidates for the office of presidential elector, at least 4,000 and not more than 5,000 voters') and sec 354(2) ('except that nomination petitions for presidential electors may be signed by any Maine voter'); Ballotpedia quote also present verbatim. Current per PL 2021 c.273. Independently re-derived from the controlling statute; no refutation found. The 2026 SOS Candidate's Guide omits presidential electors because 2026 is a non-presidential midterm, so the statute is the controlling source for the figure.

fields.reliedValue: STRICTEST/SAFE-HARBOR: deliver petitions to the municipal registrar for certification by 5 p.m. on May 25 and file with the Secretary of State by 5 p.m. on June 1 of the election year (the earlier of the two conflicting authorities — meeting these also satisfies the statute's later presidential-elector dates). Signatures may not be collected before January 1 of the election year (sec 354(6)). The statute (sec 354(7)(B), (8-A)) on its face provides a later presidential-elector carve-out of July 25 cert / August 1 SOS filing, but DO NOT rely on the later dates without SOS written confirmation.
fields.note: All three statutory quotes grounded verbatim: sec 354(7)(B) July 25 carve-out, sec 354(8-A) August 1 carve-out, sec 354(6) Jan 1 signing floor. ADVERSARIAL CONFLICT: Ballotpedia's general-election 'Independent candidates' section states for an independent presidential candidate that signatures go to registrars by 5 p.m. May 25 and to the SOS by 5 p.m. June 1 — directly contradicting the draft's July 25/Aug 1. The statute is controlling and its carve-out expressly names 'a slate of candidates for the office of presidential elector,' which matches this access path, so the draft's legal reading is likely correct. But which deadline a candidate must actually meet hinges on a legal interpretation (whether the sec 354 presidential-elector carve-out governs the independent presidential petition), the 2026 SOS guide is silent (non-presidential year), and the downside of error is loss of ballot access — so under strictest-interpretation rules the relied value is the earlier safe-harbor date, pending SOS confirmation. Jan 1 signing floor is clean.

fields.reliedValue: None. No geographic distribution requirement (no per-congressional-district or per-county minimum); presidential-elector petitions may be signed by any Maine voter statewide (21-A MRS sec 354(2)).
fields.note: Grounded verbatim at sec 354(2). Independently re-derived: sec 354 imposes signature COUNT requirements (sec 354(5)) but no distribution/allocation requirement, and the only geographic qualifier (electoral-division residency of signers) is expressly waived for presidential electors. No refuting provision found.

fields.reliedValue: STRICTEST: do not assume non-resident or non-registered-voter circulators are permitted. Safest practice is to use circulators who are Maine residents AND registered Maine voters. The statute (sec 354(7)(A)) affirmatively requires only that the circulator personally witness all signatures and swear the notarized oath; it imposes no express age/residency/voter requirement, and Ballotpedia states the circulator need not be a Maine resident — but this is a negative inference from statutory silence and should be confirmed with the SOS before relying on out-of-state or non-voter circulators.
fields.note: Grounded verbatim: Ballotpedia 'The petition circulator does not have to be a Maine resident...' and sec 354(7)(A) oath language both present. The draft's claim that NO residency/voter requirement applies rests on statutory silence (sec 354 does not affirmatively state circulators may be non-voters; it simply omits the requirement) plus a contrast with the sec 903-A initiative rule. That negative inference is a legal interpretation retrieval cannot conclusively settle, and a wrong assumption can void every signature a disqualified circulator collected. Ballotpedia supports the non-residency reading, but under strictest-interpretation + catastrophic-downside this is a CONSERVATIVE-HOLD pending SOS confirmation.

fields.reliedValue: Notarized circulator certification required: each nomination petition must carry the circulator's oath/affirmation before a notary public (or other authorized officer) that the circulator personally witnessed all signatures and that each signature is genuine and each signer resides in the named electoral division (21-A MRS sec 354(7)(A)). Separately, the candidate's consent must be sworn before a notary (sec 355(3)).
fields.note: Grounded verbatim at sec 354(7)(A) (circulator oath before a notary) and sec 355(3) ('The candidate must verify by oath or affirmation before a notary public... that the declaration is true'). Independently confirmed from primary text; both a circulator notarization and a candidate-consent notarization are required. No refutation.

fields.reliedValue: paper / in-person (wet-ink). Petitions must be on SOS-provided forms (sec 354), signers must personally sign in a manner satisfying the registrar (sec 354(3)), and the 2026 SOS forms must be printed double-sided on 8.5 x 14 legal paper. No online/remote or digitized signature-collection system exists for candidate nomination petitions.
fields.note: Grounded verbatim: sec 354 'A nomination petition shall be on a form provided by the Secretary of State'; sec 354(3) personal-signature language; and the SOS 'Becoming a Candidate' page 'Petitions must be printed double-sided on 8.5 x 14 inch (legal size) paper.' (markdown bold markers in the fetch are rendering artifacts, not page text). Wet-ink paper, circulator-witnessed, registrar-certified. No e-petition path for candidate access found.

fields.reliedValue: None. There is no filing fee in lieu of, or in addition to, signatures for an independent/non-party candidate. Ballot access is by nomination petition (4,000-5,000 signatures) plus the consent form only (21-A MRS sec 353).
fields.note: Grounded verbatim at sec 353 ('A person who seeks nomination by petition qualifies by filing a nomination petition and consent as provided in sections 354 and 355'). This is a negative finding from the controlling qualifying statute, which lists only petition + consent and references no fee; sec 354/355 likewise reference no fee. A fee alternative either exists in statute or it does not — it does not — so this is a factual negative, not a contestable legal interpretation. Well-corroborated; no fee option found in the controlling statutes.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: Maine's candidate-petition statutes (21-A §354 for independent/non-party nomination by petition, including presidential electors, and §335 for primary petitions) are entirely SILENT on circulator compensation — no per-signature prohibition and no authorization, so candidate-petition pay is unaddressed. The only Maine statute that addresses circulator payment and circulator residency, §903-A (subsections 4 and 5), sits in Chapter 11 (Ballot Questions) and applies expressly to direct initiatives / people's veto petitions "pursuant to the Constitution of Maine, Article IV, Part Third, Section 17 or 18" — it does NOT govern candidate petitions; therefore the initiative payment-disclosure and resident-voter-circulator rules do not carry over to candidate petitions, and candidate-petition circulators are not required to be Maine residents or registered voters.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent/non-party candidate's nomination petition for President under 21-A §354, may the campaign pay circulators on a per-signature basis (and may circulators be paid at all on an hourly or salaried basis), and are there any disclosure or residency requirements for paid candidate-petition circulators, given that §903-A applies only to direct initiative/people's veto petitions?

citations.quote: §354. Petition requirements ... 7. Certification of petitions. ... A. The circulator of a nomination petition shall verify by oath or affirmation before a notary public or other person authorized by law to administer oaths or affirmations that the circulator personally witnessed all of the signatures to the petition and that to the best of the circulator's knowledge and belief each signature is the signature of the person whose name it purports to be ... and each person is a resident of the electoral division named in the petition.

B. The names of presidential electors must be placed on the petition as a slate. The names of the candidates for President and Vice President must be placed on a petition for the nomination of presidential electors.

5. Circulator payment. A person or organization that pays individuals to circulate petitions, other than by reimbursing travel expenses, shall provide a list of those individuals to the Secretary of State along with information indicating the method by which the individuals are being compensated when the petitions are filed pursuant to the Constitution of Maine, Article IV, Part Third, Section 17 or 18.

4. Circulator affidavit. A person who circulates a petition shall execute an affidavit that must include: ... C. That the circulator was a resident of the State and a registered voter in the State at the time of circulating the petition

Title 21-A: ELECTIONS Chapter 11: BALLOT QUESTIONS ... §903-A. Circulation ... An applicant for a direct initiative or a people's veto referendum pursuant to section 901 shall provide to each person who will be circulating petitions a copy of the laws and rules governing the circulation of petitions for a direct initiative or people's veto

voterFile.cost: Electronic file priced by record count (tiered): $11 for 1-1,000 records up to $2,200 for 900,001+ records. A full statewide file (Maine has ~1M+ registered voters) falls in the top tier = $2,200. Printed/PDF report: $1 first page + 25 cents/additional page ($0.75/page for mailing labels). Up to 11 free updates in the 12 months after purchase.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Eligibility-gated and political-use-only. The Party/Campaign Use Voter File (21-A MRS §196-A(1)(B)) is available to parties, candidates, issue campaigns, GOTV efforts directly related to a campaign, and currently-serving elected/appointed officials. The requestor must submit proof they are authorized by the candidate, party, or issue campaign (e.g., an authorization letter). Members of the public may obtain the same file only via a written request to the Secretary of State for a purpose protected by the NVRA. Default CVR data is otherwise confidential, accessible only by state/municipal election officials.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Strict. Information from the CVR may not be used for any commercial purpose or any solicitation not directly related to political-party/campaign/GOTV activities (or NVRA-protected purposes); selling or distributing the data to others for commercial use is prohibited; officials may not transfer their copy to another entity. Discrimination on the basis of disability, race, color, age, sex, sexual orientation, religion, ancestry, or national origin using the data is prohibited. Civil penalties: up to $1,000 for a first offense and up to $5,000 for each subsequent offense (21-A MRS §196-A, sub-§5).
voterFile.requestProcess: Application form + payment + proof of authorization. The "Request for Obtaining Data from Maine CVR" form must be typed or completed in ink, signed, and submitted to the Secretary of State (Division of Elections) by mail (CVR Manager, 184 State House Station, Augusta, ME 04333-0184), fax (287-6545), or scanned email to cec.elections@maine.gov (must contain an actual handwritten signature). Campaign requestors must attach written proof of authorization from the candidate/party/campaign. Fees must be paid before data release. Office responds within 5 business days. Electronic data is delivered on physical media (thumb drive); email transfer of the data is not permitted.
voterFile.note: For petition-signature verification a campaign would obtain the Party/Campaign Use Voter File under 21-A MRS §196-A(1)(B), which includes voter name, residence and mailing address, enrollment status, and voter status (active/inactive) — sufficient to match signers to registered voters. It is restricted to political/campaign use, requires proof of campaign authorization, and a full statewide electronic file costs $2,200. (Note: the freely downloadable "Statewide Registered and Enrolled" file on the same page is only an aggregate statistical count and contains NO individual names or addresses.)

citations.quote: Title 21-A, (Elections), §196-A, governs the use and distribution of information from Maine's Central Voter Registration (CVR) system. Under this law, information contained electronically in the CVR and any information or reports generated by the system are confidential, and may be accessed only by state election officials or municipal election officials for the purposes of election and voter registration administration, except as otherwise provided in Title 21-A §196-A

Section 1- Party/Campaign Use Voter File – §196-A(1)(B) ... Available for party activities, candidate and issue campaigns, "get-out-the-vote" activities related directly to a campaign ... Contains the following data: voter's name, residence & mailing address, year of birth, enrollment status, electoral districts, voter status, date of registration, date of change, voter record #, voter participation history and special designations

Requestor must provide proof with the application that they are authorized by either the candidate or campaign to request the data (i.e., letter or other written correspondence authorized by the party, candidate or issue campaign).
Cost per number of records in the requested electronic file: For 900,001 or more records, $2,200; ... For 1,001 to 7,500 records, $22; or For 1 to 1,000 records, $11.

Information obtained from the CVR may not be used for any commercial purpose or for solicitation of any kind not directly related to activities of a political party, so-called "get out the vote" efforts directly related to a campaign or other activities directly related to a campaign. Selling or distributing the information to others to use for commercial purposes is prohibited.

Civil Penalties for violations of the CVR data use restrictions are set forth in 21-A MRS §196-A, sub-§5, and include fines of up to $1,000 for a first offense and up to $5,000 for each subsequent offense.

Municipal Clerks and Registrars, and the Secretary of State's Office, shall respond to all requests for information within 5 business days of receipt of the completed request form and payment of any applicable fees.
Central Voter Registration System (CVR) ... CVR Data Request Form Facts and Instructions (Word) ... Request Form for Obtaining Data from CVR (PDF)

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — 21-A MRSA §354 imposes NO minimum age on the circulator. The age/voter requirements in §354(2)-(3) bind the SIGNER ("voter"), not the circulator. The circulator's only statutory duty (§354(7)(A)) is to swear a notarized oath that they personally witnessed every signature. Practical caveat: because the circulator must execute an oath/affirmation before a notary publi
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a registered voter of the electoral division (for an independent presidential elector slate, "any Maine voter") per §354(2)-(3); the CIRCULATOR has NO voter-registration, residency, or stated age requirement — the circulator's sole obligation (§354(7)(A)) is to personally witness the signatures and swear a notarized oath. Maine SoS guidance (per Ballotpedia) affirmatively states the
circulator.note: 21-A MRSA §354 places the strict eligibility rules on the SIGNER, not the circulator. §354(2) "By whom signed" requires signers to be voters of the electoral division, "except that nomination petitions for presidential electors may be signed by any Maine voter." The circulator's only statutory burden is in §354(7)(A): verify by oath/affirmation before a notary that the circulator personally witnessed all signatures and that each signer is a resident of the electoral division. The statute imposes

citations.quote: 2. By whom signed. A nomination petition may be signed only by voters of the electoral division which is to make the nomination, except that nomination petitions for presidential electors may be signed by any Maine voter. Other signatures are void.

citations.field: circulatorAgeRule / circulator obligations (only duty is notarized witness oath)
citations.quote: A. The circulator of a nomination petition shall verify by oath or affirmation before a notary public or other person authorized by law to administer oaths or affirmations that the circulator personally witnessed all of the signatures to the petition and that to the best of the circulator's knowledge and belief each signature is the signature of the person whose name it purports to be; ... and each person is a resident of the electoral division named in the petition.

The petition circulator does not have to be a Maine resident. The circulator must verify before a notary public that he or she personally witnessed each signature made to the petition and that to the best of the circulator's knowledge each signature is valid.

citations.field: signer registration check (binds signer names, not circulator)
citations.quote: C. The registrar, or clerk at the request or upon the absence of the registrar, of each municipality concerned shall certify which names on a petition appear in the central voter registration system as registered voters in that municipality and may not certify any names that do not satisfy subsection 3.
A. For a slate of candidates for the office of presidential elector, at least 4,000 and not more than 5,000 voters;

signaturesHeadline: 2,000 signatures (statutory cap; 1% of statewide turnout vastly exceeds 2,000)
deadlineHeadline: 77 days before GE — HOLD: draft date Aug 20 2024 is stale; 2028 cycle = Aug 22 2028
riskNote: Signatures/fee/notary/distribution/capture all AUTO-LIVE and grounded; the only ballot-access risk is the deadline field, whose stated value (Aug 20, 2024) is for a past cycle — relying on it for a 2028 candidate would be wrong; recompute per cycle and confirm with SOS.
sosInquiries: For the 2028 general election (Tuesday, Nov. 7, 2028), what is the exact last day and time (5:00 p.m.?) by which a non-major-party presidential elector nominating petition under Minn. Stat. 204B.07/204B.09, subd. 1(c) must be RECEIVED — is it the day 77 days before the election (Aug. 22, 2028), and is that an inclusive/exclusive count?

Does Minnesota impose any age, residency, or voter-status requirement on the CIRCULATOR who collects signatures on an independent presidential nominating petition, or do requirements run only to the signers under Minn. Stat. 204B.08, subd. 2?

fields.reliedValue: 2,000 signatures from eligible Minnesota voters (Minn. Stat. 204B.08, subd. 3(a): 1% of last statewide general-election turnout or 2,000, whichever is less — 1% far exceeds 2,000, so 2,000 is the operative number).
fields.note: Statute quote verbatim at revisor.mn.gov/statutes/cite/204B.08 subd. 3(a). Ballotpedia verbatim ('At least 2,000 eligible voters must sign the petition'). 1% of ~3M MN voters is ~30,000, so 'whichever is less' fixes 2,000 as the cap. Current (2025 statutes).

fields.reliedValue: Petition must be RECEIVED by 5:00 p.m. on the day at least 77 days before general election day (Minn. Stat. 204B.09, subd. 1(c)). This recomputes each cycle: for the Nov. 7, 2028 GE the date is Aug. 22, 2028 (NOT the draft's Aug. 20, 2024, which was the 2024-cycle date). Confirm exact date/time with the SOS before relying.
fields.note: Statutory rule grounded verbatim (204B.09 subd. 1(c): 'may file petitions at least 77 days before the general election day' + 'No affidavit or petition shall be accepted later than 5:00 p.m. on the last day for filing'). BUT the draft's relied VALUE 'August 20, 2024' is stale: today is 2026-06-07 and the next presidential election is Nov 7, 2028, where 77 days before = Aug 22, 2028 (verified by date arithmetic). Relying on the 2024 date for a current candidate would be wrong. Also a latent interpretation: statute phrases the cutoff as 'may file...at least 77 days before' (an earliest-by phrasing) rather than an express last-day; practitioners read the deadline as 77 days before, but exact day/time inclusivity warrants SOS confirmation.

fields.reliedValue: None. A single statewide signature total applies (Minn. Stat. 204B.08, subd. 3(a)); no congressional-district, county, or other geographic distribution requirement. Signers need only be eligible Minnesota voters.
fields.note: 204B.08 subd. 3(a) sets one statewide number with no per-district/per-county quota anywhere in 204B.07-204B.08. Grounded verbatim. Correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: SIGNERS must be individuals eligible to vote for the candidate (eligible MN voters), may not sign more than one petition for the same office, and each must subscribe to the 204B.07 subd. 4 oath (both quotes grounded verbatim). No statutory CIRCULATOR age/residency/voter-status rule was located in 204B.07-204B.08 — but treat 'no circulator requirement' as unconfirmed (absence of a rule) and verify with the SOS rather than assuming none.
fields.note: Signer requirements and oath are grounded verbatim (204B.08 subd. 2; 204B.07 subd. 4) and correct. The circulator conclusion is a negative inference from statutory silence — defensible, but an absence is inherently harder to verify than a stated rule (SOS guidance or rule could impose one), so I hold it pending SOS confirmation. Strict posture: do not represent to volunteers that there is definitely no circulator restriction.

fields.reliedValue: Petition signatures: NO notarization or witness/circulator certification ('Notarization or certification of the signatures on a nominating petition is not required.', 204B.07 subd. 4). Candidate affidavit: YES, each nominated president/VP candidate must submit a signed, NOTARIZED affidavit of candidacy (204B.07 subd. 2(c)), signed before a notarial officer (204B.09 subd. 1(b)).
fields.note: 204B.07 subd. 4 signature-notarization quote grounded verbatim. The affidavit quote is verbatim as a prefix ('...affidavit of candidacy for president or vice president'); the statute continues '...that includes the following information:' — the draft truncated, not misquoted. 204B.09 subd. 1(b) confirms notarial signing. Correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: paper. No online/digital petition system exists; signatures collected on paper pages bearing the oath, each signer writing residence/mailing address (204B.07 subd. 4). Petition and notarized affidavits submitted by mail or hand and received by 5:00 p.m. on the last filing day; electronic submission statutorily barred (204B.09 subd. 1(e), notwithstanding ch. 325L) except a narrow emergency fax/scan option for out-of-state candidates (subd. 1a(b)).
fields.note: 204B.09 subd. 1(e) mail/hand-notwithstanding-325L quote grounded verbatim; 204B.07 subd. 4 signer-address quote grounded verbatim. Subd. 1(e) literally names 'Affidavits,' but subd. 2 also bars electronic for nominating petitions and no online petition system exists, so paper-only is correct in practice and current.

fields.reliedValue: Not applicable / no fee. Presidential electors pay no filing fee (204B.11 subd. 1(b): 'For the office of presidential elector...no filing fee is required'); the 2,000-signature nominating petition is itself the access mechanism. The separate 204B.11 subd. 2 'petition in place of a filing fee' (2,000 for president) is not the operative route because no fee applies.
fields.note: Both 204B.11 quotes grounded verbatim (subd. 1(b) no-fee; subd. 2(a) 2,000-signature fee substitute). Draft correctly distinguishes the inapplicable fee-substitution path. Correct and current.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found; MN election code does not regulate candidate-petition circulators at all (no circulator affidavit)
compensationModel.note: Minnesota's candidate-petition election code (Minn. Stat. ch. 204B and Minn. Rules ch. 8205) does NOT address compensating petition circulators — not per signature, not at all. There is no circulator affidavit, no circulator residency/registration requirement, and no pay-per-signature prohibition for candidate (independent presidential elector) nominating petitions; the code only regulates the candidate, the petition form, and the signers. Compensation is therefore unaddressed (not affirmatively authorized), so per-signature pay is treated conservatively as not-to-be-used pending confirmation. Note MN has no statewide initiative process, so there is no initiative pay-per-signature rule to carry over.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent/unaffiliated candidate's nominating petition for presidential electors under Minn. Stat. 204B.07, may a campaign pay the people who gather signatures, and specifically may they be compensated on a per-signature basis? Are there any circulator residency or voter-registration requirements?

citations.quote: Following the information required by subdivisions 1 and 2 and before the space for signing, each separate page that is part of the petition shall include an oath in the following form: "I solemnly swear (or affirm) that I know the contents and purpose of this petition, that I do not intend to vote at the primary election for the office for which this nominating petition is made, and that I signed this petition of my own free will." Notarization or certification of the signatures on a nominating petition is not required.
Other presidential electors or alternates are nominated by petition pursuant to this section.

A nominating petition may be signed only by individuals who are eligible to vote for the candidate who is nominated. No individual may sign more than one nominating petition for candidates for the same office unless more than one candidate is to be elected to that office.

8205.1020 CIRCULATING PETITIONS. Subpart 1. Applicability. This part applies to any petition required for any election in this state, including nominating petitions, recall petitions, and proposed recall petitions. Subp. 2. Photocopies. Petitioners may circulate photocopies of a sample petition page.

A person who willfully, directly or indirectly, advances, pays, gives, promises, or lends any money, food, liquor, clothing, entertainment, or other thing of monetary value ... to or for a person, in order to induce a voter to refrain from voting, or to vote in a particular way, at an election, is guilty of a felony.

voterFile.cost: $46 flat for a statewide report; $30 for any sub-state area. Pay by check, money order, or exact-change cash (no credit cards). Statutorily framed as "the cost of reproduction."
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Eligibility-gated: only a person registered to vote in Minnesota may obtain a copy ("This information is only available to registered Minnesota voters"; MN Stat. 201.091 Subd. 5 limits copies to "any voter registered in Minnesota"). Anyone may inspect the public information list in person without cost, but obtaining/purchasing a copy requires being a registered MN voter.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Political/election/law-enforcement use only. Use "for purposes unrelated to elections, political activities, or law enforcement" is prohibited (201.091 Subd. 4(b),(c) & Subd. 5). A recipient must NOT publish any of the data on the Internet in any searchable list/database, and must NOT sell, loan, provide access to, or otherwise surrender the information to others (201.091 Subd. 4(e)) — though an organization may share it with its own volunteers/employees for permitted purposes. Misuse is enforceable under Minnesota election law; the underlying restrictions are statutory.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request: complete the Secretary of State "Voter Information Request Form" (registered-voter-list-request-form.pdf) and mail or drop off the form with payment to the Office of the Secretary of State, Voter Registration Lists, 332 Minnesota Street, Suite N201, St. Paul, MN 55101. The requester must provide identification and state in writing that the data will not be used for non-permitted purposes (a sworn statement of permitted use, per 201.091 Subd. 4(c)). Data is then delivered via an emailed download link active for 7 days. Not an instant self-service online download.
voterFile.note: Minnesota does not offer a free public download of the statewide voter file. The statewide registered-voter list (name, residential address, phone if provided, year of birth, and voting history — sufficient to match a petition signer to a registered voter) is sold by the Secretary of State for $46 statewide via a mailed request form, but only to registered Minnesota voters and only for elections/political/law-enforcement use, with strong no-resale and no-internet-publication restrictions. Note the file contains year of birth only (full DOB, SSN, driver's license numbers are statutorily excluded under 201.091 Subd. 9).

citations.quote: Reports covering the whole state are $46. Reports covering any area within the state are $30.
Note: This information is only available to registered Minnesota voters, and may only be used for purposes related to elections, political activities, or law enforcement. (Minnesota Statutes 201.091)

Use the Voter Information Request Form to place an order. The completed form and payment may be mailed or dropped off to: Office of the Secretary of State, Voter Registration Lists, First National Bank Building, 332 Minnesota Street, Suite N201, Saint Paul, MN 55101.
An email will be sent with a link to the requested voter data after the order is processed. This link will be active for seven days. CD-ROMs are no longer available.

The county auditors and the secretary of state must provide copies of the public information lists in electronic or other media to any voter registered in Minnesota within five business days of receiving a complete written or electronic request accompanied by payment of the cost of reproduction.
The county auditor shall make available for inspection a public information list which must contain the name, address, year of birth, and voting history of each registered voter in the county.

No individual who inspects the public information list or who acquires a list of registered voters prepared from the public information list may use any information contained in the list for purposes unrelated to elections, political activities, or law enforcement.

a recipient of a public information list must not: (1) publish any of the information from the list on the Internet on any list, database, or other similar searchable format; or (2) sell, loan, provide access to, or otherwise surrender any information obtained from the list to any person or entity

A list provided for public inspection or purchase, or in response to a law enforcement inquiry, must not include a voter's date of birth or any part of a voter's Social Security number, driver's license number, identification card number, military identification card number, or passport number.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — the rule binds signers only. Minnesota's nominating petition statutes (Minn. Stat. ch. 204B) do not create a "circulator" role at all. There is no circulator affidavit/verification: Minn. Stat. 204B.07, subd. 4 expressly states "Notarization or certification of the signatures on a nominating petition is not required." Because no one must witness/verify/affirm the si
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be an individual "eligible to vote for the candidate" (a qualified MN voter — 18+, citizen, resident) and must take the oath under 204B.07 subd. 4; CIRCULATOR (collector) has NO statutory requirements at all — there is no circulator affidavit because notarization/certification of signatures "is not required" (204B.07 subd. 4).
circulator.note: Minnesota imposes NO requirements on the petition circulator/collector for an independent presidential nominating petition. The statutory scheme (Minn. Stat. 204B.07, 204B.08, 204B.09) regulates (a) the form of the petition and the candidate's notarized affidavit of candidacy, and (b) the qualifications and sworn oath of each SIGNER — but creates no circulator role whatsoever. Decisively, 204B.07 subd. 4 provides that "Notarization or certification of the signatures on a nominating petition is n

citations.quote: Notarization or certification of the signatures on a nominating petition is not required. Immediately after the signature, the signer shall write on the petition the signer's residence address including street and number, if any, and mailing address if different from residence address.

Subd. 4.Oath and address of signer. Following the information required by subdivisions 1 and 2 and before the space for signing, each separate page that is part of the petition shall include an oath in the following form: "I solemnly swear (or affirm) that I know the contents and purpose of this petition, that I do not intend to vote at the primary election for the office for which this nominating petition is made, and that I signed this petition of my own free will."
Subd. 2.Qualifications of signers. A nominating petition may be signed only by individuals who are eligible to vote for the candidate who is nominated.
Other candidates for presidential electors may file petitions at least 77 days before the general election day pursuant to section 204B.07.
for a federal or state office voted on statewide, one percent of the total number of individuals voting in the state at the last preceding state general election, or 2,000, whichever is less

Independent and minor party presidential candidates must petition for placement on the general election ballot. At least 2,000 eligible voters must sign the petition, which must be filed with the secretary of state no later than 77 days prior to the general election.

signaturesHeadline: 10,000 signatures (collect a cushion; statewide statutory floor)
deadlineHeadline: 15th Monday before Nov general (Jul 29, 2024 last cycle)
riskNote: All 6 citation sources grounded verbatim; core path (10k sigs, 15th-Monday deadline, paper+notary, elector district distribution) is AUTO-LIVE. Only the circulator-residency rule is held for legal interpretation; treat as MO-resident-required and confirm with SOS.
sosInquiries: For an independent/new-party presidential nominating petition under RSMo 115.321/115.325, must the circulator (affiant) be a Missouri resident as an enforceable eligibility requirement, and is there any age or registered-voter requirement for circulators? Does the office reject pages whose affiant is a non-resident?

fields.reliedValue: 10,000 signatures of registered Missouri voters (fixed statutory floor for a statewide office under RSMo 115.321.3; same 10,000 governs the new/minor-party statewide petition under RSMo 115.315.5). For president, ONE petition nominates the full slate of presidential electors, with the presidential and vice-presidential candidates named on each page. Operationally, collect well above 10,000 because the SOS validates signatures and invalidations are common.
fields.note: RSMo 115.321.3 ('signed by at least ten thousand registered voters of the state') and 115.315.5 ('at least ten thousand registered voters of the state obtained at large') both grounded verbatim on revisor.mo.gov. 10,000 is a fixed number for statewide office (does not recompute per cycle), unlike the district/county 2%-or-10k-whichever-is-less rule in 115.321.4. Statute says 10,000 registered voters must SIGN; 'valid' is the practical net after SOS review, so a cushion is advisable, but the threshold number 10,000 is correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: 5:00 p.m. on the fifteenth Monday immediately preceding the November general election (RSMo 115.329.1). For the 2024 cycle (general Nov. 5, 2024) this is July 29, 2024 — independently confirmed by date computation. It is a recomputed rule, not a fixed date. Petition cannot be filed before 8:00 a.m. on the day after the prior general election.
fields.note: RSMo 115.329.1 grounded verbatim (window between 8:00 a.m. day after prior general and 5:00 p.m. fifteenth Monday before the general). Ballotpedia quote also grounded verbatim. Independently re-derived: counting Mondays back from Nov 5, 2024 (a Tuesday), the 15th Monday is 2024-07-29 — matches the draft exactly.

fields.reliedValue: No geographic distribution quota on the SIGNATURES (the 10,000 are obtained 'at large'). There IS a distribution requirement on the ELECTOR NOMINEES: at least one qualified resident of each congressional district must be named as a presidential-elector nominee (RSMo 115.321.2). Separately, each petition page may carry signatures from only one county and pages must be numbered in sequence per county (RSMo 115.325.1) — a sheet-organization rule, not a per-county signature quota.
fields.note: RSMo 115.321.2 grounded verbatim ('At least one qualified resident of each congressional district shall be named as a nominee for presidential elector'). 'obtained at large' grounded in 115.315.5. The one-county-per-page / numbered-in-sequence rule grounded verbatim in 115.325.1. Draft correctly separates the elector-nominee district requirement from the (absent) signature distribution quota.

fields.reliedValue: STRICTEST reading: treat the circulator (affiant who obtains signatures) as required to be a resident of the State of Missouri (the statutory affidavit form in RSMo 115.325 recites 'I, ___, a resident of the state of Missouri'). Use Missouri-resident circulators to avoid any page being rejected. Each SIGNER must be a registered Missouri voter of the county shown on the page (felony to sign if not registered); no voter may sign more than one nomination for one office (RSMo 115.323).
fields.note: All quotes grounded verbatim in 115.325 (affidavit form) and 115.323. BUT the 'resident of the state of Missouri' language appears in a FORM TEMPLATE recital, not an operative eligibility clause; whether it is an enforceable circulator-residency REQUIREMENT (vs. a recital the affiant happens to make) is a legal-interpretation question retrieval cannot settle. Added constitutional doubt: circulator-residency requirements have been struck in other states (Buckley v. ACLF line). Strictest defensible posture is to assume MO residency is required and use only MO-resident circulators. Signer requirements (registered MO voter, one-signature-per-office) are clear and grounded.

fields.reliedValue: BOTH a circulator certification AND notarization are required on EVERY page. Each page carries a CIRCULATOR'S AFFIDAVIT (circulator swears each signer signed in his/her presence and is a registered voter), which must be 'Subscribed and sworn to before me' a Notary Public (notary signature, seal, and commission-expiration appear on the form) — RSMo 115.325.
fields.note: Grounded verbatim in the 115.325 statutory form: the circulator's-presence/registered-voter recital, 'Subscribed and sworn to before me this ___ day of ___, A.D.___', 'Signature of Notary', and 'Notary Public (Seal)'. Confirms notarized circulator affidavit per page. Substantial-compliance saving clause ('disregarding clerical and merely technical errors') exists but does not eliminate the notarization requirement.

fields.reliedValue: paper (wet-ink). Pages of uniform size, signatures from only one county per page, numbered in sequence per county, each page bearing a notarized circulator's affidavit (RSMo 115.325.1 + the statutory form). No Missouri online/digital signature system covers an independent/new-party PRESIDENTIAL nominating petition; collect and submit on paper to the Secretary of State.
fields.note: RSMo 115.325.1 grounded verbatim (uniform pages, one county per page, numbered in sequence). 115.321 'file a petition with the secretary of state' grounded verbatim. The per-page notarized wet-ink affidavit makes paper the operative and strictest-safe capture mode. The 'no digital system exists' point is a negative assertion, but paper is unambiguously sufficient and required by the form, so reliance carries no ballot-access risk.

fields.reliedValue: None. There is no filing-fee alternative to (or addition to) the signature petition for an independent or new-party presidential candidate; the 10,000-signature petition (RSMo 115.321 / 115.329) is the sole qualification mechanism on this path. Chapter 115 filing fees apply to party-primary declarations of candidacy, not to independent/new-party petitions. STRICTEST operating assumption: do not rely on any fee shortcut — you must complete the petition.
fields.note: Cited quote (115.321.5, 'placed on the official ballot as an independent candidate') grounds verbatim but speaks to ballot placement rather than fees; the 'None' conclusion is a negative claim derived from the absence of any fee-in-lieu provision in 115.321/115.329. 'None' is itself the conservative posture (assuming no fee shortcut never risks ballot access), so AUTO-LIVE is appropriate. If a campaign wanted to rely on a fee path it does NOT exist — so the value cannot mislead toward under-collecting signatures.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found (Ch. 115); circulator must be a Missouri resident
compensationModel.note: Missouri's candidate/independent-petition chapter (RSMo Ch. 115, incl. 115.321 for independent presidential petitions, 115.325 form/affidavit, and 115.631 election offenses) does NOT address compensating circulators at all — per-signature pay is unaddressed, neither permitted nor prohibited by any candidate-petition primary source found. The candidate circulator's affidavit in 115.325 requires the circulator to swear they are "a resident of the state of Missouri," so circulator residency IS required (a national non-resident paid pool could not lawfully circulate). Note: the per-signature rules people cite live in Ch. 116 (initiative/referendum), which does NOT govern candidate petitions and was not carried over; and even Ch. 116 (116.030 affidavit) expressly contemplates PAID circulators rather than banning per-signature pay.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an INDEPENDENT presidential candidate's nominating petition under RSMo Chapter 115 (115.321/115.325), does Missouri law permit a campaign to pay petition circulators on a per-signature basis, and must every circulator be a Missouri resident (or registered Missouri voter) as the 115.325 affidavit's "a resident of the state of Missouri" language implies?

citations.quote: 115.321.  Independent candidate, how nominated. — 1.  Any person desiring to be an independent candidate for any office to be filled by voters throughout the state ... shall file a petition with the secretary of state. ... If independent candidates for presidential elector are to be nominated, a number of independent candidates for presidential elector equal to the number of electors to which the state is entitled shall be nominated by one petition ... 3.  If an independent candidate is to be nominated for a statewide office, the petition shall be signed by at least ten thousand registered voters of the state.

CIRCULATOR'S AFFIDAVIT ... I, ______, a resident of the state of Missouri, being first duly sworn, say ... signed this page of the foregoing petition, and each of them signed his or her name thereto in my presence; I believe that each ... is a registered voter of the state of Missouri and ______ County.

115.631.  Class one election offenses. — The following offenses, and any others specifically so described by law, shall be class one election offenses and are deemed felonies connected with the exercise of the right of suffrage. [Enumerated offenses (1)-(26) concern false affidavits, illegal voting/registration, and ballot tampering; none addresses paying petition circulators per signature.]

116.080.  Qualifications of circulator — affidavit, notarization, penalty. — 1.  Each petition circulator shall be at least eighteen years of age and registered with the secretary of state. ... A petition circulator shall be deemed registered at the time such circulator delivers a signed circulator's affidavit pursuant to section 116.030, with respect to a referendum petition, or section 116.040, with respect to an initiative petition, to the office of the secretary of state.
I am at least 18 years of age. I do ______ do not ______ (check one) expect to be paid for circulating this petition. If paid, list the payer ______

voterFile.cost: Statutory fee set under Chapter 610 (Sunshine Law cost-recovery), determined by the Secretary of State / local election authority — not published as a fixed online price (varies — request quote). Ballotpedia reports the statewide file cost as "$35" (secondary source, unverified against an official price list).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Not the general public. The statewide file is restricted to a candidate, a duly authorized representative of a campaign committee, or a political party committee (terms defined in RSMo 130.011). RSMo 115.157.1 bars furnishing any registration info "to any member of the public" except as the section provides; in even-numbered years (2026 qualifies) each election authority must, upon request, supply its jurisdiction's voter registration list "to all candidates and party committees."
voterFile.useRestrictions: Political-use-only. The master voter registration list "shall not be used for commercial purposes" (RSMo 115.158.6) — defined to include resale or obtaining names/addresses for solicitation — and "nothing in this chapter shall require such voter information to be released to the public over the internet" (115.157.3). Violation of the statewide-system use restriction is a class B misdemeanor (115.158.6).
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request to the Secretary of State (statewide, electronic or printed media, for a fee under RSMo 115.157.4) or to the relevant local election authority/county clerk for its jurisdiction's list (RSMo 115.157.5). Not an instant online download — Missouri voter registration is decentralized across 116 local election jurisdictions, and the file is provided per-request for a fee; no public self-service portal is published. Contact the SOS Elections Division (573-751-2301).
voterFile.note: Missouri does not sell its voter file to the general public; the statewide registration list (Missouri Voter Registration System, RSMo 115.158) is furnished only to candidates, campaign committees, and party committees for a Chapter-610 cost-recovery fee, with political-use-only restrictions. The standard record includes voter name, address, year of birth, party, township/ward/precinct and a unique voter ID — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters. No fixed price is published on the SOS site; Ballotpedia's "$35" is a secondary, unverified figure, so treat cost as request-quote.

citations.quote: No election authority or secretary of state shall furnish to any member of the public electronic media or printout showing any registration information, except as provided in this section. ... the election authority or secretary of state shall make available electronic media or printouts showing only unique voter identification numbers, voters' names, year of birth, addresses, townships or wards, and precincts.

upon a request by a candidate, a duly authorized representative of a campaign committee, or a political party committee, the secretary of state shall furnish, for a fee determined by the secretary of state and in compliance with section 610.026, media in an electronic format or, if so requested, in a printed format

In even-numbered years, each election authority shall, upon request, supply the voter registration list for its jurisdiction to all candidates and party committees for a charge established pursuant to chapter 610.

Any information contained in any state or local voter registration system, limited to the master voter registration list or any other list generated from the information, subject to chapter 610, shall not be used for commercial purposes; provided, however, that the information may be used for elections, for candidates, or for ballot measures, furnished at a reasonable fee. Violation of this section shall be a class B misdemeanor.
Missouri | Open | $35 | Name, DOB, voter identification number, address, township or ward, precinct, voting history

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement in the statute. RSMo 115.325 prescribes the circulator's affidavit ("Circulator's Affidavit" / "Signature of Affiant (Person obtaining signatures)") and imposes only one affirmative qualification on the circulator: that the affiant be "a resident of the state of Missouri." It sets no minimum age. The only age-bearing concept in the petition is on the SIGNER side (sign
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a "citizen and registered voter of the state of Missouri" (registered voter + Missouri resident; one county per petition page). CIRCULATOR (the affiant / "person obtaining signatures") need only be "a resident of the state of Missouri" — no registered-voter, citizenship, or age requirement is imposed on the circulator by statute; the registered-voter/citizen language binds signers o
circulator.note: Controlling primary source is RSMo 115.325 (Form of petition), which prescribes the statutory text of both the new-party and independent-candidate petitions and the "Circulator's Affidavit." Independent presidential candidates qualify via the independent-candidate petition (RSMo 115.321(2), 10,000 registered-voter signatures statewide for statewide office; presidential electors nominated by one petition). The petition form (115.325.3) places ALL strict requirements on the SIGNER: "We, the unders

citations.quote: CIRCULATOR'S AFFIDAVIT ... I, ______, a resident of the state of Missouri, being first duly sworn, say (print or type names of signers) ... signed this page of the foregoing petition, and each of them signed his or her name thereto in my presence ... Signature of Affiant (Person obtaining signatures)
I believe that each has stated his or her name, registered voting address and city, town or village correctly, and that each signer is a registered voter of the state of Missouri and ______ County.

We, the undersigned, citizens and registered voters of the state of Missouri, ______ County and ______ (district if appropriate), nominate ______ (name of independent candidate) ... and each for himself or herself says: I have personally signed this petition; I am a registered voter of the state of Missouri, ______ County and ______ (district if appropriate)

I, ______, a resident of the state of Missouri, being first duly sworn, say ... Subscribed and sworn to before me this ______ day of ______, A.D.______ ... Signature of Notary ... Notary Public (Seal)

If independent candidates for presidential elector are to be nominated, a number of independent candidates for presidential elector equal to the number of electors to which the state is entitled shall be nominated by one petition ... 3. If an independent candidate is to be nominated for a statewide office, the petition shall be signed by at least ten thousand registered voters of the state.

signaturesHeadline: 5,000 signatures (5% of last governor's vote OR 5,000, whichever is less; 5,000 cap controls — unchanged by 2025 HB 207, which touched only non-presidential)
