deadlineHeadline: Petition filed with SOS 76 days before general (2024 = Aug 21); signatures to county admins ≥1 week earlier (2024 = Aug 14)
riskNote: All 7 citations grounded verbatim (NASS PDF verified via download). Four fields AUTO-LIVE; three on CONSERVATIVE-HOLD for legal-characterization questions (distribution-path separation, circulator residency/eligibility, and whether 13-10-202 fees reach a presidential petitioner) — none should be relied on as "no requirement" without SOS confirmation, to avoid ballot-access risk.
sosInquiries: Distribution: For an INDEPENDENT presidential nominating petition under MCA 13-10-504, is there any requirement that signatures be distributed across legislative or other districts, or is the only requirement the statewide total (5% of last governor's vote or 5,000 electors, whichever is less)? Please confirm the 1/3-of-legislative-districts distribution rule applies ONLY to minor-party qualification under 13-10-601 and not to the 13-10-504 candidate petition.

Circulator eligibility: For a 13-10-504 presidential petition, must signature gatherers (circulators) be Montana residents and/or registered Montana electors, or may out-of-state and non-elector circulators gather signatures? Are there any age or registration requirements for circulators beyond the prohibition on per-signature payment in 13-27-102?

Filing fee: Does an independent or non-qualified-party candidate for president/vice president filing a petition under MCA 13-10-504 owe any filing fee (e.g., under 13-10-202), or is the petition the sole condition of access with no fee due and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option?

fields.reliedValue: Signatures of electors equal to 5% or more of the total votes cast for the successful candidate for governor at the last general election, OR 5,000 electors, whichever is less. The 5,000 cap controls in practice (5% of the 2020 gubernatorial winner Gianforte's ~328,000 votes is ~16,400, far above 5,000), so the operative requirement for an independent presidential petition is 5,000 signatures. Recomputes each cycle off the most recent governor's race.
fields.note: All three citations grounded verbatim: MCA 13-10-504(3) (leg.mt.gov), NASS July-2024 PDF (verified via download — quote present p. Montana section), and ballot-access.org ('Presidential independents continue to need 5,000 signatures'). Adversarial: confirmed 2025 HB 207 lowered only NON-presidential petitions (5%->4%); the presidential path under 13-10-504 is unchanged. 5,000 cap independently re-derived from 2020 Gianforte vote (~328k). No interpretation required — statute is explicit.

fields.reliedValue: The nominating petition must be filed with the Secretary of State no later than 76 days before the general election (statutory rule, MCA 13-10-504(1)). Petition signatures must first be submitted to the county election administrator for verification at least 1 week before the SOS filing deadline (13-10-504(2)). For the 2024 cycle (Nov 5, 2024) this computed to an Aug 21, 2024 SOS deadline and an Aug 14, 2024 county-submission deadline. The calendar dates recompute each cycle off the 76-day / 1-week rule.
fields.note: 76-day SOS rule and the '1 week before' county-submission rule both grounded verbatim in MCA 13-10-504(1)-(2). Independently verified the arithmetic: Nov 5 2024 - 76 days = Wed Aug 21 2024; minus 1 week = Wed Aug 14 2024 — both match the draft. Adversarial note: a Ballotpedia 2024 'passed-deadlines' list shows '(August 19)' linked to a 2020 page (appears stale and is NOT relied on); the statutory 76-day rule is authoritative. Both the SOS filing deadline AND the earlier county-verification deadline are binding — captured.

fields.reliedValue: For the independent / non-qualified-party PRESIDENTIAL petition (MCA 13-10-504), the grounded statutory text imposes only a statewide signature total (5% / 5,000 cap) with no express per-district distribution requirement. STRICTEST reading pending SOS confirmation: do not assume zero distribution requirement — the 1/3-of-legislative-districts distribution rule that appears in the minor-PARTY qualification path (13-10-601, per the NASS summary) must be confirmed by the SOS as NOT applying to a 13-10-504 candidate petition before treating distribution as 'none'.
fields.note: Citations grounded verbatim (MCA 13-10-504(3) and NASS PDF). Adversarial: the NASS text confirms the 1/3-of-legislative-districts distribution requirement attaches to the PARTY-qualification path, not the individual candidate petition — strongly supporting 'none' for the independent path. But correctness hinges on a legal characterization (which statutory path binds an independent presidential candidate). Per gate rule, grounded + interpretation -> CONSERVATIVE-HOLD; SOS inquiry filed. Getting this wrong (missing a distribution requirement) would risk ballot access, so over-comply.

fields.reliedValue: Grounded facts: (a) a petition may be signed ONLY by a qualified Montana elector, and (b) a circulator may not be paid based on the number of signatures gathered (MCA 13-27-102). STRICTEST reading on circulator residency/eligibility: do NOT assume out-of-state or non-elector circulators are permitted, and do not rely on the 'prior residency rule held unconstitutional' assertion. Ballotpedia only states the statutes 'do not specify circulator requirements' — silence is not affirmative permission. Confirm circulator residency/registration/age rules with the SOS before deploying non-resident or non-elector circulators.
fields.note: Both citations grounded verbatim: Ballotpedia ('The statutes do not specify circulator requirements (e.g., residency status).') and 13-27-102 (signer must be a qualified MT elector; no per-signature pay). Adversarial: the draft's claims that out-of-state circulators ARE permitted and that a 'prior residency rule was held unconstitutional' are NOT supported by the grounded quotes — they are added legal conclusions from statutory silence. Strictest interpretation treats circulator eligibility as unsettled. CONSERVATIVE-HOLD; SOS inquiry filed. The pay-per-signature ban and elector-signer rule, by themselves, are safe to rely on.

fields.reliedValue: Notarized circulator affidavit required. An affidavit of circulation (in substantially the statutory form) must be attached to each petition sheet/section, in which the gatherer swears the signatures are genuine and signers knew the petition's contents; it must be subscribed and sworn before a person authorized to administer oaths (notarized) and must note the date of the first signature and the circulator's address. MCA 13-10-504(2) expressly requires the 13-27-302 affidavits of circulation.
fields.note: Both citations grounded verbatim: MCA 13-27-302 affidavit form ('Subscribed and sworn to before me this ___ day of _________, 20__' + '(Person authorized to take oaths)') and Ballotpedia ('The affidavits must also be notarized.'). The notarization requirement is explicit on the face of the statutory form — no interpretation needed. AUTO-LIVE.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Signatures are collected on paper petition sheets, each with a notarized circulator affidavit, then submitted to county election administrators for verification before the petition is filed with the Secretary of State. No remote-online or digitized capture exists for an independent presidential nominating petition in Montana.
fields.note: Both citations grounded verbatim: MCA 13-10-504(2) (county-administrator verification/certification) and 13-27-302 ('An affidavit ... must be attached to each sheet or section submitted to the county official'). The physical-sheet + county-submission model is on the face of the statute; no online capture authorized. No interpretation required. AUTO-LIVE.

fields.reliedValue: No fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option is offered for the presidential petition; the petition is the access mechanism. STRICTEST reading on whether ANY filing fee is owed: do not assume a $0 fee. MCA 13-10-504 itself contains no fee, but MCA 13-10-202 makes independent and non-qualified-party candidates liable for the same filing fees as party candidates; whether that general fee provision reaches a presidential petitioner under 13-10-504 is a legal-characterization question. Confirm with the SOS that no filing fee is due before treating the petition path as fee-free.
fields.note: Both citations grounded verbatim (MCA 13-10-504(1) and NASS PDF). Adversarial: the draft itself flags MCA 13-10-202, which imposes fees on independent / non-qualified-party candidates equal to party-candidate fees. The draft's conclusion that this does not reach the 13-10-504 presidential petitioner is plausible but is an unsettled legal characterization that retrieval cannot definitively resolve, and wrongly assuming no fee could risk a defective filing. Per gate rule, grounded + interpretation -> CONSERVATIVE-HOLD; SOS inquiry filed. (The narrower claim 'no fee-IN-LIEU-OF-signatures alternative is offered' is safe — there is no such substitution path.)

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found; initiative pay-per-signature ban is confined to Title 13 ch. 27 (ballot issues) and expressly excludes candidates
compensationModel.note: For an independent PRESIDENTIAL candidate petition, Montana's election code does NOT address circulator compensation at all — neither permitting nor prohibiting per-signature pay. Montana's only pay-per-signature ban (MCA 13-27-102(2)) sits in Title 13, ch. 27 ("Statewide Ballot Issues") and, together with the paid-signature-gatherer scheme in 13-27-112, is by its own definitions limited to initiatives/referenda/constitutional conventions and expressly excludes a "candidate"; the presidential-candidate statute (13-10-504(2)) borrows only Chapter 27's affidavit and signature-verification procedures (13-27-302 through 13-27-306), not the compensation ban. No Montana candidate-petition statute imposes a circulator residency requirement (only signers must be Montana electors of the district), so an out-of-state paid pool is legally permissible; per-signature pay is treated as unaddressed and held conservatively.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an INDEPENDENT PRESIDENTIAL candidate's nominating petition under MCA 13-10-504 (not a ballot issue under Title 13, ch. 27), may a campaign pay circulators on a per-signature basis, or does any provision (e.g., the 13-27-102(2) ban) apply to candidate-petition circulators? Are candidate-petition circulators subject to any residency or registration requirement?

citations.quote: TITLE 13. ELECTIONS / CHAPTER 27. STATEWIDE BALLOT ISSUES / Part 1. General Provisions ... 13-27-102. Who may petition and gather signatures. (1) A petition may be signed only by a qualified elector of the state of Montana. (2) A person gathering signatures for a petition may not be paid anything of value based upon the number of signatures gathered.

(5) As used in subsections (2) and (3) ... (c)(i) "Person" has the meaning provided in 13-1-101 and includes a political committee. (ii) The term does not include a candidate. (d)(i) "Person who employs a paid signature gatherer" means a political party, political committee, or other person seeking to place a ballot issue before the electors. ... (e) "Signature gatherer" means an individual who collects signatures on a petition for the purpose of an initiative, a referendum, or the calling of a constitutional convention.

13-10-504. Independent or minor party candidates for president or vice president. (1) An individual who desires to run for president or vice president as an independent candidate ... shall file a petition for nomination with the secretary of state 76 days prior to the date of the general election. (2) The petition and the affidavits of circulation required by 13-27-302 must first be submitted ... to the election administrator in the county where the signer resides for verification and certification by the procedures provided in 13-27-303 through 13-27-306. (3) The petition must have the signatures of electors equal to 5% or more of the total votes cast for the successful candidate for governor at the last general election or 5,000 electors, whichever is less.

13-10-501. ... (1) Except as provided in 13-10-504, nominations for public office by an independent candidate or a political party that does not meet the requirements of 13-10-601 may be made by filing a declaration of intent and paying the filing fee in accordance with 13-10-201 and by circulating a petition for nomination. ... (5) Each sheet of a petition must contain signatures of electors residing in only one county.

13-10-502. Signature requirements for petition. (1) The petition for nomination must be signed by active electors as defined in 13-1-101 residing within the state and district or political subdivision in which the officer or officers are to be elected.

Tuesday evening was the deadline for independent and minor party candidates to submit signed petitions to county election administrators across Montana ... Montana law outlines the signature requirements that each candidate must receive (13-10-502, MCA).

voterFile.cost: $1,000.00 flat for a single copy of the complete statewide voter file (or any single extract such as the Absentee Ballot Report or Petition Signers Report); $5,000.00 for a one-year (365-day) subscription giving unlimited downloads of all extracts/reports. Paid by Visa/MasterCard (credit or debit) online.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Any individual, for noncommercial use. By statute (13-2-122 MCA) the Secretary of State "shall furnish to any individual, for noncommercial use" extracts and reports from the statewide voter registration system — no candidate/committee/party eligibility gate. A campaign or committee qualifies as long as the use is noncommercial.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Noncommercial use only. Statute 13-2-122(1) limits furnishing to "noncommercial use"; the SOS voter file service states records "are for non-commercial use only." The SOS notes that if a requester needs a legal definition of noncommercial use, they should consult an attorney. Per 13-2-122(2), residential addresses of electors whose address is protected under 13-2-115(6)/(7) are withheld (name only listed). No solicitation/penalty language quoted on the SOS page.
voterFile.requestProcess: Instant online purchase through the SOS centralized voter file service at app.mt.gov/voterfile. The requester customizes a file by criteria (county, district, etc.), pays by credit/debit card, and is emailed a download link once the file is generated. A one-time purchase requires no account beyond payment; an annual subscription requires signing up for an Okta (okta.mt.gov) account. No notarized affidavit or sworn statement of permitted use is described.
voterFile.note: Montana sells the statewide voter file as a flat-fee online download ($1,000 single / $5,000 annual subscription) to any individual for noncommercial use under 13-2-122 MCA. Notably for signature verification, MT also offers a dedicated "Petition Signers Report" (a county-by-county record of the signers of a specific petition) that is included with the annual subscription, alongside the registered-voter file containing name, residential address, and registration status.

citations.quote: Upon request, the Secretary of State shall furnish to any individual, for noncommercial use, available extracts and reports from the statewide voter registration system, 13-2-122, MCA. If you need legal advice on the definition of non-commercial use, please consult with an attorney.

The fee for a single copy of the complete statewide voter file or for an extract of the voter file, such as the Absentee Ballot Report, is $1000.00. The fee for a one-year (365-day) subscription to the voter file service is $5000.00. Visa and MasterCard are accepted for online payment.

an annual subscription entitles the customer to unlimited downloads of any available extracts of the voter file, the absentee ballot report, and the petition signers report. To purchase a subscription, customers must sign up for an Okta account after clicking the “Purchase the Voter File” button above.

The Petition Signers Report is a county-by-county record of the signers of a specific petition. By default, it is organized by county and in the order in which the signatures were processed by county election officials.

upon request, the secretary of state shall furnish to any individual, for noncommercial use, available extracts and reports from the statewide voter registration system. ... Upon delivery, the secretary of state or the local election administrator may collect a charge not to exceed the actual cost of the register, list, mailing labels, or available extracts and reports.

For an elector whose address information is protected from general distribution under 13-2-115(6) or (7), the secretary of state or a local election administrator may not include the elector's residential address on any register, list, mailing labels, or available extracts and reports but may list only the elector's name.

The Secretary of State provides extracts of public information from the statewide voter registration system. ... Records from this database are available for purchase and are for non-commercial use only.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — the rule binds signers only. Montana's independent presidential petition statute (MCA 13-10-504(2)) borrows only the circulator affidavit form of MCA 13-27-302, which describes the circulator merely as "the signature gatherer / person who is the signature gatherer" and imposes no age, residency, citizenship, or registration requirement. The strict "active/registered
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNERS must be active/qualified Montana electors who are registered and reside in the state and the district/political subdivision (MCA 13-10-502(1)); the CIRCULATOR (signature gatherer) faces no statutory age, residency, citizenship, or voter-registration requirement — only that the circulator complete and notarize the affidavit of circulation (MCA 13-27-302) and, if applicable, not be paid per-
circulator.note: For an independent presidential nominating petition in Montana, MCA 13-10-504(2) requires the petition plus "the affidavits of circulation required by 13-27-302." That affidavit statute (13-27-302) is the only circulator-binding provision, and it identifies the circulator solely as "the signature gatherer," imposing NO age, residency, citizenship, or voter-registration requirement. The strict eligibility requirements bind the SIGNER, who must be an active/registered Montana elector residing in t

citations.field: minorCanCirculate / circulatorMustBeStateResident / circulatorMustBeUSCitizen / circulatorMustBeRegisteredVoter — affidavit is the only circulator-binding text and imposes no eligibility class
citations.quote: I, (name of person who is the signature gatherer), swear that I gathered the signatures on the petition to which this affidavit is attached on the stated dates, that I believe the signatures on the petition are genuine, are the signatures of the persons whose names they purport to be, and are the signatures of Montana electors who are registered at the address or have the telephone number following the person's signature, and that the signers knew the contents of the petition before signing the petition.

citations.field: applicability of 13-27-302 affidavit to independent presidential petition
citations.quote: The petition and the affidavits of circulation required by 13-27-302 must first be submitted, at least 1 week before the deadline for filing, to the election administrator in the county where the signer resides for verification and certification by the procedures provided in 13-27-303 through 13-27-306.

citations.field: signerVsCirculator — the strict elector/residency requirement binds the SIGNER
citations.quote: The petition for nomination must be signed by active electors as defined in 13-1-101 residing within the state and district or political subdivision in which the officer or officers are to be elected.

citations.field: circulatorMustBeRegisteredVoter / general ballot-issue circulator rule imposes no eligibility class on gatherers (only no per-signature pay)
citations.quote: (1) A petition may be signed only by a qualified elector of the state of Montana. (2) A person gathering signatures for a petition may not be paid anything of value based upon the number of signatures gathered.

citations.field: gate AUTO-LIVE — official-grade confirmation that no circulator requirements exist
citations.quote: The statutes do not specify circulator requirements (e.g., residency status).

signaturesHeadline: AL: 5,000 valid signatures (fixed statutory, independent presidential)
deadlineHeadline: AL: 74 days before general election (Aug 23, 2024) — NOT Sept 6 (draft is WRONG)
riskNote: Signature count is solid (5,000 confirmed in §17-14-31(a)); the draft DEADLINE is materially wrong — it cites Sept 6 (a repealed pre-2012 date / a party convention-certificate date Ballotpedia conflated), but the enacted law (SB324, eff. 2024) sets the independent presidential petition deadline at 74 days before the election = Aug 23, 2024. Treat the deadline as Aug 23, 2024 and verify the exact 2028 date with the SOS before relying.
sosInquiries: For an INDEPENDENT PRESIDENTIAL candidate filing a §17-14-31 petition of 5,000 qualified voters, please confirm the exact filing deadline for the next general election: is it 74 days before Election Day (as amended by SB324, 2024), and what is that calendar date?

Please confirm there are NO circulator qualification requirements (age, residency, voter-registration) for an independent presidential petition under Alabama law, and that no circulator affidavit, witness, or notarization is required on petition pages beyond Rule 820-2-4-.05's per-signer information.

fields.reliedValue: 5,000 valid signatures of qualified Alabama voters — a FIXED statutory number for an independent presidential candidate under Ala. Code §17-14-31(a). This is distinct from the 3%-of-last-gubernatorial-vote rule (42,458 statewide for 2024) that governs OTHER independent candidates under §17-9-3.
fields.note: Both Ballotpedia quotes verified verbatim (page line 232). Independently confirmed against the primary source: enacted SB324 §17-14-31(a) — presidential candidates may be nominated 'by written petition signed by at least 5,000 qualified voters of this state.' The draft correctly separates this from the §17-9-3 3%/42,458 general-independent rule (SOS PDF quote also verified verbatim, lines 11-18).

fields.reliedValue: 74 days before the general election. For 2024 this was August 23, 2024 (election day Nov 5, 2024). DO NOT rely on September 6 — that is the WRONG date for an independent presidential petition.
fields.note: Citation quotes verified verbatim, BUT the asserted VALUE (Sept 6) is wrong. The Sept 6 Ballotpedia quote describes the party national-convention certificate-of-nomination deadline (Ballotpedia line 226) and Ballotpedia conflated it onto the independent petition (line 232); Ballot Access News notes Sept 6 was Alabama's old deadline pre-2012. Enacted SB324 (LegiScan: Passed/Enacted 2024-05-03), amending §17-14-31(b), sets the deadline for presidential certificates AND petitions at '74 days before the date of the election' (strikethrough shows change from the prior '82nd day' = Aug 15). 74 days before Nov 5, 2024 = Aug 23, 2024 — independently corroborated by Ballot Access News, WVUA23, and 1819News reporting RFK Jr.'s Aug 23 deadline. Strictest-defensible value set to the earliest applicable deadline (Aug 23, 2024) to never risk a missed filing; exact 2028 date must be re-derived (74 days before that Election Day) and confirmed with the SOS.

fields.reliedValue: None. The 5,000-signature independent presidential petition is a single statewide total with no per-congressional-district or per-county distribution requirement.
fields.note: Ballotpedia quote verified verbatim (line 232). Confirmed against §17-14-31(a): 'petition signed by at least 5,000 qualified voters of this state' — a flat statewide count with no geographic allocation. No distribution language anywhere in the presidential petition statute.

fields.reliedValue: UNKNOWN / no circulator-specific qualifications are specified in Alabama law. Signatures must be gathered in person; signers must be registered Alabama voters. (Conservative posture: do not assume circulators are unrestricted — verify with SOS.)
fields.note: Ballotpedia quote ('All petition signatures must be gathered in person, but the code does not specify requirements for petition circulators') verified verbatim (line 398). SOS PDF 'signatures of registered Alabama voters' verified verbatim (line 11). Reviewed full Rule 820-2-4-.05 (Cornell) and enacted SB324 — neither imposes circulator age/residency requirements. The draft's UNKNOWN/no-specified-requirement framing is itself the strict, safe statement, so it is left AUTO-LIVE; an SOS inquiry is queued to close the gap.

fields.reliedValue: None. Petition pages require only a per-page identifying statement, pagination, and per-signer information (name, residential address, date of birth, signature) under Rule 820-2-4-.05. No circulator affidavit, witness, or notarization is required.
fields.note: SOS PDF quote (Rule 820-2-4-.05(3) per-signer info) verified verbatim (lines 63-66). Cornell LII text of Rule 820-2-4-.05 verified verbatim and read in full (subsections 1-3) — contains no notary/affidavit/witness requirement. Enacted SB324 likewise imposes none for the presidential petition.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Signatures must be gathered in person on paper petitions; a photocopiable sample form is provided by the SOS. No digital/remote-online signature system exists for an Alabama independent presidential petition.
fields.note: Ballotpedia 'gathered in person' quote verified verbatim (line 398); SOS PDF 'sample petition form, which may be photo copied' verified verbatim (lines 70-72). No source indicates any electronic submission path; in-person paper is confirmed.

fields.reliedValue: None. There is no filing/qualifying fee for an independent candidate and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures alternative; the 5,000-signature petition is the sole ballot-access mechanism.
fields.note: SOS PDF 'There is no qualifying fee' verified verbatim (line 4). §17-14-31(a) provides the petition as the only independent path (no fee alternative mentioned). Note: the draft's quote attributes 'There is no qualifying fee' to a sentence about choosing not to run as a party candidate — the exact phrase appears verbatim at line 4 of the SOS PDF, so grounding holds.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: Alabama's candidate-petition law (Ala. Code 17-9-3) and the Secretary of State's independent-candidacy administrative rules (Ala. Admin. Code ch. 820-2-4) are entirely signer-centric: they govern petition format, the information requested of each signer, and random-sample signature verification, but say NOTHING about circulators — neither their compensation (per-signature or otherwise), nor whether circulators may be paid at all, nor any circulator residency/registration requirement. Compensation of circulators on a candidate petition is therefore UNADDRESSED in Alabama's election code and SOS rules; Alabama has no statewide initiative process, so no initiative pay-per-signature rule carries over.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an INDEPENDENT presidential candidate's nominating petition under Ala. Code 17-9-3, may a campaign pay signature circulators on a per-signature basis; may circulators be paid at all (hourly/salary); and must a circulator be an Alabama resident or registered Alabama voter?

citations.quote: An election petition shall request the following information for each person signing said petition: name, residential address, date of birth, and signature. A signature shall not be deemed invalid for lacking any portion of the requested information if the disclosed information is sufficient for determining the validity of a signature.
All petition signatures must be gathered in person, but the code does not specify requirements for petition circulators.

§ 820-2-4-.05 - Petition Requirements § 820-2-4-.06 - Random Sample Of Signatures § 820-2-4-.07 - Random Sampling Formula For Verification Of Elections Petitions § 820-2-4-.08 - Certification Of Election Petition
An independent presidential candidate must petition for placement on the general election ballot. At least 5,000 qualified state voters must sign this petition.

voterFile.cost: $1,000 cap statewide (HB67, eff. 2026-06-01); legacy rate $0.01/name, $1.00 min (~$38,000 statewide pre-cap)
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — "Anyone can request the voter list through a form on the secretary of state's website." Statute (§17-4-38) directs the SoS to "provide access to lists and voter history information ... to anyone making application." No candidate/committee or political-use eligibility gate. Typical buyers are political parties, candidates, consultants, and voter-integrity groups. (Legislators get their district free; courts get a statewide copy for jury lists; other states can get a reciprocal copy once a year.)
voterFile.useRestrictions: HB67 (eff. June 1, 2026) prohibits commercial use of the voter list and bars the SoS from releasing voters' phone numbers, driver license numbers, voter ID numbers, email addresses, and month/day of birth (SSNs and emails were already withheld). Purchase certification states all sales are final and the file expires 7 days after purchase. (Specific penalty language not captured from the statute text; PDF was non-extractable.)
voterFile.requestProcess: Two routes: (1) Instant online purchase + download via the SoS portal at alabamainteractive.org (operated by Alabama Interactive, LLC) — buyer selects geography (city, county, district, or entire state), pays, and has 7 days to download; (2) a mailed/paper "Voter Registration Information Request" form to the Elections Division for city/municipal-district requests. No notarized affidavit or approval step is described for the standard purchase — the requester self-certifies the purchase terms online.
voterFile.note: Alabama sells the statewide voter file to anyone via an instant online portal; HB67, effective June 1, 2026 (days before this research date), caps the price at $1,000 (down from ~$38,000 at the old $0.01/name rate) and prohibits commercial use and release of phone numbers. The file includes name, address, race, gender, date of birth, and 10-election voting history — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters.

citations.quote: The Legislature passed HB67 by Rep. Jamie Kiel, R-Russellville, which puts a $1,000 cap on the cost of lists. That's a fraction of the current cost for a statewide list, which could run about $38,000 under the old law.

Under the old law, the secretary of state charges 1 cent per name on the list. For those buying the statewide list - 3.8 million registered voters - that's about $38,000. ... The bill takes effect June 1.

Anyone can request the voter list through a form on the secretary of state's website. The form allows the requester to choose the area for which they are requesting the file - a city, county, or district - or the entire state. The records include name, address, race, gender, date of birth and the voter's history of participation going back the last 10 elections.

Code of Alabama (1975), section 17-4-38 provides that the Office of the Secretary of State shall provide access to lists and voter history information contained on the central computer to anyone making application, and there shall be a uniform charge for the production of voter lists.
I acknowledge that I have 7 days from the time of purchasing my list to download and save the information I have purchased, after which the information I have purchased will expire.
The bill also caps the cost of Alabama's voter list at $1,000 and prohibits its use for commercial purposes.
Alabama's current fee schedule of a penny per voter record, with a $1.00 minimum fee, is based on Alabama law that authorizes a uniform charge for production of voter lists and decades of precedent.
HB67 prohibits the release of voters' driver license numbers, voter identification numbers, email addresses, month and day of birth, and phone numbers.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — the rule binds signers only. Ala. Code § 17-9-3(a)(3) requires only that the petition be "signed by electors qualified to vote" / "qualified electors," which restricts the SIGNERS. The statute imposes no circulator affidavit, no witness, and no age requirement on the person who carries/collects the petition. Alabama law (and the SoS guide) does not require a circula
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: must be a "qualified elector" / "elector qualified to vote in the election" — i.e., a registered Alabama voter (Ala. Code § 17-9-3(a)(3); SoS guide says "registered Alabama voters"). CIRCULATOR: no requirements whatsoever — the statute and SoS guide are silent on who may collect signatures; there is no circulator affidavit, registration, age, citizenship, or residency requirement. The only
circulator.note: Alabama's strict eligibility requirements bind ONLY the signers, not the circulator. Ala. Code § 17-9-3(a)(3) is the operative independent-candidacy petition statute: it requires the petition be "signed by electors qualified to vote in the election" and that "the number of qualified electors signing the petition shall equal or exceed three percent of the qualified electors who cast ballots for the office of Governor in the last general election." There is no circulator/witness/affiant role creat

citations.quote: Each candidate who has been requested to be an independent candidate for a specified office by written petition signed by electors qualified to vote in the election to fill the office when the petition has been filed with the judge of probate, in the case of a county office and with the Secretary of State in all other cases, on or before 5:00 P.M. on the date of the first primary election... The number of qualified electors signing the petition shall equal or exceed three percent of the qualified electors who cast ballots for the office of Governor in the last general election for the state, county, district, or other political subdivision in which the candidate seeks to qualify.

citations.field: circulatorMustBeRegisteredVoter / circulatorMustBeUSCitizen / circulatorMustBeStateResident (no circulator requirements specified)
citations.quote: All petition signatures must be gathered in person, but the code does not specify requirements for petition circulators.

citations.field: signerVsCirculator (signer = registered AL voter; circulator unmentioned) + petition form requirements (no circulator affidavit)
citations.quote: A ballot access petition for an independent candidate must contain signatures of registered Alabama voters. The number of valid signatures on the petition must equal or exceed at least 3 percent of the qualified electors who cast ballots for the office of governor in the last general election... Requested information for each person signing which includes name, residential address, date of birth and signature. A signature shall not be deemed invalid for lacking any portion of the requested information if the disclosed information is sufficient for determining the validity of a signature. [Alabama Administrative Code, Rule 820-2-4.05(3)]

signaturesHeadline: AZ indep pres: ~42,303 (2024 only) — RECOMPUTES each cycle, do not hardcode
deadlineHeadline: AZ pres-elector filing: 80-100 days before general (Aug 17, 2024); date recomputes
riskNote: Six of seven fields verified live; signaturesRequired held because the number is cycle-specific (3% of non-affiliated registrants as of Jan 2) and the draft's new-party comparison misreads its own Ballot Access News citation (34,116=Full Party, 41,090=Candidate, neither equals 42,303).
sosInquiries: For an independent (non-party) presidential-elector nomination petition under A.R.S. 16-341(E)-(F), what is the exact certified minimum signature count for the upcoming general-election cycle, computed as 3% of registered voters who are not members of a qualified party as of January 2 of the election year, and where is that official figure published?

Does A.R.S. 16-341(N)'s authorization of electronic petitions 'for statewide and legislative offices' extend to independent presidential-elector petitions, or are such petitions required to be collected on paper because E-Qual under A.R.S. 16-318 is limited to U.S. Senate and U.S. House candidates?

fields.reliedValue: Treat 42,303 as the 2024-cycle figure ONLY. For any live cycle the requirement must be recomputed as 3% of Arizona registered voters who are NOT members of a qualified party, measured as of January 2 of the general-election year (A.R.S. 16-341(E)-(F)), and verified against the Secretary of State's official signature-requirement table for that cycle. Any registered AZ voter may sign regardless of party. Do NOT rely on the draft's new-party comparison.
fields.note: All four quotes verified verbatim. 42,303 for 2024 is double-corroborated by azcentral (Aug 16 + Aug 19, 2024) as the SoS-reported independent statewide presidential figure. HOLD reasons: (1) the value is explicitly cycle-recomputed (3% of non-affiliated registrants as of Jan 2) and must not be hardcoded for a future cycle; (2) the draft's NOTE misreads its own Ballot Access News citation: in that table 34,116 is the 'Full Party' column and *41,090 is the 'Candidate' column for Arizona; neither is 42,303, and the draft's claim that the new-party 'Candidate' figure (34,116) is lower than the independent figure inverts the table headers. The headline number is right; the surrounding interpretation is not reliable.

fields.reliedValue: A nomination petition for the office of presidential elector must be filed with the Arizona Secretary of State not less than 80 nor more than 100 days before the general election (A.R.S. 16-341(G)). For 2024 (general election Nov 5) this was August 17, 2024 (exactly 80 days prior). The 120-150-days-before-primary window in 16-341(C) does NOT apply to presidential-elector filings. NOTE: the specific calendar date recomputes from the general-election date each cycle.
fields.note: Both quotes verified verbatim. The 80-100-day rule is a bright-line statute, correctly excluding presidential-elector petitions from the primary-tied window. Aug 17, 2024 derives correctly (80 days before Nov 5, 2024) and is independently confirmed by Ballot Access News. The rule is durable and non-interpretive; only the calendar date is cycle-dependent.

fields.reliedValue: None. A single statewide threshold (3% of statewide non-affiliated registered voters) applies; there is no geographic/congressional-district/county distribution requirement for an independent presidential-elector petition. The petition is a single group petition naming all elector candidates, and a valid signature counts for each elector candidate (A.R.S. 16-341(G)).
fields.note: Quote verified verbatim in 16-341(G). Nothing in 16-341 imposes a distribution requirement; the threshold in 16-341(E)-(F) is a flat statewide percentage. Confirmed correct and non-interpretive.

fields.reliedValue: A circulator need NOT be an Arizona resident but must otherwise be eligible/qualified to register to vote in Arizona (U.S. citizen, 18+, etc., per A.R.S. 16-101). A non-resident circulator must register as a circulator with the Secretary of State before circulating petitions (A.R.S. 16-341(G), 16-321(D)). The circulator need not be a registered Arizona voter, only eligible to register.
fields.note: Both quotes verified verbatim (16-341(G) and 16-321(D)). The statutory text supports each element, including the 'eligible to register' (not 'registered') standard and the non-resident circulator-registration requirement. Non-interpretive.

fields.reliedValue: No notarization of signature sheets is required; the circulator must certify on each sheet that every signer signed in the circulator's presence on the indicated date and that the circulator believes each signer was a qualified elector residing at the listed address (A.R.S. 16-321(D)). Separately, the candidate's filing must include a consent statement SIGNED by the vice-presidential running mate and each designated presidential elector, and a nomination paper for each elector must be filed (A.R.S. 16-341(J)).
fields.note: Both quotes verified verbatim. AZ requires circulator certification, not notarization, of the signature sheets. The azcentral notarization failure was MICHIGAN's, not Arizona's, so it does not undercut the no-notary finding. The elector-consent requirement (16-341(J)) is correctly grounded; the West 2024 filing's missing presidential/VP nomination forms and acceptance letter are corroborated by azcentral. Non-interpretive.

fields.reliedValue: Paper. No online signature system covers an independent presidential-elector petition in Arizona. E-Qual under A.R.S. 16-318 is limited to U.S. Senate and U.S. House candidates; 16-341(N) electronic petitions are discretionary and (per the SoS) have not been authorized for presidential-elector petitions. Collect on paper signature sheets (as RFK Jr. and Cornel West did in 2024).
fields.note: All three quotes verified verbatim. 16-318(B) unambiguously limits E-Qual to U.S. Senator/Representative. 16-341(N) is discretionary ('may authorize ... for statewide and legislative offices'); whether presidential-elector qualifies as 'statewide' is an open reading, BUT the operative VALUE is unaffected: no electronic system has been authorized for these petitions, so paper is correct under every reading (corroborated by the physical petition boxes filed in 2024). The residual statutory-scope question is flagged to the SoS but does not change the answer.

fields.reliedValue: None. Arizona provides no filing-fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option for an independent presidential candidate; the signature petition is mandatory, and a candidate who does not file a timely, compliant nomination petition is not eligible to appear on the ballot (A.R.S. 16-341(K)).
fields.note: Quote verified verbatim (it appears in 16-341(K), though the draft's prose loosely cites the fine provision as 16-341(M) — the verbatim quote and its eligibility-bar substance are correct). Arizona's fee-in-lieu mechanisms apply to partisan primary candidates, not to independent nomination petitions; there is no fee alternative for an independent presidential candidate. Non-interpretive.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found; per-signature ban (ARS 19-118.01) limited to statewide initiative/referendum
compensationModel.note: Arizona's candidate-petition statutes (Title 16, Article 2/3, including the independent/presidential path at ARS 16-341 and the circulator rules at ARS 16-315 and 16-321) do NOT address circulator compensation at all — neither permitting nor prohibiting per-signature pay. Arizona's only per-signature ban, ARS 19-118.01, by its own terms applies solely to "a statewide initiative or referendum petition" (Title 19) and does not reach candidate nomination petitions, so candidate-petition per-signature compensation is unaddressed rather than authorized; circulators need not be Arizona residents but must be eligible to register to vote here and (if non-resident) register with the Secretary of State.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nomination petition under ARS 16-341, may a campaign legally pay petition circulators on a per-signature basis, or is per-signature compensation prohibited or restricted for candidate (as opposed to initiative/referendum) petitions under Arizona law?

citations.quote: A. A person shall not pay or receive money or any other thing of value based on the number of signatures collected on a statewide initiative or referendum petition. Signatures that are obtained by a paid circulator who violates this section are void and shall not be counted in determining the legal sufficiency of the petition. B. A violation of this section is a class 1 misdemeanor.

G. A nomination petition for any candidate may be circulated by a person who is not a resident of this state but who is otherwise eligible to register to vote in this state if that person registers as a circulator with the secretary of state before circulating petitions.

2. Circulator is not required to be a resident of this state but otherwise must be qualified to register to vote in this state and, if not a resident of this state, shall register as a circulator with the secretary of state.

D. The person before whom the signatures were written on the signature sheet is not required to be a resident of this state but otherwise shall be qualified to register to vote in this state pursuant to section 16-101 and, if not a resident of this state, shall register as a circulator with the secretary of state.
The Supreme Court held that Ariz. Rev. Stat. 19-118.01 does not facially violate the First Amendment where it only prohibits per-signature compensation to petition circulators.

voterFile.cost: Statutory tiered formula (ARS 16-168(E)): 1–124,999 records = $93.75 + $0.0005/record; 125,000–249,999 = $156.25 + $0.000375/record; 250,000–499,999 = $203.13 + $0.00025/record; 500,000–999,999 = $265.63 + $0.000125/record; 1,000,000+ records = $328.13 + $0.0000625/record. A full statewide file (~4.3M registered voters) falls in the top tier: ~$328.13 + $0.0000625/record (on the order of a few hundred dollars total).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Not the general public for any purpose. Free electronic copies go to county/state party chairmen of qualifying parties. Anyone else (candidates, registered political committees, etc.) may purchase copies only for a statutorily "authorized use" — i.e. political/political-party activity, a political campaign, an election, redistricting, signature verification on petitions/candidate filings, or another use specifically authorized by law. Commercial use is prohibited.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Political-use-only: data "may be used only for purposes relating to a political or political party activity, a political campaign or an election... and may not be used for a commercial purpose." Signature verification on petitions and candidate filings is expressly authorized. Reselling/transferring for an unauthorized purpose or posting it on the internet is prohibited. A violation of the use restriction or resale/distribution restriction "is guilty of a class 6 felony." Sensitive fields (full DOB, SSN, driver license number, voter signature, email) are excluded from general reproduction but may be used for petition signature verification.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request for an authorized use submitted to the county recorder (or the Secretary of State / officer in charge of elections); the office prepares additional copies and furnishes them within 30 days of receipt of the request upon payment of the statutory fee. (Verifying petition signatures is an explicitly permitted use, so a campaign/committee qualifies.)
voterFile.note: Arizona does not offer a free public statewide voter file download; the file is eligibility- and use-gated (political/campaign/election use only, commercial use is a class 6 felony) and sold on a written request at a statutory tiered fee under ARS 16-168. The standard precinct-list/voter file includes full name, residence and mailing address, party, registration date, birth year and voting history — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters — and signature verification on petitions is an explicitly authorized use, so a campaign/committee can obtain it. (Official azsos.gov voter-data request page was behind a browser challenge and could not be fetched; statute ARS 16-168 is quoted as the controlling primary source for price, eligibility, fields and penalties.)

citations.quote: The county recorder, the secretary of state and other officers in charge of elections, on a request for an authorized use and within thirty days from receipt of the request, shall prepare additional copies of an official precinct list and furnish them to any person requesting them on payment of a fee equal to the following amounts for the following number of voter registration records provided: 1. For one to one hundred twenty-four thousand nine hundred ninety-nine records, $93.75 plus $0.0005 per record.
5. For one million or more records, $328.13 plus $0.0000625 per record.

Precinct registers and other lists and information derived from registration forms may be used only for purposes relating to a political or political party activity, a political campaign or an election, for revising election district boundaries or for any other purpose specifically authorized by law and may not be used for a commercial purpose as defined in section 39-121.03. The sale of registers, lists and information derived from registration forms to a candidate or a registered political committee for a use specifically authorized by this subsection does not constitute use for a commercial purpose.

for signature verification on petitions and candidate filings, for election purposes and for news gathering purposes ... A person who violates this subsection or subsection E of this section is guilty of a class 6 felony.

The electronic media copies of the precinct lists that are delivered to the party chairmen shall include for each elector the following information: 1. Name in full and appropriate title. 2. Party preference. 3. Date of registration. 4. Residence address. 5. Mailing address, if different from residence address. 6. Zip code. 7. Telephone number if given. 8. Birth year.

circulator.ageRule: No standalone "must be 18" clause is written for circulators, but the circulator must be "eligible to register to vote in this state" (A.R.S. 16-341(G), (H)) / "qualified to register to vote in this state pursuant to section 16-101" (A.R.S. 16-321(D)). Eligibility to register under 16-101(A)(2) requires the person "[w]ill be eighteen years of age or more on or before the date of the regular genera
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: must be a registered voter / qualified elector in the relevant electoral district at time of signing (16-321(B), 16-341(F)) and use actual residence address (16-321(E)). CIRCULATOR: need NOT be a resident of Arizona and need NOT actually be registered, but must be "qualified/eligible to register to vote in this state pursuant to section 16-101" — i.e., a U.S. citizen who is 18+ (by the nex
circulator.note: Arizona statute directly and separately addresses circulators of independent nominating petitions (which include presidential-elector petitions under 16-341). The circulator standard is "qualified/eligible to register to vote in this state pursuant to section 16-101" — NOT "registered voter." So: (1) Registration is NOT required of the circulator (only eligibility to register). (2) Residency is expressly waived — a non-resident may circulate, but must register as a circulator with the SoS first

citations.quote: G. A nomination petition for any candidate may be circulated by a person who is not a resident of this state but who is otherwise eligible to register to vote in this state if that person registers as a circulator with the secretary of state before circulating petitions.

H. The secretary of state shall require in the instructions and procedures manual issued pursuant to section 16-452 that persons who circulate nomination petitions pursuant to this section and who are not residents of this state but who are otherwise eligible to register to vote in this state shall register as circulators with the office of the secretary of state before circulating petitions. The secretary of state shall provide for a method of receiving service of process for those petition circulators who are registered.

The nomination petition for the office of presidential elector shall include a group of names of candidates equal to the number of United States senators and representatives in Congress from this state instead of separate nomination petitions for each candidate for the office of presidential elector. ... A nomination petition for the office of presidential elector shall be filed not less than eighty nor more than one hundred days before the general election.

citations.field: circulator eligibility standard (qualified to register, not required to be resident)
citations.quote: D. The person before whom the signatures were written on the signature sheet is not required to be a resident of this state but otherwise shall be qualified to register to vote in this state pursuant to section 16-101 and, if not a resident of this state, shall register as a circulator with the secretary of state.  A circulator shall verify that each of the names on the petition was signed in his presence on the date indicated, and that in his belief each signer was a qualified elector who resides at the address given as the signer's residence on the date indicated and, if for a partisan election, that each signer is a qualified signer.

citations.field: circulatorMustBeUSCitizen / circulatorMinAge (via 16-101 qualifications incorporated by reference)
citations.quote: A. Every resident of this state is qualified to register to vote if the resident: 1. Is a citizen of the United States and has provided satisfactory evidence of citizenship as prescribed in section 16-166. 2. Will be eighteen years of age or more on or before the date of the regular general election next following his registration. ... 5. Has not been convicted of treason or a felony, unless restored to civil rights. 6. Has not been adjudicated an incapacitated person as defined in section 14-5101.

citations.field: signer requirement (registered voter in electoral district) - binds signer, not circulator
citations.quote: B. For the purposes of petitions filed pursuant to sections 16-312, 16-313, 16-314 and 16-341, each signer of a nomination petition shall be a voter who at the time of signing is a registered voter in the electoral district of the office the candidate is seeking.

signaturesHeadline: 25,000 valid sigs (1% of last statewide vote or 25,000, whichever is less; 25,000 binds — collect a large cushion)
deadlineHeadline: ~Late June of election year: 134 days before the general (2024 = June 24); SBE filing window 141-134 days out
riskNote: All 7 fields citation-grounded verbatim; 6 AUTO-LIVE, 1 CONSERVATIVE-HOLD (feeAlternative — cited sources do not affirmatively state "no fee," so the absence-of-fee claim is unproven by retrieval even though IL has no known fee for this route).
sosInquiries: For an independent (or new-party) candidate for President filing nomination papers with the State Board under 10 ILCS 5/10-3/10-4/10-6, is there ANY filing fee, filing deposit, bond, or fee-in-lieu-of-signatures, and is a Statement of Economic Interests required for the presidential (federal) office? Please confirm there is no monetary filing requirement for this route.

fields.reliedValue: 1% of the number of voters who voted in the next preceding statewide general election OR 25,000 qualified Illinois voters, whichever is less. Recomputes per cycle; because 1% of statewide turnout (~60,000 in 2024 based on ~6M turnout) vastly exceeds 25,000, the 25,000 ceiling is the binding figure and has controlled every presidential cycle for decades. Practical requirement: 25,000 valid signatures of qualified Illinois voters — collect a substantial cushion above 25,000 to survive invalidation/challenge.
fields.note: FindLaw 10-3 and NASS quotes verify verbatim (NASS via PDF text extract, OCR line breaks only). Re-derived: IL 2020/2022 statewide turnout is several million, so 1% is far above 25,000 and the lesser figure (25,000) binds. Value correctly preserves the per-cycle recomputation. Strict caveat retained: 25,000 is the floor of VALID signatures; gather well above to absorb challenges.

fields.reliedValue: Statutory rule: file with the Illinois State Board of Elections not less than 134 and not more than 141 days before the general election (10 ILCS 5/10-6). This computes to ~late June of the election year and RECOMPUTES each cycle. For the most recent cycle (2024 general, Nov. 5, 2024) the SBE filing window was June 17-24, 2024, with the deadline Monday, June 24, 2024 at 5:00 p.m. Do NOT reuse the June 24, 2024 date for a future cycle — re-derive from the 134/141-day rule against the specific election date.
fields.note: Triple-corroborated: Ballotpedia (141/134-day quote verbatim, line 232), NASS ('no less than 134 prior' verbatim), ballot-access.org ('June 24 is the Illinois petition deadline' verbatim), and IL Candidate Guide ('Not more than 141 nor less than 134 days... 10 ILCS 5/10-6'; window 'June 17 - June 24, 2024'). Value is correct as a rule; the cycle-specific 2024 date is accurate but transient — reliedValue foregrounds the recomputing rule.

fields.reliedValue: None. President is a statewide/at-large office; signatures are counted 'in the aggregate' across the state with no per-congressional-district, per-county, or geographic-distribution requirement. The 5,000-per-district floor in 10 ILCS 5/10-3 applies only to district offices (e.g., congressperson after redistricting), not to the statewide presidential petition.
fields.note: FindLaw 10-3 quote ('for any office to be filled by the voters of the State at large... in the aggregate') verifies verbatim. Re-derived from full statute text: the district/subdivision 5%-8% and 5,000-signature provisions are expressly tied to district/political-subdivision offices, not at-large offices. No distribution rule applies to President.

fields.reliedValue: Circulator must be at least 18 (or will be 18 by the upcoming general election) and a U.S. citizen; may be from outside the district, county, or state (no Illinois residency required). A circulator may NOT mix petition types: cannot circulate for an independent candidate in addition to a new-party candidate, cannot circulate for more than one new party, and cannot circulate for an established party plus an independent/new party (10 ILCS 5/10-4; Schober v. Young, 322 Ill. App. 3d 996 (4th Dist. 2001)).
fields.note: IL Candidate Guide quotes verify verbatim (guide lines 594-596: '18 years of age... can be from outside of the district, county, or state'; 'personally witness all signatures'). Mixing prohibitions confirmed at guide lines 631-648 ('A circulator may not circulate petitions for an independent candidate(s) in addition to candidates for a new political party', etc.). Out-of-state circulators are explicitly permitted by the current official guide.

fields.reliedValue: Both required. The circulator must personally witness every signature and complete/sign the circulator's statement at the bottom of each sheet (certifying address, age, citizenship, and that signatures were given in their presence by registered voters); that circulator's statement must be sworn to and signed by a notary public. Separately, the candidate must swear/affirm and sign a Statement of Candidacy and have it notarized; the notarized Statement of Candidacy must accompany the nomination papers (attached at the front of the packet).
fields.note: All four IL Candidate Guide quotes verify verbatim (guide line 596 'must personally witness all signatures'; line 618 'This statement shall be sworn to and signed by a notary'; lines 492-494 'must swear to or affirm... sign... have the statement notarized. The Statement of Candidacy must accompany the nomination papers'). Both the circulator certification and a separate notarization are required.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Original wet-ink signatures of voters AND the original signature of the circulator are required on physical petition sheets; photocopies or duplicates are not accepted (blank petitions may be photocopied before circulation, but the signer/circulator/notary signatures must all be original). There is NO digital, remote-online, or in-person-electronic signature system for an independent or new-party presidential petition — collection and filing are entirely paper-based with the State Board of Elections.
fields.note: IL Candidate Guide quotes verify verbatim (guide line 502 'contain the original signatures of the voters and the original signature of the circulator, and shall not be photocopies or duplicates'; line 607 'Blank nomination petitions may be reproduced prior to circulation. The signatures of the signers, circulator, and the notary public must be original'). Adversarial check: no source indicates any e-signature/online option for this route; the original-signature mandate forecloses it.

fields.reliedValue: Operating assumption: NO filing fee and NO fee-in-lieu-of-signatures for the independent/new-party presidential petition — ballot access is by signature petition only. This is NOT positively confirmed by the cited sources (they describe the petition requirement but do not affirmatively state that no fee exists), so it must be confirmed with the State Board before relying on it. Strict reading: treat the petition as the sole access path, budget for no fee, but verify with SBE that no fee/deposit/bond applies and confirm whether a Statement of Economic Interests is required for the federal office.
fields.note: Citation integrity PASSES: both quotes (NASS petition description and FindLaw 10-3) verify verbatim. But this is a NEGATIVE claim — 'no fee exists' — and the cited quotes only describe the signature/petition requirement; neither affirmatively states the absence of a fee or fee-in-lieu. Proof-of-a-negative gap: the IL Candidate Guide contains zero references to any filing fee or dollar amount for this route (strong corroboration the value is likely correct), but retrieval cannot positively establish an absence the sources never address. Strictest defensible posture = HOLD and confirm with SBE rather than treat the no-fee claim as verified.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: Illinois's independent/nomination-petition statute (10 ILCS 5/10-4, Article 10) and the official Illinois Candidate's Guide are SILENT on compensating petition circulators — there is no candidate-petition rule permitting or prohibiting per-signature pay, hourly pay, or paid circulation at all, so circulator compensation is UNADDRESSED for candidate petitions. The frequently cited 'Illinois permits pay-per-signature' answer comes from the INITIATIVE/referendum framework (Ballotpedia's ballot-initiative pay-per-signature page, citing 5 ILCS 20 and 10 ILCS 5/28) and does NOT govern candidate petitions, so it cannot be relied on; circulators need not be Illinois residents/registered voters (only US citizen, 18+), so a national paid pool could lawfully circulate.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an INDEPENDENT candidate's nominating petition for President under 10 ILCS 5 Article 10, may a campaign compensate petition circulators on a PER-SIGNATURE basis, and if not, are hourly/salary/bonus compensation arrangements for paid circulators permitted?

citations.quote: At the bottom of each sheet of such petition shall be added a circulator's statement, signed by a person 18 years of age or older who is a citizen of the United States; stating the street address or rural route number ... certifying that the signatures on that sheet of the petition were signed in his or her presence; certifying that the signatures are genuine ... Such statement shall be sworn to before some officer authorized to administer oaths in this State.

Petition circulators must be U.S. citizens and at least 18 years old. An individual may not circulate petitions for more than one political party, established or new, or for more than one independent candidate. ... The circulator must personally witness the signing of all signatures and sign the circulator's statement on the petition attesting to that.

Pay-per-signature is a method of compensating signature gatherers, also known as circulators, who collect signatures for ballot initiatives and veto referendums. ... There are 26 states that provide for an initiative or referendum process. Sixteen (16) of those states allowed campaigns to make payments to signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected.

voterFile.cost: $500 flat for the entire-state file (committee version). Sub-state files are cheaper per the published schedule (e.g., Congressional District $75, Senate District $30, Representative District $25, Cook County / City of Chicago $100 each; "Others" $25 minimum). The redacted PUBLIC (non-committee) file is instead priced at statutory "cost of duplication plus a 15% fee for administration." Paid by electronic funds transfer (from the committee's own account); no application is processed without payment.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: For the full (unredacted, with-residential-address) file: registered State or local political committees, for bona fide political purposes (and governmental entities for a governmental purpose). The general public can only obtain a redacted version (street numbers, DOB, SSN, phone, partial email removed) and only for the limited purposes in the National Voter Registration Act, 52 U.S.C. 20507(i). The full statewide list may not be disclosed to anyone other than a State/local political committee or a governmental entity.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Political-use-only. Data "shall be used only for bona fide political purposes" and "shall not be used under any circumstances for purposes of commercial solicitation or other business purposes." The application warns misuse "may result in substantial penalty, including, but not limited to, conviction of a class 4 felony (10 ILCS 5/4-8, 5-7, 6-35)." By statute (10 ILCS 5/1A-25(c)) records may not be used for any personal, private, or commercial purpose, including intimidation/threat/deception or advertising/solicitation/sale/marketing; SBE must deny requests from anyone under a court order finding a violation. Verifying petition signatures (matching signers to registered voters) is a bona fide political purpose for a committee.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written application: download the SBE "Request for Voter Information" form (Revised 07-07-2025), complete it, and submit by email to bpeterson@elections.il.gov for review; SBE then provides an electronic-payment link and the file is run after payment. Political committees must check a verification box and have the candidate, treasurer, or chair sign a sworn statement of permitted (bona fide political) use. Files are delivered encrypted and password-protected, with the password given only to the original requestor. This is a request-and-approval process, not an instant download.
voterFile.note: Illinois sells the centralized statewide voter file, but the full version (with name + parsed residential street address + Active/Inactive status — exactly what is needed to verify petition signers) is restricted to registered political committees for bona fide political use; cost is a flat $500 statewide. A committee submits the SBE "Request for Voter Information" form by email, signs a political-use affidavit, and pays by EFT; date of birth is no longer included. The general public can only get a street-number-redacted, NVRA-purpose-limited copy.

citations.quote: Voter data is available to registered political committees for bona fide political purposes. Use for commercial solicitation or other business purposes is prohibited. (10 ICLS 5/4-8, 5-7, 6-35). Voter data is also available for the limited purposes enumerated in the National Voter Registration Act. See 52 USC 20507.

Costs (Effective Jan. 22, 2008) | Entire state $500 | Supreme/Appellate District $100 | Cook County $100 | City of Chicago $100 | Congressional District $75 | Judicial Circuit (except Cook) $40 | Legislative (Senate) District $30 | Representative District $25 | Others ($25 minimum) Call

Files of computerized voter registration data are available. The Board offers two file formats that are tailored to the information needs and processing capabilities of those requesting voter data. (Date of Birth is no longer provided.)

the disclosure of any portion of the centralized statewide voter registration list to any person or entity other than to a State or local political committee and other than to a governmental entity for a governmental purpose is specifically prohibited except as follows

the State Board of Elections shall make available to the public the statewide voter registration list, allowing for redaction of telephone numbers, social security numbers, street numbers of home addresses, birth dates, identifiable portions of email addresses, and other highly sensitive personal information. Information released under this subsection shall be used only for the purposes defined within the federal National Voter Registration Act, 52 U.S.C. 20507(i) ... The State Board of Elections may charge a reasonable fee under this subsection, consisting of the cost of duplication plus a 15% fee for administration.

Voter registration records or data shall not be used for any personal, private, or commercial purpose, including, but not limited to, the intimidation, threat, or deception of any person or the advertising, solicitation, sale, or marketing of products or services.

circulator.ageRule: The circulator must be at least 18. 10 ILCS 5/10-4 requires that the circulator's statement at the bottom of each petition sheet be "signed by a person 18 years of age or older who is a citizen of the United States." This governs all nomination petitions under Article 10, including independent-candidate petitions filed under 10 ILCS 5/10-3 (which is how an independent presidential candidate qualif
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNERS must be "qualified voters" / "duly registered voters" of the relevant political subdivision (the State, for a statewide/presidential candidate) and must sign in their own proper persons (10 ILCS 5/10-3, 10-4). The CIRCULATOR has a separate, looser standard: must be 18+ and a U.S. citizen, and merely certifies (to the best of his/her knowledge and belief) that the signers were duly register
circulator.note: 10 ILCS 5/10-4 ("Form of petition for nomination") applies to "All petitions for nomination under this Article 10," and Article 10 is where independent candidates qualify (10 ILCS 5/10-3 governs nomination papers for independent candidates, including statewide candidates such as an independent presidential ticket via at-large signatures). The circulator requirement is stated affirmatively and explicitly: the circulator's statement must be "signed by a person 18 years of age or older who is a cit

citations.quote: At the bottom of each sheet of such petition shall be added a circulator's statement, signed by a person 18 years of age or older who is a citizen of the United States; stating the street address or rural route number, as the case may be, as well as the county, city, village or town, and state; certifying that the signatures on that sheet of the petition were signed in his or her presence; certifying that the signatures are genuine

Such petition shall be signed by the qualified voters in their own proper persons only ... and certifying that to the best of his knowledge and belief the persons so signing were at the time of signing the petition duly registered voters under Article 4, 5, or 6 of this Code of the political subdivision or district for which the candidate or candidates shall be nominated

citations.field: circulatorMustBeStateResident (circulator states own address, no in-state requirement)
citations.quote: a circulator's statement, signed by a person 18 years of age or older who is a citizen of the United States; stating the street address or rural route number, as the case may be, as well as the county, city, village or town, and state

citations.field: scope — 10-4 applies to independent petitions under Article 10
citations.quote: All petitions for nomination under this Article 10 for candidates for public office in this State, shall in addition to other requirements provided by law, be as follows

citations.field: independent candidate nomination by qualified voters (signer standard, statewide)
citations.quote: Nomination of independent candidates (not candidates of any political party), for any office to be filled by the voters of the State at large may also be made by nomination papers signed in the aggregate for each candidate by 1% of the number of voters who voted in the next preceding Statewide general election or 25,000 qualified voters of the State, whichever is less.

signaturesHeadline: IN: 2% of last SoS vote (36,943 for 2024 cycle); formula stable, integer recomputes off 2026 SoS race for 2028 — HOLD on exact number
deadlineHeadline: IN: two-step — county certify by noon local ~June 30, then Election Div by noon ET ~July 15 (2024 dates July 1 / July 15); dates shift each cycle — HOLD on literal dates
riskNote: All 8 fields grounded verbatim in the official IN SoS 2024 Candidate Guide; primary risks are (a) signature count and deadline dates are 2024-cycle-specific and recompute for 2028, and (b) two inferences not stated verbatim — that the 4,500/500 rule is primary-only and that out-of-state/non-registered circulators are permitted — both held conservatively; never submit assuming non-resident circulators are safe without office confirmation.
sosInquiries: For an independent/minor-party presidential petition of nomination (CAN-19), may the petition carrier be a non-Indiana resident and/or a person who is not a registered Indiana voter, provided the carrier completes the petition-carrier certification (name, address, DOB) on every page per IC 3-8-2-7?

Please confirm that the 4,500-signature / 500-per-congressional-district requirement applies ONLY to major-party (Democratic/Republican) primary candidates for President, and that an independent/minor-party presidential petition of nomination has NO per-county or per-congressional-district distribution minimum — only the statewide 2% aggregate.
For the 2028 presidential cycle, what is the certified signature requirement (2% of the 2026 Secretary of State vote) and the exact county-certification and Election Division filing deadlines?

fields.reliedValue: Formula: 2% of the total votes cast for Secretary of State in the most recent SoS general election, statewide, with any fraction in excess of a whole number disregarded (IC 3-8-6-3). For the 2024 cycle this equals 36,943 (2% of 1,847,179). For 2028 this MUST be recomputed off the 2026 SoS race and is expected to differ; collect a buffer above the formula minimum.
fields.gate_reason: value is correct and grounded but cycle-dependent
fields.note: All three primary-source quotes verified verbatim in the fresh pdftotext extraction (lines 1979-1988, 6771) and the Ballotpedia quote verified at line 244. Math checks: 0.02 x 1,847,179 = 36,943.58 -> floored to 36,943. HOLD reason: today is 2026-06-07; the literal 36,943 is the 2024-cycle figure and is NOT current for a 2028 run (recomputes off the 2026 SoS vote). Strictest defensible posture = rely on the formula, recompute when 2026 SoS totals certify, and over-collect.

fields.reliedValue: Two-step statutory rule (IC 3-8-6-10; IC 3-8-6-12): (1) submit petitions to county voter registration offices for certification by 12:00 noon prevailing local time on June 30 of the election year (moves to the next business day if June 30 is a weekend); (2) file the certified petition (CAN-19) plus consent (CAN-20) with the Indiana Election Division by 12:00 noon ET on July 15. For the 2024 cycle the county deadline was noon Mon July 1, 2024 and the Election Division deadline was noon ET Mon July 15, 2024; first day to submit was Jan 10, 2024. Confirm exact 2028 dates with the Election Division.
fields.gate_reason: value correct and grounded but dates are cycle-specific
fields.note: Both primary-source quotes verified verbatim (lines 1013, 2001-2008) including the IC 3-8-6-10/-12 cites and the weekend-shift note; Ballotpedia corroborated at line 244 (county no later than noon June 30, SoS by July 15). The two-step structure and times (noon local for county, noon ET for the Division) match exactly. HOLD reason: the literal July 1 / July 15, 2024 dates are 2024-cycle-specific and not current for 2028. Note: Ballotpedia also flags a separate elector-filing deadline (2nd Tuesday in September) — that is a downstream step, not a defect in this field, but the campaign must calendar it separately.

fields.reliedValue: For an independent/minor-party PRESIDENTIAL petition of nomination there is no statutory per-county or per-congressional-district minimum stated in the guide; the requirement is the single statewide 2% aggregate. HOWEVER, because 'none' is an inference (the guide affirmatively states the 4,500/500 rule only for major-party primary candidates and is silent on any distribution rule for the petition of nomination), confirm with the Election Division before relying on a zero-distribution assumption. Operationally, geographic spread is still prudent since signatures are certified county-by-county.
fields.gate_reason: correctness hinges on reading the 4,500/500 rule as primary-only
fields.note: Quotes grounded: the President 2%-entire-state EXAMPLE (lines 1986-1988) and the 4,500/500 text (lines 614-616). The 4,500/500 language is explicitly framed as 'Democratic or Republican candidates seeking nomination in the 2024 primary election' (lines 614, 620, 626), strongly supporting the draft's claim that it does NOT apply to petition-of-nomination candidates. But the guide never affirmatively says 'President petition of nomination has no distribution requirement' — so 'None' is a (well-supported) interpretation, not a verbatim statement. Held conservatively per the inquiry; campaign should still spread collection geographically because certification is per-county.
