fields.reliedValue: Anyone may circulate a petition of nomination; the circulator (petition carrier) must complete the petition-carrier certification (signed affirmation under penalties of perjury plus printed name, address, and date of birth) on EVERY petition page before submission, or the page is rejected if not cured by the filing deadline (IC 3-8-2-7). Do NOT operationally assume out-of-state or non-registered circulators are safe until confirmed with the Election Division — the guide says 'anyone may circulate' but does not affirmatively address residency or voter-registration status.
fields.gate_reason: non-resident/non-voter permissibility is inferred, not stated verbatim
fields.note: Verbatim quote grounded at lines 759-760 ('Anyone may circulate a petition of nomination... must complete petition carrier certification on every petition form'). The draft's value is correct as to the carrier-certification requirement (IC 3-8-2-7, lines 845-847). HOLD reason: the draft extends 'anyone may circulate' to assert no residency and no voter-status restriction — that specific permission for non-residents/non-voters is an inference, not stated in the guide. Strictest defensible posture: rely on 'anyone may circulate + full carrier cert on every page' and resolve the residency question with the office before deploying out-of-state circulators.

fields.reliedValue: No notarization. A petition carrier (circulator) certification is required instead: the carrier must complete and sign, on each signature page, an affirmation under penalties of perjury that they have no reason to believe any signer is ineligible, with the carrier's printed name, address, and date of birth (IC 3-8-2-7). Signatures are separately validated by the county voter registration office. An incomplete carrier affidavit causes the page to be rejected if not cured by the filing deadline.
fields.note: Both quotes verified verbatim: the per-page affirmation-under-penalty-of-perjury with name/address/DOB and IC 3-8-2-7 cite (lines 845-847), and 'A petition carrier is required to fully complete and affix their signature to the affidavit at the bottom of each page' (line 1862 and 7 other occurrences). No notarization requirement appears anywhere in the guide. Value is current and correct; no cycle dependence.

fields.reliedValue: Paper only. Indiana requires original wet-ink signatures on the paper CAN-19 petition of nomination; photocopies, electronic, digital, and digitized signatures are all rejected (IC 3-5-4-1.7(b)). There is no online/digital petition system for an independent presidential petition; the process is paper end-to-end with county certification and physical filing with the Election Division.
fields.note: Quote verified verbatim at line 755 ('photocopies of signatures, electronic signatures, digital signatures, or digitized signatures will not be accepted. (IC 3-5-4-1.7(b))'). Confirms a strict wet-signature paper regime. Value is current and correct; no cycle dependence.

fields.reliedValue: None. Indiana provides no filing-fee option in lieu of (or in addition to) the petition signatures for an independent or minor-party presidential candidate; ballot access is by petition of nomination only.
fields.note: Quote verified verbatim at line 344 ('A minor party (other than the Libertarian Party), or independent candidate in Indiana is nominated for placement on the general election ballot by petition of nomination'). Adversarial check confirmed: the only 'fee' anywhere in the 153-page guide is an unrelated photocopying fee (line 3423), and 'in lieu' appears nowhere — so there is no fee alternative. Value is current and correct.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: NO per-signature (prohibited) — pay hourly/bonus; advocate to legalize
compensationModel.model: per-signature prohibited (Level 6 felony); flat/hourly pay expressly allowed
compensationModel.note: Candidate-petition compensation IS addressed by statute, not carried over from initiative law (Indiana has no statewide initiative process). IC 3-14-2-1(5) makes it a Level 6 felony to pay "based on the number of signatures obtained to place a candidate or public question on a ballot," but expressly permits paying circulators on a non-per-signature basis (hourly/flat). No circulator residency/voter-registration requirement exists in the petition-carrier statute (IC 3-6-12); only signers must be registered Indiana voters.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's petition of nomination under IC 3-8-6, does IC 3-14-2-1(5) permit paying circulators (petition carriers) on an hourly or flat-fee basis so long as compensation is not tied directly or indirectly to the number of signatures collected, and is there any residency or Indiana voter-registration requirement for the petition carriers themselves?

citations.quote: Sec. 1. A person who knowingly does any of the following commits a Level 6 felony: ... (5) Pays or offers to pay an individual any property based on the number of signatures obtained to place a candidate or public question on a ballot. This subdivision does not prohibit payment for gathering signatures not based, either directly or indirectly, on the number of signatures obtained to place a candidate or public question on a ballot.

5) Paying or offering to pay an individual any property based on the number of signatures obtained to place a candidate or public question on a ballot. This does not prohibit payment for gathering signatures not based, either directly or indirectly, on the number of signatures obtained to place a candidate or public question on a ballot. (IC 3-14-2-1)

As used in this chapter, "petition carrier" refers to an individual who circulates a petition that is required to place a candidate or a public question on the ballot. The term includes a candidate circulating a petition for the candidate's placement on the ballot.

Sec. 7. (a) The petition carrier must sign and date the following statement at the bottom of each signature page: "I affirm under the penalties for perjury that I have no reason to believe that any individual whose signature appears on this page is ineligible to sign this petition or did not properly complete and sign this page.". (b) The petition carrier must also print the following information below the affirmation stated in subsection (a): (1) The petition carrier's name. (2) The petition carrier's full address, including the ZIP code. (3) The petition carrier's date of birth.

Sec. 6. (a) The signatures to a petition of nomination need not be appended to one (1) paper, but a petitioner may not be counted unless the petitioner is registered and qualified to vote in conformity with section 8 of this chapter.

voterFile.cost: No published flat statewide price. Indiana does not sell a single downloadable statewide voter file to campaigns; voter registration data is obtained from the 92 county voter registration offices (or, where authorized, a copy/extract of the computerized statewide list under IC 3-7-26.4), with fees set per county/per request. Varies — request quote.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Restricted, political/election use only. The computerized statewide voter registration list (SVRS) is not a public commercial product; copies/extracts are made available for political purposes (e.g., to candidates, political party committees, and similar election-related requesters) and not for sale to the general public for commercial use. Routine voter data is requested at the county voter registration office level.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Strict: the statewide voter registration list and voter data may be used only for political/election purposes; commercial use and use for solicitation are prohibited. Indiana law (IC 3-7-26.4, with offenses in IC Title 3, Article 14) makes unauthorized acquisition or misuse of the list a criminal offense. A requester is typically required to certify the data will not be used for commercial purposes.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request to the county voter registration office (or to the Election Division for authorized statewide-list access), generally accompanied by a sworn/certified statement of permitted (non-commercial, political) use. Not an instant online download — the Indiana SOS Election Division website provides no public order form or downloadable statewide file. Process and fee must be confirmed directly with the Election Division / county.
voterFile.note: Indiana does NOT offer a public, instantly downloadable statewide voter file: a full map of the SOS Election Division site (108 URLs) and the relevant pages show only aggregate statistics (counts by county, turnout/registration reports) and voter-list-maintenance info — no "purchase/request voter file" page. The statewide list (SVRS) is restricted under IC 3-7-26.4 to election/political use; a campaign typically obtains the underlying name + residential address + registration status data at the county voter registration office level via written request with a certification of non-commercial use. Pricing is not published online — confirm fee and process directly with the Indiana Election Division (317-232-3939) or the county registration office. Statute text could not be retrieved verbatim because Justia/FindLaw returned HTTP 403 and the Indiana General Assembly code site (iga.in.gov) is a key-gated JavaScript app; the IC 3-7-26.4 framework is stated from the official site structure and established law, and should be verified against the live statute before relying on it.

A person’s voter registration record is never removed from the statewide voter registration system. Instead, the record is flagged as “active,” “inactive,” or “cancelled.”

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — Indiana's petition-of-nomination statute (IC 3-8-6) imposes NO age (or any other) requirement on the person who carries the petition and collects signatures. The statute does not define or regulate a "circulator" at all. Every eligibility requirement in the chapter binds the PETITIONER (signer), who must be "registered and qualified to vote" (IC 3-8-6-3, -5(a)(5), -
circulator.signerVsCirculator: The strict registered/qualified-voter requirement binds the SIGNER (petitioner) only; the CIRCULATOR/collector has no statutory requirements in IC 3-8-6.
circulator.note: Indiana is a "government-certification" petition state, NOT a "circulator-affidavit" state. I read the entire operative chapter (IC 3-8-6, secs. 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 10) on FindLaw (current as of Jan 2, 2024). The chapter contains no concept of a "circulator," "witness," or affidavit by the person who gathers signatures (the only affidavit is a narrow IC 3-8-6-6(b) "affidavit of assistance" for helping a SIGNER with a disability physically write their own info — not a circulator oath). Instead, IC

citations.field: circulatorAgeRule / no circulator role — validity rests on government certification of signers, not a circulator affidavit
citations.quote: For a petition of nomination to be considered valid by the officer required to receive the petition, the county voter registration office in the county where the petitioner is registered must certify that each petitioner is a voter at the residence address listed in the petition at the time the petition is being processed. The certification must accompany and be part of each petition. The certification must indicate that each petitioner is eligible to vote for the candidates being nominated by the petition.

citations.field: signerVsCirculator — requirements bind the petitioner (signer); only signer info is required on each petition paper, no circulator/witness signature
citations.quote: The signatures to a petition of nomination need not be appended to one (1) paper, but a petitioner may not be counted unless the petitioner is registered and qualified to vote in conformity with section 8 of this chapter. Each petition must contain the following: (1) The signature of each petitioner. (2) The name of each petitioner legibly printed. (3) The residence address of each petitioner as set forth on the petitioner's voter registration record.

citations.field: circulatorMustBeRegisteredVoter — the registered/qualified-voter requirement is imposed on the petitioner (signer), not on a collector
citations.quote: A petition of nomination must state all of the following: ... (5) That the petitioners desire and are registered and qualified to vote for each candidate.

citations.field: signer-only signing requirement — each petitioner signs personally (or has mark attested / authorizes assistance for a disability); no provision for a circulator to sign or swear
citations.quote: Each petitioner on a petition of nomination must, in conformity with section 6 of this chapter: (1) sign the petitioner's name; (2) have the petitioner's mark attested; or (3) authorize an individual to sign on behalf of the petitioner with a disability.

citations.field: signature threshold binds voters/signers (context: who must sign)
citations.quote: A petition of nomination must be signed by the number of voters equal to two percent (2%) of the total vote cast at the last election for secretary of state in the election district that the candidate seeks to represent.

signaturesHeadline: 3,500 signatures (flat statutory; not a %) + 100 each from 19 counties — AUTO-LIVE
deadlineHeadline: 5:00 p.m. on 81st day before Nov general election (2024 = Aug 16, 2024) — AUTO-LIVE
riskNote: Core petition burden (3,500 sigs, 19-county floor, 81-day deadline, notarized affidavit, paper-only) is fully grounded in Iowa Code 45.1/44.4/45.5 and AUTO-LIVE. Two fields held conservatively: circulator-eligibility (relying on a complete ABSENCE of circulator rules is a negative inference not provable by retrieval; sub-regulatory IAC ch. 721 rules not pulled) and the NPPO convention alternative (the "one attendee per county"/"true independents have no convention option" claims rest on Ballotpedia + un-retrieved Iowa Code ch. 44 classification). Strict posture: rely on the petition route only; never treat the convention as a shortcut.
sosInquiries: Re circulators: For an independent/NPPO presidential nomination petition under Iowa Code ch. 45, is there ANY requirement or attestation imposed on the person who circulates the petition (age, Iowa residency, voter-registration status, or a circulator oath/affidavit), whether in statute or in the Iowa Administrative Code (e.g., IAC ch. 721)? Please confirm there are no circulator requirements beyond each signer being an eligible elector.

Re NPPO/independent classification: Can a candidate with NO party or NPPO affiliation (a 'Nominated by Petition' candidate) for President/VP qualify by convention/caucus under Iowa Code ch. 44, or is the 3,500-signature/19-county petition the ONLY available route for a truly unaffiliated candidate? Separately, for an NPPO convention nomination, please confirm the exact attendance requirements (minimum total electors and whether at least one attendee is required from each of all 99 counties) and the filing deadline for the certificate of nomination.

fields.reliedValue: 3,500 signatures of eligible electors — a flat statutory minimum (not a percentage; does not recompute per cycle), including at least 100 eligible electors each from at least 19 counties (embedded distribution floor). Iowa Code 45.1(1). Same number applies to the unaffiliated 'Nominated by Petition' candidate and to an NPPO candidate using the petition route (45.4 routes petition candidates through ch. 45).
fields.note: 45.1(1) verbatim: 'not less than three thousand five hundred eligible electors, including at least one hundred eligible electors each from at least nineteen counties of the state.' Confirmed a flat number, not a percentage, in the statute itself; current (last amended 2022; 2021 amendment to subsec.1 applies to ballots in/after 2022). Ballotpedia corroborates. AUTO-LIVE.

fields.reliedValue: 5:00 p.m. on the 81st day before the November general election; earliest filing = 99 days before. For 2024 (Nov 5, 2024): deadline 5:00 p.m. Aug 16, 2024, filing window opened July 29, 2024. Iowa Code 44.4(1)(b), incorporated for petition candidates by 45.4. Recomputes each cycle.
fields.note: 44.4(1)(b) verbatim: '...not more than ninety-nine days nor later than 5:00 p.m. on the eighty-first day before the date of the general election to be held in November.' 45.4 incorporation verbatim. Date arithmetic independently verified: Nov 5, 2024 - 81 days = Aug 16, 2024; -99 days = July 29, 2024; Nov 5 2024 is a Tuesday. AUTO-LIVE.

fields.reliedValue: Yes — county distribution requirement: at least 100 eligible electors EACH from at least 19 counties (a subset of the 3,500 total). Stated by county, not by congressional district. Iowa Code 45.1(1).
fields.note: 45.1(1) verbatim: 'including at least one hundred eligible electors each from at least nineteen counties of the state.' By county, not district — confirmed. Ballotpedia corroborates. AUTO-LIVE.

fields.reliedValue: Requirements fall on SIGNERS, who must each be an 'eligible elector': U.S. citizen, Iowa resident, at least 17 (must be 18 by Election Day), need not be a registered voter but must meet all qualifications to register; not a disqualified felon, not judged incompetent to vote, not claiming the right to vote elsewhere. Any eligible elector in Iowa may sign for a statewide/presidential office. STRICT HOLD on the asserted COMPLETE ABSENCE of any circulator requirement/attestation — do not operationally assume zero circulator rules until the SOS confirms (see sosInquiries).
fields.note: Signer-eligibility facts ground verbatim on the SOS candidate FAQ (citizen/resident/17-must-be-18; 'not required to be a registered voter'; statewide-office signing). However, the claim that there is NO circulator age/residency/voter-status requirement and NO attestation step is a negative inference: chapter 45 and the FAQ do not affirmatively foreclose a sub-regulatory rule (IAC ch. 721 not retrieved). Per strict 'never risk ballot access' posture, HOLD the no-circulator-rules conclusion pending SOS confirmation; the signer facts themselves are reliable.

fields.reliedValue: Petition pages are NOT notarized and need no circulator/witness certification. The candidate's affidavit of candidacy MUST be notarized — nomination papers are rejected if the affidavit lacks the signature of a notary public under ch. 9B (or other officer empowered to witness oaths). Each signature line must include the signer's signature and residential address (street, number, city) to be counted; no per-signature witnessing required. Iowa Code 45.5(2)(c),(5)(d); SOS FAQ.
fields.note: SOS FAQ verbatim: 'A candidate's affidavit of candidacy must be notarized. Nomination petitions do not need to be notarized.' 45.5(5)(d) verbatim on notary requirement; 45.5(2)(c) verbatim on signature+residential-address counting rule. Consistent and current. AUTO-LIVE.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Wet-ink paper petitions (8.5 x 11 inch pages, securely bound into a single bundle; unbound petitions returned without examination) plus a paper notarized affidavit of candidacy, filed with the Secretary of State. No online/remote-signature system for an independent/NPPO presidential petition. Iowa Code 45.5(1),(3); SOS lists the paper President/VP nomination petition PDF.
fields.note: 45.5(1) verbatim ('eight and one-half by eleven inches...substantially the form prescribed') and 45.5(3) verbatim ('securely fastened together to form a single bundle. Nomination petitions that are not bound shall be returned without further examination'). SOS nomination-papers page lists the paper 'Nomination Petition-President & Vice President' PDF under General Election. Chapter 45 has no e-signature provision; physical bound-bundle requirement forecloses remote capture. AUTO-LIVE.

fields.reliedValue: No filing fee and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option — the 3,500-signature/19-county petition is the mandatory, SAFE route for the unaffiliated presidential candidate; rely on it exclusively. Do NOT rely on a convention/caucus alternative as an available shortcut: the claim that an NPPO may instead convene (>=500 electors, one attendee per county, certificate filed by the 81st-day deadline) and that a 'Nominated by Petition' unaffiliated candidate has NO convention option rests on Ballotpedia plus un-retrieved Iowa Code ch. 44 and is a legal-classification interpretation (see sosInquiries).
fields.note: No-fee part grounds verbatim (SOS FAQ: 'There are no candidate filing fees in Iowa') and is reliable. Convention details ground verbatim on Ballotpedia ('At least 500 eligible voters must attend... at least one attendee from each of the state's counties') but NOT on the retrieved SOS FAQ, which states only that NPPOs may nominate by convention without the 500/per-county specifics. The '>=1 attendee per county' = all 99 counties is an extreme, un-statute-confirmed bar, and the categorical 'true independents have no convention option' is a legal classification not settled by retrieval (ch. 44 not pulled). HOLD: rely solely on the petition route, which is the strictest/safest posture.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: Iowa's candidate-petition / election code (Ch. 45) and the Iowa SOS candidate guidance impose NO compensation rule on circulators of candidate nominating petitions — paying per signature is neither authorized nor prohibited; it is unaddressed for candidate petitions, so do not assume it is permitted. Iowa's pay-per-signature literature applies only to ballot initiatives/veto referendums, and Iowa is not even one of those states (no statewide citizen-initiative process), so nothing affirmatively permits per-signature pay here. Circulator residency is not required.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nominating petition under Iowa Code Ch. 45, may a campaign compensate petition circulators on a per-signature basis (and are paid circulators otherwise permitted), and must a circulator be an Iowa resident or registered voter?

citations.quote: The state code does not establish circulator requirements. Specifically, there are no residency requirements for circulators.

In Iowa, most candidates are required to file nomination petitions with the Iowa Secretary of State. Any eligible elector may sign a candidate's nomination petition. Eligible electors do not have to be registered to vote, but they do need to be U.S. citizens, Iowa residents, and 18 years of age or older.

An independent candidate for president must petition for placement on the general election ballot. At least 3,500 eligible voters from at least 19 of the state's counties must sign the petition. The petition must include the names of candidates for president, vice president, and presidential electors. The petition must be filed with the secretary of state by 5:00 p.m. on the 81st day prior to the general election.

Pay-per-signature is a method of compensating signature gatherers, also known as circulators, who collect signatures for ballot initiatives and veto referendums. The method involves paying signature gatherers at a rate based on the number of signatures collected.

There are 26 states that provide for an initiative or referendum process. Sixteen (16) of those states allowed campaigns to make payments to signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected. Ten (10) of those states prohibited campaigns from paying signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected.

voterFile.cost: Per-record formula (one-time list): $10.00 minimum data charge for 20,000 records or less, plus $0.50 per 1,000 records for lists over 20,000 records (statewide ≈ 2.3M registered voters → roughly $10 + ~$1,150 ≈ ~$1,160 one-time). E-mail delivery is "same as Data Charge(s)"; CD-ROM adds $2.00; paper adds $0.10/page. Alternatively, a "Statewide voter list with updates" subscription is $1,500/year.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Anyone may purchase, BUT use is statutorily restricted to permitted purposes only (Iowa Code 48A.38): to request a registrant's vote at an election, for a genuine political purpose, for bona fide political research, or for a bona fide official purpose by an elected official. A political campaign/committee verifying petition signatures qualifies under "genuine political purpose." Statewide and congressional-district lists must be obtained from the Secretary of State's Office.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Use is limited to the four permitted purposes above. "Using the information for any commercial purpose is a serious misdemeanor under Iowa law." Social Security numbers, driver license numbers, and non-operator ID numbers are statutorily excluded from any list.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request via the official "Request a Voter List" PDF form, submitted (mail/email/fax) to the State Registrar of Voters at the Secretary of State's Office. The form requires selecting jurisdiction, list type/election-participation history, party, voter status, and format. Credit card (Visa/MasterCard) payment is handled by phone after the office determines the purchase amount; cash and check also accepted. Lists can take up to 14 days to prepare and are delivered by email, CD-ROM, or paper.
voterFile.note: Iowa sells the statewide voter file through the Secretary of State (State Registrar of Voters) under Iowa Code 48A.38. It is not an instant free download: it requires a written request form, costs are per-record (roughly ~$1,160 one-time for the full statewide list, or $1,500/year for a subscription with updates), and use is legally gated to political/research/official purposes with commercial use a serious misdemeanor — hence RESTRICTED. The standard list includes name, residential address, party, and voter status (active/inactive/pending), which is sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters.

citations.quote: Iowa law allows for the purchase of voter registration lists (See Iowa Code section 48A.38.) Requests can be made with the Secretary of State's Office or with a county auditor. Statewide and congressional district lists must be obtained from the Secretary of State's Office.

Information from voter registration records can be used only: To request a registrant's vote at an election, For a genuine political purpose, For bona fide political research, or For a bona fide official purpose by an elected official.
Using the information for any commercial purpose is a serious misdemeanor under Iowa law.
Minimum Data Charge *20,000 records or less | $10.00
Additional Data Charges *for lists with more than 20,000 records | $0.50 per 1,000 records
Statewide voter list with updates | $1,500/year
Per Iowa law, Social Security numbers, driver license numbers, and non-operator ID numbers are not included on any list.
Lists are produced as soon as possible, but it can take up to 14 days to prepare a list.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — rule binds signers only. Iowa Code Chapter 45 (Nominations by Petition) does not establish or regulate a "circulator" or "witness" at all. Iowa nomination petitions are NOT verified by a circulator affidavit; the only affidavit required is the candidate's own notarized "affidavit of candidacy" (Iowa Code §45.5(5), requiring the candidate's name, office, the candidat
circulator.signerVsCirculator: The strict requirements bind the SIGNER only: each signer must be an "eligible elector" (registered/qualified voter), must add a residential address, and must reside in the appropriate district (Iowa Code §45.1, §45.6). The CIRCULATOR (signature collector) is not a defined or regulated role in Iowa nomination-petition law at all — there is no circulator/witness affidavit, so no age, voter-registra
circulator.note: Iowa is an unusual, circulator-friendly state for nominating petitions: Chapter 45 has NO circulator affidavit and never defines or restricts the person who collects signatures. For an independent presidential nominating petition (a "nomination by petition"), §45.1(1) requires 3,500 eligible-elector signatures (incl. ≥100 each from ≥19 counties). All eligibility requirements — eligible elector status, residence in the district — fall on the SIGNERS (§45.1, §45.6). The only verification mechanism

citations.quote: 5. The nomination papers shall be rejected if the affidavit lacks any of the following: a. The candidate's name. b. The name of the office sought, including the district, if any. c. The signature of the candidate. d. The signature of a notary public under chapter 9B or other officer empowered to witness oaths.

citations.field: circulatorAgeRule (no circulator affidavit; papers = petition + affidavit of candidacy)
citations.quote: 1. Nomination papers shall include a petition and an affidavit of candidacy.

The following requirements shall be observed in the signing and preparation of nomination petitions: ... 2. Each signer shall add the signer's residential address, with street and number, if any, and city. 3. All signers, for all nominations, of each separate part of a nomination petition, shall reside in the appropriate ward, city, county, school district or school district director district, legislative district, or other district as required by section 45.1.

citations.field: signature requirement for independent presidential petition (signers must be eligible electors)
citations.quote: 1. Nominations for candidates for president and vice president, governor and lieutenant governor, and for United States senator may be made by nomination petitions signed by not less than three thousand five hundred eligible electors, including at least one hundred eligible electors each from at least nineteen counties of the state.

citations.field: signer eligibility statement on petition (identifies signers, not circulator)
citations.quote: a. A statement identifying the signers of the petition as eligible electors of the appropriate ward, city, county, school district or school district director district, or legislative district and of the state of Iowa.

signaturesHeadline: 5,000 sigs / 500 per CD (SoS standard; bare statute reads 2,500/250 — collect 5,000/500)
deadlineHeadline: File 3rd Tue Jul–1st Fri after 3rd Tue Aug (2024: Jul 16–Aug 23); petition to registrars by Jul 24 (T-30)
riskNote: Signature/distribution count is a statute-vs-agency conflict: RS 18:465(C)(1) literally says 2,500/250 for statewide candidates, but the SoS packet and NASS both say 5,000/500 for the presidential independent slate — collect 5,000/500 (strictest) to avoid rejection. Fee changed $500 (2024 packet) -> $1,000 (current statute); confirm operative fee/dates with SoS each cycle, and the petition path is always available as a fee-free alternative.
sosInquiries: For an independent (non-party) slate of presidential electors filing a nominating petition under La R.S. 18:1254, is the required signature count 5,000 total with 500 from each of the six congressional districts (as stated in the SoS qualifying packet), or 2,500/250 per La R.S. 18:465(C)(1) for statewide candidates — and what is the controlling authority for the 5,000/500 figure?

For the upcoming presidential cycle, please confirm the exact qualifying-period open/close dates and the registrar-certification submission deadline (30 days before the period ends) for an independent presidential-elector petition under La R.S. 18:1254/1255 and 18:465(E).

Does Louisiana impose any age, residency, or voter-registration requirement on the persons who circulate/collect signatures on an independent presidential-elector nominating petition, beyond the circulator's certification duty in La R.S. 18:465(D)?
Please confirm the current qualifying fee for an independent presidential-elector slate under La R.S. 18:1254(A) — the SoS 2024 packet states $500 but the current statute reads $1,000.

fields.reliedValue: 5,000 signatures of registered voters, with at least 500 from each of Louisiana's 6 congressional districts (collect to this higher standard).
fields.note: Citations grounded verbatim (SoS packet + NASS both say 5,000/500). BUT the draft's legal derivation is wrong: RS 18:465(C)(1) — the cross-referenced statewide provision RS 18:1254(B) routes independents into — actually reads 'two thousand five hundred, not less than two hundred fifty of which shall be from each of the congressional districts' (verified at codes.findlaw.com/la, current as of 2023-01-01). So the bare statute yields 2,500/250, not 5,000/500. This is a statute-vs-administering-agency conflict that retrieval cannot settle. Strictest defensible value = 5,000/500 (the SoS's own published, administered standard, which also moots the lower reading), so relied value = 5,000/500. Do NOT rely on 2,500/250.

fields.reliedValue: File during the period beginning the third Tuesday in July and ending 4:30 p.m. on the first Friday following the third Tuesday in August (2024 cycle: Jul 16, 8:00 a.m. – Aug 23, 4:30 p.m.); the petition must additionally be submitted to parish registrars for certification no later than 30 days before the period ends (2024: July 24). Treat July-24-type certification cutoff as the binding earliest deadline and re-confirm exact cycle dates with the SoS.
fields.note: Triple-confirmed against primary sources: RS 18:1254(A) (legis.la.gov, current), RS 18:1255(A) (independently fetched — 'period beginning on the third Tuesday in July and ending at 4:30 p.m. on the first Friday following the third Tuesday in August'), and the SoS packet. The draft correctly flags and rejects NASS's conflicting 'first Friday following the first Tuesday in September' — Louisiana primary law controls and says August. HOLD (not AUTO-LIVE) because: (a) the value embeds cycle-specific 2024 calendar dates that recompute each cycle; (b) a NASS source conflict exists on record; (c) the registrar-certification sub-deadline (~30 days before close) is the true operational cutoff and must be re-derived per cycle. Strictest framing = honor the earlier certification cutoff.

fields.reliedValue: At least 500 of the 5,000 signatures from each of the 6 congressional districts (collect to this higher per-district standard). Separately, the slate must comprise 8 electors: one registered voter from each of the 6 CDs plus 2 at-large.
fields.note: Same statute-vs-agency conflict as signaturesRequired: bare RS 18:465(C)(1) sets 250 per CD for statewide candidates, while the SoS packet (grounded verbatim) says 500 per CD for the presidential independent slate. The 8-elector slate composition (1 per CD + 2 at-large) is independently confirmed verbatim in RS 18:1254(C) and is correct. Per-district signature minimum held to strictest = 500/CD. Note the draft's 'recomputes if number of districts changes' is sound conditional reasoning but not separately verified.

fields.reliedValue: No verified express age/residency/registration qualification on circulators (draft says UNKNOWN). Do NOT assume circulators may be unqualified; treat circulator age/residency as UNKNOWN and confirm with SoS, and rely only on the verified facts that signers must be registered voters in the relevant CD and that the person obtaining signatures must certify their genuineness (RS 18:465(D)).
fields.note: The supporting SoS-packet quote (8-elector slate composition) is grounded verbatim but does not itself speak to circulators. Independent check of RS 18:465(D) confirms a circulator-certification duty and no stated circulator age/residency — but this is a proving-a-negative claim that retrieval cannot conclusively confirm (a requirement could live in another Title 18 provision or SoS rule). Draft's own 'UNKNOWN' label is appropriate. HOLD as interpretation/absence-of-law; relied value preserves the UNKNOWN and adds an SoS inquiry.

fields.reliedValue: Each of the 8 electors files a notarized Affidavit of Acceptance of Nomination; each Notice of Candidacy must be executed before a notary public OR witnessed by two persons registered to vote for presidential elector (armed-forces officer may witness for candidates serving out of state). The petition itself requires the persons who obtained signatures to certify the signatures are genuine and statements true and correct, and parish registrars certify signature validity (per RS 18:465(D), the independent-path provision).
fields.note: Notary/affidavit and notice-of-candidacy quotes are grounded verbatim in the SoS packet and are correct. The circulator-certification quote is grounded verbatim at the cited legis URL, BUT that URL (d=81289) is RS 18:1280.22 — the PARTY presidential-primary provision — not the independent path; the correct parallel for the independent petition is RS 18:465(D), which I independently confirmed contains materially identical certification language ('the candidate and all persons who obtained signatures... shall certify... all of the signatures... are genuine'). Substance is right; citation is mis-targeted. HOLD due to the mixed-relevance citation; relied value re-anchored to RS 18:465(D).

fields.reliedValue: paper
fields.note: Fully grounded verbatim in the SoS packet: documents 'may be filed by mail, by courier, or in person' and 'FAXED DOCUMENTS ARE NOT ACCEPTED.' Petition is collected on paper and certified manually by parish registrars. No online/remote signature path exists for an independent presidential petition. No legal interpretation required.

fields.reliedValue: Yes — a qualifying fee is available IN LIEU OF the petition. For risk/budget purposes assume $1,000 (current RS 18:1254(A), post-2024/2025 amendments, grounded verbatim on legis.la.gov), even though the 2024 SoS packet and NASS state $500; confirm the operative fee with the SoS for the specific cycle. The petition path remains available as a fee-free alternative. Fee payable only by cash, certified/cashier's check, or money order.
fields.note: All three citations grounded verbatim: SoS packet ($500, 2024) and NASS ($500, 2024) agree for the 2024 cycle; current legis.la.gov RS 18:1254(A) reads 'qualifying fee of one thousand dollars' (independently confirmed — amendments incl. Acts 2024 1st Ex. Sess. No. 1 and Acts 2025). The draft correctly flags this change. HOLD because the operative fee is cycle/timing-dependent and the sources disagree by amendment date; strictest assumption for a campaign = budget the higher $1,000 and/or use the petition. fee-in-lieu-of-petition structure itself is correct.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: The Louisiana Election Code provisions that govern an independent presidential petition — R.S. 18:1254 (which incorporates R.S. 18:465 by cross-reference) — are entirely SILENT on whether circulators may be paid at all or per signature; candidate-petition circulator compensation is unaddressed, so per-signature pay is neither expressly permitted nor expressly prohibited. The statute imposes residency/registration requirements only on the SIGNERS (must be registered voters of the relevant area), not on the witness/circulator, so a non-resident paid pool is not facially barred by the witnessing rule, but no compensation rule was found.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent (unaffiliated) presidential-elector nominating petition under La. R.S. 18:1254 / 18:465, does Louisiana law permit a campaign to compensate signature gatherers/circulators on a per-signature basis (and may paid circulators be non-residents of Louisiana)?

citations.quote: each signature on the nominating petition shall be dated and witnessed by the candidate or the person who obtained the signature on his behalf. The candidate and all persons who obtained signatures on his behalf shall certify on the nominating petition that to the best of their knowledge, information, and belief all of the signatures on the nominating petition are genuine and all of the statements contained in the nominating petition are true and correct.

A person may only be nominated as a candidate in a primary election by persons who are registered to vote on the office he seeks who sign a nominating petition for him no more than one hundred twenty days before the qualifying period opens for candidates in the primary election.

Except as otherwise specifically provided in this Part, a nominating petition for a slate of candidates for the offices of presidential elector shall be signed, filed, and certified as provided in Chapter 5 of this Title for state candidates voted on throughout the State.

Nominating petitions for the office of presidential elector shall be in the form prescribed by R.S. 18:465(D), except that in lieu of including the recognized political party with which the candidates are affiliated, the petition shall contain, in not more than three words, the political principle which the candidates represent.

voterFile.cost: $.01 (1 cent) per name, minimum $20.00, maximum $5,000.00 per list, plus a $5.00 state fee per order (plus a $5.00 credit-card convenience fee if paying by card). A full statewide file (~3M voters) hits the $5,000 cap.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public. Anyone may purchase by creating an account on the Secretary of State "Commercial Requests" voter list portal. The process is published under "Become a Candidate > Purchase Voter Lists," but no candidate/committee/party eligibility gate is stated in the published ordering process.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Voter email addresses are protected by law and are NOT provided; home and mailing addresses, name, sex, race, party, age, voter status, registration date/number, phone, and last-vote/voting history ARE provided. Louisiana law (La. R.S. 18:154) separately limits use of voter registration data (generally to political, religious, charitable, governmental, election, or research purposes and bars certain commercial solicitation), but the exact statutory wording and penalties could not be retrieved from a primary source in this session and are not quoted here. All purchases are final and non-refundable.
voterFile.requestProcess: Online application: create an account on the voter list portal, define selection criteria, receive a cost estimate, and pay in advance. List is delivered electronically (emailed PDF or tab-delimited text file; printable address labels optional). Download link expires 7 days after purchase. Custom selections (e.g., specific precincts/districts) are processed by Election Services staff. No instant free download; pricing is fixed by administrative rule and non-negotiable.
voterFile.note: Louisiana sells the statewide registered-voter list through the Secretary of State's online "Commercial Requests" portal at $.01/name ($20 min, $5,000 max) plus a $5 state fee; the standard file includes name, residence/mailing address, and voter status — sufficient to match signers to registered voters. Pricing is set by administrative rule and the list is delivered electronically (PDF or tab-delimited text) within a 7-day download window.

citations.quote: A database of public information of registered voters is only available for purchase using specifically defined selection criteria. The voter list is available through email as a PDF or tab delimited text file. ... You can receive a cost estimate and purchase a list of registered voters through our online application. The purchaser is responsible for defining the selection criteria, reviewing the estimate and paying in advance of receipt of the requested list.

Text File (Can be converted to an Excel format, instructions provided) $.01 (1 cent) per name with a minimum cost of $20.00 and a maximum cost of $5,000.00. ... Parish, Ward/Precinct, Name, Residence/Mailing Addresses, Districts ... Sex, Race, Political Party, Age, Voter Status, Registration Date, Registration Number, Phone Number, Last Vote Date, Residence Walk List Order, and Name Order

All lists are provided electronically via the voter list portal. An account must be created to place and purchase the order. A $5.00 state fee is added to each order. Voter lists are available for 7 days after purchase. If the list is not downloaded during this time, the link will expire.

Pricing is established by administrative rule and is non-negotiable. ... Credit card payments are subject to an additional statutory convenience fee of $5.00. ... Home addresses and mailing addresses included? Yes. ... Email addresses available? No.  Voter's email addresses are protected by law. ... The purchase of a voter list is final and the cost is non-refundable.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement appears in the statute. La. R.S. 18:465(D) describes the circulator only as "the person who obtained the signature on [the candidate's] behalf," who must witness each signature and certify the petition. The statute imposes no minimum age on that witness/circulator. The only age-bearing qualification — being a "registered to vote" / "qualified voter," which implies 18+
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: must be a registered/qualified voter eligible to vote on the office (for an independent presidential-elector slate, 5,000 registered voters with at least 500 from each congressional district) per R.S. 18:465(B),(C),(E)(2) and 18:1254. CIRCULATOR ("the person who obtained the signature on his behalf"): only required to witness each signature and certify the petition's genuineness — no voter
circulator.note: Independent presidential candidates qualify a full slate of presidential electors by nominating petition (or a $1,000 fee). La. R.S. 18:1254(B) provides the petition "shall be signed, filed, and certified as provided in Chapter 5 of this Title for state candidates voted on throughout the State," and 18:1254(C) ties the form to R.S. 18:465(D). So the controlling circulator rule is R.S. 18:465(D): each signature must be "dated and witnessed by the candidate or the person who obtained the signature

citations.quote: Form. Each sheet of the nominating petition shall set forth the candidate's name... The name of each voter who signed the nominating petition shall be typed or legibly written on the petition, and each signature on the nominating petition shall be dated and witnessed by the candidate or the person who obtained the signature on his behalf. The candidate and all persons who obtained signatures on his behalf shall certify on the nominating petition that to the best of their knowledge, information, and belief all of the signatures on the nominating petition are genuine and all of the statements contained in the nominating petition are true and correct.

Method of nominating candidates. A person may only be nominated as a candidate in a primary election by persons who are registered to vote on the office he seeks who sign a nominating petition for him... each voter who signs a nominating petition shall date his signature and shall provide the ward and precinct in which he is registered to vote, his residence address...

citations.field: signerVsCirculator (certification checks SIGNER registration, not circulator)
citations.quote: The registrar for each parish shall... promptly certify the nominating petitions... by determining and certifying on each nominating petition which of the signers who provided a residence address in the parish signed the nominating petition timely and are registered to vote on the office the candidate seeks.

citations.field: note (independent presidential elector petition uses Chapter 5 / R.S. 18:465 form)
citations.quote: Except as otherwise specifically provided in this Part, a nominating petition for a slate of candidates for the offices of presidential elector shall be signed, filed, and certified as provided in Chapter 5 of this Title for state candidates voted on throughout the State. C. Nominating petitions for the office of presidential elector shall be in the form prescribed by R.S. 18:465(D)...

citations.field: note (signer count for independent presidential petition)
citations.quote: An independent presidential candidate may petition for placement on the general election ballot. At least 5,000 registered voters must sign the petition. At least 500 signatures must come from each of the state's congressional districts. Alternatively, an independent candidate may pay a filing fee in lieu of submitting a petition. The fee amounts to $500.

signaturesHeadline: 10,000 valid signatures statewide (firm cap; the lesser-of-10,000-or-1% formula resolves to the 10,000 ceiling for President) — AUTO-LIVE
deadlineHeadline: Declaration of Intent by 1st Mon in July; Certificate of Candidacy + petition by 5pm 1st Mon in Aug (Aug 5, 2024 last cycle); electors certified ≥30 days pre-election — AUTO-LIVE
riskNote: 6 of 7 fields AUTO-LIVE on grounded primary sources (EL 5-703, 6-201/203/204, 8-502/503, 4-102, SBE pages). Sole hold: circulatorEligibility — the negative claim that out-of-state/non-registered circulators are permitted is literally accurate in statute and COMAR but is a litigated, risk-bearing point; over-comply by using MD-registered-voter circulators pending SBE confirmation.
sosInquiries: For an independent/unaffiliated presidential candidate's General Election Candidate Nomination Petition under EL 5-703, may a petition circulator be a non-Maryland resident and/or a person who is not a registered Maryland voter, or does the State Board require circulators to be Maryland registered voters/residents in practice or by any policy beyond EL 6-204 and COMAR 33.06.03.07-.08?

fields.reliedValue: 10,000 valid statewide signatures. Statutory formula: lesser of 10,000 registered voters OR 1% of registered voters eligible to vote for the office, floor of 250; for President (statewide) 1% far exceeds 10,000, so the binding number is the 10,000 cap. Count fixed as of January 1 of the primary-election year. New-party path under EL 4-102 is also 10,000.
fields.note: All 4 citations verbatim: EL 5-703(e)(1) lesser-of-10,000-or-1%/250 floor; EL 5-703(e)(3) Jan 1 of primary year; SBE candidacy page explicitly states 10,000 for unaffiliated statewide candidates; Ballotpedia corroborates. President-is-statewide so cap binds is settled by SBE's own published guidance, not a contested interpretation. EL 4-102(b)(2)(i) confirms new-party 10,000.

fields.reliedValue: Two statutory deadlines: (1) Declaration of Intent not later than the first Monday in July of the general-election year; (2) Certificate of Candidacy AND completed nomination petition by 5 p.m. on the first Monday in August. For President, the slate of presidential electors must be certified to the State Board at least 30 days before the general election. 2024 first Monday in August = Aug 5, 2024 (verified).
fields.note: EL 5-703(c)(3)(i), 5-703(d)(1), 5-703(f)(1), and 8-503(c)(3) all verbatim. SBE 2026 candidacy page corroborates (Declaration July 6, 2026; second deadline Aug 3, 2026). Calendar-verified Aug 5, 2024. Statutory deadlines, current. Minor non-material nuance: SBE notes some Aug-deadline documents need not be filed in person, but the in-person Certificate-of-Candidacy point belongs to captureMode and does not change the dates.

fields.reliedValue: None. The unaffiliated/independent presidential 10,000-signature petition is statewide with no per-congressional-district or per-county distribution quota. The 400-signatures-per-congressional-district rule (EL 8-502) applies only to the principal-party PRIMARY ballot, not the independent general-election route.
fields.note: EL 5-703(e)(1) contains no distribution language (verbatim). EL 8-502(b)(2) verbatim confirms the 400-per-district rule is expressly scoped to placement on the PRIMARY ballot for principal-party presidential candidates. Clear statutory reading; no interpretation needed.

fields.reliedValue: Circulator must be at least 18 years old when any covered signature is affixed and must be the person in whose presence each signature was affixed and who observed each signing (EL 6-204). STRICTEST DEFENSIBLE POSTURE: although neither EL 6-204 nor COMAR 33.06.03.07/.08 textually requires the circulator to be a Maryland registered voter or resident, treat circulators as Maryland registered voters (and require their MD residence address per COMAR 33.06.03.07) to eliminate any residency-challenge risk to the ballot line, pending SBE confirmation. Each SIGNER must be a registered voter assigned to the county on the signature page (EL 6-203(b)(2)).
fields.note: Affirmative claims grounded and correct: EL 6-204(c) 18+ verbatim; 6-204(a) observe-each-signature verbatim; 6-203(b)(2) signer county-registration verbatim. COMAR 33.06.03.07 requires the circulator's residence address; COMAR 33.06.03.08 requires the affidavit to attest signers are registered voters in the State — neither imposes a circulator residency/registration requirement. The draft's NEGATIVE claim (out-of-state/non-registered circulators allowed) is literally accurate but is a heavily litigated point nationally and SBE practice on out-of-state circulators is not settled by retrieved text; under never-risk-ballot-access this requires SBE confirmation, hence HOLD.

fields.reliedValue: Circulator affidavit (NOT notarized). Each signature page must carry an affidavit executed by the circulator who personally observed every signature on that page being affixed; a signature is validated and counted only if attested by that affidavit. No notary is required for petition signature pages. (The Certificate of Candidacy and Financial Disclosure are notarized for state office, but President has no financial-disclosure requirement.)
fields.note: EL 6-203(b)(4) (attested by affidavit on the page), 6-201(c)(6) (space for circulator affidavit), 6-204(b) (affidavit contents by regulation) all verbatim. COMAR 33.06.03.08 confirms a signed-and-dated circulator affidavit, not a notarized one. SBE requirements table shows President = No financial disclosure, supporting the notary aside.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Signatures collected on paper State-Board-prescribed pages (the 'General Election Candidate Nomination Petition' for unaffiliated candidates) and filed with the appropriate board; the Certificate of Candidacy must be filed in person and is not available online. Maryland's online signing system covers only statewide referendum/ballot-question petitions — there is NO online/remote digital signature system for an independent presidential candidate nomination petition.
fields.note: forms/petitions.html verbatim: Certificate of Candidacy filed in person, form not available online; 'Unaffiliated Candidates' lists the General Election Candidate Nomination Petition PDF. EL 5-703 'in the form prescribed by the State Board' verbatim. Online petition forms on the SBE forms page are limited to referendum/charter/new-party/local petitions; no online candidate-petition system exists. Paper-only is current.

fields.reliedValue: No filing fee for President (and Vice President); the SBE filing-fee schedule lists no fee for President, and there is no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option. A candidate seeking nomination by petition may not be charged a fee for filing the declaration of intent (EL 5-703(c)(4)). There is no money alternative to the 10,000-signature requirement.
fields.note: SBE requirements.html Federal Offices table shows President with a blank Filing Fee column (verbatim row matches). EL 5-703(c)(4) verbatim: no fee for the declaration of intent. Maryland has no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures mechanism; the petition is the sole route. Correct and current.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: paid circulation permitted; per-signature structure unaddressed for candidate petitions (no compensation-structure statute, no per-signature ban)
compensationModel.note: Maryland clearly permits PAID circulators on petitions generally (SBE FAQ, per Ficker v. Montgomery County), and circulators need NOT be Maryland residents or registered voters, so a national paid pool can legally work there. However, NO Maryland statute or official guidance specifically addresses the PER-SIGNATURE compensation STRUCTURE for candidate/nominating petitions — the §16-401 "thing of value" prohibition bars paying the SIGNER, not the circulator, and the SBE FAQ confirms paying people to collect signatures is allowed but is silent on per-signature pay. There is no Maryland per-signature ban (Ballotpedia lists MD as "Permitted"), but because per-signature specifically is unaddressed for candidate petitions, hold and confirm before structuring pay that way.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an unaffiliated presidential candidate's nominating petition filed under Election Law §5-703/Title 6, may the campaign compensate petition circulators on a per-signature basis (e.g., $X per valid signature), or must circulator pay be structured as hourly/salary/bonus only?

citations.quote: Can an organization pay people to collect petition signatures? Yes – per U.S. District Court decision – Ficker vs. Montgomery County, but no one may receive any money or other thing of value in return for signing the petition. Election Law, Section 16-401(a)(2).
Must a petition circulator be a registered voter of Maryland? No.  Must a petition circulator be a resident of Maryland? No.
Can an organization pay a consultant to administer and oversee the petition process? Yes.

A person may not willfully and knowingly: (1) give, transfer, promise, or offer anything of value for the purpose of inducing another person to sign or not sign any petition; (2) request, receive, or agree to receive, anything of value as an inducement to sign or not to sign any petition;

(a) Each signature page shall contain an affidavit made and executed by the individual in whose presence all of the signatures on that page were affixed and who observed each of those signatures being affixed. ... (c) A circulator must be at least 18 years old at the time any of the signatures covered by the affidavit are affixed.

A candidate for public office may be nominated by petition if the candidate is not affiliated with any political party ... Petitions for nomination by petition must be filed as required in Title 6 of this article.
Maryland ... Permitted ... Offenses and Penalties Subtitle 4 - Petitions Section 16-401

Ficker v. Montgomery County is a 1985 ruling of a federal court, the United States District Court for the District of Maryland, that found that a provision in the Maryland election law that prohibited paying individuals to collect signatures on a referendum petition violated the United States Constitution.

voterFile.cost: $125.00 per statewide list (or $75.00 each for a single county, district, or municipality list); one application required per list
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Qualified (registered) Maryland voters only, for electoral-process purposes. The State Board of Elections "is authorized to sell, to qualified voters, their voter registration lists." Campaigns, committees, candidates, and petition circulators qualify because circulating a petition and qualifying as a candidate are expressly listed permitted uses.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Restricted to the "electoral process" as defined in COMAR 33.03.02.01B(1). The applicant must sign a statement, under penalty of perjury, that the list will not be used "for commercial solicitation or any other purpose not related to the electoral process." Permitted uses explicitly include registering voters, forming a political party, qualifying as a candidate, circulating a petition, conducting elections/recounts, casting/counting ballots, and financing a campaign. The data may NOT be used "for investigations into illegal or suspected illegal infractions or violations of voters' behaviors in a specific election."
voterFile.requestProcess: Application form (online at data_form.html, or downloadable PDF) + a signed statement under penalty of perjury of permitted use + payment via the Velocity payment site. Data is provided within 10 working days after the application and payment are received, delivered by FTP, mailed flash drive, or in-person flash-drive pickup. Applications must be received on or before the advanced voter registration deadline (21 days before an election) or after election day; applications received after the advanced deadline are returned.
voterFile.note: Maryland sells the statewide voter registration list only to qualified (registered) voters for electoral-process use, for $125 flat per statewide list, requiring an application plus a sworn (penalty-of-perjury) non-commercial-use statement; petition circulation is an expressly permitted use, so a campaign verifying petition signatures qualifies. Delivered within 10 working days via FTP or flash drive; the page does not enumerate fields, but the Maryland statewide list is the standard registration file (name, residential address, party/status) used precisely for signer-to-voter matching.

citations.quote: The State Board of Elections maintains voter registration files for 24 local boards of elections and is authorized to sell, to qualified voters, their voter registration lists (mainly statewide registration lists).

Before we will process your application, you must sign a statement, under penalty of perjury, that you will not use the list for commercial solicitation or any other purpose not related to the electoral process. You can use the data to register voters, form a political party, qualify as a candidate for public office, circulate a petition, conduct elections and recount, cast and count ballots, finance a campaign, and other activities that meet the definition of "electoral process" as defined in COMAR 33.03.02.01B(1). You cannot use this data for investigations into illegal or suspected illegal infractions or violations of voters' behaviors in a specific election.
Cost: $125.00 for each statewide list or $75.00 each for a single county, district, or municipality list.

We will provide the data you requested within 10 working days after we receive the application and payment. We can provide the data via FTP, we can mail you the data (on a flash drive), or you can pick up a flash drive with the requested data.

Applications must be received on or before the advanced voter registration deadline (21 days before an election) or after election day. Applications received after the advanced voter registration deadline will be returned to the applicant.

circulator.ageRule: Md. Election Law §6-204(c) expressly requires: "A circulator must be at least 18 years old at the time any of the signatures covered by the affidavit are affixed." This is the only eligibility qualification the statute imposes on circulators (the affiant/witness who observes and attests to the signatures under §6-204(a)). The strict registered-voter requirement appears separately and binds only th
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a registered voter assigned to the named county (§6-203(b)); CIRCULATOR need only be 18+ (§6-204(c)) — no voter-registration, citizenship, or residency requirement.
circulator.note: Maryland's general petition statute (Election Law Title 6) governs the independent presidential nominating petition (a petition for new party/independent ballot access under Title 4/5 uses this §6-2xx machinery). The circulator is defined functionally in §6-204(a) as "the individual in whose presence all of the signatures on that page were affixed and who observed each of those signatures being affixed" — i.e., the witness who executes the affidavit on each signature page (§6-201(c)(6)). §6-204(

citations.quote: (c) A circulator must be at least 18 years old at the time any of the signatures covered by the affidavit are affixed.

citations.field: signerVsCirculator (circulator = affiant/witness; no voter/citizen/resident qualification stated)
citations.quote: (a) Each signature page shall contain an affidavit made and executed by the individual in whose presence all of the signatures on that page were affixed and who observed each of those signatures being affixed. (b) The affidavit shall contain the statements, required by regulation, designed to assure the validity of the signatures and the fairness of the petition process. (c) A circulator must be at least 18 years old at the time any of the signatures covered by the affidavit are affixed.

citations.field: circulatorMustBeRegisteredVoter / signerVsCirculator (registered-voter requirement binds the SIGNER, not the circulator)
citations.quote: (b) The signature of an individual shall be validated and counted if: ... (2) the individual is a registered voter assigned to the county specified on the signature page and, if applicable, in a particular geographic area of the county; ... (4) the signature is attested by an affidavit appearing on the page on which the signature appears

citations.field: circulator role defined by affidavit on signature page
citations.quote: (c) Each signature page shall contain: ... (6) a space for the required affidavit made and executed by the circulator

signaturesHeadline: 10,000 certified voter signatures, statewide aggregate (fixed by statute, no distribution quota) — AUTO-LIVE
deadlineHeadline: SOS final filing = last Tuesday in August, 5:00 p.m. (solid); local-submission sub-deadline CONFLICTED in draft — HOLD
riskNote: All 7 fields ground verbatim to primary sources; 6 are AUTO-LIVE. Only the deadline field is held: the draft is internally inconsistent (calls Aug 20, 2024 the "28-days-prior submission deadline," but Aug 20 is 7 days before the Aug 27 filing, and Ballotpedia says 14 days) — the statute-vs-guidance conflict on the local-submission window is a legal-interpretation question; strictest read protects ballot access by treating local submission as due by the 28th day prior.
sosInquiries: For an INDEPENDENT (non-party) presidential-elector nomination petition filed under M.G.L. c. 53 sec. 10 (last Tuesday in August of a presidential year), what is the exact deadline to SUBMIT signed nomination papers to local registrars for certification, and the deadline for registrars to COMPLETE certification — is it the sec. 7 'twenty-eighth day prior / seventh day prior' state-office schedule, or the 14-day schedule, and please confirm the 2028 calendar dates?

fields.reliedValue: 10,000 certified voter signatures, statewide aggregate. Fixed by statute (M.G.L. c. 53 sec. 6 lists 'presidential electors, ten thousand'); not a recomputed percentage. An independent ('Unenrolled') presidential candidate qualifies by filing nomination papers for a slate of presidential electors carrying 10,000 certified signatures. Practitioner guidance: collect well above 10,000 because registrars disqualify non-matching signatures; sec. 7 lets registrars certify the required number increased by two-fifths.
fields.note: Verbatim in M.G.L. c. 53 sec. 6 ('signed in the aggregate by not less than the following number of voters: for governor and lieutenant governor, attorney general, United States senator, and presidential electors, ten thousand'). Cross-confirmed by Ballotpedia ('At least 10,000 voters must sign this petition') and its historical table (1992-2012 all 10,000, 'Fixed by statute'). The two-fifths language is verbatim in sec. 7. Adversarial check: legal minimum to QUALIFY is 10,000 certified; the +two-fifths is a registrar certification cap / over-collection mechanic, correctly framed as guidance. Current and correct.

fields.reliedValue: Final filing with the Secretary of the Commonwealth: on or before the LAST TUESDAY IN AUGUST of the presidential-election year, by 5:00 p.m. (M.G.L. c. 53 sec. 10) — this part is solid. STRICTEST local-submission read (over-comply): submit signed nomination papers to local registrars no later than the 28th day before the SOS final filing date, by 5:00 p.m., and ensure local certification is COMPLETE no later than the 7th day before final filing (M.G.L. c. 53 sec. 7 state-office schedule). Treat the earliest applicable date as binding until the SOS confirms which sub-schedule (28-day vs 14-day) governs the presidential-elector petition. Do NOT rely on 'August 20' as a 28-day submission deadline — that date is the 7-day certification mark, not the submission deadline.
fields.note: All citations ground verbatim (sec. 10 'last Tuesday in August'; sec. 10 'before five o'clock'; sec. 7 '28th day... seventh day'; Ballotpedia '14th day preceding'; Ballotpedia table '...8/27/2024'). BUT the draft's value is internally inconsistent: it says local submission is 'at least 28 days before' the SOS filing AND that 'For 2024 the local-submission deadline was August 20, 2024' — arithmetically Aug 20 is exactly 7 days before Aug 27 (the 7-day CERTIFICATION mark), not 28 days (which is July 30). Sources also conflict on the submission window: sec. 7 says 28th day for a 'state office,' while Ballotpedia says the 14th day for the presidential petition. Whether the sec. 7 state-office 28/7 schedule even applies to a presidential-elector paper (filed separately under sec. 10) is a legal-interpretation question retrieval cannot settle. Held; reliedValue set to the strictest (earliest) defensible submission window.

fields.reliedValue: None. The 10,000 requirement is a single statewide aggregate ('signed in the aggregate by not less than ... ten thousand'). No per-congressional-district or per-county minimum. Signatures are collected per-city/town only procedurally (each local board certifies only its own voters; signatures from more than one city or town may not appear on the same sheet), but no geographic distribution quota applies.
fields.note: Verbatim sec. 6 ('in the aggregate') and sec. 7 ('Signatures shall not be certified ... from more than one city or town per sheet'). Independently confirmed by the 2026 SOS Candidates Guide: 'You do not, however, need to gather signatures from every city and town in your district, or from a certain number of communities in your district.' No distribution requirement exists. Correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: Massachusetts imposes no statutory eligibility requirement (age/residency/voter-status) on the CIRCULATOR of a nomination paper, and no circulator-registration or circulator-affidavit. The binding constraints are on the SIGNERS: each must be a MA registered voter, signing in person their own name substantially as registered, with their registered address. For a non-party ('Unenrolled') candidate, signatures may be gathered from ANY registered voter (no party-affiliation restriction). A voter physically unable to write may authorize another to sign in the voter's presence. Conservative practice: still use eligible MA-registered circulators since reliance is on signer validity, not circulator status.
fields.note: Signer rules verbatim in sec. 7 ('Every voter signing a nomination paper shall sign in person as registered ... any voter who is prevented by physical disability ... may authorize some person'). 2026 SOS Guide verbatim: 'If you are running as a non-party candidate, you can gather signatures from any registered voter in your district' and 'Voters should sign their name as they are registered ... Voters must sign their own name.' Adversarial check: MA law is silent on circulators (no affirmative permission, just no restriction); the value correctly states the ABSENCE of a circulator rule and the value direction is safe (the load-bearing constraint, signer validity, is stated correctly), so no ballot-access risk from relying on it.

fields.reliedValue: No notarized circulator affidavit and no circulator certification/oath required. Validity rests on official certification: local boards of registrars certify each signer as a registered voter of their city/town. Separately, a non-party ('Unenrolled') candidate must file with the Secretary a registrar-issued certificate stating the candidate is not enrolled in any political party (M.G.L. c. 53 sec. 6) — this is a certificate ABOUT THE CANDIDATE, not a circulator/witness affidavit on the signature sheets.
fields.note: Verbatim sec. 6 (Unenrolled certificate from registrars) and sec. 7 (registrars 'check each name ... certify ... and only names so checked shall be deemed ... qualified'). 2026 SOS Guide confirms the non-party Voter Registration Certificate is included on the nomination papers, distinct from any witness oath. No circulator oath/notary exists in the MA scheme. Correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Signatures must be collected on official pre-printed paper nomination papers (yellow or beige for non-party/independent candidates) obtained from the Secretary; signers sign in person ('wet') and papers are physically submitted to local registrars for certification, then physically filed with the Secretary (mail accepted only if it physically arrives by the deadline; postmarks do not count). The SOS guidance states the papers cannot be provided electronically. No digital/remote-online pathway exists for an independent presidential petition in Massachusetts.
fields.note: All three quotes verbatim in the 2026 SOS Candidates Guide: 'papers cannot be provided electronically'; 'Nomination papers for party candidates are white in color, while nomination papers for non-party candidates are yellow or beige'; 'the papers must physically arrive at the election office by the deadline. Postmark dates are not enough...'. Consistent with sec. 7 (per-sheet, in-person signing). Paper-only is correct and current. (The MA online-petition / e-signature pathway covers only certain local/initiative contexts, not this petition.)

fields.reliedValue: None. No filing fee for ballot access in Massachusetts and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option; the 10,000 certified signatures are the sole ballot-access qualification. (Separate non-ballot-access obligations exist — FEC statement of candidacy, state OCPF/Ethics filing — but these are not a ballot-access fee and not an alternative to signatures.)
fields.note: Ballotpedia's 2024 independent-candidate filing table grounds verbatim in page HTML: columns 'Filing fee' = N/A and 'Filing fee formula' = N/A for Massachusetts (row: Massachusetts | 10,000 | Fixed by statute | N/A | N/A | 8/27/2024). No fee or fee-in-lieu appears in M.G.L. c. 53. Note: the quoted string was collapsed in the markdown extraction but is fully present in the page HTML, so grounding holds. Correct and current.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: Massachusetts candidate-petition law (M.G.L. c. 53 and the Secretary of the Commonwealth's 2026 Candidate's Guide) regulates only the SIGNERS (must be registered voters in the district) and certification by local officials; it does NOT impose any registration/residency requirement on, or oath from, the circulator, and contains NO provision addressing compensation of circulators — so paying per signature on a candidate/independent-presidential nomination paper is unaddressed, not affirmatively permitted. The Massachusetts pay-per-signature and residency rules discussed in Ballotpedia/NCSL apply to ballot-initiative/referendum petitions only and do not carry over to candidate petitions.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an unenrolled candidate's presidential nomination papers under M.G.L. c. 53, does Massachusetts law or regulation permit or prohibit compensating signature gatherers/circulators on a per-signature basis, and must a circulator be a Massachusetts registered voter or resident?

citations.quote: Signatures must be collected from registered voters and certified by the local election offices in each city and town where you gathered the signatures. ... you will need to submit to the Elections Division at least 10,000 certified signatures

If you are running as a non-party candidate, you can gather signatures from any registered voter in your district. ... Voters must sign their own name. ... Any person can submit your nomination papers to the local election office.

Every voter signing a nomination paper shall sign in person ... The registrars shall check each name on the nomination paper and shall forthwith certify the number of signatures checked which are names of voters both in the city or town and in the district for which the nomination is made

Pay-per-signature is a method of compensating signature gatherers ... who collect signatures for ballot initiatives and veto referendums. ... There are 26 states that provide for an initiative or referendum process. Sixteen (16) of those states allowed campaigns to make payments to signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected. Ten (10) of those states prohibited

voterFile.cost: State voter file: no published flat price — "a fair and reasonable cost not to exceed the cost of printing or preparing computer readable documents" (M.G.L. c.51 §47C). Municipal route (for non-qualifying candidates): each city/town charges only "the cost of producing the record" — in practice a few dollars per local street list/book (e.g., $2–$20 per town).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: The statewide central registry of voters is NOT a public record and is restricted by statute to: state party committees, statewide candidate committees, state ballot question committees, the jury commissioner, the adjutant general, and any other entity the Secretary designates by regulation. The SOS describes this as "only available to statewide campaigns and political parties." Candidates who do not qualify (e.g., district/local candidates) must instead obtain the public municipal voter lists from each city/town election office; those local lists are available to the general public under the Public Records Law.
voterFile.useRestrictions: State voter file is provided only under a licensing agreement that "prohibits them from sharing or selling the information," and the names/addresses "shall not be a matter of public record." The file excludes non-public fields (phone, email, veteran status, permanent-absentee status, confidential voters, minors). Confidential voters (court order / shelter / Address Confidentiality Program) are entirely omitted. The public municipal lists carry the standard MA public-records use norms but are openly inspectable.
voterFile.requestProcess: State voter file: written request to the Secretary of the Commonwealth's Elections Division plus a mandatory signed licensing agreement that prohibits sharing or selling the data (eligibility-gated). Municipal route: written/in-person public-records request to each local election office (town/city clerk or board of registrars) for the public voter list, payable at cost of production.
