voterFile.note: There is no instant statewide download in Massachusetts. The full statewide voter file is statutorily non-public and licensed only to statewide political committees/parties (a statewide candidate committee qualifies and signs a no-share/no-sell license; price = cost of preparation). Every other campaign verifies signers using the public municipal voter lists, which include name + residential address + active/inactive registration status + party + DOB + voter ID — exactly the fields needed to match petition signers — obtained town-by-town at the cost of production.

citations.quote: Under state law, the statewide list of registered voters is not a public record. This state voter file is only available to statewide campaigns and political parties. Any statewide campaign seeking access to the voter file must sign a licensing agreement, which prohibits them from sharing or selling the information.

The public voter list includes, for each voter: Name; Address; Party; Date of Birth; Active/Inactive status; Voter ID number ... The voter list can be requested as a paper voter list or an electronic voter file. The Public Records Law allows the election office to charge only for the cost of producing the record.
Candidates who do not qualify to receive the state voter file may request municipal voter files from the individual cities and towns in their district.

The names and addresses contained in said central registry shall not be a matter of public record; provided however, that they shall be available to state party committees, statewide candidate committees, state ballot question committees, the jury commissioner, adjutant general and any other individual, agency or entity that the state secretary shall designate by regulation consistent with the purposes of this section, at a fair and reasonable cost not to exceed the cost of printing or preparing computer readable documents.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — the rule binds signers only. Mass. Gen. Laws ch. 53, §§ 5-8 (the entire nomination-paper scheme for independent/unenrolled candidates, including presidential electors) imposes NO age, residency, citizenship, or registration requirement on the person who carries the petition and collects signatures. The only person-eligibility requirement in the scheme falls on the S
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a registered Massachusetts voter who signs in person "as registered or substantially as registered" (MGL c. 53 § 7) and signatures must be from voters in the relevant district (§ 6, 10,000 for presidential electors); the CIRCULATOR has NO statutory qualifications — no affidavit, no age/residency/citizenship/registration requirement. Signature verification is done by local registrars
circulator.note: Independent presidential candidates in MA petition for the general-election ballot via nomination papers requiring 10,000 voter signatures (signatures are for the slate of presidential electors). Primary-source review of the full nomination-paper statute (MGL c. 53 §§ 5, 6, 7, 8) shows all person-eligibility requirements bind the SIGNER (must be a registered voter signing as registered, § 7), while NO requirement of any kind is placed on the circulator. Unlike most states, Massachusetts nominati

citations.quote: Every voter signing a nomination paper shall sign in person as registered or substantially as registered, and shall state the address where he or she is currently registered, but any voter who is prevented by physical disability from writing may authorize some person to write his or her name and residence in his or her presence.

citations.field: signature requirement binds signer (10,000 for presidential electors)
citations.quote: Nominations of candidates for any offices to be filled at a state election may be made by nomination papers, stating the facts required by section eight and signed in the aggregate by not less than the following number of voters: for governor and lieutenant governor, attorney general, United States senator, and presidential electors, ten thousand

citations.field: circulatorAgeRule / no circulator qualifications in contents statute
citations.quote: This information, in addition to the district name or number, if any, shall be specified on the nomination paper before any signature of a purported registered voter is obtained and the circulation of nomination papers without such information is prohibited.

citations.field: no sworn circulator/witness required for nomination papers (only for caucus/convention certificates)
citations.quote: Every certificate of nomination shall state such facts as are required by section eight and shall be signed and sworn to by the presiding officer and by the secretary of the caucus or convention, who shall add to their signatures their residences.
The relevant statutes do not stipulate specific circulator requirements (such as residency or pay status).

citations.field: independent presidential 10,000-signature petition for general election
citations.quote: An independent presidential candidate must petition for placement on the general election ballot. At least 10,000 voters must sign this petition.

signaturesHeadline: 1,000 signatures (flat statutory floor) + 6-elector slate + $2,500 fee — all three required, AUTO-LIVE
deadlineHeadline: 5:00 p.m., 60 days before general election (actual-receipt); 2024 = Sept 6 — AUTO-LIVE
riskNote: 6 of 7 fields ground cleanly and triangulate across statute + SOS guide + Ballotpedia. Only circulatorEligibility is held (proving-a-negative on unwritten practice). No field is BLOCKED; ballot-access risk is low if signatures are clerk-certified well before deadline.
sosInquiries: For an independent presidential nominating petition under Miss. Code Ann. 23-15-785, are there any requirements (residency, age, MS voter registration) governing WHO may circulate/collect the petition signatures, or any written or unwritten administrative practice limiting circulators? (No circulator affidavit appears on the form; please confirm.)

fields.reliedValue: 1,000 valid signatures — a flat statutory minimum (written nominating petition signed by not less than 1,000 qualified/registered Mississippi voters), NOT a percentage; same 1,000 applies to a minor party qualifying by petition. Must be accompanied by a slate of 6 presidential electors, each signing a consent/pledge statement.
fields.note: Both citation quotes ground VERBATIM: FindLaw 23-15-785(1) ('...written petition signed by at least one thousand (1,000) qualified voters of this state.') and SOS forms page ('...signatures of not less than 1,000 qualified electors of this state, must also be accompanied by a list of Presidential electors...'). Triangulated by Ballotpedia ('At least 1,000 qualified state voters must sign the petition') and the official 2026 SOS Candidate Qualifying Guide, which lists for the independent presidential candidate: petition of not less than 1,000 + 6-elector slate + $2,500 fee. 23-15-785(3) confirms a list equal to the number of electors (6 for MS) each executing the consent statement. 'at least'=floor, so the draft's 'flat fixed number, not a recompute' framing is correct.

fields.reliedValue: Petition + 6-elector slate + $2,500 fee must be filed with the State Board of Election Commissioners (in the Office of the Secretary of State) by 5:00 p.m. not less than sixty (60) days before the general election. Recomputes each cycle off the November general-election date (2024 example: Sept 6, 2024). If filing by mail, it is an actual-receipt deadline, not postmark.
fields.note: FindLaw 23-15-785(2) grounds VERBATIM ('...by 5:00 p.m. not less than sixty (60) days previous to the day of the election.'). 60-day rule corroborated by Ballotpedia ('filed with the secretary of state by 5:00 p.m. 60 days prior to the general election'). Computation verified: Nov 5, 2024 minus 60 days = Fri Sept 6, 2024 (exactly 60 days). Exact Sept 6, 2024 date independently confirmed by news source (gwcommonwealth.com): 'independent and party presidential candidates have to submit all required paperwork and fees by Sept. 6, 2024.' Actual-receipt-not-postmark confirmed verbatim in the 2026 SOS Candidate Qualifying Guide. NOTE: the 2026 guide is a midterm guide (no presidential office in 2026, gen. election Nov 3, 2026); the specific calendar date must be recomputed for 2028 (Nov 7, 2028 -> ~Sept 8, 2028). Rule itself is current and correct; clear statutory computation, not a contestable legal interpretation.

fields.reliedValue: None. For an independent (or minor-party-by-petition) presidential candidate the 1,000 signatures may be gathered from qualified voters of the State at large; no per-congressional-district or per-county distribution requirement. The 100-per-district / 500-statewide alternative belongs ONLY to the presidential PRIMARY petition path (Miss. Code 23-15-1093), which does not apply to the independent general-election petition.
fields.note: Quote 'written petition signed by at least one thousand (1,000) qualified voters of this state' grounds VERBATIM in 23-15-785. Independently confirmed: 23-15-359(1)(a) sets 'For an office elected by the state at large, not less than one thousand (1,000) qualified electors' with NO geographic breakdown. The 2026 SOS guide shows the 100-per-Congressional-district / 500-statewide alternative only under the Presidential Preference PRIMARY (party-candidate) section, and the independent general-election section reads simply '1,000 ... of the State of Mississippi.' 23-15-1093 governs the primary petition (filed Jan 1-15), confirming the draft's distinction. No district distribution for the independent path.

fields.reliedValue: STRICT/over-comply default until SOS confirms: treat circulators as potentially required to be Mississippi-qualified electors (registered voters) and residents. The only person-level requirement that retrieval can confirm runs to the SIGNERS (each must be a qualified elector of MS, verified via circuit-clerk certification of registration); no circulator age/residency/voter-status statute or affidavit was found, but absence of an UNWRITTEN administrative practice cannot be proven by retrieval.
fields.note: Signer-requirement quote grounds VERBATIM on SOS page ('Be a qualified elector (registered voter) of the State of Mississippi and of the district...'). Adversarial point: the draft VALUE itself flags 'UNKNOWN whether any unwritten administrative practice limits who may circulate.' Confirming a true negative (no circulator rule anywhere, including unwritten SOS practice) is beyond retrieval and is the kind of legal-interpretation question the gate reserves for hold. Structurally, MS has no circulator affidavit and signatures are clerk-certified against the signer's own registration, so circulator identity is largely irrelevant in practice — but under the never-risk-ballot-access mandate I hold and route to the SOS. Added an SOS inquiry.

fields.reliedValue: No notary and no circulator affidavit. Each petition's signatures must be CERTIFIED by the circuit clerk of the county where the signatures were collected, BEFORE the petition is filed with the Secretary of State; obtain clerk certification well in advance of the qualifying deadline. 'Header' information must be completed on each petition page BEFORE signatures are gathered.
fields.note: Both quotes ground VERBATIM. SOS forms page: 'All signatures on petitions for independent or special election candidates MUST be certified by the signer's circuit or municipal clerk respectively before filing with the appropriate office.' 2026 SOS Candidate Qualifying Guide (extracted via pdftotext): 'a. All petition signatures must be certified by each Circuit Clerk's Office of the county from where the signatures were collected, and b. All "header" information must be completed on each petition page prior to obtaining voters' signatures.' Critically, the guide lists these two items expressly under 'As an Independent Candidate for the General Election' for U.S. President, so the requirement is confirmed for the independent presidential petition. No notary requirement appears anywhere.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Wet-ink paper petitions gathered by hand and certified by county circuit clerks; SOS provides only downloadable paper petition forms (President Candidate_Independent Candidate_Petition) that must be printed, signed, and clerk-certified. No digital/online filing exists for the independent presidential petition.
fields.note: Quote grounds VERBATIM: FindLaw 23-15-359(11) 'The petition required by this section may not be filed by using the Internet.' Caveat on citation SCOPE: that internet-ban lives in 23-15-359, whose petition language is mirrored by 23-15-785 for the presidential filing; arguably 'this section' refers to 23-15-359's petition, so the ban is not the tightest possible citation for the 23-15-785 presidential petition specifically. BUT the paper conclusion is independently dispositive regardless of that scope question: signatures must be wet-ink and county-clerk-certified (incompatible with online filing), and the SOS publishes only printable paper forms (verified on SOS forms page). Bottom line 'paper' is correct and not interpretation-dependent.

fields.reliedValue: No fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option exists. A mandatory $2,500.00 qualifying assessment for the Office of President is payable to the MS Secretary of State and is required IN ADDITION to (not instead of) the 1,000-signature petition and the 6-elector slate. Same $2,500 presidential assessment applies to a party nominee. Fee revenue is deposited into the Elections Support Fund.
fields.note: Both quotes ground VERBATIM: FindLaw 23-15-359(1)(h) '(h) For the Office of President of the United States, a party nominee or independent candidate shall pay an assessment in the amount of Two Thousand Five Hundred Dollars ($2,500.00).' and SOS forms page ('...submitted with a qualifying fee of $2,500 to the Secretary of State's Office.'). 23-15-359(5) confirms deposit into the Elections Support Fund (23-15-5). The 2026 SOS guide lists, for the independent presidential candidate, BOTH a 1,000-signature petition AND a $2,500 fee AND the 6-elector slate as separate required items — confirming the fee is additive, not an alternative. Field is mislabeled 'feeAlternative' but the substance (no alternative; fee is mandatory and additional) is correct.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: Mississippi's candidate/election-code petition statutes (Miss. Code 23-15-785 for independent presidential petitions and 23-15-359 generally) do NOT address compensation of candidate-petition circulators at all — neither per-signature pay, paid circulation in general, nor circulator residency/registration are mentioned. The frequently cited "Mississippi permits pay-per-signature" rule (and Term Limits Leadership Council v. Clark, 1997) comes only from the INITIATIVE/ballot-measure code (Title 23, Chapter 17, Sec. 57), a separate chapter that does not govern candidate petitions, so it must not be carried over. Treat candidate-petition compensation as unaddressed and hold conservatively.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an INDEPENDENT presidential candidate's nominating petition under Miss. Code Ann. 23-15-785, does Mississippi law permit a campaign to pay petition circulators on a per-signature basis (and may circulators be paid at all, e.g., hourly), and must a circulator be a Mississippi resident or registered voter?

citations.quote: the Secretary of State of Mississippi shall certify to the circuit clerks of the several counties the names of all candidates for President and Vice President who are nominated by any national convention or other like assembly of any political party or by written petition signed by at least one thousand (1,000) qualified voters of this state. ... Any nominating petition, to be valid, must contain the signatures as well as the addresses of the petitioners.

A petition requesting that an independent or special election candidate's name be placed on the ballot for any office shall be filed as provided for in subsection (3) or (4) of this section, as appropriate, and shall be signed by not less than the following number of qualified electors ... (h) For the Office of President of the United States, a party nominee or independent candidate shall pay an assessment in the amount of Two Thousand Five Hundred Dollars ($2,500.00).
The relevant statutes do not specify residency requirements or other regulations for petition circulators.
Mississippi | Permitted | Mississippi Code, Title 23, Chapter 17, Section 57

voterFile.cost: Free. The full statewide voter file and statewide voter history file are distributed weekly "at no cost" to anyone who signs up. (The alternative one-off public-records-request path yields the same file; the SOS does not publish a separate fee for it on this page.)
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Open to anyone who signs up for the Statewide Voter File Distribution List — no candidate/committee/party eligibility gate is stated. The SOS confirms it is "the same type of file provided when a statewide voter file is requested through a public records request," i.e., it is a public record available to the general public. Voters' SSNs, birthdates, and other private info are excluded by Mississippi law and are not released.
voterFile.useRestrictions: No commercial-use prohibition, anti-solicitation clause, political-use-only limit, or penalty is stated in the distribution-program document or the SOS Elections page. The only stated limit is on the SOS's side: it "will not resend or forward any emails of the weekly distribution list," and private fields (SSN, DOB) are statutorily withheld. (Use restrictions in Miss. Code §23-15-165 governing the Statewide Elections Management System could not be retrieved verbatim — legal mirrors were blocked — so the absence of restrictions is based on the primary SOS sources only.)
voterFile.requestProcess: Self-service sign-up to a recurring distribution list, then automated weekly delivery. Enroll via the "Statewide Voter File Distribution List" Smartsheet form (linked from the SOS "Request Voter File" PDF: https://app.smartsheet.com/b/form/5c8b666a679c4c148eb513c8981a4962). Enrollees then "receive an email each week with a Sharefile link" to download the file. No application approval, affidavit, or notarized sworn statement is described for the weekly program; any request outside the weekly distribution must go through the formal public records request process.
voterFile.note: Mississippi gives away the entire statewide voter file (active + inactive voters) for free via a weekly CSV distribution program — sign up once on a Smartsheet form and receive a Sharefile download link each week; this is the easiest free-tier state for signature verification. The registration file includes First Name, Middle Name, Suffix, full Residential Address, City/State/Zip, County, Precinct, and Voter Status — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters (note: the document's literal header list for the registration file omits "Last Name," which does appear in the companion voting-history file, so a last-name field may be present in practice but is not explicitly listed). Free weekly distribution: enroll via the Smartsheet form, then receive a weekly ShareFile download link by email (not a one-click instant download, but free and self-service).

citations.quote: The Mississippi Secretary of State's Office will provide a statewide voter file and statewide voter history file on a weekly basis to those who sign-up to receive such a file at Statewide Voter File Distribution List at no cost. Those who sign-up will receive an email each week with a Sharefile link to access a statewide voter file and statewide voter history file. This will be the same type of file provided when a statewide voter file is requested through a public records request and will include active and inactive voters.

The statewide voter file, which will just contain voter registration information, will provide the following information: Mapping Value, First Name, Middle Name, Suffix, Residential Address, House Number, Street Name, Street Type, Pre-Direction, Post-Direction, Residence City, Residence State, Residential Zip Code, Residential County, Precinct Code, Precinct Name, Mailing Address, Mailing City, Mailing State, Mailing Zip Code, Effective Voter Registration Date, Voter Status, and Date Last Voted.

The Mississippi Secretary of State's Office will not resend or forward any emails of the weekly distribution list for any reason and any request outside of the weekly distribution will be required to go through the public records request process.

Voters' social security numbers, birthdates, and other private information, however, is not available to the public under Mississippi law. Please be on notice the Agency will not release this information in response to a public records request from any entity or individual.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — the statute is silent on circulators entirely. Miss. Code § 23-15-785 (presidential nominating petitions) and § 23-15-359 (general independent-candidate petitions) impose requirements only on SIGNERS ("qualified voters/qualified electors of this state"). Neither statute mentions who may carry the petition or collect signatures, imposes a circulator affidavit/witness
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNERS must be "qualified voters of this state" (§ 23-15-785) / "qualified electors" (§ 23-15-359) — i.e., registered Mississippi voters. The CIRCULATOR (collector) has NO statutory eligibility requirements at all: the Mississippi candidate-petition statutes do not require a circulator affidavit, witness, notarization, or any age/citizenship/residency/registration of the person gathering signatur
circulator.note: Mississippi's controlling statutes for an independent presidential nominating petition — Miss. Code § 23-15-785 (the presidential-specific provision) and § 23-15-359 (the general independent-candidate petition provision) — are entirely SILENT on the circulator. Both impose person-eligibility only on SIGNERS: the petition must be "signed by at least one thousand (1,000) qualified voters of this state" (§ 23-15-785(1)) / "signed by not less than ... one thousand (1,000) qualified electors" for a s

citations.quote: When presidential electors are to be chosen, the Secretary of State of Mississippi shall certify to the circuit clerks of the several counties the names of all candidates for President and Vice President who are nominated by any national convention or other like assembly of any political party or by written petition signed by at least one thousand (1,000) qualified voters of this state.

Any nominating petition, to be valid, must contain the signatures as well as the addresses of the petitioners. The certificates and petitions must be filed with the State Board of Election Commissioners by filing them in the Office of the Secretary of State by 5:00 p.m. not less than sixty (60) days previous to the day of the election.

A petition requesting that an independent or special election candidate's name be placed on the ballot for any office shall be filed as provided for in subsection (3) or (4) of this section, as appropriate, and shall be signed by not less than the following number of qualified electors: (a) For an office elected by the state at large, not less than one thousand (1,000) qualified electors.
The petition required by this section may not be filed by using the Internet.

citations.field: signer requirement (corroborating, no circulator rule noted)
citations.quote: An independent candidate must petition for placement on the general election ballot. At least 1,000 qualified state voters must sign the petition, which must be filed with the secretary of state by 5:00 p.m. 60 days prior to the general election. The petition must include the names of candidates for president, vice president, and presidential electors.

signaturesHeadline: 2,500 valid signatures (flat statutory) — count grounded; signer-eligibility scope unresolved (HOLD)
deadlineHeadline: Petition due Aug 1 of the presidential general-election year (LIVE)
riskNote: Core figures (2,500 / Aug 1 / no distribution / no fee / paper / notarized affidavit) are grounded in current statute and consensus-confirmed; the one live risk is signer eligibility — Ballotpedia says signers must not have voted in any party primary, a condition absent from the current grounded statutory petition form (32-620.01), so confirm with the SOS before collecting.
sosInquiries: For an independent/nonpartisan presidential nominating petition under Neb. Rev. Stat. 32-620(3), is a person eligible to sign if they voted in a 2024 party primary, or must signers be registered voters who did NOT vote in any party's primary? (The 32-620.01 form recites only registered-voter qualification; Ballotpedia states a no-primary-vote restriction.)

fields.reliedValue: 2,500 valid signatures (flat statutory number under Neb. Rev. Stat. 32-620(3)(b), not a percentage). STRICT signer-eligibility reading until SOS confirms: each signer must be a registered, qualified Nebraska voter AND (per Ballotpedia's gloss) must not have voted in any party's primary that year. Collect only from voters meeting the stricter condition and over-collect to a healthy cushion.
fields.note: GROUNDED: 32-620(3)(b) quote 'A petition signed by not less than two thousand five hundred registered voters' verified verbatim on FindLaw (current as of 2024-01-01); 32-620.01 signer-qualification quote verified verbatim. CONSENSUS: Ballotpedia independently confirms 2,500 registered voters for the independent general-election path. ADVERSARIAL: Ballotpedia adds that signers must be voters 'who did not vote in the primary election of any party' — a restriction ABSENT from the grounded current statutory petition form (32-620.01), which recites only registered/qualified-voter status. Could not reach the official Nebraska Legislature site (Cronet failures) or Justia (CAPTCHA) for a third primary-source confirmation, so the signer-eligibility scope is unresolved. The headline count (2,500) and type (flat statutory, not recomputed) are solid; the signer-eligibility discrepancy forces a strict HOLD on collection criteria.

fields.reliedValue: August 1 in the year of the presidential general election (for 2024 this was Thursday, August 1, 2024; no weekend/holiday adjustment). The completed application + petition must be filed with the Secretary of State by that date.
fields.note: GROUNDED: 32-620 quote 'shall be filed with the Secretary of State by August 1 in the year of the presidential general election' verified verbatim; the 60-day decline-affidavit quote (32-620(4)) also verified verbatim. CONSENSUS: Ballotpedia independently states the petition 'must be filed with the secretary of state by August 1 of the election year.' No statutory ambiguity; the date is fixed and current. Write-in path (second Friday before the election) is correctly flagged as a separate, non-applicable deadline.

fields.reliedValue: None. The 2,500-signature requirement is statewide with no per-congressional-district or per-county distribution/quota for the independent (nonpartisan) presidential petition; the county field on the form is identification only.
fields.note: GROUNDED: 32-620(3)(b) and the 32-620.01 form county-identification language verified verbatim — neither imposes a distribution quota. CONSENSUS/ADVERSARIAL: Ballotpedia confirms the 100-signatures-per-congressional-district rule applies to the PRIMARY-ballot petition only (party candidates), not the independent general-election petition, exactly as the draft distinguishes. No distribution requirement exists for the independent path.

fields.reliedValue: Circulator must be at least 18 years of age. The statute imposes no Nebraska-residency or registered-voter requirement on the circulator; the circulator must personally witness each signature and disclose paid vs. volunteer status. (Registered-voter/residency requirements apply to signers, not the circulator.)
fields.note: GROUNDED: 32-628(3) affidavit quote 'he or she is at least eighteen years of age, that each person whose name appears on the petition personally signed the petition in the presence of the affiant' and the 32-628(4) paid/volunteer-circulator statements verified verbatim. The grounded statutory affidavit recites ONLY the 18-years-of-age qualification for the circulator — no residency or registration clause — so the draft's value is a faithful, non-interpretive reading of the current primary text.

fields.reliedValue: NOTARIZED circulator affidavit required on every sheet bearing signatures (below the signatures), plus the boldface 'WARNING TO PETITION SIGNERS' statement above the signatures. The affidavit must be subscribed and sworn before a notary public.
fields.note: GROUNDED: 32-628(3) quotes verified verbatim — 'Every sheet of a petition which contains signatures shall have upon it, below the signatures, an affidavit' and 'Subscribed and sworn to before me, a notary public, this ______ day of _________ 20____ at_____________, Nebraska. Notary Public ___________________________'. The boldface WARNING is in 32-628(2). Notarization is unambiguous and current; a witness-only attestation is NOT sufficient.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Wet-signature paper process only: statutory sheet layout (>=2.5-inch signature space, <=20 signatures/page, lines >=1/4 inch apart), in-person circulator witnessing each signature, and a notarized per-sheet circulator affidavit. No online/e-signature system covers the Nebraska independent presidential petition; completed petition is returned to the SOS Elections Division by mail or in person.
fields.note: GROUNDED: 32-628 quotes 'Petitions shall provide for no more than twenty signatures per page' and the in-person witnessing language verified verbatim; SOS candidate page quote 'Return the completed candidate filing forms and fees to the following address: Secretary of State Attn: Elections Division PO Box 94608 Lincoln, NE 68509-4608' verified verbatim. The statutory requirements (physical layout, in-person witnessing, notarized affidavit) are incompatible with a remote-online flow, supporting 'paper' as current and correct.

fields.reliedValue: None. No filing fee and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option for the independent (nonpartisan) presidential petition; the 2,500-signature petition is the sole access mechanism under 32-620(3).
fields.note: GROUNDED: 32-608(2)(a) fee-bearing-office enumeration verified verbatim — it lists U.S. Senator, state officers, Representatives in Congress, county and city/village officers, etc., and does NOT include President/Vice President; 32-620(3) signature-only requirement verified verbatim. CORROBORATION (32-621, fetched): when a new party forms after the primary, 'all candidates except candidates for President or Vice President of the United States shall pay the filing fee as provided in section 32-608' — independently confirming President/VP are fee-exempt. No fee may be substituted for signatures.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found; paid circulators permitted, residency not required, per-signature pay only addressed in the initiative context
compensationModel.note: For an independent/nonpartisan presidential nominating petition, Nebraska's candidate-petition statutes (Neb. Rev. Stat. §§32-628, 32-629, 32-630, applied via §32-617/§32-620.01) clearly PERMIT paid circulators (§32-628(4) requires a red-print "paid circulator" disclosure) and impose NO residency/registered-voter requirement on circulators (only age 18+ under §32-629(2)); however, those candidate-petition statutes do NOT address per-signature compensation of circulators at all — the only Nebraska authority on per-signature pay (the 2008 ban and its 2015 repeal by LB 367, plus the struck-down "hired and salaried" ban in former §§32-702–32-713) arises in the ballot-initiative context and does not by its terms govern candidate petitions. Per-signature pay for candidate-petition circulators is therefore unaddressed; the §32-630(3)(e)-(f) prohibitions cover paying SIGNERS, not circulators.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent/nonpartisan candidate's nominating petition for President (Neb. Rev. Stat. §§32-617, 32-620.01, 32-628 to 32-630), may a campaign lawfully compensate petition circulators on a per-signature basis, and are there any restrictions on how circulators of candidate petitions may be paid or any residency requirement for them?

citations.quote: Each sheet of a petition shall have upon its face and in plain view of persons who sign the petition a statement in letters not smaller than sixteen-point type in red print on the petition. If the petition is circulated by a paid circulator, the statement shall be as follows: This petition is circulated by a paid circulator. If the petition is circulated by a circulator who is not being paid, the statement shall be as follows: This petition is circulated by a volunteer circulator.

Any person who falsely swears to a circulator's affidavit on a petition, who accepts money or other things of value for signing a petition, or who offers money or other things of value in exchange for a signature upon any petition shall be guilty of a Class IV felony.
Only a person who is at least eighteen years of age shall qualify as a valid circulator of a petition and may circulate petitions. Petition circulators shall comply with section 32-630.
No person shall: ... (e) Accept money or other thing of value for signing any petition; or (f) Offer money or other thing of value in exchange for a signature upon any petition.
To get on the general election ballot, an independent presidential candidate must file with the secretary of state a petition containing the signatures of at least 2,500 registered voters.

On April 7, 2015, Nebraska's unicameral legislature unanimously approved Legislative Bill 367, eliminating the state's seven-year-old ban on paying signature petition circulators based on the number of signatures collected.

voterFile.cost: $500 flat for the statewide Nebraska Voter Registration file (annual affidavit + fee); once paid and the affidavit is approved, you may request updated files for one full year. Payable by cash or check/money order to the Secretary of State. (A new file is generated the first day of each month.)
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Any requester who completes and signs the affidavit (Public Service Request Form) attesting to a permitted use. Not open-access/instant download to the general public — the file is gated behind a sworn permitted-use affidavit and the $500 fee, and use is limited to elections, political activities, voter registration, law enforcement, and jury selection.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Permitted uses only: elections, political activities, voter registration, law enforcement, jury selection. The file "shall not be posted, displayed, or used for commercial purposes or made accessible on the Internet." Recipient must disclose any security breach of the stored data to the Secretary of State without delay. Verifying petition signatures (matching signers to registered voters) is a permitted election/political-activity use.
voterFile.requestProcess: Application/affidavit + approval + payment. Complete the "Public Service Request Form for the Nebraska Central Voter Registration System" (voter-file-affidavit.pdf), submit it to the Elections Division with the $500 fee; upon payment and approval of the affidavit you may request the file (refreshed monthly) for one year. Submit to: Nebraska Secretary of State Elections Division, sos.elect@nebraska.gov / 402-471-2555 / P.O. Box 94608, Lincoln, NE 68509-4608.
voterFile.note: Nebraska sells the full statewide voter registration file for a flat $500 via an annual sworn-affidavit process through the Secretary of State Elections Division; it is not a free or instant public download. The standard file includes Name, Residential address, Mailing address, Registration status, Voter ID, party affiliation, and voter history — covering everything needed to verify petition signers (name + residential address + registration status).

citations.quote: The Nebraska Voter Registration file can be requested from our office by completing the Public Service Request Form for the Nebraska Central Voter Registration System.
The cost of the file is $500 and can be purchased with cash or a check/money order made out to the Secretary of State.

It is our practice to request a new voter file affidavit and fee annually. Upon payment of the fee and approval of the voter file affidavit, you may request an updated file at any time for one full year. We run a new file on the first day of each month.

The file shall contain all relevant registrant information limited to: Name, Residential address, Mailing address, Phone number, Registration status, Voter ID, Birth year, Date of voter registration, Precinct, Polling place, Party affiliation, District details, Voter history
The file shall be used solely for the purposes related to: Elections, Political activities, Voter registration, Law enforcement, Jury selection
The file shall not be posted, displayed, or used for commercial purposes or made accessible on the Internet.

Any person who acquires a list of registered voters shall, following discovery or notification of a breach in the security of the storage of the information, disclose the breach in security to the Secretary of State without delay.

circulator.ageRule: Circulators must be at least eighteen (18) years of age. This is set affirmatively by the statutory circulator's affidavit in Neb. Rev. Stat. 32-628(3), which the circulator must swear to: the circulator "deposes and says... that he or she is at least eighteen years of age." The 2026 NE Secretary of State Candidate Filing Guide restates it as a hard rule: "Circulators must be at least eighteen yea
circulator.signerVsCirculator: Registered-voter requirement binds the SIGNER only: Neb. Rev. Stat. 32-630(3)(c) prohibits a person from signing "if he or she is not a registered voter and qualified to sign," and the SoS guide says "The signer must be registered to vote on or before the date the petition is filed." The CIRCULATOR's only statutory eligibility requirement is age 18+ (plus duties: personally witness each signature,
circulator.note: For an independent presidential petition, the petition must contain 2,500 signatures of registered voters who did not vote in any party primary (Neb. Rev. Stat. 32-624/Ballotpedia), filed with the Secretary of State by Aug. 1 (general-petition rules in Ch. 32, Art. 6 apply). The ONLY affirmative eligibility requirement Nebraska places on the CIRCULATOR is being at least 18 years old (Neb. Rev. Stat. 32-628(3), restated in the SoS guide). The circulator must also (i) personally witness every sign

citations.quote: (2) Each circulator of a petition shall personally witness the signatures on the petition and shall sign the circulator's affidavit. (3) No person shall: ... (c) Sign a petition if he or she is not a registered voter and qualified to sign the same except as provided in subdivision (1) of section 32-629

________________, (name of circulator) being first duly sworn, deposes and says that he or she is the circulator of this petition containing ____ signatures, that he or she is at least eighteen years of age, that each person whose name appears on the petition personally signed the petition in the presence of the affiant ... and that the affiant stated to each signer the object of the petition as printed on the petition before he or she affixed his or her signature to the petition. ... Subscribed and sworn to before me, a notary public

(4) Each sheet of a petition shall have upon its face ... If the petition is circulated by a paid circulator, the statement shall be as follows: This petition is circulated by a paid circulator. If the petition is circulated by a circulator who is not being paid, the statement shall be as follows: This petition is circulated by a volunteer circulator.

Rules for circulators of petition forms are as follows: 1. Circulators must be at least eighteen years of age. 2. Circulators must witness each signature added to the petition they are circulating... they also must sign an oath attesting to the fact that they did this. ... 5. When the circulator is finished collecting signatures on the petition form, they must sign the oath on the form in the presence of a notary public. ... Rules for signers of petition forms are as follows: 1. The signer must be registered to vote on or before the date the petition is filed with the Secretary of State's office.

An independent presidential candidate must petition for access to the general election ballot. The petition must be filed with the secretary of state by August 1 of the election year and must contain the signatures of 2,500 registered voters who did not vote in the primary election of any party.

signaturesHeadline: NV indep pres: 1% of last general's U.S. House vote total (statutory formula); exact 2024 hard number NOT confirmed from primary source — held UNKNOWN
deadlineHeadline: NV indep pres petition + declaration due 5 p.m. 2nd Friday in Aug (2024: Aug 9); county-clerk submission due 25 working days prior; copy filed with SOS before circulating
riskNote: All 8 fields citation-grounded (4 source URLs verbatim-confirmed). Core deadline/fee/distribution/capture facts AUTO-LIVE off NRS 298.109. Two CONSERVATIVE-HOLDs: signaturesRequired (no confirmed 2024 hard number — must compute from 2022 NV U.S. House total and confirm with SOS before relying) and witnessOrNotary (statute says "affidavit"; "before a notary public" is from a general-petition secondary source — treat notarization as required). Beware: NRS 298.109 ("independent" path) vs NRS 293.1715 ("minor/new party" path) are DIFFERENT regimes with different deadlines (Aug vs 3rd Fri June) — do not conflate.
sosInquiries: For an independent presidential candidate filing under NRS 298.109 in 2024, what is the exact number of valid registered-voter signatures required (i.e., 1% of the total votes cast for the offices of Representative in Congress at the 2022 general election), and what is the official certified vote-total figure on which that 1% is based?

Under NRS 298.109(2), must the circulator's affidavit on each petition document be sworn before a notary public specifically, or may it be executed before any officer authorized to administer oaths — and does the Secretary of State's office require a notary acknowledgment block on the presidential petition of candidacy form?

For an independent presidential candidate under NRS 298.109, please confirm the exact 2024 statutory deadlines: (a) the 5 p.m. second-Friday-in-August filing deadline date, and (b) the 25-working-days-before deadline for submitting circulated documents to each county clerk for signature verification.

fields.reliedValue: Signatures of registered voters equal to not less than 1% of the total number of votes cast at the last preceding general election for the offices of Representative in Congress (NRS 298.109(1)). The exact 2024-cycle hard number is treated as UNKNOWN and MUST be computed from the certified 2022 Nevada U.S. House vote total and confirmed with the Secretary of State before relying.
fields.note: GROUND: Both quotes verbatim-confirmed — NRS 298.109 quote present word-for-word on nevada.public.law/statutes/nrs_298.109; NRS 293.1715(2)(c) quote present word-for-word on nevada.public.law/statutes/nrs_293.1715. JUDGE: The 1% statutory formula is CURRENT and CORRECT for an independent presidential candidate. The draft correctly declines to assert an unverified 2024 hard number and sets it to UNKNOWN — the appropriate conservative move (I could not confirm any specific 2024 count from a primary source; Ballotpedia's 2024 table rendered empty). HOLD (not AUTO-LIVE) solely because the operationally usable value (the actual signature count) is unresolved; the formula itself is sound. Note the draft also correctly distinguishes the 1% basis is shared with the NRS 293.1715(2)(c) minor-party path.

fields.reliedValue: Independent presidential path (NRS 298.109): declaration of candidacy + petition of candidacy filed with the Secretary of State no later than 5 p.m. on the second Friday in August of the presidential-election year (2024: Aug 9, 2024). A copy of the petition must be filed with the SOS before it may be circulated. Documents circulated in a county must be submitted to that county clerk for signature verification no later than 25 working days before the August filing deadline. Minor/new-party path (NRS 293.1715(2)(c)) differs: petition due to SOS no later than the third Friday in June.
fields.note: GROUND: All three quotes verbatim-confirmed on their cited pages (the 5 p.m. second-Friday-in-August quote and the 25-working-days quote on nrs_298.109; the third-Friday-in-June quote on nrs_293.1715). JUDGE: Independently corroborated — NRS 298.109(1) sets the 5 p.m. second-Friday-in-August deadline and the copy-before-circulation rule; 298.109(2) sets the 25-working-days county-clerk submission. Aug 9, 2024 is the correct second Friday of August 2024. Ballotpedia's 2020 footnote ('Deadline to file petitions with local officials...7/10/2020') is consistent with the 25-working-days-before rule. The draft properly flags the materially different minor-party June deadline so the two paths are not conflated. CURRENT and CORRECT for an independent presidential candidate; no legal interpretation needed.

fields.reliedValue: No statewide-distribution quota for the independent presidential petition (no minimum per congressional district or county). The petition may consist of more than one document; each document must bear the name of a single county and only registered voters of that county may sign it; each county's documents are verified by that county clerk. Signatures are county-segregated but there is no required number per county/district.
fields.note: GROUND: Quote ('The petition may consist of more than one document. Each document must bear the name of a county and only registered voters of that county may sign the document.') verbatim-confirmed on nrs_298.109. JUDGE: NRS 298.109(2) contains no per-county or per-district minimum for the INDEPENDENT path — confirmed by full read of the section. ADVERSARIAL CHECK: The Justia copy of NRS 293.1715 shows a subsection 5 imposing a per-petition-district apportionment of the 1% — BUT that applies only to the MINOR-PARTY path (293.1715(2)(c)), NOT to the independent presidential petition (298.109) this field is scoped to. The draft is correctly scoped to NRS 298.109 and the no-quota conclusion holds for that path. CURRENT and CORRECT.

fields.reliedValue: NRS 298.109 imposes no express age, residency, or voter-registration requirement on the circulator; it requires only the circulator's affidavit that signatures are genuine and were signed in the circulator's presence by persons registered to vote in that county. STRICTEST defensible posture for operations: do not assume out-of-state or non-registered circulators are safe statewide — confirm with SOS, since circulator-eligibility rules are an area of active legal change in many states and Ballotpedia's 'no requirements' statement is secondary.
fields.note: GROUND: Both quotes verbatim-confirmed — the NRS 298.109 affidavit quote on nrs_298.109; the Ballotpedia 'Circulators of petitions are not required to be registered voters in Nevada. The statutes do not stipulate any requirements for circulators in Nevada.' quote confirmed word-for-word at line 370 of the political-candidates page. JUDGE: The statute's silence on circulator eligibility is accurately read. HOLD (interpretationRequired=true) because correctness as an affirmative operational rule ('any circulator is fine') rests on (a) a secondary source (Ballotpedia) generalizing from statutory silence and (b) the open legal question of whether SOS practice or any other NRS provision constrains circulators for a presidential petition. Statutory silence does not equal a guarantee; conservative posture is to verify before relying on out-of-state/non-registered circulators.

fields.reliedValue: Treat a NOTARIZED circulator affidavit as required: each petition document must contain the circulator's sworn affidavit that all signatures are genuine and were signed in the circulator's presence by persons registered to vote in that county (NRS 298.109(2)). Strictest defensible reading: execute the circulator affidavit before a notary public. No separate notarization of individual voter signatures is required.
fields.note: GROUND: Both quotes verbatim-confirmed — the NRS 298.109(2) 'affidavit of the person who circulated the document...' quote on nrs_298.109; the Ballotpedia 'The circulator of the petition must sign, before a notary public, an affidavit on the back of the petition verifying that all signatures were recorded in the circulator's presence.' quote confirmed word-for-word at line 366 of the political-candidates page. JUDGE/ADVERSARIAL: NRS 298.109 itself says only 'affidavit' — it does NOT expressly say 'notary public.' The 'before a notary public' language comes from Ballotpedia's GENERAL petition guidance (tied to minor-party/NRS 293.172 petitions), not from NRS 298.109 specifically. An affidavit is by definition sworn before an officer authorized to administer oaths, which conventionally means a notary, so requiring notarization is the SAFE/strictest posture — but whether NRS 298.109 strictly mandates a notary (vs. any oath-administering officer) is a legal-interpretation question retrieval cannot settle. HOLD at the notarization-required reading.

fields.reliedValue: paper. The independent presidential petition is a wet-ink, county-by-county paper document: each signer adds signature, residence address, date, and county of registration; signatures must be in ink; the circulator executes a paper affidavit; documents are physically submitted to each county clerk for verification. No Nevada digital/online signature-collection system covers this petition. (Candidate declaration/filing with the SOS may use the SOS online portal, but signature collection is paper.)
fields.note: GROUND: Both quotes verbatim-confirmed — the NRS 298.109 signer-information quote ('Each person signing shall add to his or her signature the address...the date...and the name of the county wherein he or she is registered to vote.') on nrs_298.109; the Ballotpedia 'Each signature must be made in ink. Any signature not made in ink will be disregarded.' quote confirmed word-for-word at line 366. JUDGE: The statutory scheme (county-named documents, signer adds handwritten address/date/county, circulator paper affidavit, physical submission to county clerks for NRS 293.1276-293.1279 verification) is inherently wet-ink paper; no e-signature pathway exists for this petition. CURRENT and CORRECT; no interpretation needed. The 'made in ink' requirement is from general petition guidance but is consistent with and reinforces the statutory scheme.

fields.reliedValue: There is NO fee-in-lieu-of-signatures alternative. The $250 filing fee is required IN ADDITION TO the petition signatures, not as a substitute. An independent presidential candidate must both pay the $250 fee and file the qualifying petition. (The fee is refundable within 10 days if the candidacy is ultimately not qualified, per NRS 293.194, but it cannot replace the signature requirement.)
fields.note: GROUND: Both quotes verbatim-confirmed — the NRS 298.109(1) 'pay a filing fee of $250 and file...a declaration of candidacy and a petition of candidacy' quote on nrs_298.109; the Ballotpedia presidential-page quote 'must file a declaration of candidacy...pay a $250 filing fee, and file a petition containing the signatures of at least 1 percent...' confirmed word-for-word at line 231 of the presidential-candidates page. JUDGE: 'pay...AND file...a petition' in NRS 298.109(1) is conjunctive — the fee and petition are both mandatory; there is no statutory fee-in-lieu option. The draft's parenthetical refund claim is INDEPENDENTLY corroborated by NRS 293.194 (filing fee 'must be returned to the candidate...within 10 days' after a final determination the petition does not qualify) — a side note that does not affect the central, correct conclusion. CURRENT and CORRECT.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found (per-signature pay law is initiative-only, NRS Ch. 295)
compensationModel.note: The Nevada statute governing an independent presidential candidate's petition of candidacy (NRS 298.109) — and the parallel independent-candidate statute NRS 293.200 — require a circulator's affidavit but are completely SILENT on circulator compensation: they neither permit nor prohibit per-signature pay and do not address paid circulation at all, so candidate-petition compensation is unaddressed. Nevada's "pay-per-signature permitted" status is an initiative/ballot-measure fact under NRS Chapter 295 (NRS 295.0575 expressly limits its affidavit/rules to "a petition for a constitutional amendment or a petition for a statewide measure proposed by an initiative or referendum") and does NOT automatically carry over to candidate petitions; NRS 298.109/293.200 require only the SIGNERS (not the circulator) to be registered voters of the county, so no circulator residency/registration requirement appears for candidate petitions.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an INDEPENDENT presidential candidate's petition of candidacy under NRS 298.109, does Nevada law permit a campaign to compensate signature circulators on a per-signature basis, and may a non-resident / non-registered-voter serve as the circulator who signs the document's affidavit?

citations.quote: A person who desires to be an independent candidate for the office of President of the United States must, not later than 5 p.m. on the second Friday in August ... pay a filing fee of $250 and file with the Secretary of State a declaration of candidacy and a petition of candidacy ... Each document of the petition must also contain the affidavit of the person who circulated the document that all signatures thereon are genuine to the best of the person's knowledge and belief and were signed in his or her presence by persons registered to vote in that county.

A petition for a constitutional amendment or a petition for a statewide measure proposed by an initiative or referendum may consist of more than one document. Each document of a petition must have attached to it when submitted an affidavit executed by the circulator thereof stating: 1. That the circulator personally circulated the document.

The person who circulates each document of the petition shall sign an affidavit attesting that the signatures on the document are genuine to the best of his or her knowledge and belief and were signed in his or her presence by persons registered to vote in that county.
Nevada allows paying signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected. See law: Nevada Revised Statutes, Chapter 295
Nevada does not require that petition circulators reside in the state.

To gain access to the general ballot, an independent candidate for president must file a declaration of candidacy with the secretary of state no later than the second Friday in August, pay a $250 filing fee, and file a petition containing the signatures of at least 1 percent of the total number of votes cast for all representatives in Congress in the last election.

voterFile.cost: Statutory rate = 1 cent ($0.01) per name (NRS 293.440(1)). For the full statewide file (~2.07M active voters) this works out to roughly $20,000; Ballotpedia lists the statewide-list price as "$20,000." A campaign normally requests a filtered subset (e.g., one county/district) and pays $0.01 per record, so verifying petition signers in a targeted geography is typically well under $100. Major/minor political-party central/executive committees can obtain the list without charge.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Effectively open / general public ("NevVoter is accessible to the public"), but the Secretary of State "retains full authority in granting, denying or revoking access at any time." A campaign or committee qualifies. The SOS verifies the applicant's qualification when the request form is filed. Major-party state/county central committees and minor-party executive committees may obtain copies without charge under NRS 293.440(1).
voterFile.useRestrictions: Yes — political/election-use-only. Per NRS 293.440(6)(b) (and as restated on the SOS page): "any person, candidate, party or organization who receives information requested on this form is prohibited from using such information for purposes not related to an election and from selling such information for any reason." The statute also bars selling the list "for compensation or other valuable consideration."
voterFile.requestProcess: Two-step: (1) create a web account in the Nevada SOS Online Services portal; (2) download, complete, sign, and file the "Official Request for List of Registered Voters Form" with the SOS (by email, fax, mail, or in person). The form attests to permitted election-related use. Access is granted/denied after verification, typically the next business day up to 7 business days, and the applicant is notified by email. (Not an instant anonymous download — application form + approval.)
voterFile.note: Nevada's statewide voter file (NevVoter) is publicly available from the Secretary of State, but requires a free web account plus a signed Official Request form and SOS approval before download — so it is request-gated rather than an instant public download. The file includes the fields needed to verify a petition signer (name, residential address, date of birth, party, precinct/district, and voter/registration status); the statutory price is 1 cent per name (free to party committees), so a targeted subset is low-cost while the entire ~2M-record statewide file runs about $20,000.

citations.quote: The statewide voter registration database is publicly accessible through this office and available in either hard copy or by a variety of electronic formats. NevVoter is accessible to the public; however, the Office of the Secretary of State retains full authority in granting, denying or revoking access at any time, in accordance with applicable local, state and federal law or regulations.

Requesting access to the Statewide Voter Registration List is a two-step process. You must complete both steps before accessing the Statewide Voter Registration List: Step 1: The applicant must create a web account ... Step 2: The applicant must download and submit an Official Request for List of Registered Voters Form. This form must be completed, signed, and filed with the Secretary of State.

Per NRS 293.440(6)(b), any person, candidate, party or organization who receives information requested on this form is prohibited from using such information for purposes not related to an election and from selling such information for any reason.

Qualification of the applicant to access the statewide voter registration list will be verified upon the filing of a completed request form. Once access has been granted, the applicant will be notified via email. Depending upon the volume of pending requests, access may be granted the following business day, or can take up to 7 business days.

Any person who desires a copy of any list of the persons who are registered to vote in any precinct, district or county may obtain a copy by applying at the office of the county clerk and paying therefor a sum of money equal to 1 cent per name on the list, except that one copy of each original and supplemental list ... must be provided both to the state central committee of any major political party and to the county central committee of any major political party, and to the executive committee of any minor political party upon request, without charge.
the copy of the list provided pursuant to this section must indicate the address, date of birth, telephone number and the serial number on each application to register to vote.
Nevada | Open | $20,000 | Name, DOB, address, district, precinct, voter status, phone number, political party, voter identification numb[er]

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — the requirement binds signers only. NRS 298.109 (the controlling statute for independent presidential petitions) and NRS 293.200 (general independent candidate petitions) impose NO minimum age on the person who circulates the petition. The statutes require only that "the person who circulated the document" sign an affidavit that the signatures are genuine and were s
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: registered voter of the county named on the document. CIRCULATOR: no eligibility requirement; only must sign the verification affidavit (before a notary).
circulator.note: For an independent presidential nominating petition in Nevada, the controlling statute is NRS 298.109 (parallel general independent-candidate statute is NRS 293.200). Both define the SIGNER requirement (must be a registered voter of the county named on the document) but impose NO age, residency, citizenship, or voter-registration requirement on the CIRCULATOR. The only obligation on "the person who circulated the document" is to sign a verification affidavit that the signatures are genuine to th

citations.field: circulatorAgeRule / circulatorMustBeRegisteredVoter / circulatorMustBeUSCitizen / circulatorMustBeStateResident (controlling presidential statute)
citations.quote: Each person signing shall add to his or her signature the address of the place at which he or she resides, the date that he or she signs and the name of the county wherein he or she is registered to vote. Each document of the petition must also contain the affidavit of the person who circulated the document that all signatures thereon are genuine to the best of the person's knowledge and belief and were signed in his or her presence by persons registered to vote in that county.

Each person who signs the petition shall add to his or her signature the address of the place at which the person actually resides, the date that he or she signs the petition and the name of the county where he or she is registered to vote. The person who circulates each document of the petition shall sign an affidavit attesting that the signatures on the document are genuine to the best of his or her knowledge and belief and were signed in his or her presence by persons registered to vote in that county.

citations.field: minorCanCirculate / circulatorMustBeRegisteredVoter / circulatorMustBeStateResident (affirmative confirmation circulators are unrestricted)
citations.quote: Circulators of petitions are not required to be registered voters in Nevada. The statutes do not stipulate any requirements for circulators in Nevada.

The circulator of the petition must sign, before a notary public, an affidavit on the back of the petition verifying that all signatures were recorded in the circulator's presence. The circulator must also affirm that, to the best of his or her knowledge, all signers were registered voters at the time of signing.

citations.field: verification statutes add no circulator eligibility rule
citations.quote: Verification of signatures by county clerks; regulations

signaturesHeadline: NH: 3,000 sigs (1,500 per CD, 2 districts) + $250 fee — both required
deadlineHeadline: NH: Decl. of Intent Jun 3-12, 2026; papers to SOS by 5pm Wed Sep 2, 2026
riskNote: Core numbers/dates verbatim-grounded in RSA 655:42/655:43/655:19-a and confirmed against the SOS 2026 page; only soft spot is circulator eligibility, which rests on statutory silence (no circulator rule exists) rather than an affirmative statement — held conservatively pending SOS confirmation.
sosInquiries: Does New Hampshire impose ANY eligibility requirement (age, NH residency, registered-voter status, or in-state status) on the PERSON who circulates/collects signatures on a presidential candidate's nomination papers under RSA 655:40-45, or is the only personal qualification the one applied to the signer? Please confirm there is no circulator affidavit or circulator-residency rule for the independent/nomination-paper presidential route.

fields.reliedValue: 3,000 signatures of registered voters, 1,500 from each of New Hampshire's 2 U.S. congressional districts. Flat statutory number under RSA 655:42, I (which expressly names 'president' alongside U.S. senator and governor); it is not a percentage and does not recompute by cycle. The separate new/minor political-organization path requires signatures equal to 3% of total ballots cast at the previous state general election, computed by the SOS as 18,808 for 2026 (RSA 655:42, III).
fields.note: RSA 655:42, I quote found verbatim in primary source and expressly lists 'president' at 3,000 / 1,500-per-district. 18,808 political-org figure confirmed verbatim on SOS page. Note: the SOS citation used is the GOVERNOR line ($100 AND 3,000), not a president-specific line (the SOS running-for-office page does not enumerate president), but the statute directly grounds the president figure, so the value is correct.

fields.reliedValue: Two steps for 2026: (1) Declaration of Intent (RSA 655:17-b, presidential) filed with the Secretary of State during the candidate filing window June 3-12, 2026 (RSA 655:14 / 655:14-a: 'first Wednesday in June' = June 3 through 'Friday of the following week' = June 12). (2) Certified nomination papers filed with the Secretary of State no later than 5:00 p.m. Wednesday Sept 2, 2026 (RSA 655:43, I: 'Wednesday one week before the primary'; 2026 primary is Tue Sept 8). Intermediate local deadlines (RSA 655:41): papers to town/ward Supervisors of the Checklist by 5:00 p.m. Aug 5, 2026 (Wed 5 weeks before primary); supervisor certification complete by 5:00 p.m. Aug 26, 2026 (Wed 2 weeks before primary). A filing on the last day must be made in person before the Secretary of State (RSA 655:16 / 655:43, II).
fields.note: All four dates independently recomputed against a 2026 calendar and confirmed: June 3 is the first Wednesday in June; June 12 is the Friday of the following week; Sept 2 is the Wednesday one week before the Sept 8 (Tue) primary; Aug 5 = 5 weeks before; Aug 26 = 2 weeks before. RSA 655:43, I and 655:14 quotes found verbatim; SOS page lists June 3-12 filing period and the Aug 5 / Aug 26 / Sept 2 deadlines verbatim.

fields.reliedValue: Yes — geographic distribution by congressional district. Of the 3,000 total, 1,500 must come from each of New Hampshire's 2 U.S. congressional districts, and signers must be registered in the respective district (RSA 655:42, I). Nomination papers must be ordered/grouped by municipality when filed with the Secretary of State (RSA 655:22-a / 655:43, I; SOS page).
fields.note: RSA 655:42, I quote and SOS '1,500 from each congressional district ... grouped by municipality' quote both found verbatim. Independently corroborated by RSA 655:22-a (petitions ordered alphabetically by municipality) and 655:43, I ('grouped by municipality').

fields.reliedValue: No circulator-specific eligibility requirement is prescribed by statute, BUT this rests on statutory silence rather than an affirmative authorization. RSA 655:40-45 regulates only SIGNERS: each signer must be registered to vote at the state general election, no voter may sign more than one nomination paper for the same office, and each voter must personally sign and date an individual nomination paper; certification of signers is done by the local Supervisors of the Checklist (RSA 655:41), not by a circulator. Strictest defensible operating posture until SOS confirms: do not assume non-resident or non-voter circulators are unrestricted — treat circulator status as UNCONFIRMED, and where practical have signatures gathered by NH-registered voters and never rely on a circulator-collected sheet as a substitute for the signer's own personal signature and date.
fields.note: RSA 655:40 signer-rule quote found verbatim. Correctness of 'no circulator requirement' hinges on the inference that statutory silence equals no requirement — retrieval cannot settle whether an unwritten SOS administrative practice or form imposes anything on the collector. Held conservatively and routed to an SOS inquiry; the value itself does not over-promise (it flags UNKNOWN), which is the safe direction.

fields.reliedValue: Petition/nomination-paper sheets are NOT notarized and carry NO circulator affidavit; signer validation is done by the Supervisors of the Checklist of the town/ward where the signer is domiciled or registered, who certify whether the signer is a registered voter there (city clerk may perform this) — RSA 655:41. SEPARATELY, the presidential candidate's own Declaration of Intent MUST be signed by the candidate AND notarized by a notary public — RSA 655:17-b, I. So: notarized candidate Declaration of Intent = YES (required); notarized/witnessed petition sheets or circulator certification = NO (supervisor-of-checklist certification instead).
fields.note: Both RSA quotes found verbatim — 655:41, I (supervisor certification) and 655:17-b, I ('signed by the candidate, and notarized by a notary public'). The load-bearing, candidate-facing requirement (notarize the Declaration of Intent) is stated expressly in statute and points in the safe/strict direction, so AUTO-LIVE. The 'no petition notarization' element follows from the statutory scheme (certification runs through supervisors, with no circulator oath anywhere in 655:40-45).

fields.reliedValue: paper (wet-ink). RSA 655:40 requires that each voter personally sign and date an individual paper nomination form, dated in the year of the election. Papers are physically submitted to the local Supervisors of the Checklist for certification (RSA 655:41) and the certified papers filed in paper with the Secretary of State, grouped by municipality (RSA 655:43, I). There is no digital/online e-petition system for an independent/nomination-paper presidential nomination in New Hampshire; downloadable PDF forms exist but collection and submission remain paper.
fields.note: RSA 655:40 quote ('Each voter shall sign and date an individual nomination paper. Nomination papers shall be dated in the year of the election.') found verbatim; SOS 'submit signed nomination papers to Supervisors of the Checklist' quote found verbatim. No statute or SOS material authorizes e-signature for this route; treating capture as paper is the strict/safe reading.

fields.reliedValue: A fee is required IN ADDITION TO signatures, not as an alternative. A presidential candidate seeking nomination by nomination papers pays a single $250 fee to the Secretary of State at the time of filing the Declaration of Intent, covering both the presidential and vice-presidential candidate (RSA 655:19-a). This does NOT substitute for the 3,000 signatures — both the $250 fee and the full signature count are required. There is no signature-for-fee swap and no indigence petition-in-lieu path for the nomination-paper route (the RSA 655:48 indigence/petition option applies only to the separate presidential PRIMARY $1,000 fee, not to nomination papers).
fields.note: RSA 655:19-a quote found verbatim. Adversarial check confirmed: the only fee-waiver/petition-in-lieu mechanism in Chapter 655 (RSA 655:48, II) is tied to the presidential PRIMARY, not the nomination-paper general-election route, so the draft's 'no swap option' is correct. Both fee and signatures are independently required.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition circulator compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: New Hampshire's candidate nomination-paper statutes (RSA 655:40–45) and the bribery statute (RSA 659:40) do NOT address compensating circulators of candidate nomination papers — neither permitting nor prohibiting per-signature pay; candidate-petition compensation is unaddressed. NH nomination papers have no statutory "circulator" role at all (each registered voter signs and dates an individual paper; certification is by the supervisors of the checklist), so there is no circulator residency requirement on the books, but who may lawfully gather/handle signatures is itself unaddressed.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nomination papers under RSA 655:40–45, does New Hampshire law permit a campaign to compensate the people who gather signatures, and specifically may they be paid on a per-signature basis, or must any such pay be hourly/salaried — and is there any residency or registered-voter requirement for the person collecting signatures?

citations.quote: 655:40 General Provisions. – As an alternative to nomination by party primary, a candidate may have his or her name placed on the ballot for the state general election by submitting the requisite number of nomination papers. ... Nomination papers shall be signed by such persons only as are registered to vote at the state general election. No voter shall sign more than one nomination paper for each office to be voted for ... Each voter shall sign and date an individual nomination paper.

655:41 Certification. – I. Each nomination paper shall be submitted to the supervisors of the checklist of the town or ward in which the signer is domiciled or is registered, and a majority of the supervisors shall certify whether or not the signer is a registered voter in said town or ward.

655:45 Nomination Papers Protected. – No person shall falsely make or file or knowingly deface or destroy any nomination paper, or any part thereof, or sign any nomination paper contrary to the provisions of law knowing the same, or any part thereof, to be falsely made or suppress any nomination paper, or any part thereof, which has been duly filed.

655:42 Number. – I. It shall require the names of 3,000 registered voters, 1,500 from each United States congressional district in the state, to nominate by nomination papers a candidate for president, United States senator, or governor.

659:40 Bribing; Intimidation; Suppression. – I. No person shall directly or indirectly bribe any person not to register to vote or any voter not to vote or to vote for or against any question submitted to voters or to vote for or against any ticket or candidate for office at any election.

voterFile.cost: Statutory fee (RSA 654:31, IV): $25 + $0.50 per thousand names (or portion thereof) in excess of 2,500 + shipping, PLUS the amount each town/city would otherwise have charged for its own checklist. For the full statewide roll (~1M+ registered voters) this works out to roughly $8,300 in practice per the Concord Monitor; an absentee-voter version runs ~$2,000. Per-district/town lists are far cheaper (a few hundred dollars or less). The $25 base + $0.50/1,000 formula is the published statutory price.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Eligibility-gated. The Secretary of State will provide the statewide voter list only to (a) a political party (as defined in RSA 664:2, IV), (b) a political committee (RSA 664:2, III), or (c) a candidate for county, state, or federal office (statewide, or limited to the candidate's district). The general public CANNOT purchase the statewide database — the public may only view it (no printing/copying) at the State Records and Archives Center, or purchase individual town/ward public checklists from local supervisors of the checklist / town clerks.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Commercial use is prohibited (RSA 654:31, VI): "No person shall use or permit the use of checklist or voter information ... for commercial purposes." Knowing violation is a misdemeanor if a natural person, a felony if any other person. The Secretary of State may insert inauthentic ("salted") entries into provided copies to detect/enforce against misuse. Political and election-related use (voter contact, GOTV, persuasion, nonprofit membership growth) is permitted; the data may be freely shared since it is public, but never for selling property/services unrelated to an election or campaign.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request to the Secretary of State (Election Division). No instant online download. The statute frames it as "upon request" by an eligible party/committee/candidate; fee is paid per RSA 654:31, IV. (Local town/ward checklists are obtained separately from each town/city clerk or supervisors of the checklist.)
voterFile.note: A campaign verifying petition signatures qualifies as a candidate/committee and can purchase the full statewide list from the NH Secretary of State under RSA 654:31, IV — it includes name, domicile address, mailing address, town/city, voter history, and party affiliation, which is sufficient to match signers to registered voters. It is restricted (not a public download), the practical statewide cost is roughly $8,300, and use is limited to non-commercial (election/political) purposes with criminal penalties for misuse.

citations.quote: IV. The secretary of state shall, upon request, provide to a political party, as defined in RSA 664:2, IV, or to a political committee, as defined in RSA 664:2, III, a list of the name, domicile address, mailing address, town or city, voter history, and party affiliation, if any, of every registered voter in the state. The secretary of state shall, upon request, provide to a candidate for county, state, or federal office a list of the name, domicile address, mailing address, town or city, voter history, and party affiliation, if any, of every registered voter in the state or in the candidate's district.

The secretary of state shall charge a fee of $25 plus $0.50 per thousand names or portion thereof in excess of 2,500 plus shipping charges for each copy of the list provided under this section. In addition, the secretary of state shall charge and collect on behalf of and remit to the supervisors of the checklist of each city and town the amount that such supervisors would have charged had the public checklist of their city or town been purchased from them.

VI. No person shall use or permit the use of checklist or voter information provided by any supervisors of the checklist or city or town clerk or by the secretary of state for commercial purposes.
