Whoever knowingly violates any of the provisions of this section shall be guilty of a misdemeanor if a natural person or guilty of a felony if any other person. The secretary of state may insert inauthentic entries into copies of the public checklist provided under this section for purposes of facilitating enforcement of this paragraph.

III. Any person may view the data that would be available on the public checklist ... on the statewide centralized voter registration database maintained by the secretary of state at the state records and archives center during normal business hours, but the person viewing data at the state records and archives center may not print, duplicate, transmit, or alter the data.
Pricing varies, but the cost of the statewide voter roll hovers around $8,300. The same sort of checklist exists for absentee voters – that typically costs $2,000.

The main restriction is that it can’t be used for commercial purposes. People can use it to solicit votes, or nonprofit organizations can use it to increase their membership, but it can’t be used to sell anything.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — the statute imposes no requirements on the person who collects signatures. New Hampshire's independent-presidential nomination-papers scheme (RSA 655:40–655:45, applied to president by RSA 655:53) has NO circulator/witness role at all. There is no circulator affidavit. Each voter signs and dates an individual single-signer nomination paper (RSA 655:40), and signatur
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER requirements (strict): must be registered to vote at the NH state general election and domiciled/registered in the town or ward (RSA 655:40, 655:41). CIRCULATOR/COLLECTOR requirements: NONE — NH nomination papers have no circulator or witness role; each voter signs an individual paper and a government official (supervisors of the checklist / city clerk) certifies it, so no age, citizenship,
circulator.note: New Hampshire's nomination-papers process for an independent presidential candidate (RSA 655:40–655:45, made applicable to president by RSA 655:53) does NOT use a circulator/witness model. RSA 655:40 requires "Each voter shall sign and date an individual nomination paper," and that papers be "signed by such persons only as are registered to vote at the state general election" — a SIGNER requirement. Authentication is performed by government officials, not a private circulator: RSA 655:41 directs

citations.quote: 655:40 General Provisions. – As an alternative to nomination by party primary, a candidate may have his or her name placed on the ballot for the state general election by submitting the requisite number of nomination papers... Nomination papers shall be signed by such persons only as are registered to vote at the state general election. No voter shall sign more than one nomination paper for each office to be voted for... Each voter shall sign and date an individual nomination paper. Nomination papers shall be dated in the year of the election.

citations.field: circulatorMustBeRegisteredVoter / certification by government officials, not a circulator
citations.quote: 655:41 Certification. – I. Each nomination paper shall be submitted to the supervisors of the checklist of the town or ward in which the signer is domiciled or is registered, and a majority of the supervisors shall certify whether or not the signer is a registered voter in said town or ward... II. The city clerk may perform the responsibilities of the supervisors of the checklist under this section.
655:53 By Nomination Papers. – Candidates for president may be nominated by nomination papers as provided in RSA 655:40 through 655:45.

655:42 Number. – I. It shall require the names of 3,000 registered voters, 1,500 from each United States congressional district in the state, to nominate by nomination papers a candidate for president, United States senator, or governor.

citations.field: corroboration of signer requirement; no circulator mentioned
citations.quote: To qualify for placement on the general election ballot, an independent presidential candidate must submit a petition containing the signatures of at least 3,000 registered voters and pay a $250 filing fee.

signaturesHeadline: NJ signatures: HOLD — collect to 2,000 (strict) but the 2,000 figure is documented only for Governor, not President; primary statute still reads 800. Confirm with Division of Elections.
deadlineHeadline: NJ deadline: LIVE — 99th day before the general election (N.J.S.A. 19:13-9 presidential-elector carve-out); recompute the calendar date each cycle.
riskNote: Only real risk is signaturesRequired: the 2,000 value is an inference (statewide-office aggregate applied to President), but every grounded source ties 2,000 to "Governor" and Ballotpedia's presidential page + the cited statute still say 800 — unresolved legal-interpretation question; over-comply to 2,000 and verify. All other fields are grounded and correct.
sosInquiries: For an INDEPENDENT candidate for President/Vice President (slate of presidential electors, not nominated at a party convention) filing a direct nomination petition under N.J.S.A. 19:13 for the November general election, how many petition signatures are required after A-5117/S-3994 (P.L. 2025) — is it the 2,000 statewide-office aggregate now applied to Governor, or does the presidential-elector petition retain the prior 800 figure?

Does the NJ-residency requirement for petition circulators in N.J.S.A. 19:13-7 apply to (and is it enforced against) a circulator of an INDEPENDENT general-election presidential-elector petition, in light of Arsenault v. Way and out-of-state-circulator case law?

fields.reliedValue: At least 2,000 signatures of qualified registered NJ voters statewide (use the higher figure to over-comply and never fall short). CAUTION: the 2,000 figure (A-5117/S-3994, eff. Feb 3, 2025) is documented in the grounded sources only for GOVERNOR; whether it governs an independent PRESIDENTIAL-elector petition is an unresolved statutory-interpretation question. The cited primary statute (N.J.S.A. 19:13-5) and Ballotpedia's NJ presidential page both still state 800. Confirm the operative number with the NJ Division of Elections before filing.
fields.note: All citations grounded VERBATIM, but they do not support the draft's value. FindLaw 19:13-5 (current as of Jan 1, 2024) reads 'eight hundred signatures in the aggregate' — the draft cites this to support 2,000, a self-contradiction. NJ Spotlight ('from 800 to 2,000 for governor') and NJ.gov press ('Independent candidates for Governor...2,000 signatures') both tie 2,000 specifically to Governor, NEVER to President. Ballotpedia's presidential page (cited in the deadline field, post-2025) still says 800. NJ nominates a slate of presidential electors under a distinct scheme (the 19:13-9 carve-out), so the draft's inference that the single-statewide-office aggregate automatically applies to President is contestable. Could not retrieve operative amended 19:13-5 text or NJ Div. of Elections presidential guidance (legislature/state pages CAPTCHA/404/unreachable). Disagree + interpretation-required → HOLD; strictest defensible posture is to collect to 2,000.

fields.reliedValue: Filed with the Secretary of State before 4:00 p.m. of the 99th day preceding the general election (N.J.S.A. 19:13-9 carve-out for presidential electors not nominated at a party convention). The exact calendar date recomputes each cycle as the 99th day before that cycle's general election.
fields.note: Grounded VERBATIM: FindLaw 19:13-9 ('...electors of candidates for President and Vice President...not been nominated at a convention...filed with the Secretary of State before 4:00 p.m. of the 99th day preceding the general election') and Ballotpedia presidential page ('no later than 99 days before the general election'). The statute is explicit and the presidential-elector carve-out is correctly identified; no contested interpretation. Note: the 2024 worked example (Aug 1, 2024) is a past-cycle illustration only — the rule is the 99-day formula.

fields.reliedValue: None. Flat statewide aggregate of signatures of qualified voters of the entire State; no per-congressional-district or per-county distribution requirement. Petition is addressed to (filed with) the Secretary of State.
fields.note: Grounded VERBATIM: 19:13-5 ('...office to be filled by the voters of the entire State...in the aggregate...') and 19:13-3 ('Petitions naming candidates for office to be filled by voters of the entire State...shall be addressed to the Secretary of State'). NJ imposes no geographic distribution requirement for statewide office; uncontested. (Independent of the 800-vs-2,000 dispute, which is about quantity, not distribution.)

fields.reliedValue: Circulator need not be a registered voter but must be voter-eligible: at least 18 years of age, a resident of New Jersey, a U.S. citizen, and not otherwise disqualified under the NJ Constitution. The candidate may sign and/or circulate their own petition.
fields.note: Grounded VERBATIM in 19:13-7 ('shall not be required to be a registered voter, but shall be voter eligible, which means at least 18 years of age, a resident of this State, a citizen of the United States...'); candidate self-circulation confirmed by 19:13-5. Adversarial note flagged in sosInquiries: the NJ-residency bar may be constitutionally vulnerable as applied to an independent general-election presidential circulator (cf. Arsenault v. Way; Buckley v. ACLF), but that doubt cuts toward MORE circulator flexibility, so using only NJ-resident circulators (the value as written) over-complies and cannot threaten ballot access. Value tracks the statute verbatim → LIVE.

fields.reliedValue: Yes — notarized circulator affidavit. Before filing, the circulator (or a candidate who signs/circulates) must make oath by affidavit before a duly qualified officer (notary) that the petition is made in good faith, that the affiant personally circulated it and saw all signatures made, and verily believes the signers are duly qualified voters.
fields.note: Grounded VERBATIM: 19:13-7 ('...shall make oath by affidavit before a duly qualified officer that the petition is made in good faith, that the affiant personally circulated the petition and saw all the signatures made thereto and verily believes that the signers are duly qualified voters') and Ballotpedia ('Petition circulators must complete and sign an affidavit. The affidavit must also be notarized'). Current and uncontested.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Hand-circulated paper nominating petition, wet-ink voter signatures, notarized circulator affidavit, filed with the Secretary of State. No standing digital/online petition system. The COVID-era EO 216 (Jan 25, 2021) was a temporary emergency order and is not available for current cycles.
fields.note: Grounded VERBATIM: Ballotpedia quotes EO 216 ('...submitted by hand delivery and electronically'; 'signatures to be collected electronically') and correctly frames it as a one-time 2021 emergency order; 19:13-7's wet-ink affidavit requirement supports paper as the standing default. No permanent e-petition system identified. Current and uncontested.

fields.reliedValue: None. No filing fee and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option; ballot access for an independent presidential candidate is by nominating petition (signatures) only.
fields.note: Grounded VERBATIM: Ballotpedia ('File a petition with the required number of signatures for the office being sought, including a guarantee that signers are qualified voters...') and 19:13-5 (signatures of qualified voters). No fee or fee-alternative appears in the statutory petition scheme. Uncontested; note this is a negative finding (absence of a fee mechanism) rather than an affirmative 'fee = $0' statement.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: New Jersey's independent/direct-nomination petition statutes (Title 19, Chapter 13) and the official Division of Elections petition forms/instructions (N.J.S.A. 19:13-4 and 19:13-7) do NOT address compensation of candidate-petition circulators at all — neither authorizing nor prohibiting per-signature pay, so compensation is UNADDRESSED for candidate petitions. Paying circulators (e.g., hourly) is not prohibited and is implicitly permitted (the recall chapter, 19:27A, expressly contemplates "paid circulator[s]," but that is a different petition type); per-signature pay specifically is unaddressed and carries some risk given a separate NJ provision against paying people to sign, so it should not be relied upon. NJ is not a statewide initiative state, so the national pay-per-signature initiative framework does not apply here. Circulators need not be registered voters but must be NJ voter-eligible (18+, U.S. citizen, not otherwise disqualified under NJ election laws), which effectively requires New Jersey residency — a purely out-of-state paid pool would not qualify.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's direct-nomination petition under N.J.S.A. 19:13, may a campaign lawfully pay signature circulators on a per-signature basis; if not, may they be paid hourly/salaried; and must each circulator be a New Jersey resident/NJ-voter-eligible per the 19:13-7 affidavit?

citations.quote: The person who circulates the petition does not have to be a signer of the petition and shall not be required to be a registered voter, but shall be at least 18 years of age, a citizen of the United States, and not otherwise disqualified from voting under the State Constitution or election laws of New Jersey. ... (N.J.S.A. 19:13-7)

AFFIDAVIT OF PERSON WHO CIRCULATES THIS PETITION AND WITNESSES SIGNATURES (N.J.S.A. 19:13-7) ... I, ____, being duly sworn, upon my oath say that the petition is made in good faith, that I personally circulated the petition and saw all the signatures made thereto and verily believe that the signers are duly qualified voters. I am at least 18 years of age, a citizen of the United States, and not otherwise disqualified from voting under the State Constitution or election laws of New Jersey.

There are 26 states that provide for an initiative or referendum process. Sixteen (16) of those states allowed campaigns to make payments to signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected. Ten (10) of those states prohibited campaigns from paying signature gatherers based on the number of signatures collected.
For an office to be filled by the voters of the entire State, 2,000 signatures in the aggregate for each candidate nominated in the petition shall be sufficient.

i. ... if a recall petition is presented to such a prospective signer by a paid circulator, the solicitation or petition, respectively, shall disclose prominently in a statement printed in at least 10-point type (1) the identity of the person paying for the printed or personal solicitation, and (2) that the circulator is paid.

voterFile.cost: Up to $375 per county for a computer-generated/electronic copy (charge must "reflect only the cost of reproducing the list" and "shall not exceed $375"); printed per-election-district lists are $0.25 each. There is no single statewide fee — a statewide file means requesting from each of the 21 counties, so roughly up to ~$375 x 21 (~$7,875) at the cap, though most counties charge far less (actual reproduction cost).
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Any registered New Jersey voter. The statute says the commissioner "shall furnish ... to any voter requesting it." (Not open to the non-registered general public; a campaign obtains it through a registered-voter requester such as the candidate, treasurer, or staff.)
voterFile.useRestrictions: Political use (e.g., petition-signature verification) is permitted. Commercial or charitable solicitation of the listed voters is prohibited: "No person shall use voter registration lists or copies thereof prepared pursuant to this section as a basis for commercial or charitable solicitation of the voters listed thereon. Any person making such use ... shall be a disorderly person, and shall be punished by a fine not exceeding $500.00." Lists also exclude voter signatures and the home address of protected "covered persons."
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request to the county Commissioner of Registration (county election office) — NOT the Secretary of State. New Jersey has no centralized statewide list-sale program; the file is assembled county-by-county (21 counties). The commissioner furnishes a computer-generated or electronic copy. (Separately, a State party committee chair may obtain a copy of each county's lists once per calendar year under 19:31-18.1(a), but the general mechanism is the per-voter request under subsection (b).)
voterFile.note: Per N.J.S.A. 19:31-18.1(b), New Jersey voter lists are obtained from each county's Commissioner of Registration (no central statewide download), capped at $375/county for an electronic copy from the Statewide voter registration system. The list includes name, address, date of birth, party affiliation, and voting history (signatures excluded) — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters; use is restricted to registered voters and bars commercial/charitable solicitation.

citations.quote: The commissioner of registration shall furnish a computer-generated or electronic copy of a list of registered voters in any or all election districts in the county to any voter requesting it, for which copy such commissioner shall make a charge which shall be uniform in any calendar year and which shall reflect only the cost of reproducing the list, but which in any case shall not exceed $375.

No person shall use voter registration lists or copies thereof prepared pursuant to this section as a basis for commercial or charitable solicitation of the voters listed thereon. Any person making such use of such lists or copies thereof shall be a disorderly person, and shall be punished by a fine not exceeding $500.00.

The list of registered voters shall include only the following information for each registered voter: name, address, date of birth, political party affiliation, and voting history. Except when so ordered by a court, the list of registered voters shall not include voter signatures.

The county clerk in all counties shall cause copies of the registry lists, certified and transmitted under R.S.19:31-18, to be printed, and shall furnish to any voter applying for the same such copies, charging therefor $0.25 per copy of the list of voters of each election district.

circulator.ageRule: N.J.S.A. 19:13-7 expressly requires the circulator to be at least 18: "The person who circulates the petition shall not be required to be a registered voter, but shall be voter eligible, which means at least 18 years of age, a resident of this State, a citizen of the United States, and not otherwise disqualified under the New Jersey Constitution." So there IS a 18+ age requirement on circulators (
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNERS must be "duly qualified voters" / "legally qualified to vote for such candidates" — i.e., registered/qualified NJ voters in the relevant district (N.J.S.A. 19:13-4, 19:13-7). The CIRCULATOR is held to a looser, expressly-defined standard: NOT required to be a registered voter, but must be "voter eligible" = 18+, a NJ resident, and a U.S. citizen (N.J.S.A. 19:13-7). So registration binds on
circulator.note: AUTO-LIVE: N.J.S.A. 19:13-7 explicitly defines circulator eligibility for the petition of nomination (independent / direct-nomination route, which is what an independent presidential ticket files in NJ — see 19:13-4 expressly covering petitions "nominating electors of president and vice president of the United States"). Circulator must be: (1) 18+, (2) a U.S. citizen, (3) a NJ resident, and not otherwise disqualified under the NJ Constitution — but is NOT required to be a registered voter. Signe

citations.quote: Before any petition shall be filed as hereinafter provided, the person who circulates the petition, or a candidate who signs or circulates, or both signs and circulates, such a petition, shall make oath by affidavit before a duly qualified officer that the petition is made in good faith, that the affiant personally circulated the petition and saw all the signatures made thereto and verily believes that the signers are duly qualified voters. The person who circulates the petition shall not be required to be a registered voter, but shall be voter eligible, which means at least 18 years of age, a resident of this State, a citizen of the United States, and not otherwise disqualified under the New Jersey Constitution.

citations.field: signerVsCirculator (signers must be qualified voters; presidential electors petitions covered by this chapter)
citations.quote: Such petition shall set forth the names, places of residence and post-office addresses of the candidates for the offices to be filled... that the petitioners are legally qualified to vote for such candidates and pledge themselves to support and vote for the persons named in such petition... In the case of a petition or petitions nominating electors of president and vice president of the United States, the names of the candidates for president and vice president for whom such electors are to vote may be included in the petition or petitions...

Petition circulators must complete and sign an affidavit. The affidavit must also be notarized. The circulator does not have to be a registered voter of the district, but must be a voter-eligible New Jersey resident and provide his or her address on the petition in the designated location.
PETITION OF DIRECT NOMINATION FOR THE GENERAL ELECTION... Instructions for the nominating petition for the General Election... Petition Filing Deadline: Before 4 p.m. on June 2, 2026.

signaturesHeadline: Signature % DISPUTED: statute = 0.5% of gov vote (1-8-51(B)->1-7-2(A)); NASS = 3%. HOLD to 3% until SoS confirms.
deadlineHeadline: Deadline DISPUTED: current statute = 23rd day after primary; NASS/Ballotpedia = 21st. HOLD to earlier (21st) date.
riskNote: Two unresolved conflicts (signature % and deadline day) both go to ballot-access survival; neither resolvable by retrieval — the SoS per-cycle Nominating Petition Calculations doc and posted filing calendar are the controlling sources and must be obtained before relying on any number/date.
sosInquiries: For the independent presidential nominating petition, what is the EXACT number of valid voter signatures required this cycle, and is the percentage basis one-half of one percent (per 1-8-51(B) cross-referencing 1-7-2(A)) or three percent (as stated in the NASS summary) of the total vote cast for governor at the last preceding general election?

Is the operative filing deadline for the independent presidential declaration and nominating petition the 23rd day following the primary (current 1-8-52(B)) or the 21st day (as listed by NASS and Ballotpedia), and what is the exact posted date and hours for this cycle?

fields.reliedValue: At least 3% of the total vote cast for governor at the last preceding general election at which a governor was elected (strictest defensible figure pending SoS confirmation). NOTE: the statutory chain (NMSA 1-8-51(B) -> 1-7-2(A)) reads to only one-half of one percent (0.5%); the 3% comes from the NASS summary and Ballotpedia's overview blurb and is NOT supported by the statutes those sources cite. Because over-collecting never risks the ballot line but under-collecting is fatal, plan to 3% until the SoS per-cycle Nominating Petition Calculations document gives the controlling exact number.
fields.note: GROUNDED: 1-8-51(B) ('signed by a number of voters equal to the number of signatures required to form a new political party'), 1-8-51(A) (basis = total vote for governor at last election), and 1-7-2(A) ('one-half of one percent of the total votes cast for the office of governor') all verified verbatim on FindLaw; NASS '3%' quote verified verbatim in the downloaded PDF. ADVERSARIAL: re-deriving from primary law gives 0.5% (1-8-51(B)->1-7-2(A)); Ballotpedia's statute-citing detail section also says 'one-half of one percent.' The 3% appears to be a propagated error — even the NASS entry cites 1-7-2/1-8-51, which yield 0.5%. But correctness hinges on a statutory cross-reference interpretation retrieval cannot settle, and the controlling per-cycle SoS calculation could not be retrieved. HOLD to the higher (3%) figure to never risk the ballot line.

fields.reliedValue: File the declaration of independent candidacy and nominating petition between 9:00 a.m. and 5:00 p.m. on the 21st day following the primary election (strictest/earliest of the two conflicting dates), pending SoS confirmation. Current NMSA 1-8-52(B) text says the 23rd day, but NASS and Ballotpedia say the 21st; treat the earlier (21st) date as the operative deadline so the filing is never late.
fields.note: GROUNDED: 1-8-52(B) 'twenty-third day following the primary election' verified verbatim on FindLaw (current as of Jan 1, 2024); NASS '21st day following the primary election' verified verbatim in the PDF; Ballotpedia detail section independently says '21st day.' ADVERSARIAL: genuine conflict between the current statute (23rd) and two secondary sources (21st) — possibly a recent amendment from 21st to 23rd not yet reflected downstream, or a downstream error. Cannot confirm the operative posted date without the SoS filing calendar. A missed deadline is fatal to ballot access, so HOLD to the earlier 21st-day date.

fields.reliedValue: None. New Mexico imposes no congressional-district or county distribution requirement on an independent presidential nominating petition; the requirement is a single statewide number tied to the total vote cast for governor at the last preceding general election.
fields.note: GROUNDED: 1-8-51(A) and (B) verified verbatim on FindLaw. ADVERSARIAL: read the full text of 1-8-51 (all subsections A-G) and 1-8-49/1-8-50 — none contains any per-district or per-county allocation for the presidential petition; it is a single statewide figure. Confirmed no distribution rule exists.

fields.reliedValue: No separate statutory circulator qualification. The constraint is on SIGNERS, who must each be a registered voter of New Mexico; a voter may not sign more than one independent-presidential petition. The named presidential electors must be registered NM voters (U.S. senators, U.S. representatives, and persons holding federal offices of trust or profit are ineligible). The Election Code specifies no separate age/residency/voter-status requirement for the person who circulates/collects signatures, as distinct from the signers.
fields.note: GROUNDED: 1-8-50(D) presidential petition form ('I, the undersigned, a registered voter of New Mexico...' and the no-double-signing declaration) and 1-8-49(D) (electors must be registered NM voters; federal officeholders ineligible) all verified verbatim on FindLaw. ADVERSARIAL: searched 1-8-49 through 1-8-52 for any circulator affidavit/qualification — none exists for this petition (contrast with states that require a circulator oath). The value's claim of no separate circulator rule is accurate to the statute.

fields.reliedValue: The candidate's declaration of independent candidacy for president (and vice president) must be a sworn statement made under oath and notarized ('Subscribed and sworn to me this ___ day of ___ / notary public'), per NMSA 1-8-49(B)-(C). The signature petition itself carries no circulator affidavit and no notary block; each signer self-certifies as a registered NM voter on the 1-8-50(D) form. So: notarized sworn candidate declaration = YES; circulator/witness certification or notarization of the petition signatures = NONE.
fields.note: GROUNDED: 1-8-49 'I make the foregoing affidavit under oath...' and 'Subscribed and sworn to me this ___ day of ___, ... notary public' verified verbatim on FindLaw; 1-8-50(D) petition form (no notary/circulator block) verified verbatim. ADVERSARIAL: the petition form text in 1-8-50(D) contains only signer self-declarations and no notarization line, confirming the petition itself is not notarized while the candidate's declaration is.

fields.reliedValue: paper. The statute prescribes a physical paper petition (approximately 8.5 x 11 inches, numbered lines spaced ~3/8 inch apart, multi-sheet petitions firmly secured by staple/fastening per 1-8-50(D),(F)). No online/remote e-signature or in-person-digitized system covers an independent presidential nominating petition in New Mexico; signatures must be collected on the prescribed paper form.
fields.note: GROUNDED: 1-8-50(D) ('on paper approximately eight and one-half inches wide and eleven inches long with numbered lines for signatures spaced approximately three-eighths of an inch apart') and 1-8-50(F) ('firmly secured by a staple or other suitable fastening') verified verbatim on FindLaw. ADVERSARIAL: no statutory authorization for electronic/remote signature capture for this petition exists in 1-8-49 through 1-8-52; paper is correct.

fields.reliedValue: None. New Mexico provides no filing fee in lieu of (or in addition to) the signature petition for an independent presidential candidate. Ballot access is obtained solely by filing the notarized declaration of independent candidacy plus the signed nominating petition (NMSA 1-8-49(A)); the statute specifies no fee and no fee-for-signatures alternative.
fields.note: GROUNDED: 1-8-49(A) ('Nomination as an independent candidate for president or vice president shall be made by filing a declaration of independent candidacy ... The candidate for president shall also at the same time file a nominating petition with the required number of signatures.') verified verbatim on FindLaw; NASS quote verified verbatim in PDF. ADVERSARIAL: nothing in 1-8-49 through 1-8-52 authorizes a fee alternative for the independent presidential route; the 'None' value is correct.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: New Mexico's candidate / independent-presidential nominating-petition law (NMSA 1978 Ch. 1, Arts. 1 & 8) and the official Secretary of State 2026 Candidate Information Guide are entirely SILENT on circulator compensation — they address signature counts, petition form, who may sign, falsification, and challenges, but never per-signature or any other circulator pay. Compensation of candidate-petition circulators is therefore UNADDRESSED (the per-signature bans New Mexico discusses elsewhere are initiative/referendum rules, not candidate-petition rules, and were not carried over). No circulator-residency/registration requirement applies (the registered-voter/resident requirement falls on SIGNERS, not the circulator), and there is no circulator-affidavit requirement, so a non-resident paid pool is not facially barred.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nominating petition under NMSA 1978 §§ 1-8-3.1, 1-8-51, and 1-8-52, may a campaign compensate petition circulators on a per-signature basis, and must circulators be New Mexico residents or registered voters?

citations.quote: CIRCULATING NOMINATING PETITIONS — Candidates are ultimately responsible for the contents of their petitions and for the actions of the individuals representing them in the public. It is extremely important that individuals assisting candidates with collecting petition signatures are adequately trained and appropriately informed. ... A person knowingly falsifying any information on a nominating petition is guilty of falsifying an election document (see 1-8-32 (A), NMSA 1978). ... It is a misdemeanor to knowingly circulate, present, or offer to present a nominating petition for the signature of a voter that does not contain the information specified under the law (see 1-8-32 (B), NMSA 1978).

Independent and Minor Party candidates: Voters signing petitions for independent and minor party candidates are not required to be registered in the minor candidate's political party. Instead, the voters certify that they are residents of the state, district, county, or area to be represented by the office for which the person seeking nomination is a candidate (see 1-8-2 (B) and 1-8-50, NMSA 1978).

B. Nominating petitions for an independent candidate for president of the United States shall be signed by a number of voters equal to the number of signatures required to form a new political party. ... G. A voter shall not sign a petition for an independent candidate as provided in this section if the voter has signed a petition for another independent candidate for the same office.

To qualify as an independent candidate, the candidate must meet all requirements for an independent candidate in Section 1-8-45 NMSA 1978 and submit the required number of petition signatures for an independent candidate as prescribed in Section 1-8-51 NMSA 1978. No candidate may circulate petitions for candidacy for more than one political party in an election cycle.

D. The procedures set forth in this section shall be used to validate signatures on any petition required by the Election Code, except that Paragraph (4) of Subsection C of this section shall not apply to petitions filed by unaffiliated candidates or petitions filed by candidates of minor political parties.

voterFile.cost: Per-record fee with a hard cap. Electronic file: $3.00 per 1,000 records without voting history, or $4.00 per 1,000 records with voting history; plus a flat $15.00 setup fee on every request. Statute-set cap: "The maximum fee for any request will not exceed $600.00" (so a full statewide file is at most $600 + setup, i.e. effectively capped at ~$600 total). Printed list $5.00/1,000; mailing labels $20.00/1,000. For petition-signature verification, request the electronic statewide file including inactive voters.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Any requester, BUT use is statutorily restricted to governmental or election/election-campaign purposes only — so in practice candidates, campaigns, committees, parties, and government users. The requester must sign an affidavit limiting use to those purposes. Per NMSA 1-4-5.5(C): "Each requester of voter data, mailing labels or special voter lists shall sign an affidavit that the voter data, mailing labels and special voter lists shall be used for governmental or election and election campaign purposes only and shall not be made available or used for unlawful purposes."
voterFile.useRestrictions: Strong. Use limited to "governmental or election and election campaign purposes only" (NMSA 1-4-5.5(C)); commercial use is excluded ("governmental purposes" defined as noncommercial). Data may not be posted publicly on the internet or otherwise re-disclosed in a way that identifies a voter. Misuse is criminal: NMSA 1-4-5.6(B) — unlawful use "is guilty of a fourth degree felony and upon conviction shall be fined one hundred dollars ($100) for each line of voter information that was unlawfully used," and each unlawful use is a separate offense. Files exclude SSNs, registration-agency codes, day/month of birth, and (if the voter prohibits) phone numbers.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request to the Secretary of State (Bureau of Elections) plus a signed authorization/affidavit on the prescribed form. Not an instant download. Submit the SOS "Voter Data Request Form" (revised 05/15/2024), which requires selecting media/jurisdiction (Statewide), stating the purpose and intended use of the data, and signing as requestor. Contact: Bureau of Elections, NM Secretary of State, (505) 827-3600 / (800) 477-3632, Elections@sos.nm.gov; mailing address State Capitol North Annex, Suite 300, Santa Fe, NM 87503.
voterFile.note: New Mexico sells the statewide SERVIS voter file to political/governmental requesters who sign the prescribed affidavit limiting use to governmental or election/campaign purposes (NMSA 1-4-5.5); misuse is a 4th-degree felony at $100/line (NMSA 1-4-5.6). The standard file includes name, physical + mailing address, year of birth, party affiliation, precinct/jurisdiction and a registrant ID — sufficient to match petition signers — and the SOS expressly advises petition-validation requesters to include inactive voters; total cost is low (per-1,000 fees plus a $15 setup, capped at $600).

citations.quote: Electronic Format without voting history | $3.00 per 1,000 records without voting history | Unsorted ... There will be a $15.00 Setup Fee on all voter data information requests. The maximum fee for any request will not exceed $600.00

SERVIS ... data may be purchased. This data includes each voter's name, address, telephone # (with voter's consent), year of birth, party affiliation, and registration data such as county, precinct, and district information. ... Anyone who purchases SERVIS data must sign an Authorization Form
those who want to validate petition signatures definitely want their lists to include inactive voters

The county clerk or secretary of state shall furnish voter data, mailing labels or special voter lists only upon written request ... Each requester ... shall sign an affidavit that the voter data, mailing labels and special voter lists shall be used for governmental or election and election campaign purposes only and shall not be made available or used for unlawful purposes.

Any person, organization or corporation or agent, officer, representative or employee thereof who commits unlawful use of voter data, mailing labels or special voter lists is guilty of a fourth degree felony and upon conviction shall be fined one hundred dollars ($100) for each line of voter information that was unlawfully used.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — the rule binds signers only. The New Mexico Election Code provisions governing independent-candidate nominating petitions (NMSA 1978 §§ 1-8-45 through 1-8-52) impose NO age requirement on the person who circulates/collects the petition. There is no circulator affidavit or witness oath at all in the independent presidential petition scheme. The only age-relevant requ
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: must be "a registered voter of New Mexico" who signs on their own behalf — the independent presidential petition (NMSA 1978 § 1-8-50(D)) is self-attesting ("I am that person whose name appears hereon"), so each signature line is the signer's own declaration of registration. CIRCULATOR: New Mexico's independent-candidate petition scheme has NO circulator affidavit, witness, or notarization
circulator.note: New Mexico's independent presidential nominating petition is a self-attesting voter petition with NO regulated circulator role. Under NMSA 1978 § 1-8-50(D), the petition for an independent presidential candidate is signed directly by each "registered voter of New Mexico" on their own behalf — there is no separate person who witnesses/verifies the signatures and swears a circulator affidavit. The governing sections (§§ 1-8-45 to 1-8-52) contain no circulator eligibility provision: § 1-8-52, despi

citations.quote: D. The nominating petition for an independent candidate for the office of president of the United States shall be ... in the following form: “NOMINATING PETITION FOR INDEPENDENT CANDIDACY FOR THE OFFICE OF PRESIDENT OF THE UNITED STATES — I, the undersigned, a registered voter of New Mexico, by endorsement hereon, petition that the name of __________ be printed on the general election ballot as an independent candidate for the office of president of the United States ... I also declare that I am that person whose name appears hereon and that I have not signed, nor will I sign, any nominating petition for any other candidate seeking the office of president of the United States at the next ensuing general election.”

§ 1-8-52. Independent candidates for general or United States representative special elections; nominating petitions; circulation; date of filing. A. Declarations of independent candidacy and nominating petitions shall be filed with the proper filing officer between 9:00 a.m. and 5:00 p.m. on the twenty-third day following the primary election of each even-numbered year ... B. Declarations of independent candidacy and nominating petitions for the office of president of the United States shall be filed with the proper filing officer between 9:00 a.m. and 5:00 p.m. on the twenty-third day following the primary election.

It is unlawful for any person to knowingly circulate, present or offer to present for the signature of another person a nominating petition that does not clearly show on the face of the petition the name of the candidate, the address at which the candidate resides, the candidate's county of residence and the office for which the candidate seeks nomination.

The nominating petition for an independent candidate for any office except president of the United States shall be ... in the following form: “NOMINATING PETITION FOR INDEPENDENT CANDIDACY — I, the undersigned, a registered voter of New Mexico, hereby nominate __________ ... and I declare that I am a registered voter of the state, district, county or area to be represented by the office for which the person being nominated is a candidate.”

The nominating petition shall be ... in the following form: “NOMINATING PETITION — I, the undersigned, a registered voter of New Mexico, and a member of the __________ party, hereby nominate __________ ... and I declare that I am a registered voter of the state, district, county or area to be represented by the office for which the person being nominated is a candidate.”

signaturesHeadline: 4,000 signatures (flat statutory number; no distribution)
deadlineHeadline: 4:00 p.m. on the 64th day before the general election
riskNote: 7 of 8 fields AUTO-LIVE and grounded against the SOS guide, NASS summary, and primary statute NDCC 16.1-12-02; only witnessOrNotary is on CONSERVATIVE-HOLD because the exact on-form certification block of SFN 58578 is unverifiable (sos.nd.gov form server returns HTTP 522, reproduced independently).
sosInquiries: Does the Petition/Certificate of Nomination form (SFN 58578) for a non-established-party / independent presidential candidate require a circulator certification or notarized circulator affidavit, or is the candidate Affidavit of Candidacy (SFN 52500) the only sworn document in a complete filing? Please confirm the exact certification block printed on SFN 58578.

fields.reliedValue: 4,000 qualified North Dakota electors (flat statutory number, not a percentage; does not recompute per cycle). Independent and non-established-party presidential candidates use the same form (SFN 58578), checking the 'independent candidate' box for the independent path. Authority: NDCC 16.1-12-02(3).
fields.note: SOS-guide quote and NASS quote both ground verbatim (SOS running-president.pdf lines 50-53; NASS report lines 803-806). Independently confirmed against primary statute NDCC 16.1-12-02 subsection 3 (FindLaw, current as of Jan 1 2024): 'If the nomination is for the office of president, there must be no fewer than four thousand signatures.' Flat number, current and correct for an independent presidential candidate.

fields.reliedValue: 4:00 p.m. on the 64th day before the November general election (statutory rule, NDCC 16.1-12-02). The complete filing (petition + affidavit of candidacy + statement of interests + certificate(s) of nomination of presidential electors) must be in the physical possession of the Secretary of State's office by that deadline. The 2024 instance of this rule was September 3, 2024; the date shifts each cycle to the 64th day before that November election.
fields.note: SOS-guide quote (lines 85-93) and NASS quote (lines 803-806) ground verbatim. Primary statute confirms: petition 'shall submit ... before four p.m. on the sixty-fourth day before the general election.' Relied value is the recurring 64th-day rule, not the one-cycle Sept 3 2024 date. Physical-possession requirement confirmed in SOS guide.

fields.reliedValue: None. A single statewide total of 4,000 qualified-elector signatures; no per-congressional-district or per-county distribution requirement (ND has one at-large U.S. House district). NDCC 16.1-12-02(3).
fields.note: NASS quote grounds verbatim (lines 805-806). Adversarially checked the statute: subsection 3 (president) sets only the flat 4,000 figure with no geographic distribution. The 2%-of-district rule in subsection 2 applies to sub-state district offices, NOT to president. No distribution rule appears in SOS guide, NASS summary, or the statute. None confirmed.

fields.reliedValue: Circulators of this presidential nominating petition are NOT required to be ND qualified electors (no age/residency/voter-status requirement on circulators). The petition may not be circulated or signed before January 1 of the presidential election year. Signers must be qualified ND electors (U.S. citizen, 18+, 30 days' ND residency). Note: ND initiative/ballot-measure petitions under ch. 16.1-01 are different (resident circulators + notarized affidavits) and must not be conflated.
fields.note: SOS-guide quote grounds verbatim (lines 74-77): 'Petition circulators do not need to be ND qualified electors.' Statute confirms the Jan-1 signing window and exempts presidential petitions from the general form spec (16.1-11-16). Adversarial nuance: statute 16.1-12-02(3) requires the elector NOMINEES to be qualified ND electors, but that is a requirement on the presidential electors named on the petition, not on circulators or signers, so it does not contradict this field.

fields.reliedValue: Strictest posture: ASSUME the SFN 58578 petition may carry a circulator certification or affidavit block that must be completed (and possibly notarized) before filing, and prepare to satisfy it; do NOT assume the signatures need no certification. The candidate Affidavit of Candidacy (SFN 52500) is independently required in any case. Cited sources (SOS guide, NASS, statute 16.1-12-02/16.1-11-16) describe no notary requirement on the signatures, but the exact on-form certification wording is UNCONFIRMED.
fields.note: Cited quotes ground verbatim (SOS guide line 59-61; NASS lines 803-806). Statute exempts presidential petitions from 16.1-11-16, and 16.1-11-16 itself contains no notary/circulator-affidavit clause, which supports the draft's reading. HOWEVER the precise certification block printed on form SFN 58578 could not be retrieved: the nd.gov/sos.nd.gov form server returns HTTP 522, which I reproduced independently via both curl and wick. Because the form's actual certification wording could change the answer and cannot be settled by retrieval, held conservatively and an SOS inquiry is filed.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Signatures are collected on the paper Petition/Certificate of Nomination (SFN 58578); the complete filing must be in the physical possession of the Secretary of State's office by the deadline. ND has no remote/online or in-person-digitized signature-capture system for this presidential nominating petition.
fields.note: Both SOS-guide quotes ground verbatim (lines 91-93 and 50-53). Physical-possession requirement plus paper SFN 58578 confirmed by statute (petition 'submitted' to SOS). No online-capture path exists for this petition.

fields.reliedValue: None. No state filing fee for the independent/non-established-party presidential nominating petition and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures alternative; the 4,000-signature petition is the sole ballot-access mechanism. The $5,000 figure in the SOS guide is the federal FECA registration trigger (a campaign-finance obligation), NOT a state ballot-access fee.
fields.note: Both quotes ground verbatim (SOS guide lines 89-92; NASS lines 803-806). No filing fee appears in the SOS complete-filing checklist, the NASS summary, or statute 16.1-12-02. The $5,000 FECA figure is correctly characterized as federal, not a state fee. Adversarially: statute lists petition contents only, no fee.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: North Dakota's candidate-petition statutes (NDCC 16.1-11-16 "Form of nominating petitions" and 16.1-12-02, which sets the 4,000-signature presidential petition) regulate only the petition's contents and require signers to be qualified ND electors; they are SILENT on who may circulate, circulator residency, and circulator compensation. The state's per-signature pay ban and circulator residency/affidavit/age rules sit only in the initiative/referendum framework (NDCC 16.1-01-09, titled "Initiative or referendum petitions" and tied to Article III of the ND Constitution) and the SoS "Petition Signature Gathering Guidance FOR CIRCULATORS OF INITIATED AND REFERENDUM PETITIONS" — neither applies to candidate petitions. So candidate-petition compensation is unaddressed, not permitted or prohibited; hold conservatively rather than carrying over the initiative rule.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an INDEPENDENT presidential candidate's nominating petition under NDCC 16.1-12-02 (the 4,000-signature petition), may a campaign pay circulators per signature, and must those circulators be North Dakota residents or qualified electors — or do the per-signature ban and circulator residency rules in NDCC 16.1-01-09 apply only to initiative/referendum petitions?

citations.quote: 1. Each nominating petition circulated by candidates for any state, district, county, or other political subdivision office must include or have attached the following information... 2. Only qualified electors of the state, district, county, or other political subdivision, as the case may be, may sign nominating petitions.

3. If the nomination is for the office of president, there must be no fewer than four thousand signatures and the petition must contain the names of the presidential and vice presidential candidates along with the names of the elector nominees and alternate elector nominees for the office of the North Dakota presidential electors. The elector nominees and alternate elector nominees must be qualified electors of North Dakota.

PETITION SIGNATURE GATHERING GUIDANCE FOR CIRCULATORS OF INITIATED AND REFERENDUM PETITIONS ... Any person gathering signatures for a statewide petition may not be paid anything of value based on the number of signatures gathered.

§ 16.1-01-09. Initiative or referendum petitions--Signature--Form--Circulation ... No petition may be circulated under the authority of article III of the Constitution of North Dakota by an individual who is less than eighteen years of age ... The chairperson also shall submit a complete list of petition circulators which must include each circulator's full name and residential address. The residential address must be in this state and identify the circulator's street address, city, and zip code.
The relevant statutes do not stipulate any requirements (such as residency or pay status) that petition circulators must meet.

voterFile.cost: N/A — no statewide voter registration file exists for sale or download. North Dakota has no voter registration. The state's internal "central voter file" (NDVOICES) is restricted to election officials via a secure login; it is not offered as a purchasable/downloadable public product, so no public price list exists. If pursued at all, treat as "varies / not publicly available."
voterFile.whoCanObtain: No general public voter-file product. The central voter record system (NDVOICES — North Dakota Voter Information & Central Election Systems) is accessible only to election officials (Secretary of State Elections Division and the 53 county auditors) through a secure login area. There is no candidate/committee voter-list purchase program comparable to other states, because there is no registration list.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Not applicable as a sold dataset. The central voter record is an internal election-administration system (it even flags non-citizens for officials). For petition use, the binding legal constraint is that ballot-measure/nominating petition signers must be North Dakota qualified electors (U.S. citizen, 18+, ND resident 30 days); SOS/county auditors review signatures against signer-supplied name + residential address + date. A campaign cannot obtain a state registration roster to pre-match signers, because none is published.
voterFile.requestProcess: No standard request product. There is no online download, application form, or affidavit process to buy a statewide voter file, because ND does not register voters. Practical signature verification is done by the official filing officer (Secretary of State for statewide measures; county auditors for local). Any inquiry about voter-record data goes to the SOS Elections Division (701-328-4146), but the central file is not made available to campaigns as a list.
voterFile.note: North Dakota is the only U.S. state without voter registration, so there is NO statewide voter registration file a campaign can download, request, or buy to pre-verify petition signers. ND keeps an internal central voter record (NDVOICES) for election officials only; signature verification is performed by the SOS/county auditors against signer-provided name, residential address, and date, and signers must be qualified electors.

citations.quote: North Dakota is the only state without voter registration as our Voter ID law requires a valid form of ID to vote.
Voter Qualifications & ID Requirements – Learn more about voter ID requirements in North Dakota. North Dakota does not do voter registration but has voter ID laws in place for voting.
Election training videos can be found in the secure login area of NDVOICES - North Dakota Voter Information & Central Election Systems.
North Dakota’s voter record flags anyone marked as a non-citizen so election officials can ensure only U.S. citizens are allowed to vote.

A sponsoring committee looking to circulate a statewide ballot measure petition must first have the form of the petition approved by the Secretary of State. They then have 1 year to circulate the petition and gather signatures.

circulator.ageRule: No age requirement is stated in North Dakota's nominating-petition statutes (Ch. 16.1-11 / 16.1-12) for the CIRCULATOR — those chapters impose age/residency/citizenship only on SIGNERS ("qualified electors"). The only explicit "petition may not be circulated by an individual who is less than eighteen years of age" rule (N.D.C.C. 16.1-01-09.1(6)) is expressly limited to petitions circulated "under
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: must be a "qualified elector" of North Dakota — i.e., U.S. citizen, at least 18, and a 30-day ND resident (N.D.C.C. 16.1-11-16(2); for president, signers must be 4,000 qualified ND voters under 16.1-12-02(3)). CIRCULATOR: the nominating-petition chapters (16.1-11, 16.1-12) impose NO stated qualifications on the circulator — no age, no residency, no citizenship, no registered-voter requirem
circulator.note: For an INDEPENDENT/PRESIDENTIAL nominating (certificate of nomination by) petition in North Dakota, the strict eligibility requirements bind the SIGNER, not the circulator. Signers must be "qualified electors" — U.S. citizen, 18+, ND resident 30 days (N.D.C.C. 16.1-11-16(2)); a presidential petition needs 4,000 such signatures (16.1-12-02(3)), filed with the Secretary of State by 4 p.m. on the 64th day before the general election; signatures may not be gathered before January 1 of the presidenti

In addition to signing and printing the individual's name, petition signers shall include the following information: a. The date of signing. b. Complete residential address.

Each nominating petition circulated by candidates for any state, district, county, or other political subdivision office must include or have attached the following information, which must be made available to each signer at the time of signing... [the section states qualifications only for signers ('Only qualified electors ... may sign'), and imposes no age, residency, citizenship, or qualified-elector requirement on the circulator, nor any circulator affidavit].

Except for candidates for the office of president of the United States, each certificate of nomination by petition must meet the specifications for nominating petitions set forth in section 16.1-11-16... 3. If the nomination is for the office of president, there must be no fewer than four thousand signatures and the petition must contain the names of the presidential and vice presidential candidates along with the names of the elector nominees and alternate elector nominees for the office of the North Dakota presidential electors. The elector nominees and alternate elector nominees must be qualified electors of North Dakota.

6. A petition may not be circulated under the authority of article III of the Constitution of North Dakota, section 44-08-21, or section 44-08-21.1 by an individual who is less than eighteen years of age, nor may the affidavit called for by subsection 3 be executed by an individual who is less than eighteen years of age at the time of signing. [This 18+ circulator bar is expressly limited to Article III / recall petitions and does NOT apply to candidate nominating petitions in Ch. 16.1-11 or 16.1-12.]

3. Each copy of a petition provided for in this section, before being filed, must have attached an affidavit executed by the circulator in substantially the following form:... I, ___, being sworn, say that I am a qualified elector; that I reside at ___; that each signature contained on the attached petition was executed in my presence; and that to the best of my knowledge and belief each individual whose signature appears on the attached petition is a qualified elector... [This qualified-elector/resident circulator affidavit is the RECALL/Article III form; the nominating-petition chapters do not contain an equivalent statutory circulator affidavit.]

Independent and non-established party candidates for president must petition for placement on the general election ballot. The candidate must submit a petition containing the signatures of at least 4,000 qualified North Dakota voters. This petition must be filed with the secretary of state no later than 64 days before the general election.
