fields.reliedValue: At least 3% of the total votes cast in the last General Election for Governor. The signature count recomputes each presidential cycle off the most recent gubernatorial general election; rely on this formula, not a fixed number. (For the 2024 cycle the figure was 34,599 = 3% of 1,153,284, but that is a prior-cycle number and the 2028 figure depends on the Nov. 2026 gubernatorial vote, not yet held as of 2026-06-07.)
fields.note: GROUNDED: packet lines 101-111 (3% language + Total Votes 1,153,284 / 34,599) and NASS lines 867-868 confirmed verbatim. Independently re-derived: 1,153,284 x 3% = 34,598.52 -> 34,599, and 2022 gubernatorial total of 1,153,284 confirmed against Ballotpedia citing OK SEB certified results. The 3% RULE is correct and current. HOLD reason is temporal currency, not citation failure: 34,599 is the 2024-cycle figure; the live number for the next cycle is not yet determinable. Strictest treatment is to rely on the formula and avoid presenting the stale count as the current requirement.

fields.reliedValue: July 15 of the presidential election year. An independent presidential candidate must file the Statement of Candidacy (with petition or filing fee) with the Secretary of the State Election Board no later than July 15 of a presidential election year (26 O.S. Sec. 10-101.1(A)(1)). For 2024 the last day was Monday, July 15, 2024.
fields.note: GROUNDED: packet line 144 (statute: 'no later than July 15 of a presidential election year'), line 35 ('Monday, July 15, 2024'), and NASS line 869 confirmed verbatim. Fixed statutory date, not a cycle-variable number, so it remains live. The draft's '30 days' detail is correctly the Secretary's petition-sufficiency determination window (packet lines 38, 166), not a separate candidate deadline; it does not affect the July 15 filing deadline.

fields.reliedValue: Strictest defensible: treat the single-county-per-page rule as a binding mandatory format requirement — each petition page must contain only registered voters from one Oklahoma county; do not mix counties on a sheet. Do NOT assume any relief beyond this: presence/absence of a per-county or per-district signature-distribution quota is not established by the form and should be confirmed with the Election Board before relying on 'no quota.'
fields.note: GROUNDED: packet lines 113 and 163 ('Each page... shall contain the names of registered voters from a single county') confirmed verbatim. But the draft's headline conclusion ('no geographic distribution quota') is a NEGATIVE INFERENCE (proving absence of a requirement) that retrieval cannot settle. The single-county-per-page rule IS confirmed; whether a distribution minimum exists elsewhere in 26 O.S. is not. Hold and over-comply by treating the per-page county rule as strict and not assuming quota relief.

fields.reliedValue: Confirmed: circulator must be at least 18 years old and attest all signatures were signed in their presence; signers must be registered Oklahoma voters of the county listed on the page (packet lines 358, 373). STRICTEST treatment of the unconfirmed part: assume the circulator may also need to be a registered Oklahoma voter/resident — do NOT rely on the claim that there is 'no residency requirement beyond age 18' until confirmed with the Election Board.
fields.note: GROUNDED: packet line 373 ('at least eighteen (18) years old and that all signatures... were signed in my presence') and line 358 (signer voter-certification) confirmed verbatim. The age-18 and in-presence requirements are solid. But the draft's assertion that the packet imposes 'no separate Oklahoma-residency requirement on the circulator' is a NEGATIVE INFERENCE; circulator residency/voter-registration status is genuinely ambiguous from the form. Hold and assume the stricter requirement pending office confirmation.

fields.reliedValue: Notarization required in two places: (1) the candidate's Statement of Candidacy oath must be witnessed by a notary public; and (2) each petition page's circulator affidavit ('TO BE COMPLETED BY PERSON CIRCULATING PETITION') must be signed and sworn before a notary public.
fields.note: GROUNDED: packet line 152 ('the candidate's signature shall be witnessed by a notary public'), line 372 ('TO BE COMPLETED BY PERSON CIRCULATING PETITION'), and line 379 ('Signed and sworn to before me on') all confirmed verbatim. Both notarization points are directly stated in the statute text and the prescribed form. No interpretation needed.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Oklahoma uses a paper petition on the form prescribed by the Secretary of the State Election Board (packet Page 10), circulated and signed in wet ink, organized by county, with a notarized circulator affidavit. There is no online/remote or digitized-signature system for an independent presidential petition; the only non-petition alternative is the paper filing fee. Submission is by mail, private delivery service, or in person.
fields.note: GROUNDED: packet line 114 ('Candidates must use the provided petition form (Page 10)') and lines 58-60 ('may be submitted by mail, private delivery service or in person') confirmed verbatim. No evidence of any electronic/online signature path; paper-only is the correct and current capture mode.

fields.reliedValue: Yes — a filing fee may be submitted in lieu of the petition. For the current/2024 cycle the fee is $5,000 per Presidential Elector; Oklahoma has 7 electors, so the total is $35,000, payable by cashier's check or certified check to the Secretary of the State Election Board (campaign checks, personal checks, and cash are not accepted). Statutory per-elector basis: 26 O.S. Sec. 10-101.1 referencing the fee in Sec. 20-102.
fields.note: GROUNDED: packet lines 90-96 ('thirty-five thousand dollars ($35,000.00)... five thousand dollars ($5,000.00) for each Presidential Elector... seven (7) Presidential Electors... Campaign checks, personal checks, and cash cannot be accepted') and line 168 ('in lieu of the petition... a filing fee... may be submitted') confirmed verbatim. Minor currency caveat: the per-elector fee is statutorily tied to 26 O.S. Sec. 20-102; the $5,000/$35,000 figures are the packet's stated amounts for the current cycle, so they are grounded for now but worth reconfirming each cycle (captured in the SOS inquiry).

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found (per-signature ban is initiative-only under SB 1027)
compensationModel.note: For Oklahoma CANDIDATE / independent-presidential petitions, compensation of circulators is UNADDRESSED: the election code and State Election Board guidance describe circulator duties but contain no per-signature or paid-circulator rule, and Oklahoma's 2025 pay-per-signature ban plus registered-Oklahoma-voter requirement (SB 1027) apply only to INITIATIVE/REFERENDUM petitions, not candidate petitions. The candidate-petition code imposes no circulator residency/registered-voter requirement, so a national paid pool is not statutorily barred — but because per-signature pay is unaddressed (neither permitted nor prohibited) for candidate petitions, hold conservatively and do not pay per signature.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nominating petition (not an initiative/referendum): does Oklahoma law permit paying circulators per signature, and must candidate-petition circulators be Oklahoma residents or registered voters?

citations.quote: Oklahoma Governor Kevin Stitt (R) signed Senate Bill 1027 (SB 1027) on May 27, enacting changes to the state's initiative and referendum processes.
Senate Bill 1027 also prohibits signature gatherers from being paid per signature, making Oklahoma the 10th state to enact such a ban.
The bill also made Oklahoma the ninth state with an initiative process to enact a residency requirement for signature gatherers, requiring signature gatherers to be registered voters in the state.
It shall be the responsibility of the circulator to ensure that voters sign the petition page for the correct county. (See OKLA. ADMIN. CODE 230:20-9-2).

A signature on a petition in support of a Declaration of Candidacy filed by a candidate for a federal, state, or county office shall be the original, personally handwritten signature of a registered voter eligible to vote for the candidate and office.

In order to qualify for placement on the general election ballot, an independent candidate must submit a petition containing signatures equaling at least 3 percent of the total votes cast in the last general election for governor.
The Oklahoma Code did not stipulate any requirements for circulators of petitions, specifically with no residency requirements for circulators.

voterFile.cost: Free (no charge). "The Secretary of the State Election Board shall ensure that an electronic copy of the statewide voter registration list is made available, at no cost" via the OK Election Data Warehouse (EDW). No per-record or admin fee.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Eligibility-gated (effective Nov. 1, 2025, per 26 O.S. § 7-103.2). Full access to the voter registration list is limited to: bona fide Oklahoma residents who are U.S. citizens; official representatives of recognized Oklahoma political parties; candidates for Oklahoma office and their official representatives; or other persons authorized by state law. A political campaign/committee qualifies as a candidate's representative. (Non-qualifying requesters may get only "basic" EDW access, which excludes voter registration lists.)
voterFile.useRestrictions: Yes. Requestor must acknowledge it is unlawful to use the voter registration list for commercial marketing or advertising purposes (this restriction does not apply to campaigns, political parties, government officials, or public opinion pollsters), and that it is unlawful to knowingly make the Oklahoma voter list available to a foreign national, foreign commercial interest, or foreign government entity. The eligibility attestation is made under penalty of perjury. Driver license and SSN are never released; Restricted Records / Address Confidentiality Program participants are excluded.
voterFile.requestProcess: Application form + approval. Submit the "Request to Access Voter Registration List" form to info@elections.ok.gov (or by fax 405-521-6457 / mail). The form requires attestation "under penalty of perjury" that the requestor meets the legal requirements, plus a signed acknowledgment of the use restrictions. After processing, an automated email provides a link, username, and password to download the statewide CSV from the EDW. Access is valid for one year and renewable.
voterFile.note: Oklahoma provides the statewide voter registration list free, but as of Nov. 1, 2025 access is eligibility-gated under 26 O.S. § 7-103.2 — a campaign/candidate qualifies and applies for "full" EDW access via a perjury-attested form emailed to the Election Board, then downloads daily-refreshed statewide CSVs. The file includes voter name, residential address (street/city/zip), active/inactive status, party, and registration date — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters; lists/labels cannot be produced directly from the files (must import into your own software).

citations.quote: Effective Nov. 1, 2025: In compliance with 26 O.S. § 7-103.2, access to Oklahoma's voter registration list will be limited to the following: Bona fide residents of Oklahoma who are U.S. citizens, or Official representatives of recognized political parties in Oklahoma, or Candidates for offices in Oklahoma and their official representatives, or Other persons, as authorized by state law.

Users who meet the requirements of 26 O.S. § 7-103.2 and wish to access the voter registration list through the EDW may apply for "full" access to the EDW by submitting a Request to Access Voter Registration List Form to info@elections.ok.gov.

The Secretary of the State Election Board shall ensure that an electronic copy of the statewide voter registration list is made available, at no cost, to those individuals and organizations that meet the requirements of 26 O.S. § 7-103.2.
There is no charge to access the EDW, but you must submit a Request to Download Files Form. Access is valid for one year, but may be renewed.

I attest under penalty of perjury that I meet the legal requirements to obtain the Oklahoma voter registration list; I understand that it is unlawful to use the voter registration list for commercial marketing or advertising purposes. However, this restriction shall not include the use of the voter registration list by campaigns, political parties, government officials, or public opinion pollsters; I understand that is unlawful to knowingly make the Oklahoma voter list available or provide such list to a foreign national, foreign commercial interest, or foreign government entity;

Files may be downloaded, but must be imported into user software in order to sort information. Lists or labels cannot be produced directly from these files. Data is written in CSV format and is refreshed every weeknight.
Status……………………………………………………………….. "A" active, "I" inactive ... PoliticalAff ... City …Residence city/municipality ... Zip ... RegistrationDate

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — the rule binds signers only. Oklahoma's Election Code (Title 26) sets no age, registration, residency, or citizenship qualification for the person who carries an independent-presidential nominating petition. The independent-president petition statute, 26 O.S. § 26-10-101.1(A)(4), only requires that the petition bear "signatures of registered voters" and that "the fo
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: must be a registered Oklahoma voter (26 O.S. § 26-10-101.1(A)(4)(c), incorporating the 3%-of-last-gubernatorial-vote figure from § 26-1-108; each petition page limited to registered voters of a single county). CIRCULATOR: no statutory qualification at all — the Election Code is silent on who may carry/collect the petition and delegates the petition form to the Secretary of the State Electi
circulator.note: Primary source: official Oklahoma Statutes, Title 26 (Elections), downloaded from the Oklahoma Senate (oksenate.gov). The controlling provision for an independent presidential nominating petition is 26 O.S. § 26-10-101.1 ("Candidates pledged to Independent candidate for President"). It requires (a) a Statement of Candidacy filed with the Secretary of the State Election Board by July 15 of the presidential year, witnessed by a notary as to the candidate's own oath, and (b) an accompanying petitio

citations.quote: the Statement of Candidacy shall be accompanied by a petition supporting a candidate's filing, ... the form of the petition shall be prescribed by the Secretary of the State Election Board. Each page of the petition must contain the names of registered voters from a single county, the petition shall bear the same number of signatures of registered voters that is required to form a recognized political party as described in Section 1-108 of this title (26 O.S. § 26-10-101.1(A)(4))

petitions seeking recognition of a political party, in a form to be prescribed by the Secretary of the State Election Board, shall be filed with such Secretary, bearing the signatures of registered voters equal to at least three percent (3%) of the total votes cast in the last General Election for Governor. Each page of such petitions must contain the names of registered voters from a single county. (26 O.S. § 26-1-108)

A declaration of candidacy must be accompanied by: 1. A petition supporting a candidate's filing signed by not fewer than two percent (2%) of the number of registered voters in the district, county or state, as appropriate for the office sought (26 O.S. § 26-5-112(A)) — the statute conditions the SIGNERS (registered voters) but states no qualification for the person circulating the petition.

In order to qualify for placement on the general election ballot, an independent candidate must submit a petition containing signatures equaling at least 3 percent of the total votes cast in the last general election for governor. — Ballotpedia describes only signer (registered-voter) requirements and identifies no circulator eligibility rule for Oklahoma presidential petitions.

signaturesHeadline: ~23,744 valid signatures (1% of 2020 presidential vote, 2,374,321); confirm SOS-certified count
deadlineHeadline: Filing deadline Aug 25, 2026 (70th day before 11/3 election); submit by ~Aug 11 for guaranteed verification
riskNote: Core ballot-access rule is solid and grounded; treat the precise signature count and circulator eligibility details as unconfirmed (over-collect signatures; do not assume any circulator-eligibility leniency) until SOS confirms.
sosInquiries: For an independent presidential candidate filing by Individual Electors for the November 3, 2026 general election, what is the exact SOS-certified number of valid signatures required (1% of the 2020 presidential vote), and what is the 2026 guaranteed-verification submission deadline?

For a candidate Individual Electors nominating petition (not an initiative), does Oregon impose any eligibility requirement on signature circulators as to minimum age, Oregon residency, or voter registration, and must paid circulators register with the Secretary of State before circulating?

fields.reliedValue: Petition path (Individual Electors): valid signatures of active registered Oregon electors equal to not less than 1% of the total votes cast for all candidates for presidential electors at the last general election (ORS 249.735). For the 2024/2026 baseline (2020 Oregon presidential total = 2,374,321) that is approximately 23,744 (1% rounded up), but the operative requirement is the EXACT figure certified by the Secretary of State for the cycle; collect a comfortable cushion above 23,744. Names of the presidential electors must be filed with the petition. Alternative: nomination by an Assembly of at least 1,000 electors gathered in one place for no longer than 12 hours.
fields.note: All three citations confirmed verbatim: NASS PDF (lines 902-905: '...signatures of individual electors equal to not less than 1% of the total votes cast for all candidates for presidential electors...names of the presidential electors must be filed with the petition'), Ballotpedia ('...at least 1 percent of the total votes cast for presidential electors...'), Wikipedia total 2,374,321 (the official Total row). Independent re-derivation: 1% of 2,374,321 = 23,743.21, rounds up to 23,744 -> draft's ~23,744 is correct. CONSERVATIVE-HOLD because the precise operative count is set/certified by the SOS each cycle (draft itself flags this); the '~23,744' is a derived estimate, not the certified figure. The 1% legal rule itself is unambiguous; only the exact number needs SOS confirmation. Strictest defensible stance: treat 23,744 as a floor, over-collect, and verify the certified number before relying on it.

fields.reliedValue: 70th day before the general election (ORS 249.722). For the November 3, 2026 general election that date is August 25, 2026. The certificate of nomination / petition signatures and the names of presidential electors must be filed by this deadline. SOS strongly advises submitting signatures earlier for verification time (the 2024 advice was 8/13/24); the 2026 guaranteed-verification signature submission date is on or about August 11, 2026 (signatures must be received at least 10 business days before the filing deadline).
fields.note: Independently verified the date math: Election Day 2026 = Tuesday Nov 3, 2026; 70 days before = Tuesday Aug 25, 2026 (exact match). SOS 2026 calendar row confirmed: 'August 25, 2026 / Last day for nonaffiliated or minor political party candidate, other than elected incumbent...to file certificate of nomination / 249.722 / -70' (calendar lines 1514-1558; reassembled across the PDF table columns, every token verbatim). NASS 70th-day quote verbatim (PDF lines 902-905). Ballotpedia 8/13/24 advice verbatim (line 592). The Aug 11, 2026 guaranteed-verification date is corroborated in the SOS calendar (lines 1398-1400) and the manual's 10-business-day rule (lines 1066-1067). Statutory date, no interpretation.

fields.reliedValue: None for a nonaffiliated/independent presidential candidate. The 1%-of-presidential-vote petition is a single statewide total with no per-county or per-congressional-district distribution quota. Signers should sign a petition sheet designated for their county of residence for sorting/verification only, which is not a distribution requirement. The 100-signatures-per-congressional-district rule applies to major-party statewide partisan petitions-in-lieu-of-fee and to nonpartisan statewide offices (Labor Commissioner, appellate judges), NOT to the nonaffiliated presidential petition.
fields.note: Both citations confirmed. NASS quote verbatim (flat statewide 1%, no distribution language). SOS manual county-sheet quote is verbatim but split across the PDF two-column break: 'Sign a petition sheet that is designated for' (line 1903) + 'their county of residence, if applicable.' (line 1932) form one continuous bullet. Independently confirmed the draft's congressional-district caveat is correct: the 100-per-CD rule appears at manual lines 740-742 (major-party statewide partisan petition) and lines 1384-1401 (nonpartisan statewide: Labor Commissioner, appellate judges), and is absent from the Individual Electors presidential petition definition. Line 1796 confirms county sorting is a verification convenience ('sheets are sorted by county if required'), not a quota. No real interpretation needed; primary sources are unambiguous.

fields.reliedValue: Do NOT assume any circulator-eligibility leniency. Confirmed by the manual: every circulator must personally witness each signature and complete the circulator certification with a legal signature and date; an insufficient certification rejects the sheet; circulator pay status must be designated on the SEL 114 prospective-petition form. The candidate must be registered to vote as a nonaffiliated voter at least 180 days before the filing deadline (deadline to register nonaffiliated = February 26, 2026). UNVERIFIED and treated strictly: the manual does NOT affirmatively state there is no circulator age/residency/voter-registration requirement (the draft infers this from silence), and it does NOT affirmatively state that paid circulators must register with the SOS for candidate petitions. Pending SOS confirmation, assume circulators may need to meet eligibility requirements and that paid circulators may need to register.
fields.note: All three citations confirmed verbatim (manual lines 1816-1819 circulator definition; lines 917-918 prospective petition / pay status; lines 904-906 180-day nonaffiliated registration; cross-confirmed Feb 26, 2026 at quick-guide line 8 and manual line 907). 'Personally witness each signature' also verbatim (lines 1826/1830). DISAGREE / interpretationRequired because the VALUE makes two claims weaker than the sources support: (1) 'no age/residency/voter-registration requirement' is inferred from ABSENCE of a stated rule -- absence is not proof none exists (the draft openly flags this), and (2) 'Paid circulators must be registered with the SOS' is NOT affirmatively stated in the manual (the manual only references 'a paid circulator's registration' in the legal-signature definition, lines 1842/1979). Strictest defensible interpretation: do not rely on circulator leniency; assume eligibility limits and paid-circulator registration may apply until SOS confirms. Routed to sosInquiries.

fields.reliedValue: Individual Electors petition path: NO notarization. The circulator must personally witness each signature and complete a circulator certification (legal signature + date) on each signature sheet; an incomplete/insufficient certification rejects the sheet. Notarization is required only for the alternative Assembly of Electors path (SEL 115 certificate of nomination executed and notarized by the assembly's presiding officer and secretary) and for the minor-party SEL 110 form.
fields.note: All three citations confirmed verbatim: certification-by-signing quote (manual lines 1977-1978), 'Personally witness each signature collected' (lines 1826/1830, split across columns), and 'Notarized form SEL 115...executed by the presiding officer and secretary of the assembly' (lines 1056-1057). Independently confirmed the SEL 110 minor-party notarization claim (manual lines 296, 893-894, 2257). The Individual Electors path nowhere requires notarization. Correct and current; no interpretation.

fields.reliedValue: paper. The candidate files the prospective petition / certificate of nomination and gathers wet-ink signatures on Elections Division-issued paper signature-sheet templates; circulators must personally witness and certify each sheet. The manual states minor party and nonaffiliated candidates in the general election must file paper forms. ORESTAR is used only for the separate Voters' Pamphlet statement and fee-based primary/nonpartisan filings, not for collecting the nonaffiliated presidential nominating-petition signatures. No remote-online or digitized signature system covers an independent presidential petition.
fields.note: Both citations confirmed verbatim: 'Minor party or nonaffiliated candidates in the general election must file paper forms.' (manual line 379) and 'The Elections Division will provide signature sheet templates for candidates filing by individual electors' process.' (lines 920-921). Quick guide independently confirms ORESTAR is used only for the Voters' Pamphlet statement (lines 35-39), not for nominating-petition signatures. Correct; no interpretation.

fields.reliedValue: There is NO filing-fee alternative to the signature requirement for a nonaffiliated presidential candidate. Ballot access at the general election is obtained ONLY by (1) the Individual Electors petition (1% of presidential vote) or (2) an Assembly of at least 1,000 electors. No fee-in-lieu path exists. A separate optional Voters' Pamphlet filing fee exists solely to appear in the pamphlet (payable or substitutable with a separate pamphlet petition) and is not a route onto the ballot.
fields.note: All three citations confirmed: quick-guide Individual Electors quote verbatim incl. source typo 'By submit' (lines 10-12); NASS Assembly-of-1,000 quote verbatim (PDF lines 907-908); Voters'-Pamphlet pay-or-petition quote verbatim (quick-guide lines 35-39). Independently confirmed the two-and-only-two ballot routes for nonaffiliated candidates (quick-guide lines 9-13; manual Individual Electors / Assembly sections) with no fee-in-lieu option onto the ballot. The Voters' Pamphlet fee is explicitly a separate publication matter, not ballot access. Correct; no interpretation.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: NO per-signature (prohibited) — pay hourly/bonus; advocate to legalize
compensationModel.model: per-signature prohibited (candidate petitions expressly covered)
compensationModel.note: Candidate-petition compensation IS expressly addressed in Oregon. ORS 260.569 and OAR 165-014-0260 specifically prohibit paying or receiving money based on the number of "signatures a person obtains for purposes of nominating a candidate for elective public office" — i.e., per-signature pay is illegal for candidate (including independent presidential) nominating petitions. Circulators may still be paid hourly/salary/bonus, and there is no Oregon residency or voter-registration requirement for circulators (paid circulators must instead register with the SoS and complete training per ORS 250.048).
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent (unaffiliated) presidential candidate's nominating petition under ORS 249.740, may circulators be paid by hourly wage, salary, or discretionary bonus (not per signature), and what registration/training and residency requirements apply to those paid circulators?

citations.quote: A person may not pay or receive money or another thing of value based on the number of: (1) Signatures a person obtains for purposes of nominating a candidate for elective public office; or (2) Signed voter registration cards a person collects.

Prohibition on Paying or Receipt of Payment based on the Number of Signatures Obtained on an Initiative, Referendum, Candidate Nominating Petition or Voter Registration Cards ... A person may not pay or receive money or another thing of value based on the number of: signatures a person obtains for purposes of nominating a candidate for elective public office

paying an hourly wage or salary, using express minimum signature requirements (quota), terminating those who do not meet the productivity requirements, adjusting salaries prospectively relative to productivity, and paying discretionary bonuses based on reliability, longevity and productivity, provided no payments are made on a per signature basis.
whether one or more persons will be paid money or other valuable consideration for obtaining signatures

A person may not pay money or other valuable consideration to another person for obtaining signatures...unless the person obtaining the signatures: (a) Registers with the Secretary of State...and (b) Completes the training program

voterFile.cost: $500 flat for the statewide voter list (set by statute, ORS 247.945(4) and confirmed on the SOS page). A less-than-statewide (county/partial) list is $25 set-up fee plus 2.5 cents per 100 records.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — "any person." There is no eligibility gate (no candidate/committee/party requirement). Oregon law expressly directs the Secretary of State to deliver a statewide list to "any person" upon request.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Commercial use is prohibited by ORS 247.955: no person supplied a list under ORS 247.940/247.945 "shall use any information in the list for commercial purposes," except resale to candidates or political committees for political purposes only. Campaign/political use (including petition signature verification) is permitted. The file by statute excludes the elector's signature, SSN, driver license number, birth month/day, and Address Confidentiality Program participants (ORS 247.948(2)); candidates/officeholders may suppress their residence address (ORS 247.948(3)).
voterFile.requestProcess: Written/online request to the Oregon Secretary of State, Elections Division (form SEL 510, "Request for Statewide and Less Than Statewide Voter List"). The statewide electronic list is delivered via SFTP; allow 10 business days. Not an instant self-service download. Hardcopy/labels/non-electronic statewide formats are not provided by SOS — those must be requested from county elections officials.
voterFile.note: Oregon makes its statewide voter (elector) list available to anyone for a flat $500 fee, delivered electronically by SFTP from the Secretary of State after a ~10-business-day turnaround; the file includes name, residence/ballot address, party, year of birth, precinct, phone, and vote history — enough to match a petition signer to a registered voter by name and address — but it does NOT include signature images (excluded by ORS 247.948(2)(f)), so it supports name/address matching, not on-file signature comparison.

citations.quote: Any person may get an electronic copy (via SFTP) of a statewide or less than statewide voter list from the Office of the Secretary of State, Elections Division.
Fee for statewide voter list is $500. Fee to provide a less than statewide list consists of: $25 set up fee, plus 2.5 cents per 100 records provided
Allow 10 business days for our staff to complete your request.

Upon request, the Secretary of State shall deliver to any person a statewide list of electors. Except as provided in ORS 247.940 (Statewide list of electors) (4), the secretary shall charge a fee of $500 for delivering a list under this subsection.
no person to whom a list of electors is made available or supplied under ORS 247.940 or 247.945 shall use any information in the list for commercial purposes.

A person shall not be considered to use for commercial purposes any information contained in a list of electors ... if the person obtains the list of electors for the purposes of resale to candidates or political committees for political purposes only.

the following information about an elector ... shall be included in lists delivered under ORS 247.940 ... and 247.945: ... (B) ... the residence address of an elector; ... (A) The major political party or minor political party, if any, with which an elector is affiliated; ... (D) The year in which an elector was born; ... (I) Whether or not an elector voted in previous elections
the following information about an elector ... may not be disclosed by the Secretary of State or a county clerk: ... (f) The signature of an elector.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — the rule binds signers only. The Oregon candidate-petition statutes (ORS 249.061 and ORS 249.740) impose NO minimum age on the circulator. The only duties placed on the circulator are to (a) personally witness each signature and (b) certify their belief that each signer is a qualified elector. There is no statutory floor of 18 (or any age) for circulators. The "regi
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: must be an "elector" (an active registered voter) in the electoral district — for a non-affiliated/independent presidential candidate "any elector may sign" (ORS 249.076(2)), and signers provide a residence/mailing address (ORS 249.064(2)). CIRCULATOR: must only (a) personally witness each signature and (b) certify a belief that each signer is a qualified elector (ORS 249.061(4); ORS 249.7
circulator.note: For an INDEPENDENT/non-affiliated presidential candidate, Oregon's path is a "certificate of nomination made by individual electors" (ORS 249.740) requiring signatures equal to >=1% of total presidential-elector votes cast at the last presidential election. The circulator's ONLY statutory obligations are the certification duties in ORS 249.740(4) (and the parallel ORS 249.061(4) for candidate nominating petitions generally): witness each signature and believe each signer is a registered elector

citations.quote: The circulator shall certify on each signature sheet that the circulator: (a) Witnessed the signing of the signature sheet by each individual whose signature appears on the signature sheet; and (b) Believes each individual is an elector registered in the electoral district.

The circulator shall certify on each signature sheet that the circulator: (a) Witnessed the signing of the signature sheet by each individual whose signature appears on the signature sheet; and (b) Believes each individual is an elector qualified to sign the petition.

citations.field: signerVsCirculator — who may sign (signer requirement, independent candidate)
citations.quote: Any elector may sign: ... (b) A nominating petition or certificate of nomination of any nonaffiliated candidate; and (c) Nominating petitions or certificates of nomination for more than one candidate for the same office.
“Elector” means an individual qualified to vote under Article II, section 2, Oregon Constitution.

citations.field: circulatorMustBeStateResident / circulatorMustBeRegisteredVoter — affirmative confirmation circulators are unrestricted (SoS-derived)
citations.quote: Circulators must personally witness each signature collected and complete a circulator certification after witnessing all signatures collected on a sheet. Circulators may be paid or unpaid, but the signature sheet must note the pay status of the circulator. The relevant statutes do not note additional requirements that circulators must meet (including residency requirements).
Signers of candidate petitions are required to provide an original signature and residence or mailing address. ... Only active registered voters may sign any petition.

citations.field: independent presidential nomination path (individual electors, 1% signatures)
citations.quote: A certificate of nomination made by individual electors shall contain a number of signatures of electors in the electoral district equal to not less than one percent of the total votes cast in the electoral district for which the nomination is intended to be made, for all candidates for presidential electors at the most recent presidential election.

signaturesHeadline: 1,000 signatures (flat statewide aggregate) — AUTO-LIVE
deadlineHeadline: File w/ local board 60 days before general (Sep 6 in 2024) — HOLD: date recomputes each cycle
riskNote: 7 of 8 fields grounded and AUTO-LIVE; only the deadline is held because the specific calendar date is cycle-specific and one cited URL (justia §17-14-12) was unreachable, though the operative 60-day statutory rule is grounded verbatim elsewhere.
sosInquiries: For an independent presidential candidate in the [next] general election, please confirm the exact date by which signed nomination papers must be returned to the local Board of Canvassers (the 60-days-before-general date) and the separate date by which local boards must file certified papers with the Secretary of State (54 days before), so we can verify both against the official election calendar.

fields.reliedValue: 1,000 signatures of registered voters, in the aggregate (a flat statewide number, not a percentage). Per RI Gen. Laws § 17-14-7(a), nomination papers of an independent candidate for presidential elector must be signed, in the aggregate, by at least 1,000 voters. Fixed statutory figure; does not recompute per cycle.
fields.note: All three citations ground VERBATIM: findlaw §17-14-7(a) ('signed, in the aggregate, by at least one thousand (1,000) voters'); SOS Nominations page table ('President of the United States | 1000'); Ballotpedia presidential page ('This petition must contain 1,000 signatures'). Adversarial check: the §17-14-7 'for the 2020 election only' reduction to 500 applied ONLY to US Senator and Representative in Congress, NOT to presidential elector, and has expired — so president remains at 1,000. Current and correct.

fields.reliedValue: Operative candidate deadline: signed nomination papers must be returned to the local Board of Canvassers no later than 60 days before the general election (RI Gen. Laws § 17-14-11(a)). For Nov 5, 2024 that date was September 6, 2024 (matching the SOS official calendar). Separately, local boards must file certified papers with the Secretary of State no later than 54 days before the election (§ 17-14-12; Sept 12, 2024). STRICT reliance: treat the 60-day-before-general local-board filing as binding and RECONFIRM the exact calendar date against the SOS election calendar for each cycle — the specific date recomputes annually.
fields.note: Facts grounded: findlaw §17-14-11(a) ('shall be filed not later than sixty (60) days prior to the general election') VERBATIM; SOS page ('Presidential Electors | September 6') VERBATIM; §17-14-12 54-day boards-to-SOS deadline VERBATIM on findlaw. Date math confirms Nov 5 2024 −60d = Sep 6, −54d = Sep 12. Held (not AUTO-LIVE) for two reasons: (1) the cited justia URL for §17-14-12 returned a CAPTCHA and could not be grounded at the cited URL (the fact is grounded on findlaw instead) — citation-integrity gap; (2) 'September 6' is cycle-specific and recomputes, and two distinct deadlines exist (60-day candidate→local board vs 54-day local board→SOS) — relying on a stale calendar date could risk ballot access, so the durable statutory rule plus a per-cycle reconfirmation is the strictest defensible posture.

fields.reliedValue: None. No per-congressional-district or per-county signature quota; the requirement is a single statewide aggregate of 1,000 voters (RI Gen. Laws § 17-14-7(a)). A procedural per-sheet organization rule applies (not a distribution quota): each nomination-paper sheet lists one city/town at the top and may carry only signatures of voters registered in that city/town, because that sheet is verified by that locality's Board of Canvassers.
fields.note: Both citations ground VERBATIM: findlaw §17-14-7(a) (aggregate 1,000); SOS page ('Signers on a nomination paper must be from the city or town listed at the top of the nomination paper'). Adversarial check confirmed the city/town rule is an organizational/verification mechanism, not a geographic distribution minimum — there is no minimum-per-locality quota. Characterization is accurate.

fields.reliedValue: The signature collector (circulator) may be any person 18 years of age or older (410-RICR-20-00-10 §10.4(A)). No circulator residency requirement, no requirement that the circulator be a registered voter, and no restriction on paid circulators. Signers must be RI registered voters eligible to vote for the office and need not be members of the candidate's party.
fields.note: Both citations ground VERBATIM against the CURRENT regulation (amendment effective 07/22/2024): reg §10.4(A) ('A collector, who may be any person 18 years of age or older, must personally witness...'); Ballotpedia political-candidates page ('The relevant statutes do not specify any additional circulator requirements (such as residency or pay status)'). Note for downstream use: this same regulation requires the collector to execute a NOTARIZED affidavit (see witnessOrNotary). Current and correct.

fields.reliedValue: Notarized circulator affidavit required. The collector must personally witness each signature and must sign and attest BEFORE A NOTARY PUBLIC that each signer appeared personally and signed in the collector's presence; the collector must also provide their printed name, residence address, and telephone number on the nomination paper (410-RICR-20-00-10 §10.4(A), adopted under RI Gen. Laws § 17-14-10).
fields.note: Citation grounds VERBATIM in the current 07/22/2024 regulation, including the (sic) truncated phrase 'in the presence of the.' which appears in the source itself. 'shall sign and attest before a notary public' confirms notarization is mandatory, not optional witnessing. Current and correct.

fields.reliedValue: paper. In-person, paper-based process: the candidate (or authorized designee for federal/presidential papers) obtains physical nomination papers from the Department of State's Elections Division (148 W. River Street, Providence); signers sign in person with name and residence as on the voting list; the collector witnesses each signature in close physical proximity and executes a notarized affidavit; signed papers are returned to local Boards of Canvassers for manual verification. No online/remote-digital or digitized petition system exists for a presidential petition in RI.
fields.note: All three citations ground VERBATIM: SOS page ('Federal offices: You must obtain your nomination papers from the Department of State's Elections Division, 148 W. River Street, Providence, RI.'); reg ('Every voter signing a nomination paper shall sign in person with his or her name, place of residence, and street number, as it appears on the voting list.'); reg definition of 'Presence' ('close physical proximity coupled with awareness sufficient...'). No evidence of any electronic petition pathway. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: None. Rhode Island has no filing fee for ballot access and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option; qualification is by nomination papers (signatures) only.
fields.note: SOS quote grounds VERBATIM ('In order to be on the ballot, you will need to obtain a specified number of valid signatures on your nomination papers. All candidates for federal, state and local public office need nomination papers.'). Adversarial check: searched the SOS Nominations page and both Ballotpedia pages for any filing-fee or fee-in-lieu provision — none found; RI ballot access is signature-only. This is an absence-of-evidence finding but is consistent across all sources, hence AUTO-LIVE.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found; collectors need only be 18+ (no residency requirement)
compensationModel.note: For Rhode Island candidate/independent-presidential nomination papers, circulator COMPENSATION is unaddressed: neither the candidate-petition statute (RIGL 17-14-10, the affidavit of person obtaining signatures), the controlling Board of Elections regulation (410-RICR-20-00-10), nor the official SOS candidate guide says anything about paying collectors per signature, hourly, or at all — so per-signature pay is neither expressly permitted nor prohibited. Residency is clearly NOT required: a collector need only be "any person 18 years of age or older" (the statutory qualification in RIGL 17-23-12 applies only to home-rule charter petitions, not ordinary candidate petitions), so a national paid pool could legally collect.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nomination papers under R.I. Gen. Laws ch. 17-14, may a campaign compensate signature collectors on a per-signature basis (and is paying collectors hourly/salary otherwise permitted), or does any RI statute, regulation, or Board of Elections policy restrict paying nomination-paper collectors?

citations.quote: Every person who obtains signatures of voters upon nomination papers shall under oath sign the following statement: "I, __________, of the city/town of __________, under oath, make affidavit and say that the signers of the within nomination paper did so sign the paper in my presence."
"Collector" means any individual who gathers the signatures and addresses of eligible voters upon nomination papers.

A collector, who may be any person 18 years of age or older, must personally witness the signature of each person and shall sign and attest before a notary public that each person who signed the nomination papers appeared personally before him or her and signed the nomination papers in the presence of the. Said collector must have personal knowledge that each person actually signed the document and must provide on the nomination paper form the collector's printed name, residence address, and telephone number.
Any individual over the age of 18 can collect signatures. ... The candidate does not need to be the one to collect the signatures.

No person, except one qualified to sign a petition for the adoption of a home rule charter as provided in § 6 of article XIII of the Constitution of this state, shall circulate any petition, or obtain signatures on any petition ... Nor shall any person not qualified as provided in subsection (a) of this section, obtain signatures on nomination papers of any elector seeking to be a candidate at any election held for the purpose of selecting the members of a charter commission

voterFile.cost: Statewide list of registered voters: $25 via email or CD (includes three free data updates within a year of purchase); $700 if a printed printout. (Smaller scopes: Congressional District $15 email/$350 printout; State Senate/Rep district and City/Town $25 printout, free by email.) Credit-card payment adds a 3% service fee.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public. The registration list and the requests for it are public records under R.I. Gen. Laws § 17-6-9, and the SOS "List of Registered Voters Order Form" imposes no candidate/committee/party or political-use eligibility gate — any purchaser may order.
voterFile.useRestrictions: No use restriction (e.g., no commercial-use prohibition, political-use-only requirement, or anti-solicitation clause) appears on the order form or in § 17-6-9; the registration list is treated as a public record. (No use-limitation penalty was found tied to obtaining this list.)
voterFile.requestProcess: Application/order form. Complete the RI Department of State "List of Registered Voters Order Form" (purchaser contact info, scope selection, format, payment), sign and date it, and submit by email (elections@sos.ri.gov) or mail (Elections Division, 148 W. River St. Ste 1A, Providence, RI 02904). Payment (credit card +3%, check/money order payable to "State of RI", or cash) must be received before the order is processed. No notarized affidavit or sworn statement of permitted use is required by the form.
voterFile.note: Rhode Island sells the statewide "List of Registered Voters" through the Secretary of State / Elections Division order form for a flat $25 (email or CD) — cheap, but it requires the signed form plus pre-payment, so it is LOW_COST rather than a free instant download. The public "Registered Voters" data hub (datahub.sos.ri.gov) is only an aggregate Power BI dashboard, not the individual-level file.

citations.quote: Statewide (includes three (3) free data updates within a year of purchase) ... Prices Printout Disk Email / Statewide $700 $25 $25 / Congressional District $350 $15 $15 / State Senate District $25 NA Free / State Representative District $25 NA Free / City $25 NA Free / Town $25 NA Free
Completed forms can be submitted by: Email: elections@sos.ri.gov  Mail: Elections Division | 148 W. River St. Ste 1A | Providence, RI 02904

We accept the following payment methods: 1. Credit Card (3% service fee will be added) ... 2. Check or Money Order: Made payable to “State of RI” 3. Cash  We must receive payment before your order is processed.

All requests for registration lists, and the action taken on those requests by the secretary of state, shall be deemed to be public records and shall be open for inspection during regular business hours.

The active RI Board of Elections regulation 410-RICR-20-00-10 § 10.4(A) (effective 07/22/2024) states: "A collector, who may be any person 18 years of age or older, must personally witness the signature of each person..." The underlying statute (R.I. Gen. Laws § 17-1

CIRCULATOR (collector) need only be 18+, personally witness each signature, and attest under oath before a notary, providing printed name, residence address,
circulator.note: Rhode Island independent presidential candidates file nomination papers requiring at least 1,000 registered-voter signatures, due 60 days before the general election (R.I. Gen. Laws § 17-14-1 et seq.). The collector/circulator eligibility is governed by the active RI Board of Elections regulation 410-RICR-20-00-10 (effective 07/22/2024), which is the authoritative official source. It affirmatively defines the collector as "any person 18 years of age or older" and imposes NO requirement that the

Said collector must have personal knowledge that each person actually signed the document and must provide on the nomination paper form the collector's printed name, residence address, and telephone number.

The signature shall be accepted as valid if it can be reasonably identified to be the signature of the voter it purports to be.

signaturesHeadline: 10,000 valid signatures (statewide cap; 5% of registered electors but capped at 10k, which always controls)
deadlineHeadline: Noon, July 15 of general-election year (next Monday if July 15 is Sat/Sun)
riskNote: All seven fields grounded against primary sources (verbatim) and corroborated by Ballotpedia; six AUTO-LIVE. Only captureMode held: digitized signatures are permitted but the SEC explicitly warns they may be unverifiable, so rely on paper to avoid any ballot-access risk.
sosInquiries: For an independent presidential petition (10,000 signatures), will the State Election Commission accept digitized (touchscreen/stylus + DocuSign Certificate of Completion) signatures on the same footing as pen-and-ink for the full 10,000, and are there any volume, format, or assembly conditions that affect how digitized signatures are counted toward the 10,000-signature requirement?

fields.reliedValue: 10,000 valid signatures of active, registered South Carolina voters (statewide cap). Statutory basis: 5% of qualified registered electors of the office's geographic area, capped at 10,000 for any office (S.C. Code 7-11-70). For statewide President, 5% of ~3.39M registered voters (~170k) far exceeds the cap, so 10,000 always controls. Electorate baseline = registrations 120 days before the election.
fields.note: Both citations verbatim in sources (7-11-70 statute; scvotes.gov 'valid signatures of 10,000 active, registered'). Independently corroborated by Ballotpedia (5% / max 10,000) and by the SEC page's live registered-voter count of 3,392,645, confirming the cap controls. The cap is a fixed hard number; it could only stop controlling if SC registration fell below 200,000, which is impossible.

fields.reliedValue: Noon (12:00 p.m.) on July 15 of the general-election year, filed with the State Election Commission. If July 15 falls on a Saturday or Sunday, the deadline is noon the following Monday (S.C. Code 7-13-351). For Nov 2024 this was Monday, July 15, 2024.
fields.note: 7-13-351 quote verbatim in fetched statute; scvotes.gov presidential-section quote verbatim. Ballotpedia corroborates July 15. Draft correctly tracks the statute's 'Saturday or Sunday' rule (the SEC general petition section drops 'Saturday', but the statute and the presidential-specific section both include it). July 15, 2024 was indeed a Monday. Note: a Governor-declared emergency grants +5 days (7-13-351), an omitted contingency, not a defect in the standard deadline.

fields.reliedValue: None. No geographic distribution requirement. For statewide President the petition is a single statewide aggregate with no per-county or per-district minimum. Administrative formatting only: no single petition page may contain signatures of voters from different counties (S.C. Code 7-11-80(4)) — a per-page sorting rule, not a distribution quota.
fields.note: 7-11-80(4) and scvotes.gov 5% quotes both verbatim. Statute imposes no geographic spread requirement; the one-county-per-page rule is correctly characterized as formatting, not a quota.

fields.reliedValue: None specified. S.C. petition statutes (7-11-70, 7-11-80, 7-11-85) impose no circulator/collector requirements (no oath, age, residency, or voter-status). The petition form collects only signer information; there is no circulator role. Validity rests on matching each signer against county registration records.
fields.note: 7-11-80(3) quote verbatim. Confirmed no circulator role exists anywhere in 7-11-70/80/85. 'None specified' is the correct, accurate statement of SC law.

fields.reliedValue: None. No circulator affidavit, witness, or notarization requirement for the petition pages. The 7-11-80 form has only the statement of purpose and voter-signature columns. Validity is determined by county boards matching signatures against original registration applications (S.C. Code 7-11-85). (Separately the candidate self-certifies qualifications and files a Statement of Economic Interests, but the petition pages need no notary/witness.)
fields.note: 7-11-85 verification quote verbatim. No notary/witness/affidavit block exists on the petition form per 7-11-80. Accurate.

fields.reliedValue: paper (original pen-and-ink petition pages on 8.5 x 14 bond paper, filed with the State Election Commission). Photocopies are NOT accepted. The SEC permits 'digitized signatures' (human-readable, drawn on touchscreen/stylus, with an accompanying Certificate of Completion) as an OPTIONAL add-on, but expressly warns the petitioner 'assumes the risk that such differences may render the signature unverifiable,' and there is no dedicated e-petition portal. Cryptographic 'digital signatures' are NOT accepted. To never risk ballot access, treat capture as paper-primary and do not rely on digitized signatures for the bulk of the 10,000.
fields.note: All three digitized/digital quotes verbatim on scvotes.gov, so the field is fully grounded. Held because the draft's 'hybrid' label is an operational interpretation that retrieval cannot settle: the SEC explicitly disclaims reliability of digitized signatures and provides no e-petition system, so leaning on the digital path could jeopardize the ballot line. Strictest defensible mode = paper; digitized signatures are permitted-but-risky, not a true digital capture channel.

fields.reliedValue: None. No filing fee for petition candidates and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option for an independent presidential petition. Filing fees apply only to candidates seeking a party nomination by primary. (A local jurisdiction could require a fee in addition to a petition, but that does not apply to a statewide presidential petition filed with the SEC.)
fields.note: Both scvotes.gov quotes verbatim ('candidates filing by petition do not pay a filing fee'; the local-jurisdiction caveat). No fee-in-lieu path exists for the petition route; accurate.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: South Carolina's candidate-petition statutes (Title 7, Chapter 11) and the election-offenses chapter (Title 7, Chapter 25) are entirely silent on petition circulators: they neither define a circulator role nor impose any residency/voter-registration requirement, and they neither authorize nor prohibit paying circulators (per signature or otherwise). Candidate-petition circulator compensation is therefore unaddressed by any primary source found; SC also has no statewide initiative/referendum process, so there is no initiative pay-per-signature law to carry over. A widely-repeated secondary claim that SC "prohibits paid circulators" and requires county residency could not be corroborated in the actual statutes and should not be relied upon.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nominating petition under S.C. Code Title 7, Chapter 11, does South Carolina law restrict compensating petition circulators — specifically, may a campaign pay circulators per signature, and must a circulator be a South Carolina resident or registered voter?

citations.quote: SECTION 7-11-70. Nomination by petition. A candidate's nominating petition for any office in this State shall contain the signatures of at least five percent of the qualified registered electors of the geographical area of the office for which he offers as a candidate; provided, that no petition candidate is required to furnish the signatures of more than ten thousand qualified registered electors for any office.

SECTION 7-11-80. Form of nominating petition. All nominating petitions for any political office or petition of any political party seeking certification as such in the State of South Carolina shall be standardized as follows: (1) Shall be on good quality original bond paper sized 8 1/2 '' X 14''. (2) Shall contain a concise statement of purpose; in the case of nomination of candidates, the name of the candidate, the office for which he offers and the date of the election for such office shall be contained in such petition. (3) Shall contain in separate columns from left to right the following: (a) Signature of voter and printed name of voter; (b) Address of residence where registered; and (c) Precinct of voter.

SECTION 7-25-50. Bribery at elections; unlawful to accept bribery. It is unlawful for a person to procure, by the payment, delivery, or promise of money or other article of value, another to vote for or against any particular candidate or measure at any election held within this State...
South Carolina is one of the 24 states that do not have initiative and referendum. ... citizens do not have the power to initiate ballot measures at the state level.

voterFile.cost: Statewide electronic list (~3.39M records) = $2,500 (the capped maximum). Electronic per-record schedule: 1,000–5,000 = $25; 5,001–50,000 = $25 + $1 per additional 200 records; 50,001–75,000 = $275; 75,001+ = $275 + $75 per additional 25,000 records, up to $2,500 max. (District flat fees: State House $160, State Senate $300, US House $1,200.)
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Registered South Carolina voters only — "You must be a registered South Carolina voter to purchase a list (S.C. Code 7-3-20(D)(13))." Not the general public; not restricted to candidates/committees, but limited to anyone who is a registered SC voter (and prohibited commercial/solicitation uses).
voterFile.useRestrictions: Yes. Obtaining or using public records for commercial solicitation directed to any person in the State is prohibited under S.C. Code 30-2-50; a person/private entity also may not knowingly obtain or use "personal information" from a public body for commercial solicitation. PENALTY: knowing violation of 30-2-50(A) is a misdemeanor, fine up to $500 and/or up to 1 year imprisonment. (Petition-signature verification is a permitted non-commercial political use.)
voterFile.requestProcess: Purchase online through the SEC SaleableData portal (https://vrems.scvotes.sc.gov/SaleableData); electronic file delivered via online download (or flash drive/DVD by pickup or mail). First-time purchasers are advised to contact the SEC at (803) 734-9060 / elections@elections.sc.gov. No notarized affidavit indicated, but eligibility (registered SC voter) gates access.
voterFile.note: South Carolina sells the statewide voter file online through the State Election Commission, but only to registered SC voters; the electronic file contains name, residential address, registration number/date, precinct, districts, and voter participation history — everything needed to match petition signers. A full statewide electronic list costs the capped maximum of $2,500.

citations.quote: You must be a registered South Carolina voter to purchase a list (S.C. Code 7-3-20(D)(13)).

A new fee schedule for electronic lists was implemented effective March 1, 2022, to lower the base fee from $160 to $25. The fee for electronic U.S. House District lists increased from $1,000 to $1,200.
75,001 and more:  $275 + $75 for each additional 25,000 records or fraction thereof (up to $2,500 max)

Includes: Name, Address, Race, Gender, Date of Birth, Voter Registration Number and Date Registered, County, Precinct, Voting Districts, and Voter Participation History (past two statewide primaries and general elections.
Delivery: Electronic download (online) or flash drive or DVD (pickup or mail)
Obtaining or using public records for commercial solicitation directed to any person in this State is prohibited under South Carolina Code Section 30-2-50 .

PENALTY: A person knowingly violating the provisions of 30-2-50(A) is guilty of a misdemeanor and, upon conviction, must be fined an amount not to exceed five hundred dollars or imprisoned for a term not to exceed one year, or both.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — South Carolina's nominating-petition statute (S.C. Code Title 7, Chapter 11, Article 1) imposes NO age requirement on the circulator/collector. The age and qualification rules bind only the SIGNER (must be a "qualified registered elector"). SC's petition form (§7-11-80) has no circulator affidavit or witness block at all, so no minimum age is statutorily fixed for t
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: must be a "qualified registered elector" of the geographical area of the office (§7-11-70); signatures are checked against the county voter-registration rolls (§7-11-85). CIRCULATOR: South Carolina law imposes NO requirements — there is no circulator/witness affidavit on the petition form (§7-11-80), and no age, residency, citizenship, or voter-registration requirement for the collector an
circulator.note: South Carolina's complete nominating-petition law is S.C. Code §§7-11-70 (signature count), 7-11-80 (petition form), and 7-11-85 (signature verification). All three address only the SIGNER: §7-11-70 requires "qualified registered electors" (5% of the office's electorate, capped at 10,000); §7-11-80 specifies the petition form columns (signature of voter, printed name, address of residence where registered, precinct) — notably it contains NO circulator affidavit, witness certification, or oath bl

citations.quote: SECTION 7-11-70. Nomination by petition. A candidate's nominating petition for any office in this State shall contain the signatures of at least five percent of the qualified registered electors of the geographical area of the office for which he offers as a candidate; provided, that no petition candidate is required to furnish the signatures of more than ten thousand qualified registered electors for any office.

SECTION 7-11-80. Form of nominating petition. ... (3) Shall contain in separate columns from left to right the following: (a) Signature of voter and printed name of voter; (b) Address of residence where registered; and (c) Precinct of voter. (4) No single petition page shall contain the signatures of registered voters from different counties. (5) All signatures of registered voters shall be numbered consecutively.

SECTION 7-11-85. Verification of petition; bases for rejection of petitioners. Every signature on a petition ... must be checked for validity by the respective county board of voter registration and elections against the signatures of the voters on the original applications for registration on file in the board office. ... The signature of a voter may only be rejected if it is illegible and cannot be found in the records of the county board of voter registration and elections is missing from the petition, or is not that of the voter, or if the registration of the voter has been deleted ...

citations.field: corroboration — no circulator section, only form/signer requirements
citations.quote: A petition must adhere to the following formatting requirements: 1. The petition must be printed be on good quality original bond paper sized 8 1/2 by 14 inches. 2. The petition must include a concise statement of purpose ... 3. The petition must contain the following information in separate columns (from left to right): Signature of voter and printed name of voter; Address of residence where registered; Precinct of voter. 4. No single petition page can contain the signatures of registered voters from different counties. 5. All signatures must be numbered consecutively.

An independent candidate must be nominated by petition. The petition must contain signatures equaling at least 5 percent of the qualified registered electors in the geographical area of the office being sought.
