signaturesHeadline: 3,502 valid sigs (1% of combined 2022 gov vote, 350,166) — AUTO-LIVE
deadlineHeadline: First Tue in Aug, 5:00 PM CT (Aug 6, 2024) — AUTO-LIVE
riskNote: 6 of 7 fields AUTO-LIVE on grounded primary statute + SOS sources; only circulatorEligibility is CONSERVATIVE-HOLD because SD's resident-circulator rule is constitutionally contested and 12-1-4 was amended (SL 2025, partly eff. 1/1/2026) so the 2024-cycle residency text is uncertain — strictest path (SD-resident, 18+ circulators) does not risk ballot access.
sosInquiries: For the 2024 general election cycle, did South Dakota enforce the SDCL 12-1-3(12)/12-1-4 requirement that a nominating-petition circulator be a South Dakota resident (and what residency definition, e.g. the 30-consecutive-day standard, applied), given federal litigation over resident-circulator requirements? Please confirm whether a non-resident circulator's signatures would be rejected for an independent presidential petition.

fields.reliedValue: 3,502 valid signatures for the 2024 cycle (1% of the 350,166 total combined vote cast for Governor at the 2022 election). Recomputes each cycle off the most recent certified gubernatorial total. SDCL 12-7-7.
fields.note: Both citations grounded verbatim: SDCL 12-7-7 ('not less than one percent of the total combined vote cast for Governor at the last certified gubernatorial election') and the SOS 2024 page ('Independent: 3,502 signatures (1% of the total vote for governor in 2022 - 350,166)'). Re-derived: 1% of 350,166 = 3,501.66 -> 3,502. Ballotpedia's dedicated presidential page independently confirms the same combined-gubernatorial-vote basis (distinct from the per-party 1% basis used for partisan candidates). Operative SOS figure is correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: Certificate of nomination with nominating petitions, declaration of candidacy, and VP certification must be filed with the Secretary of State by the first Tuesday in August at 5:00 p.m. central time before the election (August 6, 2024 for the 2024 general). Mailing by registered mail by the deadline is timely. Petitions may not be circulated before January 1 of the election year. SDCL 12-7-7; 12-6-4.1.
fields.note: SDCL 12-7-7 grounded verbatim ('after December thirty-first and by the first Tuesday in August at 5:00 p.m. central time before the election ... If the certificate of nomination is mailed by registered mail ... it is timely submitted'). SDCL 12-6-4.1 grounded verbatim at its section URL ('No petition or certificate of nomination ... may be circulated prior to the first day of January'). First Tuesday in August 2024 = Aug 6, 2024 (Aug 1, 2024 was a Thursday). Confirmed by Ballotpedia ('filed by 5:00 p.m. on the first Tuesday in August'). Note: the draft cites the chapter URL /Statute/12-6 for the 12-6-4.1 quote; the text lives at section 12-6-4.1, which I fetched and confirmed verbatim.

fields.reliedValue: None. Single statewide total (1% of the statewide combined gubernatorial vote); no per-congressional-district or per-county distribution requirement for a statewide independent presidential candidate. Filed with the Secretary of State because the office is voted on in more than one county.
fields.note: SDCL 12-7-7 grounded verbatim; the statute imposes only a single statewide 1%-of-combined-gubernatorial-vote total with no geographic distribution. Reviewed full chapter 12-6 (incl. 12-6-7) and found per-county/per-district splits only for legislative/county offices and vote-center counties, not for statewide/presidential petitions. No distribution requirement applies.

fields.reliedValue: STRICTEST: Use only circulators who are South Dakota residents (actual fixed permanent dwelling, lives/usually sleeps there for the requisite consecutive days per SDCL 12-1-4) and at least 18 years old (SDCL 12-1-3(12)). Signers must be registered voters entitled to vote for the office; a party-affiliated voter may sign an independent's petition only if they have not previously signed another petition for that office (SDCL 12-6-8). Do NOT rely on non-resident circulators.
fields.note: All quoted text grounded verbatim: SDCL 12-1-3(12) ('a resident of the State of South Dakota as defined under 12-1-4, who is at least eighteen years of age'), 12-1-4 residency definition, and 12-6-8 signer-eligibility/cross-party provision. HOLD for two reasons: (1) SD's resident-circulator requirement has a known constitutional vulnerability (federal courts have enjoined SD resident-circulator requirements in the ballot-measure context), so whether a non-resident circulator's signatures can lawfully be rejected is a legal-interpretation question retrieval cannot settle; (2) SDCL 12-1-4 was amended by SL 2025 ch 57 and ch 58 (the latter eff. Jan 1, 2026), so the current 'thirty consecutive days' text may not be the exact version in force for the 2024 cycle the draft labels it for. The over-comply path (SD-resident, 18+ circulators) never risks ballot access, so it is the relied value; sent an SOS inquiry to confirm enforcement and the applicable residency standard.

fields.reliedValue: Circulator certification under oath (verification by the circulator on each self-contained sheet), not a per-signer notarized affidavit. The circulator's verification may NOT be notarized by the candidate the petition nominates. Falsely attesting is a Class 6 felony. SDCL 12-6-8; 12-1-3(11).
fields.note: Grounded verbatim at SDCL 12-6-8 ('The petition must be verified under oath by the persons circulating the petition. The verification ... may not be notarized by the candidate whom the petition is nominating. Any person ... who falsely attests to the verification is guilty of a Class 6 felony') and SDCL 12-1-3(11) (form contains 'the verification of the circulator'; multi-sheet petitions each separately verified). Correct and current. (Draft cites chapter URL /Statute/12-6; the operative text is at section 12-6-8, fetched and confirmed verbatim.)

fields.reliedValue: paper. Physical paper nominating petitions, wet-ink signatures by registered voters, verified under oath by a resident circulator on each self-contained sheet. No remote/online or in-person-digitized e-signature system exists for SD candidate petitions. SDCL 12-1-3(11); 12-6-8.
fields.note: Grounded verbatim: SDCL 12-6-8 ('The circulator or the individual who signs the petition shall add the address at which the individual is currently registered to vote, the county ..., and the date of signing'; 'A nominating petition for any election shall be a self-contained sheet of paper') and 12-1-3(11) (each sheet self-contained and separately verified). The self-contained-paper-sheet + handwritten signer info + circulator oath structure confirms paper-only; no statutory e-signature petition portal for SD candidate petitions. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: None. No filing fee in lieu of or in addition to the signature requirement for an independent presidential candidate. Ballot access is obtained solely by filing the certificate of nomination with the required petition signatures. SDCL 12-7-7; chapter 12-6.
fields.note: SDCL 12-7-7 grounded verbatim; it conditions nomination only on filing the certificate with the required petition signatures and prescribes no fee alternative. Reviewed full chapter 12-6 and found no filing-fee-in-lieu provision for nominating petitions. Absence-of-fee-alternative independently confirmed; no contrary provision located.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found (per-signature ban SDCL 12-13-28 applies only to ballot measures); circulator must be a SD resident
compensationModel.note: For CANDIDATE/independent nominating petitions, South Dakota law does NOT address circulator compensation at all — there is no candidate-petition per-signature statute and the Secretary of State's candidate-circulation guide is silent on pay. The only per-signature prohibition, SDCL 12-13-28, applies expressly and solely to ballot measures (initiated measures, referred laws, constitutional amendments), so it does NOT control candidate petitions; per-signature pay for candidate circulators is therefore unaddressed (not affirmatively permitted). Residency IS clearly required for candidate-petition circulators: they must be SD residents and 18+ (registration not required).
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an INDEPENDENT presidential candidate's nominating petition (not a ballot measure), may a campaign pay petition circulators on a per-signature basis, or is per-signature pay limited to hourly/salary/bonus, and does any compensation restriction in Title 12 apply to candidate petitions as opposed to initiated measures under SDCL 12-13-28?

citations.quote: No person may employ, reward, or compensate any person to circulate a petition for an initiated measure, referred law, or proposed amendment to the South Dakota Constitution based on the number of registered voters who signed the petition. Nothing in this section prohibits any person from employing a petition circulator based on one of the following practices: (1) Paying an hourly wage or salary; ... (4) Paying discretionary bonuses based on reliability, longevity, and productivity. Any violation of this section is a Class 2 misdemeanor.
A petition circulator must be a resident of the state of South Dakota who is at least eighteen years of age. They are not required to be a registered voter.

"Petition circulator," a resident of the State of South Dakota as defined under § 12-1-4, who is at least eighteen years of age who circulates nominating petitions or other petitions for the purpose of placing candidates or issues on any election ballot

voterFile.cost: $225 for the electronic statewide data file of all registered voters (SDCL 12-4-39(5)(f) cap). Paper statewide list up to $1,000; legislative district up to $100; county/subdivision up to $50; partial/update lists $75-$100. Make check payable to "SD Secretary of State." Note: an Aug 12, 2025 administrative-rules hearing was scheduled to revise this cost structure, so confirm the current electronic-file fee with the SOS Elections office.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Available to the public but issuance is conditioned on a signed permitted-use certification and disclosure of the political/non-commercial user. The request form requires naming "the candidate, ballot question or party committee utilizing the voter registration file." Statewide lists are pulled only by the Secretary of State's office; county/legislative/municipal/school sublists must be requested from the relevant county auditor.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Strict, statutory (SDCL 12-4-41): voter registration information "may not be sold, may not be used for any commercial purpose, and may not be placed for unrestricted access on the internet." Crucially, "'commercial purpose' does not include campaign or political polling activities" — so campaign/petition signature-verification use is permitted. Penalty: Class 1 misdemeanor plus a civil penalty up to $2,000 per violation. The requester must sign a sworn acknowledgment of these limits (no electronic signature font allowed).
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request via the official "South Dakota Voter Registration File—Data Request Form" (PDF), emailed/mailed/faxed to the SOS Elections Division (elections@state.sd.us; SD Secretary of State, Attn: Elections, 500 East Capitol, Ste 204, Pierre, SD 57501; phone 605-773-3537, fax 605-773-6580), with payment and a signed permitted-use statement under SDCL 12-4-41. Not an instant online download; the file is pulled and emailed/provided by SOS staff.
voterFile.note: South Dakota sells a statewide electronic voter registration file for $225 (statutory cap, SDCL 12-4-39(5)(f)) via a mailed/emailed/faxed request form requiring a signed non-commercial/permitted-use certification (SDCL 12-4-41); campaign and petition-verification use is explicitly allowed. The underlying county master file (SDCL 12-4-9) contains each voter's registration-card info, residential address, status, and last-voted date — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters — and is itself open to public inspection free of charge (SSN/DL number/birth month-day excluded), but the consolidated statewide data file comes only from the SOS at the stated fee. A rules hearing set for Aug 12, 2025 may have changed the fee, so verify the current electronic-file price before purchase.

citations.quote: Pursuant to SDCL 12-4-39, the fees for voter lists are as follows... (a) One thousand dollars per paper copy of a list of the registered voters in the state; ... (f) Two hundred twenty-five dollars for an electronic data file containing a list of the registered voters in the state.

Any information obtained from the statewide voter registration file or any county voter registration file may not be sold, may not be used for any commercial purpose, and may not be placed for unrestricted access on the internet. For the purposes of this section, "commercial purpose" does not include campaign or political polling activities. ... Any violation of this section is a Class 1 misdemeanor. In addition to any criminal sanctions, the court may impose a civil penalty not to exceed two thousand dollars for each violation.

If you are purchasing a statewide list email, mail or fax this form to: elections@state.sd.us; South Dakota Secretary of State, Attention: Elections, 500 East Capitol, Ste 204, Pierre, SD 57501; phone 605-773-3537 or fax 605-773-6580. **If you are paying by check, please make payable to SD Secretary of State for ALL purchases**
Please list the name of candidate, ballot question or party committee utilizing the voter registration file:

The county auditor shall maintain and safeguard a digital master registration file that contains: (1) The information from the voter registration card of each individual registered to vote in the county; (2) The date of the last election at which the individual has voted... The master registration file must be open to public inspection, free of charge. An individual's social security number, driver license number, South Dakota nondriver identification number, or month and day of birth is not open to public inspection.
The South Dakota Secretary of State's Office will hold an administrative rules hearing on August 12, 2025, to review and discuss proposed changes to the cost structure for voter file lists.

Purchase the South Dakota Voter Registration File ... Please note: If you are purchasing a county, legislative, school, municipality or any other type of list, please contact the county auditor in that jurisdiction for that list. Statewide lists are pulled by the secretary of state's office.

circulator.ageRule: SDCL 12-1-3(12) defines a "petition circulator" as a SD resident "who is at least eighteen years of age." The official SoS "Circulating a Nominating Petition" guide restates this under "Circulator Requirements": "Must be at least eighteen years of age and a resident of the state of South Dakota." The 18+ minimum binds the circulator directly (not merely the signer).
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: must be a registered voter (active or inactive) in SD in the jurisdiction for which the petition is circulated (SDCL 12-4-34; 12-6-8). CIRCULATOR: must be a SD resident and at least 18 years old, but EXPRESSLY does NOT need to be a registered voter (SDCL 12-1-3(12); SoS guide). So the "registered voter" requirement binds only the signer; the circulator's only hard requirements are age 18+
circulator.note: South Dakota's statutory definition of "petition circulator" in SDCL 12-1-3(12) governs candidate nominating petitions (the umbrella definition cited by the SoS for candidate petitions, including independent presidential petitions under SDCL ch. 12-7). It requires the circulator to be (1) a resident of the State of South Dakota and (2) at least eighteen years of age. The current official SoS "Circulating a Nominating Petition" guide breaks the rules into "Circulator Requirements" vs "Signer Requ

citations.quote: (12) "Petition circulator," a resident of the State of South Dakota as defined under § 12-1-4, who is at least eighteen years of age who circulates nominating petitions or other petitions for the purpose of placing candidates or issues on any election ballot;

citations.field: circulatorMustBeRegisteredVoter / circulatorMinAge / circulatorMustBeStateResident (official SoS candidate-petition guide)
citations.quote: Circulator Requirements: Must be at least eighteen years of age and a resident of the state of South Dakota. Does not need to be a registered voter. Must personally witness each signature on the petition being circulated.
Signer Requirements - Must be a registered voter, active or inactive, in the state of South Dakota in the jurisdiction for which the petition is circulated (SDCL 9-13-9).

(1) "Petition circulator," any resident of the State of South Dakota who is at least eighteen years of age and who circulates a nominating petition or other petition for the purpose of placing a candidate or issue on any election ballot;

citations.field: litigation caveat: enjoined residency requirement was for BALLOT-QUESTION petitions (SDCL 2-1-1.3), not candidate nominating petitions
citations.quote: 2020 SB 180 rewrote SDCL 2-1-1.3 to define “petition circulator” as an adult who has resided in South Dakota for at least 30 days. In January, the state agreed with the plaintiffs who sued over 2020 SB 180 that it cannot and will not enforce that residency requirement... but one does not have to be a registered voter to circulate a petition.

citations.field: independent presidential candidate petition is the relevant vehicle (SDCL ch. 12-7)
citations.quote: An independent candidate for President shall file a declaration of candidacy and a certification of the candidate's selection for Vice President with the secretary of state with the candidate's nominating petition.

signaturesHeadline: 275 signatures (25 × 11 electors), statewide, no distribution — VERIFIED
deadlineHeadline: Noon CT, 3rd Thursday in August (Aug 15, 2024) — physical receipt, no postmark — VERIFIED
riskNote: Core ballot-access facts (275 sigs, Aug deadline, paper, no fee) are grounded and independently corroborated; two negative-fact fields (circulator eligibility, no notary) rest on statutory silence — circulator field has zero citations (BLOCKED) and notary held conservative; confirm both with TN SOS before relying on unrestricted circulators or unwitnessed pages.
sosInquiries: Does Tennessee impose any age, residency, or voter-registration requirement on a person who circulates or collects signatures for an independent presidential nominating petition under TCA 2-5-101, or may a non-resident/paid circulator collect signatures?

Does an independent presidential nominating petition under TCA 2-5-101 require any circulator affidavit, oath, witness, or notarization on each petition sheet, or is the signer's residence address the only verification requirement?

fields.reliedValue: 275 signatures (25 registered voters per elector × 11 electors). Signers must be registered voters anywhere in Tennessee; no per-county or per-district distribution. Candidate must also sign. Recomputes after each census-driven reapportionment of TN's electoral votes.
fields.note: Both citations verbatim: FindLaw TCA 2-5-101(b)(1) ('twenty-five (25) or more registered voters for each elector allocated to the state'; 'must designate the full number of electors') and SOS guide ('at least two hundred seventy-five (275) voters registered anywhere within the State'). Independently corroborated by Ballotpedia (lines 98, 229: '25 voters per state elector (275 total)') and that TN was allocated 11 electoral votes in 2012/2016/2020/2024. Math 25×11=275 is current through 2030 census cycle. Per-elector formula recompute claim is correct.

fields.reliedValue: 12:00 noon Central time on the third Thursday in August of the election year (Aug 15, 2024 for that cycle). Original petition plus one certified duplicate must be physically received by the deadline; a postmark does not suffice. If the deadline falls on a Saturday, Sunday, or state holiday it moves to noon on the previous business day (TCA 2-5-101(g)(2)). Recomputes each cycle.
fields.note: SOS quotes verbatim ('third Thursday in August'; 'A postmark will not suffice'). TCA 2-5-101(g)(2) text confirmed verbatim on FindLaw: deadline on Sat/Sun/state holiday moves to noon previous business day. Ballot Access News (2024-08-13) and Ballotpedia independently confirm Aug 15, 2024 and the third-Thursday rule (changed from third Tuesday in 2021). Third-Thursday-in-August derives from the general (a)(1) rule (third Thursday of third calendar month before a November election) and is stated explicitly in the SOS guide.

fields.reliedValue: No distribution requirement on petition signatures — the 275 signers may be registered voters anywhere in Tennessee. A separate distribution rule applies only to the elector slate: 11 electors total, 1 resident from each of the 9 congressional districts plus 2 at-large electors who may reside anywhere in the state.
fields.note: Both SOS quotes verbatim ('voters registered anywhere within the State'; '1 elector from each of Tennessee's 9 congressional districts, and 2 additional electors who may be residents of any part of the state'). The signer-vs-elector distinction is explicit in the guide, not an inference. Ballotpedia corroborates 9 congressional districts and 11 electors. Structural fact, not a contested legal interpretation.

fields.reliedValue: none
fields.note: Field has ZERO citations, so it cannot be grounded per gate rules. The assertion (statute/guide silent on circulator qualifications) is consistent with the full TCA 2-5-101 text I read and with the SOS guide — no circulator provision appears — but a negative claim with no supporting citation is unverifiable as drafted. Strictest posture: do NOT assume circulators are unrestricted; confirm with TN SOS before using non-resident or paid circulators. SOS inquiry added.

fields.reliedValue: As read, TCA 2-5-101 and the SOS guide impose no per-sheet witness/notary/circulator-affidavit requirement; the only signer-verification rule is that each signer include the residence address shown on the signer's voter registration card (TCA 2-5-101(b)(2)). TREAT AS UNCONFIRMED until the TN SOS confirms no notarization/oath is required, since this rests on statutory silence.
fields.note: Cited quote is verbatim from TCA 2-5-101(b)(2) ('must include the address of the signer's residence as shown on the signer's voter registration card in order for that person's signature to be counted'). I confirmed the full statute text contains no witness/notary/affidavit provision. BUT the cited quote only establishes the signer-address rule; the 'no notary required' conclusion is an inference from statutory silence that retrieval cannot fully settle — hence held conservatively. SOS inquiry added.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Physical paper petition only: candidate picks up the petition from the Division of Elections (in person or by mail) and files the wet-ink original plus one certified duplicate by the deadline; a postmark does not suffice and faxed/emailed documents are not accepted. No remote-online or in-person-digitized e-signature system exists for this petition.
fields.note: Both SOS quotes verbatim (90-day pick-up window, in person or mailed; 'A postmark will not suffice'; certified-duplicate photocopy definition). Physical-receipt requirement plus the write-in section's 'No faxed or emailed documents will be accepted' support paper-only. No e-signature pathway found in any source. Current factual matter.

fields.reliedValue: None. No filing fee for an independent presidential nominating petition, and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option. The petition signatures are the sole path to the general-election ballot.
fields.note: SOS quote 'There are no filing fees.' is verbatim; filing-location quote (note the source's own 'with with' typo) is verbatim. No fee-in-lieu provision appears in TCA 2-5-101 or the SOS guide. The 'no fee-in-lieu' claim is supported by the affirmative 'no filing fees' statement plus absence of any alternative path; uncontested.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: Tennessee's candidate-petition statute (TCA § 2-5-101) sets only the signature counts (an independent presidential candidate's petition needs the candidate plus 25 registered voters per allocated elector — 275 total) and its sole "prohibited acts" subsection (f) addresses dual qualification, not paying signature gatherers; nothing in the candidate election code, the Secretary of State / CTAS guidance, or Ballotpedia addresses whether candidate-petition circulators may be paid, paid per signature, or must be Tennessee residents. Ballotpedia states verbatim that "The relevant statutes do not address petition circulator requirements," so candidate-petition compensation is genuinely UNADDRESSED — do not carry over any initiative/ballot-measure per-signature rule, and treat per-signature pay as not-clearly-permitted pending confirmation.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent candidate for President, does Tennessee law permit a campaign to pay people who circulate the nominating petition on a per-signature basis (versus hourly/salary), and must a paid petition circulator be a Tennessee resident or registered voter?

citations.quote: (b)(1) Nominating petitions shall be signed by the candidate and twenty-five (25) or more registered voters who are eligible to vote to fill the office. Nominating petitions for independent presidential candidates shall be signed by the candidate and twenty-five (25) or more registered voters for each elector allocated to the state. Each independent candidate must designate the full number of electors allocated to the state.

(f)(1) It is unlawful for any person to qualify as a candidate in a primary election with more than one (1) political party in which such person seeks the same office. (2) It is unlawful for any person to qualify as an independent candidate and as a primary candidate for the same office in the same year.
The relevant statutes do not address petition circulator requirements.
An independent presidential candidate must petition for placement on the general election ballot. This petition must contain 275 signatures.
the nominating petition must be signed by the candidate as well as a minimum of 25 or more registered voters who are eligible to fill the office.

voterFile.cost: Low flat fee, NOT free. Uniform pricing set by the State Election Commission (per Davidson County/Nashville Election Commission, updated Aug 4, 2025): Computer Disk = $40 setup fee (100,000 voters or less) / $75 setup fee (100,001+ voters); Computer Printouts = $30 per 1,000 voters. The full statewide file (~4.5M registrants) on disk therefore falls in the $75 setup-fee tier. The statewide list compiled by the Coordinator of Elections is "sold at a price established by the secretary of state" (TCA 2-2-138(e)); that statewide-specific figure is not separately published online — confirm exact amount with the Division of Elections when ordering.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Political use only. Available to any person — but ONLY upon certifying on a state-provided form that the list "will be used for political purposes only" (TCA 2-2-138(e)). Candidates, campaigns, committees, and parties qualify; general commercial/non-political requesters do not. Records exclude Social Security and driver-license numbers, and "Safe at Home" program participants appear only by an ID number.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Political-purposes-only restriction is mandatory and is the gating condition. A false certification of permitted use is a Class B misdemeanor punishable by a $500 fine (TCA 2-2-138(d)(2)). Vendors with access to the data may use it only to service the election commission/coordinator; non-authorized vendor use is a Class B misdemeanor and grounds for decertification (TCA 2-2-138(b)(2)). The data remains the property of the county election commission and the state.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request to the Division of Elections / State Coordinator of Elections (for the statewide list) or a county election commission (for county lists), accompanied by a signed certification/affidavit form (provided by the State Election Commission) attesting the list will be used for political purposes only, plus payment. No instant online self-service download is published on the SOS Elections site; ordering is by direct contact (e.g., Davidson County directs requesters to contact the election commission). Delivered as a non-proprietary, non-encrypted data file (disk/electronic media) or paper printout.
voterFile.note: Tennessee sells, rather than freely posts, its voter file; access is gated on a political-use certification (TCA 2-2-138), so it is RESTRICTED with a low fee.

citations.quote: Such list, and any other voter registration information such as voter history, if compiled, shall be available for purchase for a price not to exceed the cost of production. This list shall be available to any person who certifies on a form provided by the state election commission that such list will be used for political purposes. The state election commission shall establish a uniform cost for this information.

Any list of registered voters compiled by the coordinator of elections shall be sold at a price established by the secretary of state. ... This list shall be available for purchase to any person who certifies on a form provided by the state election commission that such list will be used for political purposes only.
A false certification made pursuant to this subsection (d) is a Class B misdemeanor, punishable only by a fine of five hundred dollars ($500).

As required by Tennessee election law, voter registration data may be purchased for political purposes. Uniform pricing as established by the State Election Commission is as follows: Computer Disk $40.00 – computer set up fee (100,000 voters or less) $75.00 – computer set up fee (100,001 voters or more) Computer Printouts $30.00 – paper list (per 1,000 voters)
Voter registration records are public records, open to inspection by any citizen of Tennessee (excluding social security and driver's license numbers).

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement — rule binds signers only. Tennessee's nominating-petition statutes (T.C.A. §§ 2-5-101, 2-5-102, 2-5-103, 2-1-107) impose no age requirement on the person who collects signatures. The statutory petition form prescribed in § 2-5-102 contains no circulator/witness affidavit, oath, or verification section at all — only the election-official issuance block, the candidate
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER: must be a registered voter whose residence address matches the address on their voter registration card (T.C.A. § 2-5-101(b) and § 2-1-107); for an independent presidential petition, 275 such registered voters total (25 per elector). CIRCULATOR: not defined or regulated anywhere in the petition statutes — no registration, citizenship, residency, age, or affidavit requirement; the statutory
circulator.note: Tennessee's independent presidential nominating petition requires 275 registered voters' signatures (25 per each of the 11 electors), filed by the candidate by noon on the third Thursday in August (T.C.A. § 2-5-101; SoS Qualifying Procedures). The statute and the official SoS guide are entirely SILENT on who may circulate/collect signatures. Crucially, the prescribed petition form (T.C.A. § 2-5-102) contains NO circulator affidavit, witness attestation, oath, or verification — signatures are sim

citations.quote: (b)(1) Nominating petitions shall be signed by the candidate and twenty-five (25) or more registered voters who are eligible to vote to fill the office. Nominating petitions for independent presidential candidates shall be signed by the candidate and twenty-five (25) or more registered voters for each elector allocated to the state. ... (2) The signer of a petition must include the address of the signer's residence as shown on the signer's voter registration card in order for that person's signature to be counted.

citations.field: circulatorAgeRule / no circulator affidavit in prescribed form
citations.quote: (a) Nominating petitions shall be in substantially the following form: ... This petition was issued by ____ (signature of administrator of elections, deputy or election commissioner) ... NOMINATING SIGNATURES (Must be registered voters who are eligible to vote to fill this office) Signature of Voter | Address of Residence as Shown on Voter Registration

(a) Any person signing a petition required under this title, whether for nomination of a candidate, for a referendum or for any other purpose, shall include the address of such person's residence. The signer of a petition must include the address of such person's residence as shown on such person's voter registration card in order for that person's signature to be counted... (b) Any person who signed a permanent registration card shall sign any petition signed under this title

citations.field: no verification/witness step (officials certify, no circulator)
citations.quote: (a) Each independent or primary candidate for an office elected by the voters of the entire state shall file the candidate's original nominating petition in the office of the state election commission and a certified duplicate with the coordinator of elections... (b) The chair of the state election commission shall... certify to the chairs of the county election commissions the names of all candidates who have qualified under this section

citations.field: official SoS guide silent on circulators
citations.quote: A candidate must obtain the signatures and addresses of at least two hundred seventy-five (275) voters registered anywhere within the State of Tennessee. ... The original petition and one certified duplicate must be filed by the deadline. ... The qualifying deadline to file the petition is 12:00 noon Central time on the third Thursday in August.

signaturesHeadline: 1,000 valid registered-voter signatures statewide (flat statutory number) + $500 fee
deadlineHeadline: DISPUTED: signatures due June 15 (20A-9-502(5)(a)); Aug 15 only for running-mate/VP filing — treat June 15 as binding for signatures
riskNote: Only real exposure is the deadline: statute sets June 15 for signature submission while 504 sets Aug 15 for the running-mate/VP step; collect and submit signatures by June 15 to never risk the ballot line. Circulator non-residency relied on a 2015 snapshot; current statute dropped the residency clause but confirm before staffing non-resident circulators.
sosInquiries: For an unaffiliated/independent candidate for President of the United States, what is the firm deadline to SUBMIT the 1,000 signatures / certificate-of-nomination petition to the county clerks and to FILE the certificate with the Lieutenant Governor — is it the June 15 deadline in Utah Code 20A-9-502(5)(a)/20A-9-503(1), or the August 15 date in 20A-9-504(2), which on its face governs only selection of a running mate and the VP's 20A-9-202(7) filing?

Under the current (post-2024) version of Utah Code 20A-9-502, is there any residency requirement for the person who circulates and signs the verification on an unaffiliated U.S. President certificate-of-nomination petition, or may a non-resident circulate and verify?

fields.reliedValue: 1,000 signatures of registered voters statewide — a flat statutory number set by Utah Code 20A-9-502(5)(a)(ii)(A) for an office filled by the voters of the entire state (President); not a percentage and does not recompute by cycle. Each must be a VALID signature certified by the county clerks.
fields.note: Both quotes ground verbatim. FindLaw 20A-9-502(5)(a)(ii)(A) (current as of Jan 1, 2025) sets 'at least 1,000 registered voters residing within the state' for statewide offices; Ballotpedia independently confirms 1,000 for an independent presidential candidate. President is an office filled by Utah voters statewide. Note clerks count/certify only valid holographic signatures of registered voters (502(5)(b)-(c)), so collect a cushion above 1,000.

fields.reliedValue: STRICTEST READING: submit the 1,000 signatures to the county clerks AND file the certificate of nomination + $500 fee with the Lieutenant Governor no later than 5 p.m. on JUNE 15 of the general election year. The August 15 date in 20A-9-504(2) on its face governs only (a) selection of the unaffiliated running mate and (b) the VP candidate's compliance with 20A-9-202(7) — NOT the signature/petition submission. Operate to the earlier June 15 date to avoid any risk to ballot access; confirm with the Lt. Governor's office before relying on August 15 for signatures.
fields.note: DISAGREE with the draft's headline that Aug 15 is the presidential SIGNATURE/petition deadline. Primary text conflicts: 20A-9-502(5)(a) sets the signature-submission deadline at 'no later than 5 p.m. on June 15,' and 20A-9-503(1) ties certificate filing + fee to that same 502(5)(a) deadline (June 15). 20A-9-504(2)(a) only requires the presidential candidate to 'select a running mate' by Aug 15, and 504(2)(b) requires the VP to satisfy 20A-9-202(7) by Aug 15 — neither sets the signature deadline. Ballotpedia (secondary) says petition filed 'no later than August 15,' contradicting the plain June 15 statutory text. The draft itself flags the June 15 vs Aug 15 tension and that the Lt. Gov used discretion amid 2024 litigation. Correctness hinges on a legal interpretation retrieval cannot settle; strictest defensible posture is the earlier June 15 for signatures. Aug 15 grounds verbatim but the VALUE's interpretation (Aug 15 = signature deadline) is not supported by the statute it cites.

fields.reliedValue: None. No geographic distribution requirement — a single flat statewide total of 1,000 valid registered-voter signatures, with no minimum per congressional district or county. Packets are submitted to the county clerk where the majority of that packet's signatures were collected, but that is an administrative routing rule, not a distribution quota; the 1,000 total is statewide.
fields.note: Quote grounds verbatim in current 20A-9-502(5)(a)(ii). The statute imposes only a single statewide 1,000 threshold for a statewide office; the per-political-division minimums in (5)(a)(ii)(B) apply only to sub-state offices, not President. The 'county clerk where the majority of signatures were collected' language is a submission-routing rule, correctly characterized as not a distribution requirement.

fields.reliedValue: Circulator (the person who witnesses each signature and signs the verification) must be at least 18 years old; need NOT be a registered voter. Residency: the CURRENT statute (FindLaw, current to Jan 1, 2025) requires only age 18 in 20A-9-502(3)(a)(i) and contains no circulator residency requirement, so a non-resident may circulate. CAUTION: the draft's basis for non-residency is the superseded 2015 ut.elaws.us snapshot (which had a presidential-specific residency waiver against a now-removed general residency rule). Confirm current residency rules with the Lt. Governor before staffing non-resident circulators; using Utah-resident circulators is the zero-risk posture. Signers must be registered to vote in Utah (or intend to register before certification) and may sign for only one candidate for the office.
fields.note: Age-18 and not-required-to-be-a-registered-voter sub-claims ground in current 20A-9-502(3)(a) and are correct. The non-residency conclusion is also TRUE under current law, but the CITED basis is stale: the ut.elaws.us quote ('A person who is not a resident may sign the verification...for president') is from a January 2015 snapshot whose structure (general residency requirement under 20A-2-105 + presidential carve-out in 502(3)(b)) no longer matches the current statute, which dropped the residency clause entirely (now no residency requirement for any office). So the draft reaches a right answer via outdated authority and a presidential-specific rationale that no longer exists. Because the current contours rest on confirming that no residency requirement survives, treat as CONSERVATIVE-HOLD; strictest safe posture is to not depend on non-resident circulators without office confirmation.

fields.reliedValue: Both required: (1) the candidate's certificate of nomination is a sworn statement that must be NOTARIZED ('Subscribed and sworn to before me... Notary Public (or other officer qualified to administer oaths)'); and (2) each signature packet must include a circulator VERIFICATION attesting that each signer signed in the circulator's presence — signed by the circulator (with residence address and date) but not separately notarized. False verification is a class A misdemeanor.
fields.note: Both quotes ground verbatim in current 20A-9-502: 502(1)(a) ends with the notary jurat (notarized candidate certificate), and 502(1)(c) sets out the circulator 'Verification' signed by the circulator. 502(4) makes false signing/verification a class A misdemeanor. The notarized-affidavit-plus-non-notarized-circulator-verification structure is correctly stated for the current statute.

fields.reliedValue: paper / wet-ink. The statute mandates handwritten 'holographic' signatures on printed 8-1/2 x 11 signature sheets, each signed in the circulator's physical presence; the county clerk counts/certifies only persons who signed 'with a holographic signature' (20A-9-502(1)(b)(viii)(C), (5)(c)). Treat digital/online capture as NOT available for the unaffiliated presidential certificate-of-nomination petition.
fields.note: Both quotes ground verbatim in current 20A-9-502 (the 'Holographic Signature of Registered Voter' column heading and the clerk counting 'only those persons who signed with a holographic signature'). The holographic mandate makes wet-ink/paper the legally safe answer, and the draft's conclusion is already the strictest posture. Caveat retained: the draft could not confirm from a primary source that any Utah state-approved signature app covers this specific path, so reliance on digital capture is unverified and should be avoided — which the relied value reflects.

fields.reliedValue: A $500 filing fee is required IN ADDITION TO the 1,000-signature petition — it is NOT an alternative that can substitute for collecting signatures. An individual filing a certificate of nomination for President pays the $500 fee at filing alongside the petition (20A-9-503(4)(a)); filing and fee are due with the certificate per 20A-9-503(1).
fields.note: Both quotes ground verbatim. 20A-9-503(4)(a) requires a $500 filing fee for a presidential certificate of nomination; nothing in 502/503 lets the fee substitute for the 1,000 signatures (503(1) requires the candidate to file the certificate, file the declaration, AND pay the fee, on top of the 502 signature requirement). The fee is additive, not a fee-in-lieu alternative — correctly stated. Ballotpedia independently confirms both the $500 fee and the 1,000 signatures.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found (per-signature ban in 20A-7-104 is limited to initiatives/referenda)
compensationModel.note: Utah's candidate-petition statute for unaffiliated candidates (20A-9-502, which expressly covers president of the United States) sets out circulation/verification mechanics — circulator must be 18+, need not be a Utah resident for a presidential petition — but is SILENT on compensating circulators; it neither permits nor prohibits per-signature pay. Utah's per-signature ban (20A-7-104) applies only "under this chapter," i.e., to initiative/referendum petitions in Title 20A, Chapter 7, and does NOT reach candidate petitions in Chapter 9. Because no candidate-petition-specific compensation rule exists, per-signature pay is unaddressed, not clearly authorized — so CONSERVATIVE-HOLD applies.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent/unaffiliated candidate for President of the United States gathering nominating-petition signatures under Utah Code 20A-9-501 to -503, may the campaign pay circulators on a per-signature basis, or does any statute, rule, or office policy require hourly/salaried compensation (as 20A-7-104 does for initiatives/referenda)? Also, must petition circulators be Utah residents or registered voters?

citations.quote: 20A-9-502 Certificate of nomination -- Contents -- Circulation -- Verification -- Criminal penalty -- Removal of petition signature. (3)(a) The candidate shall circulate the nomination petition and ensure that the person in whose presence each signature sheet is signed: (i) is at least 18 years old; and (ii) verifies each signature sheet by completing the verification bound to one or more signature sheets that are bound together.

I will be out of the state of Utah during the entire candidate filing period [applies if filing via a designated agent for an office other than president of the United States] ... at least 1,000 registered voters residing within the state when the nomination is for an office to be filled by the voters of the entire state

20A-7-104 Signature gatherers -- Payments -- Badges ... (1) A person may not pay a person to gather signatures under this chapter based on a rate per signature, on a rate per verified signature, or on the initiative or referendum qualifying for the ballot. (2) A person that pays a person to gather signatures under this section shall base the payment solely on an hourly rate.

voterFile.cost: $1,050.00 flat for a one-time statewide purchase (no subscriptions, updates, or refunds). Separate election-period reports (Who Has Voted / Who Has Been Sent an Absentee Ballot) are $35.00 per election each; precinct-level results are free.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public. Per the Lt. Governor's Office, "portions of a voter registration form is available to the public, unless a voter has requested that his or her voter information is classified as an At-Risk record." No candidate/party/registered-user gating for the public statewide file.
voterFile.useRestrictions: At-Risk-classified voter records are withheld: "These records cannot be provided to members of the public, political parties, or candidates." Universally protected data (DOB, driver license / state ID / SSN, and—absent voter consent beginning Jan 2027—email and phone) is never released to the public. The statewide file's contents and release are governed by Utah Code 20A-2-602 (contents), 20A-2-601 (age range only, not exact DOB), and 20A-2-607 (timing). County/district lists must be obtained from the relevant county clerk(s).
voterFile.requestProcess: Online request form submitted to the Lieutenant Governor's Office at https://cs.utah.gov/s/elections-request. Not an instant download: "You will receive an email that allows you to download the database within one business day of your purchase." Note the pre-election blackout under Utah Code 20A-2-607 (see note).
voterFile.note: Utah's statewide voter registration database is sold by the Lieutenant Governor's Office for a $1,050 one-time fee as a ~150MB tab-delimited file delivered via email download within one business day; it includes full legal name, complete residential address, mailing address, party affiliation/unaffiliated, and active/inactive status — everything needed to match petition signers to registered voters. Caveat for 2026: per Utah Code 20A-2-607, current voter registration information "will not be provided to any requestor or entity until May 25, 2026," and requests received before that date are fulfilled using March 8, 2026 data.

citations.quote: Fee: $1,050.00 for a one-time purchase. Refunds, subscriptions, or updates without a fee are not offered.
State law requires that portions of a voter registration form is available to the public, unless a voter has requested that his or her voter information is classified as an At-Risk record.

The database includes the following information allowed by Utah Code 20A-2-602: Full legal name ... Complete residential address, including the unit type and number ... Mailing address, including the city ... Party affiliation or status as unaffiliated ... Status as an active or inactive voter

Access to At-Risk Voter Registrations: Utah law allows certain categories of individuals to classify their voter registrations as “At-risk”. These records cannot be provided to members of the public, political parties, or candidates.
Delivery: You will receive an email that allows you to download the database within one business day of your purchase. Please note that the database file size is large (~150MB).

Per Utah Code 20A-2-607, current voter registration information will not be provided to any requestor or entity until May 25, 2026. Any requests for information received prior to May 25, 2026, will be fulfilled using data from March 8, 2026.
Voter registration records are generally considered public information, and a list of voters is available to the public upon request.

circulator.ageRule: Utah Code 20A-9-502(3)(a)(i) requires the candidate to ensure that the person in whose presence each signature sheet is signed (the circulator) "is at least 18 years old." The current circulator verification sheet, Utah Code 20A-1-1004(1) (VERIFICATION OF SIGNATURE-GATHERER), independently requires the signature-gatherer to attest under penalty of perjury "I am at least 18 years old." So the minim
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER requirements (strict): must be a registered voter in Utah, or intend to become registered before the county clerk certifies signatures, and reside within the relevant political division (the state, for a statewide/presidential office) — see 20A-9-502(1)(b)(v)-(vi), (4)(a)(i)(C), (5)(a)(ii)(A), (5)(c). CIRCULATOR requirements (loose): only that the circulator be at least 18 years old, witnes
circulator.note: For an INDEPENDENT (unaffiliated) PRESIDENTIAL candidate, ballot access via certificate of nomination is governed by Utah Code 20A-9-502 (current version effective 5/25/2026) and the circulator verification sheet at Utah Code 20A-1-1004 (current version effective 5/6/2026). The ONLY affirmative eligibility requirement the statute imposes on the CIRCULATOR (called the "signature-gatherer" / the person in whose presence each sheet is signed) is that the person be AT LEAST 18 YEARS OLD. The statute

citations.quote: (3)(a) The candidate shall circulate the nomination petition and ensure that the person in whose presence each signature sheet is signed: (i) is at least 18 years old; and (ii) verifies each signature sheet by completing the verification bound to one or more signature sheets that are bound together. (b) A person may not sign the circulator verification if the person signed a signature sheet bound to the verification.

(1) The final page of each petition packet shall contain a circulator verification sheet featuring the following printed or typed statement to be completed by the individual who gathers signatures for the petition: VERIFICATION OF SIGNATURE-GATHERER I, _____________________, hereby state, under penalty of perjury, that: I am at least 18 years old; ... I completed the signature gatherer training available on the lieutenant governor's website at www.vote.utah.gov before gathering signatures for this petition. Yes ____ No ____ (Check one) ... (Printed Name) (Signature) (Residence Address) (Date)

(2) A clerk shall reject a petition packet if: (a) all or any portion of the circulator verification sheet described in this section is not completed by the individual who gathered signatures for the petition; or (b) the individual who gathered signatures for the petition checked "No" in response to the signature gatherer training statement.

It is a class A misdemeanor for anyone to knowingly sign a certificate of nomination signature sheet with any name other than the person's own name or more than once for the same candidate or if the person is not registered to vote in this state and does not intend to become registered to vote in this state before the county clerk certifies the signatures.

citations.field: signerVsCirculator (signer residency / threshold for statewide-presidential office)
citations.quote: (5)(a)(ii) submit each signature packet to the county clerk where the majority of the signatures in the packet were collected, with signatures totaling: (A) at least 1,000 registered voters residing within the state when the nomination is for an office to be filled by the voters of the entire state ... (c) In reviewing the signature packets, the county clerk shall count and certify only those persons who signed with a holographic signature, who: (i) are registered voters within the political division that the candidate seeks to represent; and (ii) did not sign any other certificate of nomination for that office.

(4) An individual filing a certificate of nomination for president of the United States under this section: (a) shall pay a filing fee of $500; and (b) may use a designated agent to file the nomination petition.

signaturesHeadline: VT: 1,000 valid signatures, flat statewide, no distribution — AUTO-LIVE
deadlineHeadline: VT deadline CONFLICT: statute/SoS say Aug 1; NASS says June — HOLD to earlier June deadline for planning
riskNote: Signature count and capture mode are firm; the only material ballot-access risk is the deadline (two authoritative sources conflict — plan to the earlier June date) and unverified-by-omission claims (circulator eligibility) that should be confirmed with the VT SoS before relying on them.
sosInquiries: For an INDEPENDENT presidential/VP candidate filing under 17 V.S.A. 2402(d)(1)(A), is the 2028 filing deadline 5:00 p.m. on August 1 (per 2402 and your 2024 candidate form), and NOT the 'second Thursday after the first Monday in June' deadline that the NASS July-2024 summary lists for Vermont? Please confirm the controlling deadline and statute.

Does Vermont impose ANY eligibility requirement (age, in-state residency, voter registration) on the PERSON who circulates an independent presidential nomination petition, or may a non-resident / non-registered person circulate so long as every SIGNER is a registered Vermont voter? Is there any circulator affidavit, oath, or registration step?

Is there any approved electronic or remote method to collect or submit signatures for an independent presidential nomination petition, or must all signatures be wet-ink on paper, certified by each town clerk, and physically filed at the SoS office before the deadline?

fields.reliedValue: 1,000 valid signatures (flat statewide number, not a percentage), per 17 V.S.A. 2402(b)(1)(A). Only signatures certified by a town clerk as belonging to registered voters count toward the 1,000.
fields.note: GROUNDED: 2402(b)(1)(A) '...for presidential and vice presidential offices, 1,000' verified verbatim on FindLaw; SoS 2024 form 'For President - 1,000' verified verbatim in extracted PDF text. Independently re-derived: flat statutory number, no percentage, does not recompute per cycle. Town-clerk-certification-only counting confirmed by 2402(a)(4)(B)(ii). Correct and current.

fields.reliedValue: PLAN to the EARLIER of the two conflicting authorities: file no later than the second Thursday after the first Monday in June of the election year (the NASS-stated date), even though the controlling presidential-specific statute 17 V.S.A. 2402(d)(1)(A) and the VT SoS form both state 5:00 p.m. on August 1. Filing window opens the fourth Monday in April. All signatures must be town-clerk-certified BEFORE filing, so begin certification weeks earlier. Treat August 1 as the statutory backstop but do not rely on it without SoS confirmation given the documented NASS conflict.
fields.note: GROUNDED: statute quote (2402(d)(1)(A) 'not earlier than the fourth Monday in April and not later than 5:00 p.m. on the August 1...') verified verbatim on FindLaw; SoS form 'between Monday, April 22 and 5:00 p.m. on Thursday, August 1, 2024' verified verbatim; NASS quote (June deadline) verified verbatim in NASS PDF. ADVERSARIAL: the draft correctly diagnoses that NASS conflated the independent-presidential path with the 2356/2381 minor-party/general-independent June path (NASS's own citation list is 2103,2319,2356,2381,2382,2385 — it does NOT cite 2402). Ballotpedia INDEPENDENTLY corroborates 'fourth Monday in April...no later than August 1.' So August 1 is almost certainly correct. BUT: a national SoS-association source conflicts on a deadline, no human counsel is available, and missing the true deadline forfeits ballot access while filing early costs nothing — strictest defensible posture is to plan to the earlier June date and confirm with the SoS. Note 2103(13): if the deadline falls on a weekend/holiday it extends to the next business day (Aug 1 2024 was a Thursday, so no shift that cycle); the deadline is date-defined, not a fixed weekday, so re-check the actual calendar date each cycle.

fields.reliedValue: None. Vermont requires a flat statewide total of 1,000 signatures for President/VP with no geographic distribution requirement (no per-district or per-county minimums). Signatures may be collected anywhere in the state and need not all be on one paper (17 V.S.A. 2402(b)(2)); the only locality dimension is administrative town-clerk certification.
fields.note: GROUNDED: '(2) Signatures need not all be contained on one paper' and the flat 1,000 figure both verified verbatim on FindLaw. ADVERSARIAL: re-read all of 2402 — no subsection imposes any geographic/county/district distribution; the per-town element is purely the clerk-certification mechanism, not a distribution minimum. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: Strictest posture: use only circulators who are themselves Vermont-resident REGISTERED VOTERS until the SoS confirms no circulator restriction. The grounded SIGNER rule is firm: each signer must, at the time of signing, be registered and qualified to vote for the candidate and must indicate their town of residence next to the signature (17 V.S.A. 2354(b)); any number of voters may sign and there is no limit on petitions a voter may sign (2354(a), SoS form). The draft's claim of 'no circulator-eligibility requirement' is an inference from statutory silence, not an affirmative grant, so do not rely on out-of-state or unregistered circulators without confirmation.
fields.note: GROUNDED: 2354(b)(1)-(2) signer/town-of-residence quote verified verbatim on FindLaw; SoS form 'no restrictions on the number of petitions a voter may sign. 17 V.S.A. 2354' verified verbatim. ADVERSARIAL: the SIGNER requirements are explicit and correct. The 'no circulator requirement' conclusion rests on ABSENCE of statutory text — retrieval cannot prove a negative, and a restriction could live elsewhere in Title 17 ch. 49 or in SoS administrative guidance. Because mis-assuming out-of-state circulators are permissible could taint signatures and risk ballot access, hold to the strict interpretation and confirm with SoS.

fields.reliedValue: No notarized affidavit and no circulator oath. The operative requirement is TOWN-CLERK certification: before filing, the town clerk of each town where signers appear to be voters must certify on the statement of nomination that the named signers are registered voters in that town and the total number of valid signers; only so-certified signatures count (17 V.S.A. 2402(a)(4)(B)(i)-(ii)). The candidate's statement also attests each signer is qualified and the residence shown is correct (2402(c)). Comply by routing every petition page through the relevant town clerk for certification BEFORE filing.
fields.note: GROUNDED: 2402(a)(4)(B)(i)-(ii) town-clerk-certification quote verified verbatim on FindLaw; SoS form certification-before-filing language verified verbatim. ADVERSARIAL: the AFFIRMATIVE compliance mechanism (town-clerk pre-certification) is explicit and is itself the strict/verification-heavy posture, so the actionable requirement is firm. The 'no notary' element is an absence inference, but nothing in 2402, 2354, or the SoS form suggests an additional notary/oath; the compliance action (get town-clerk certification) is unchanged regardless. Treated as AUTO-LIVE on the affirmative requirement.

fields.reliedValue: paper. The statement of nomination is a paper form circulated for wet-ink signatures, taken to each town clerk for certification against the voter checklist, then physically filed with the Secretary of State in Montpelier ('filed' = 'deposited in the regularly maintained office of the official', 17 V.S.A. 2103(13); a document is not filed until received at the office). No remote/online digital signature-collection system exists for an independent presidential petition; forms are print-sign-file PDFs.
fields.note: GROUNDED: SoS form certify-before-file and 'deposited in the regularly maintained office...' (2103(13)) quotes verified verbatim; 2103(13) full text independently confirmed on FindLaw (also adds the not-filed-until-received and weekend-extension rules). ADVERSARIAL: the 'no online system' element is an absence inference, but the strict/safe posture is identical to the stated value (assume paper, wet-ink, physical filing). Aligned with strict interpretation; AUTO-LIVE.

fields.reliedValue: None. No filing fee for an independent presidential candidate and no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures option — the 1,000-signature petition (plus consent forms and three electors) is the sole ballot-access path. The $2,000 fee applies only to MAJOR-PARTY candidates seeking the presidential PRIMARY ballot, not to independent general-election candidates.
fields.note: GROUNDED: Ballotpedia quote ('...A primary candidate must also pay a $2,000 filing fee. An independent candidate...must file a petition containing the signatures of 1,000 registered voters.') verified verbatim; SoS form '1,000' requirement verified verbatim and contains no fee reference. ADVERSARIAL: 2402 imposes no fee on independents; the strict compliance action is to collect 1,000 certified signatures, which holds whether or not a fee theoretically existed, so no ballot-access risk arises from the 'no fee' conclusion. Correct; AUTO-LIVE.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: Vermont's candidate-petition statutes (17 V.S.A. §§ 2402, 2353, 2354) regulate only the signers (registered/qualified voters, town of residence) and impose NO requirements on petition circulators — there is no circulator-affirmation, no residency requirement, and crucially NO statute addressing whether circulators on an independent/presidential nominating petition may be compensated or paid per signature. Compensation of candidate-petition circulators is therefore unaddressed (not affirmatively permitted), so per-signature pay is NOT clearly authorized and falls under CONSERVATIVE-HOLD; this is distinct from any initiative/ballot-measure rule, which does not apply to candidate petitions.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent candidate's presidential nominating petition (17 V.S.A. § 2402), does Vermont law permit a campaign to pay petition circulators on a per-signature basis, and are there any restrictions on paying circulators (hourly/salary) or any residency/registered-voter requirement for who may circulate the statement of nomination?

citations.quote: (b)(1) To constitute a valid nomination, a statement shall contain signatures of voters qualified to vote in an election for the office in question, equal in number to at least: (A) for presidential and vice presidential offices, 1,000... (c) A statement shall state that each signer is qualified to vote in an election for the office in question and that the voter's residence is as set forth next to the voter's name.

(a) Any number of voters may sign the same petition. (b)(1) A voter's signature shall not be valid unless at the time he or she signs, the voter is registered and qualified to vote for the candidate whose petition he or she signs. (2) Each voter shall indicate his or her town of residence next to his or her signature.

The name of any person shall be printed upon the primary ballot as a candidate for nomination by any major political party for the office indicated, if a petition containing the requisite number of signatures made by registered voters, in substantially the following form, is filed with the proper official... and I certify that I am at the present time a registered voter and am qualified to vote for a candidate for this office.
The relevant statutes do not stipulate any particular requirements on petition circulators.

voterFile.cost: Free (no fee). The statewide voter checklist is provided at no charge upon submission of the signed affidavit.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Any person ("Any person wishing to obtain a copy of all of the statewide voter checklist"). Not limited to candidates/committees/parties — but the requester must swear/affirm permitted use. (Confidential fields are excluded for everyone; full confidential data is reserved to elections officials for elections purposes.)
voterFile.useRestrictions: Sworn, under penalty of perjury: (1) will NOT use the checklist for commercial purposes; and (2) will not knowingly disclose it to any foreign government or federal agency/commission (or persons acting for them) in circumvention of the prohibited purposes in 17 V.S.A. § 2154(b)(2) (voter registration based on the data, public disclosure of voter info, or cross-matching to other federal/state databases). Selling/licensing the data to third parties may trigger "data broker" registration duties under 9 V.S.A. § 2430(4)/§ 2446. Verifying petition signatures (matching signers to registered voters) is a permitted, non-commercial use.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request via a signed, sworn affidavit/affirmation ("Request for Electronic Copy of the Vermont Statewide Checklist") filed with the VT Secretary of State, Elections Division. Submitted by email (sos.elections@vermont.gov) or physical mail. Not an instant online download — the requester selects either a one-time file at "the next monthly issuing" or enrollment on a "monthly email list." Affirmation is made under penalty of perjury per 13 V.S.A. ch. 65.
voterFile.note: Vermont provides the full statewide voter checklist (the official registration list) free to any requester who files a signed sworn affidavit; it is emailed as a one-time or monthly file (CSV, or .TXT on request), not an instant self-serve download. The file includes name, residential address, and party affiliation (registration status is inherent — the checklist IS the registered-voter list), while DOB month/day, DL/ID number, phone, email, and last-4 SSN are confidential and excluded — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters.

citations.quote: Pursuant to 17 V.S.A. §2154, I request an electronic copy of the statewide checklist. I swear (or affirm) under penalty of perjury pursuant to chapter 65 of Title 13 of the Vermont Statutes that; 1. I will not use the information in the statewide checklist for commercial purposes, or 2. Knowingly disclose the checklist to any foreign government or to a federal agency or commission ...

Please check ONE of the below options: [ ] Email me a one-time statewide voter file at the next monthly issuing. [ ] Put me on the monthly email list. I want to receive the statewide voter file every month. [ ] Please check this box if you would like to receive the statewide voter file in .TXT format. Please return this signed form to the Vermont Secretary of State. Send via email to sos.elections@vermont.gov or physical mail to: Vermont Secretary of State; Election Division, 128 State St, Montpelier VT 05602

(c)(1) Any person wishing to obtain a copy of all of the statewide voter checklist must swear or affirm, under penalty of perjury pursuant to 13 V.S.A. chapter 65, that the person will not: (A) use the checklist for commercial purposes; or (B) knowingly disclose the checklist to any foreign government or to a federal agency or commission ... (2) The affirmation shall be filed with the Secretary of State.

(b)(1) A registered voter's month and day of birth, driver's license or nondriver identification number, telephone number, e-mail address, and the last four digits of his or her Social Security number shall be kept confidential and are exempt from public inspection and copying under the Public Records Act.
I would like to receive the Vermont Statewide Voter Checklist—is there a fee? To receive a copy of the Checklist, please complete and return the signed affidavit to the Vermont Secretary of State
All voter registration information, except what is confidential. SSN, month and day of birth. Names, addresses, party affiliation.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement. Vermont's nominating-petition statute (17 V.S.A. § 2402) and the petition-form statute (§ 2353) impose qualification requirements (registered voter, qualified to vote) ONLY on the SIGNER, and contain no provision regulating the person who carries or collects the petition. There is no circulator affidavit/oath in the statute or on the official Secretary of State indep
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a registered Vermont voter qualified to vote for the office (17 V.S.A. § 2402(b)(1), (c); confirmed by the form's per-signer certification), verified by each town clerk. The CIRCULATOR is not addressed anywhere in the statute or form — no age, residency, registration, citizenship, or affidavit requirement attaches to the person who collects the signatures.
circulator.note: Independent presidential ballot access in Vermont is by "statement of nomination" under 17 V.S.A. § 2402 (1,000 signatures for President/VP). (Note: § 2702, in the Presidential Elections chapter, is the MAJOR-PARTY PRIMARY petition route — 1,000 voters + $2,000 fee — not the independent route; the independent route is § 2402.) The statute imposes its qualification requirements exclusively on SIGNERS: § 2402(b)(1) requires "signatures of voters qualified to vote in an election for the office in q

citations.quote: (b)(1) To constitute a valid nomination, a statement shall contain signatures of voters qualified to vote in an election for the office in question, equal in number to at least: (A) for presidential and vice presidential offices, 1,000
(c) A statement shall state that each signer is qualified to vote in an election for the office in question and that the voter's residence is as set forth next to the voter's name.

citations.field: circulatorAgeRule (verification is by town clerk, not a circulator affidavit)
citations.quote: (a)(4)(B)(i) The original statement of nomination shall include a certification by the town clerk of each town where the signers appear to be voters that the persons whose names appear as signers of the statement are registered voters in the town and of the total number of valid signers from the town.
