signaturesHeadline: 1,000 registered WA voters (flat; conv. attendees)
deadlineHeadline: Cert+petition to SOS by 1st Fri of Aug (2028: Aug 4)
riskNote: 7 fields grounded; 6 AUTO-LIVE, 1 CONSERVATIVE-HOLD (circulator eligibility — absence-of-rule cannot be retrieval-confirmed). Watch SOS "last Saturday in July" vs statutory "fourth Saturday" convention-window discrepancy (differs in 2028: Jul 29 vs Jul 22).
sosInquiries: Beyond requiring that each petition signer be a registered Washington voter who attends a qualifying state convention, does Washington impose any separate qualification (age, residency, voter-registration, or affidavit) on the person who circulates/collects an independent or minor-party presidential nominating petition?

fields.reliedValue: 1,000 signatures of registered Washington voters (flat statewide number; not a percentage). An independent (or minor-party) presidential candidate is nominated by convention, and the certificate of nomination must be accompanied by nominating petition(s) bearing the signatures of at least 1,000 registered Washington voters. These voters must attend a state convention held in support of the candidate; a candidate holding multiple conventions may aggregate signatures across conventions to reach 1,000.
fields.note: All 3 citations grounded verbatim. RCW 29A.56.640(5): 'at least one thousand registered voters of the state of Washington'; RCW 29A.56.610 confirms multi-convention aggregation and 100-attendee minimum; SOS page confirms 1,000 signatures. Statute last amended 2013, no newer amendment found; SOS 2028 page still describes same process. Flat number, no interpretation needed.

fields.reliedValue: First Friday of August of the presidential election year (for 2028 = August 4, 2028). The certificate of nomination (with the petition(s)) must be submitted to the Secretary of State not later than the first Friday of August. Conventions must be held no earlier than the first Saturday in May and no later than the fourth Saturday in July per RCW 29A.56.610 (for 2028: May 6 to July 22). Date recomputes each cycle.
fields.note: RCW 29A.56.640(7) 'not later than the first Friday of August' and RCW 29A.56.610 convention-window quote grounded verbatim; SOS quote grounded. Submission deadline value is correct and verified. NOTE discrepancy: SOS website says conventions run to the 'last Saturday in July' while the controlling statute (RCW 29A.56.610) says 'fourth Saturday in July' — these differ in years with five July Saturdays (2028: 4th=Jul 22 vs last=Jul 29). The draft value correctly uses the stricter statutory 'fourth Saturday'. Updated illustrative date from 2024 (Aug 2) to 2028 cycle (Aug 4).

fields.reliedValue: None. Washington requires a flat statewide total of 1,000 registered-voter signatures with no geographic distribution requirement across congressional districts or counties.
fields.note: RCW 29A.56.640(5) grounded verbatim; statute requires only a statewide total of 1,000 'registered voters of the state of Washington' with no district/county distribution language anywhere in RCW 29A.56.610-.640. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: Each person who signs the nominating petition must be a registered Washington voter and must attend a qualifying state convention (the convention must be attended by at least 100 registered voters), and no person may sign more than one nominating petition for an office for an election. Treat any circulator as needing to be a registered Washington voter unless the SOS confirms otherwise. UNKNOWN whether a distinct circulator-qualification rule (age/residency/voter-status of the petition collector, as opposed to the signer) exists beyond these signer/attendee requirements.
fields.note: RCW 29A.56.630 and 29A.56.610 grounded verbatim (signer must be registered WA voter; no person signs more than one petition; convention needs 100 registered voters). However the field's core claim is an absence-of-law assertion ('no separate statutory circulator qualification') which retrieval cannot affirmatively confirm; the draft itself marks this UNKNOWN. Per strict-interpretation rule, hold and adopt the strictest defensible reading (circulator treated as needing registered-WA-voter status) pending SOS confirmation.

fields.reliedValue: No notarized circulator affidavit is required on the petition itself. Verification attaches to the certificate of nomination: it must be verified by the oath of the convention's presiding officer and secretary, and must contain a sworn statement from both nominees consenting to the nomination. The certificate must also contain proof of publication of the convention notice (published in a newspaper of general circulation at least ten days before the convention).
fields.note: RCW 29A.56.640(2) (sworn consent of nominees) and (4) (verified by oath of presiding officer and secretary) grounded verbatim; RCW 29A.56.620 (newspaper notice at least ten days before convention) grounded verbatim. No petition-level notarized circulator affidavit appears in statute. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: paper. The petition is gathered on paper from registered voters who physically attend the state convention, and the certificate of nomination plus petitions are submitted to the Secretary of State by mail or by in-person appointment. There is no remote-online or digitized signature system covering an independent presidential nominating petition in Washington (Washington is not among the jurisdictions that allow electronic signing of candidate nominating petitions).
fields.note: SOS quote (voters must attend a state convention) grounded verbatim; 2024 candidate guide PDF grounded verbatim ('Call (360) 902-4180 to schedule an in-person appointment, or submit by mail to:' — split across PDF lines 102-103 but exact). Ballotpedia electronic-signing list grounded (Arizona/New Mexico online, Denver/DC eSign) and does NOT include Washington, confirming no e-signature path. Guide's 'online' references are voters' pamphlet only, not petition signing. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: None. There is no filing fee for a presidential candidate in Washington, and there is no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures alternative; the signature/convention requirement is the sole path.
fields.note: SOS quote 'There is no filing fee' grounded verbatim; 2024 guide independently confirms 'Washington state does not collect filing fees for the offices of President and Vice President.' No fee-in-lieu provision exists in RCW 29A.56.610-.640. Correct.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found; WA per-signature ban is initiative/referendum-only
compensationModel.note: Washington's candidate (minor party/independent presidential) nominating-petition statutes (RCW 29A.56.630 and 29A.56.640) and the candidate-petition crime statute (RCW 29A.84.311) are SILENT on circulator compensation — they regulate signers (must be registered WA voters), not circulators, and impose no circulator residency or pay rules. Washington's pay-per-signature ban (RCW 29A.84.250(2), with the 29A.84.280 finding) applies by its express terms ONLY to "initiative or referendum petitions," so it does not automatically reach candidate petitions; candidate-petition compensation is therefore unaddressed rather than clearly permitted or prohibited.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For a minor-party/independent presidential candidate's nominating petitions under RCW 29A.56.630/.640, may the campaign compensate signature gatherers on a per-signature basis, and is there any circulator residency or registered-voter requirement — given that RCW 29A.84.250(2)'s per-signature ban is written only for initiative/referendum petitions?

citations.quote: (2) Provides or receives consideration for soliciting or procuring signatures on an initiative or referendum petition if any part of the consideration is based upon the number of signatures solicited or procured, or offers to provide or agrees to receive such consideration any of which is based on the number of signatures solicited or procured

The legislature finds that paying a worker, whose task it is to secure the signatures of voters on initiative or referendum petitions, on the basis of the number of signatures the worker secures on the petitions encourages the introduction of fraud in the signature gathering process.

Every person who: (1) Knowingly provides false information on his or her declaration of candidacy or petition of nomination; or (2) Conceals or fraudulently defaces or destroys a certificate that has been filed with an elections officer under chapter 29A.20 RCW or a declaration of candidacy or petition of nomination that has been filed with an elections officer ... is guilty of a class C felony

The petition shall also contain a statement that the person signing the petition is a registered voter of the state of Washington and shall have a space for the voter to sign his or her name and to print his or her name and address. No person may sign more than one nominating petition under this chapter for an office for an election.
(5) Be accompanied by a nominating petition or petitions bearing the signatures and addresses of at least one thousand registered voters of the state of Washington

voterFile.cost: Free in practice. No fee is shown or collected on the request form; the governing statute requires lists be furnished "at actual reproduction cost," which for the emailed electronic CSV extract is effectively $0. Conservatively: free / actual reproduction cost.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — the WA Secretary of State "shall promptly furnish current lists of registered voters in his or her possession, at actual reproduction cost, to any person requesting such information" (RCW 29A.08.720(2)). No candidate/committee/registered-user eligibility gate.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Strong. The list "shall not be used for the purpose of mailing or delivering any advertisement or offer... or... any solicitation for money, services, or anything of value." However "the lists and labels may be used for any political purpose" — defined to include support of/opposition to candidates and ballot measures — which covers petition-signature verification for a campaign. Penalty for prohibited (commercial/solicitation) use is a class C felony (up to 5 years imprisonment and/or $10,000 fine) plus $5-per-item civil liability and possible class action (RCW 29A.08.740).
voterFile.requestProcess: Online webform on the SOS "Washington Voter Registration Database Extract" page. Requester submits name, email, phone, and mailing address and checks a box agreeing to lawful use under RCW 29A.08.720, 29A.08.740, and 42.56.070(8). "You will be sent an email with a download link upon approval." Fulfillment "can take up to five business days... in most cases, the request is satisfied immediately." No notarized affidavit; just a click-through use agreement.
voterFile.note: Washington publishes one statewide Voter Registration Database (VRDB) extract — "a comprehensive list of all active registered voters" with district/precinct info and one to two years of voting history — orderable free via an online form; a download link is emailed upon approval (usually immediate). Use for political purposes (including signature verification) is expressly permitted; commercial/solicitation use is a class C felony. Note WA records no party preference, so the file contains no party affiliation.

citations.quote: The Voter Registration Database (VRDB) is a comprehensive list of **all** active registered voters, including the current year and previous year of voting history and district/precinct information.
Requests for the VRDB made below can take up to five business days to be fulfilled; in most cases, the request is satisfied immediately.
You will be sent an email with a download link upon approval.

The county auditor or secretary of state shall promptly furnish current lists of registered voters in his or her possession, at actual reproduction cost, to any person requesting such information. The lists shall not be used for the purpose of mailing or delivering any advertisement or offer for any property, establishment, organization, product, or service or for the purpose of mailing or delivering any solicitation for money, services, or anything of value. However, the lists and labels may be used for any political purpose.

"Political purpose" means a purpose concerned with the support of or opposition to any candidate for any partisan or nonpartisan office or concerned with the support of or opposition to any ballot proposition or issue.

Any person who uses registered voter data furnished under RCW 29A.08.720 for the purpose of mailing or delivering any advertisement or offer... or... any solicitation for money, services, or anything of value is guilty of a class C felony punishable by imprisonment in a state correctional facility for a period of not more than five years or a fine of not more than ten thousand dollars or both such fine and imprisonment, and is liable to each person provided such advertisement or solicitation... at five dollars for each item mailed or delivered to the person's residence.
Washington voter registration does not record party preferences; this database does not contain any information about political affiliation.
The Secretary of State's Office maintains one statewide list of voters that serves as the official list of registered voters for Washington.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement. Washington's minor-party/independent presidential nomination uses a CONVENTION model (RCW ch. 29A.56), not a conventional circulator-affidavit petition. The statute and the official SoS petition form impose requirements ONLY on the SIGNER (must be a registered WA voter who personally attended a nominating convention). There is no "circulator" role with any statutory
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a registered Washington voter who personally attended a nominating convention; CIRCULATOR has no statutory eligibility requirement (no circulator affidavit exists — the convention's presiding officer and secretary verify the certificate instead).
circulator.note: Washington does NOT use a circulator-affidavit petition for independent/minor-party presidential candidates; it uses a CONVENTION + nominating-petition model under RCW ch. 29A.56 (recodified from RCW 29A.20). A minor party or independent candidate must hold one or more conventions attended by at least 100 registered WA voters each, and submit petitions bearing the signatures of at least 1,000 registered WA voters who personally attended a convention (RCW 29A.56.610, 29A.56.640(5)), plus a Certif

citations.quote: A nominating petition submitted under this chapter shall clearly identify the name of the minor party or independent candidate convention as it appears on the certificate of nomination... The petition shall also contain a statement that the person signing the petition is a registered voter of the state of Washington and shall have a space for the voter to sign his or her name and to print his or her name and address. No person may sign more than one nominating petition under this chapter for an office for an election.

A certificate evidencing nominations made at a convention must: ... (4) Be verified by the oath of the presiding officer and secretary; (5) Be accompanied by a nominating petition or petitions bearing the signatures and addresses of at least one thousand registered voters of the state of Washington;

To be valid, a convention must be attended by at least one hundred registered voters, but a minor party or independent candidate holding multiple conventions may add together the number of signatures of different individuals from each convention in order to obtain and submit to the secretary of state the signatures of at least one thousand registered voters of the state of Washington.

Upon the receipt of the certificate of nomination, the secretary of state shall check the certificate and canvass the signatures on the accompanying nominating petitions to determine if the requirements of RCW 29A.56.640 have been met.

citations.field: signerVsCirculator (petition voter's oath, no circulator section)
citations.quote: I, the undersigned, declare that, • I am a registered voter of the State of Washington. • I personally attended a nominating convention held on ______________________ , 2024 in the city of ______________________________. • I support with my signature the nominees listed above. • I have not signed another nominating petition for this election year.

To appear on the General Election ballot either as a nominee of a minor party or as an independent candidate, candidates must demonstrate support by Washington voters through petition of 1,000 signatures of registered voters. These voters must attend a state convention held in support of the party or candidate.

signaturesHeadline: 7,947 sigs (1% of 2020 WV presidential vote; min 25) — recomputes each cycle
deadlineHeadline: Aug 1 — nomination certificate + $2,500 fee to Secretary of State
riskNote: 7 of 8 fields AUTO-LIVE on strong primary+consensus grounding; only circulatorEligibility is CONSERVATIVE-HOLD (statute is silent on whether a non-resident/non-registered person may circulate beyond the county-clerk credential requirement — an interpretation gap). No field is BLOCKED. Note the credentials doc is officially "Form P-1," not "P-3" as the captureMode/witness fields imply for the credential.
sosInquiries: West Virginia SOS Elections Division: Beyond obtaining county-clerk credentials under W. Va. Code §3-5-23(b), is a petition circulator for an independent presidential nomination certificate required to be (a) a West Virginia resident, (b) a registered voter, and/or (c) at least 18 years old, or may an out-of-state/non-registered person circulate so long as they hold valid county credentials?

fields.reliedValue: Signatures equal to at least 1% of the entire vote cast for president in the last preceding presidential election (statewide), but in no event fewer than 25. For the 2024 cycle the threshold was 7,947 (1% of West Virginia's 2020 presidential vote of 794,731). Recomputes each presidential cycle.
fields.note: All three citations verified verbatim: §3-5-23 ('not less than one percent... but in no event... less than 25'; also a second sentence keyed to 'any statewide, congressional, or presidential candidate'), NASS report ('equal to at least 1% of the entire vote cast for president in the last preceding presidential election'), and WV Watch ('Kennedy... needed 7,947 signatures'). Independently re-derived: 1% of WV 2020 presidential total (794,731) = 7,947 exactly. Cross-confirmed by Ballotpedia ('1 percent of the total votes cast in the state for president in the most recent election'). The 25 floor is statutory.

fields.reliedValue: August 1 preceding the November general election. The nomination certificate (petition) and filing fee must be filed with the Secretary of State no later than August 1 (e.g., August 1, 2024 for the 2024 cycle).
fields.note: §3-5-24(a) verbatim: 'All certificates nominating candidates for office under section twenty-three... shall be filed not later than August 1 preceding the November general election.' NASS report verbatim ('no later than August 1st preceding the general election'). Cross-confirmed by Ballotpedia ('by August 1 in the year of the election').

fields.reliedValue: None for a presidential (statewide) candidate. The 1% threshold is a single statewide total against the entire presidential vote; there is no per-congressional-district or per-county minimum. Separate petition forms per county exist solely for county-clerk verification, with no minimum-per-county quota.
fields.note: §3-5-23 quote verified verbatim. Statute measures 1% against the statewide vote with no geographic-distribution language; 'Offices to be filled by the voters of more than one county shall use separate petition forms... for each county' is administrative (verification), not a quota. Ballotpedia describes only a flat statewide 1% with no district distribution (the only 'distribution' reference is to Electoral College apportionment). No source found imposing a per-county/per-district signature minimum.

fields.reliedValue: STRICT: The circulator must obtain Official Credentials (Form P-1) from the clerk of the county commission for EACH county in which they canvass and must exhibit those credentials to every voter solicited. Until the SOS confirms otherwise, assume the strictest reading — that a circulator should be a West Virginia resident and a registered voter (matching the residency framing of the credential and the signer requirements) — rather than relying on the absence of an explicit circulator age/residency rule.
fields.note: Credentialing requirement verified verbatim in §3-5-23(b) and on the credentials form (extracted from media/413). The statute imposes residency/registration on SIGNERS, not expressly on circulators, and the draft honestly flags this as UNKNOWN. But the credential authorizes soliciting 'voters residing in [county],' leaving genuinely unsettled whether a non-resident/non-registered person may circulate — an interpretation retrieval cannot resolve. Per over-comply mandate, HOLD and query the SOS. (Also: the credentials doc is officially 'Official Form P-1,' which the draft's P-3-centric framing conflates.)

fields.reliedValue: No notarized circulator affidavit is required. Each signer personally signs in their own handwriting (or by a duly witnessed mark). The official paper petition (Form P-3) contains only signer rows plus a 'County Use' VLD/INV validity column; county-commission clerks verify signatures against county voter-registration records. There is no sworn circulator certification.
fields.note: Both §3-5-23 quotes verified verbatim ('personally signed by duly registered voters, in their own proper handwriting or by their marks duly witnessed' and the clerk-comparison-to-registration-records provision in subsection (e)). P-3 form text extracted directly confirms only signer fields plus a 'County Use / VLD INV' column and no notary/circulator-oath block. Statute imposes no circulator oath or notarization.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Signatures are collected on the Secretary of State's paper 'Minor Party or Independent Candidate Nomination Petition' (Official Form P-3), with separate forms per county, and verified by county clerks. There is NO digital/remote-online or in-person-digitized petition system for an independent presidential candidate in West Virginia.
fields.note: Form P-3 PDF (media/414) text extracted and matches the quote verbatim: 'MINOR PARTY OR INDEPENDENT CANDIDATE NOMINATION PETITION... we, the undersigned, are registered voters of ____ County... Official form P-3/Issued by the Office of the Secretary of State (WV Code §3-5-23).' §3-5-23 verbatim: 'Offices to be filled by the voters of more than one county shall use separate petition forms... for each county.' No statute, form, or SOS source indicates any electronic/online signature option; paper is the only mode.

fields.reliedValue: A filing fee is required IN ADDITION to (not in lieu of) the signature petition. The fee equals 1% of the annual salary of the office, capped at $2,500; for president/vice president the fee is $2,500, paid when the nomination certificate is filed. A separate impecuniosity path (§3-5-8a) lets a candidate unable to pay petition in lieu of the fee (four signatures per dollar of fee), but the standard independent-presidential path is petition PLUS the $2,500 fee.
fields.note: NASS quote verbatim ('must also pay a fee equivalent to 1% of the annual salary... must not exceed $2500'). §3-5-24(c) verbatim ('Each candidate shall pay the filing fee required by section eight... at the time of the filing of the nomination certificate'). §3-5-8(a)(1) independently confirms the $2,500 cap for president/VP. Ballotpedia confirms the fee is additive ('In addition, the candidate must pay a $2,500 filing fee') and the §3-5-8a waiver ('four signatures for each dollar of the filing fee'). The draft's framing of the waiver as 'additional signatures' is slightly loose (it substitutes for the FEE, not the nomination petition) but does not affect the relied value for the standard path.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute found
compensationModel.note: West Virginia's candidate-petition statute for independent/group ("certificate") nominations, including president (W.Va. Code §3-5-23, with §3-5-24 on filing), is SILENT on compensating circulators: it does not permit, prohibit, or otherwise address per-signature pay or paid circulation at all. Circulator compensation for candidate petitions is therefore unaddressed by any primary source (and WV has no statewide initiative process to even arguably borrow a pay-per-signature rule from); §3-5-23(b) does require circulators to obtain credentials from the county commission clerk but does not expressly impose a circulator residency/registration requirement.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nomination certificate under W.Va. Code §3-5-23, may the campaign compensate signature gatherers (a) per valid signature and (b) by hourly wage or salary, and must a credentialed circulator be a West Virginia resident or registered voter?

citations.quote: (b) The person or persons soliciting or canvassing signatures of duly qualified voters on the certificate or certificates, may solicit or canvass duly registered voters residing within the county, district, or other political division represented by the office sought, but must first obtain from the clerk of the county commission credentials which must be exhibited to each voter canvassed or solicited

(c) The certificate shall be personally signed by duly registered voters, in their own proper handwriting or by their marks duly witnessed, who must be residents within the county, district, or other political division represented by the office sought wherein the canvass or solicitation is made by the person or persons duly authorized.

(h) In addition to penalties prescribed elsewhere for violation of this chapter, any person violating the provisions of this section is guilty of a misdemeanor and, upon conviction, shall be fined not more than $1,000, or confined in jail not more than one year, or both fined and confined: Provided, That a criminal penalty may not be imposed upon anyone who signs a nomination certificate and votes in the primary election held after the date the certificate was signed.
(a) All certificates nominating candidates for office under section twenty-three of this article shall be filed not later than August 1 preceding the November general election

voterFile.cost: $500 flat for the full "Statewide Voter Registration List" (paper or delimited text/CSV/Excel/PDF). Other tiers: $1,000 Election Cycle Subscription Service; $500 Master Voter History List Export; $200 each for Statewide Early Voters / Absentee Requests / Absentee Received lists; partial/county/district lists and updates are billed at $25/hour (current hourly rate, estimated before fulfillment). Payment (cash, check, prepaid account, or credit card) is due before delivery.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Effectively any person or organization for noncommercial use. WV Code §3-2-30(a) provides "Any person may examine the active, inactive, rejected and canceled voter registration records," and §3-2-30(e) directs the Secretary of State to make statewide voter lists available for sale. The request form is not gated to candidates, committees, or parties; the only condition is a signed certification of noncommercial, non-solicitation use. A campaign/committee verifying petition signatures qualifies as a permitted noncommercial use.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Yes. WV Code §3-2-30(f): "No voter registration lists or data files containing voter names, addresses or other information derived from voter data files obtained pursuant to the provisions of this article may be used for commercial or charitable solicitations or advertising, sold or reproduced for resale." The purchaser must sign a certification to this effect. The distributed file also excludes telephone number, e-mail address, SSN, and DMV driver's/non-operator ID numbers by statute.
voterFile.requestProcess: Written request via the official "Voter Registration Data Services Request Form" (from the SoS "Request for Voter Registration Data Instructions"), submitted by mail, fax, or e-mail (svrs@wvsos.com) to the Elections Division. The form includes a required signed certification (a sworn-style statement of permitted use) that the purchaser will not use the list for commercial/charitable solicitation, advertising, sale, or resale. Not an instant online download. Total cost is payable before delivery; requests filled first-come, first-served.
voterFile.note: A campaign obtains the WV statewide voter file by submitting the Secretary of State's Voter Registration Data Services Request Form (mail/fax/email) with a signed noncommercial-use certification and paying a $500 flat fee; the file auto-includes name, residence address (+county), party affiliation, status, and registration date — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters. Pricing is fixed by statute (WV Code §59-1-2b) and §3-2-30; the form is not a candidate/committee-only product but bars commercial/solicitation use and resale.

citations.quote: (a) ... the Secretary of State shall charge the following fees for data originating in the statewide voter registration system ... (1) Election Cycle Subscription Service $1,000 (2) Statewide Voter Registration List $500 (3) Master Voter History List Export $500 (4) Statewide Early Voters List $200 (5) Statewide Absentee Requests List $200 (6) Statewide Absentee Received List $200 (7) Partial Voter Registration List Current hourly rate

(e) The Secretary of State shall make voter lists available for sale subject to the limitations as provided in subsection (a) of this section. The fees for the voter lists shall be as prescribed in section two-b, article one, chapter fifty-nine of this code.

(f) No voter registration lists or data files containing voter names, addresses or other information derived from voter data files obtained pursuant to the provisions of this article may be used for commercial or charitable solicitations or advertising, sold or reproduced for resale.
(1) The name, residence address, political party affiliation and status of the registrant;

The attached "Voter Registration Data Services Request Form" must be used when requesting voter data. The request may be mailed, faxed, or e-mailed using the contact information below. The total cost due is payable before delivery of voter data. Requests are filled on a first-come, first-served basis.
Your voter list will automatically include the following voter information: ✓ Name ✓ Residence Address (+ County) ✓ Party Affiliation ✓ Status (Active or Inactive) ✓ Registration Date

I, (name of purchaser) for (organization, if any) understand that West Virginia law prohibits the sale and use of the list of registered voters for commercial or charitable solicitations or advertising, and that I or my organization may not sell the list or reproduce it for resale. I hereby certify that the purposes for which the names and addresses of voters will be used are not in violation of this prohibition.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement is stated in the statute. W. Va. Code §3-5-23(b) defines the circulator as "[t]he person or persons soliciting or canvassing signatures" and imposes only one affirmative duty on them — to "first obtain from the clerk of the county commission credentials which must be exhibited to each voter canvassed or solicited." No minimum age (and no 18+ language) appears anywhere
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a duly registered WV voter residing within the political division represented by the office (for president, the state) — §3-5-23(c),(d). CIRCULATOR ("person...soliciting or canvassing signatures") has no stated registration/residency/citizenship/age requirement; the circulator's only express duty is to obtain and exhibit credentials issued by the county clerk under §3-5-23(b).
circulator.note: Independent/group presidential candidates in WV qualify via the "certificate nomination" (petition) process in W. Va. Code §3-5-23 (1% of last presidential vote, min 25; $2,500 fee; due Aug 1). The circulator-vs-signer split is clear in the statute: subsection (b) is the only provision addressing the circulator ("[t]he person or persons soliciting or canvassing signatures... must first obtain from the clerk of the county commission credentials which must be exhibited to each voter canvassed or s

citations.quote: The person or persons soliciting or canvassing signatures of duly qualified voters on the certificate or certificates, may solicit or canvass duly registered voters residing within the county, district, or other political division represented by the office sought, but must first obtain from the clerk of the county commission credentials which must be exhibited to each voter canvassed or solicited

The certificate shall be personally signed by duly registered voters, in their own proper handwriting or by their marks duly witnessed, who must be residents within the county, district, or other political division represented by the office sought wherein the canvass or solicitation is made by the person or persons duly authorized.

The certificates shall state the name and residence of each of the candidates; that he or she is legally qualified to hold the office; that the subscribers are legally qualified and duly registered as voters and desire to have the candidates placed on the ballot
The clerk of each county commission, upon proper application made as herein provided, shall issue such credentials and shall keep a record thereof.

citations.field: jurisdiction / petition route for independent presidential candidates
citations.quote: An independent presidential candidate must petition for placement on the general election ballot. The petition must contain signatures equaling at least 1 percent of the total votes cast in the state for president in the most recent election.

signaturesHeadline: 2,000–4,000 valid elector signatures (flat statutory band; AUTO-LIVE)
deadlineHeadline: 5 p.m. first Tuesday in August of the presidential year (Aug 6 in 2024); circulate from July 1; file with WEC — AUTO-LIVE
riskNote: All fields grounded; one HOLD — 2025 Act 126 (eff. 2026-03-30) made circulators be qualified WI-resident electors, with a presidential-papers carve-out the stale eLaws citation does not surface, so circulatorEligibility is held to the strictest (WI-resident) reading pending WEC confirmation.
sosInquiries: Confirm in writing: for INDEPENDENT PRESIDENTIAL nomination papers (EL-167) filed for the 2028 general election, does the 2025 Act 126 presidential carve-out still allow a circulator who is an adult U.S. citizen, 18+, not a Wisconsin resident, but who would be qualified to vote under Wis. law — or must all circulators now be qualified Wisconsin-resident electors?

fields.reliedValue: Not less than 2,000 nor more than 4,000 valid signatures of electors — a flat statutory band specific to independent presidential electors voting for the same president/VP candidates (Wis. Stat. 8.20(4)). It is NOT a percentage of votes cast and does not recompute by cycle. Collect a cushion above 2,000 to survive challenges, but do not file more than 4,000.
fields.note: Both citations verified VERBATIM. eLaws 8.20(4): 'For independent presidential electors intending to vote for the same candidates for president and vice president, the number of required signatures shall be not less than 2,000 nor more than 4,000 electors.' NASS July-2024 report (downloaded, pdftotext): 'nomination papers containing between 2,000 and 4,000 signatures.' Adversarial check: 2025 Act 126 amended 8.20 but only as to circulator qualifications, not signature counts; no later act changes the band. Value current and correct.

fields.reliedValue: Nomination papers may be circulated no sooner than July 1 and must be filed no later than 5:00 p.m. on the first Tuesday in August preceding the presidential election (Wis. Stat. 8.20(8)(am)), filed with the Wisconsin Elections Commission. For 2024 this was 5:00 p.m. on August 6, 2024. The date recomputes each presidential cycle.
fields.note: Both citations verified VERBATIM. eLaws 8.20(8)(am): 'may be circulated no sooner than July 1 and may be filed not later than 5 p.m. on the first Tuesday in August preceding a presidential election.' Lisbon WI Type-A notice (downloaded, pdftotext, line 12-14): 'Independent candidates for President or Vice President may circulate nomination papers beginning July 1, 2024, and must file nomination papers with the Wisconsin Elections Commission no later than 5:00 p.m. on August 6, 2024.' NASS corroborates filing 'no later than the first Tuesday in August.' Current and correct.

fields.reliedValue: None for the independent presidential petition. The 2,000–4,000 signatures may be gathered anywhere in Wisconsin (signers must be electors eligible to vote in the state) with no per-congressional-district or per-county minimum. (Separate, materially different path: forming a recognized political party requires 10,000 electors including at least 1,000 in each of at least 3 congressional districts — this is NOT the independent path.)
fields.note: Both citations verified VERBATIM. eLaws 8.20(5): 'Signers of each nomination paper shall reside in the same jurisdiction or district which the candidate named therein will represent, if elected.' NASS report: 'A political organization may also become a recognized political party by filing... a petition containing the signatures of at least 10,000 electors, including at least 1,000 electors residing in each of at least 3 separate congressional districts.' Adversarial check: statute imposes only a same-jurisdiction (statewide, for president) residency requirement and no district/county allocation for the independent petition; the distribution rule applies only to the distinct party-formation path. Correct; the contrast is properly flagged.

fields.reliedValue: STRICTEST: treat every circulator as required to be a QUALIFIED ELECTOR OF WISCONSIN — i.e., a U.S. citizen, age 18+, a Wisconsin resident at their residence for 28+ days, not serving a felony sentence, not adjudicated incompetent to vote, who intends to support the candidate and signs the s.8.15(4)(a) circulator certification (no notarization). A non-resident-circulator allowance MAY exist for presidential papers, but do not rely on it without written WEC confirmation. If a circulator lists an out-of-state address, WEC strikes the entire page.
fields.note: The eLaws quotes (8.15(4)(a) non-resident allowance; 8.20(3) certification-appended) match VERBATIM — but eLaws is a JAN-2017 snapshot and is STALE. 2025 Wisconsin Act 126 (signed/effective 2026-03-30) AMENDED 8.10, 8.15, 8.20, 8.40, 9.10 to require circulators be a 'qualified elector of this state,' removing the old non-resident allowance. WEC's official 2026-04-08 Act 126 memo (fetched) says the change applies 'except Presidential nomination papers,' and notes presidential circulators 'may still be adult U.S. citizens who are not Wisconsin residents but who would be qualified to vote under Wisconsin law.' So the draft's bottom-line outcome is likely correct FOR THE PRESIDENTIAL PATH — but it now hinges entirely on a non-obvious statutory carve-out the stale citation never surfaces, and the general headline is now 'must reside in Wisconsin.' Per over-comply mandate (never risk ballot access), HOLD to the strict WI-resident reading and confirm the carve-out with WEC in writing.

fields.reliedValue: No notarization required. Each nomination paper requires a circulator certification (signed and dated by the circulator at the bottom of the sheet under s.8.15(4)(a)) attesting that the circulator personally circulated the paper and personally obtained each signature, knows the signers are electors of the required jurisdiction, knows they signed knowingly on the stated dates, and that falsifying the certification is punishable under Wis. Stat. 12.13(3)(a). The certification may be made by the candidate or any qualified circulator.
fields.note: eLaws 8.15(4)(a) quote verified VERBATIM, incl. 'personally circulated the nomination paper and personally obtained each of the signatures... aware that falsifying the certification is punishable under s. 12.13 (3) (a)... may be made by the candidate or any qualified circulator.' Adversarial check: Wisconsin uses circulator certification, not a notary/witness; Act 126 changes WHO may certify (qualifications) but does not add a notarization requirement. The no-notary conclusion is unaffected and correct. (Note the linked qualified-circulator definition is the field handled under circulatorEligibility.)

fields.reliedValue: paper. Wisconsin requires physical paper nomination papers (EL-167 for independent president/VP) with wet-ink signatures: each signer must legibly print name, list municipality of residence, street and number, and date of signing next to their signature, and a circulator must physically circulate each sheet and sign the certification. There is NO official online/remote-electronic petition for an independent presidential petition; the official filing is paper, delivered to the Wisconsin Elections Commission.
fields.note: Both citations verified VERBATIM. eLaws 8.20(5): 'each signer shall legibly print his or her name in a space provided next to his or her signature and shall list his or her municipality of residence for voting purposes, the street and number, if any, on which the signer resides, and the date of signing.' eLaws 8.15(4)(a): 'personally circulated the nomination paper and personally obtained each of the signatures.' WEC pages confirm paper EL-167 forms and in-person filing. No e-signature path exists. Correct.

fields.reliedValue: None. Wisconsin imposes no filing fee for ballot access and provides no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures alternative for independent presidential candidates — qualification is by nomination-paper signatures only (2,000–4,000) plus a declaration of candidacy under s.8.21. (Separately, 2025 Act 43 imposes a fee only to WITHDRAW a name from the ballot — that is not a ballot-access fee and does not create a fee path.)
fields.note: Both citations verified VERBATIM. eLaws 8.20(6): 'Nomination papers shall be accompanied by a declaration of candidacy under s. 8.21.' NASS report lists qualification by signatures only with no fee. Adversarial check: I checked for any fee path. The only fee found (WEC FAQ, 2025 Act 43) is for REMOVING a name from the ballot via sworn statement — a withdrawal fee, not an access fee or fee-in-lieu of signatures. No fee-in-lieu exists. Correct.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute; per-unit pay banned only for voter-registration forms (s. 12.13(3)(ze)), not nomination papers
compensationModel.note: Wisconsin's candidate/independent-presidential petition statutes (Wis. Stat. ss. 8.20 and 8.15) are SILENT on circulator compensation — they neither authorize nor prohibit paying circulators, per signature or otherwise. Notably, Wisconsin's election-fraud statute s. 12.13(3)(ze) DOES ban variable/per-unit compensation, but ONLY for collectors of VOTER REGISTRATION forms, not for nomination-paper signatures; the legislature did not extend that ban to candidate petitions, so per-signature candidate-petition pay is unaddressed (not clearly prohibited, but not affirmatively permitted by any primary source). For PRESIDENT specifically, circulator residency is NOT required: s. 8.15(4)(a) lets the circulator be "a qualified elector of this state, or if not a qualified elector of this state, ... a U.S. citizen age 18 or older who, if he or she were a resident of this state, would not be disqualified from voting," and 2025 Wisconsin Act 126 (signed March 30, 2026) — which newly requires circulators to be Wisconsin qualified electors — expressly EXEMPTS U.S. president, so a national/out-of-state paid circulator pool remains legal for the presidential petition. Caveat: the circulator certification still requires the circulator to attest "that he or she intends to support the candidate," which is an operational constraint on any paid pool.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nomination papers (Wis. Stat. ss. 8.20/8.15), may a campaign compensate circulators on a per-signature basis, given that the variable-rate compensation ban in s. 12.13(3)(ze) by its terms applies only to voter-registration forms and not to nomination papers?

citations.quote: (ze) Compensate a person who obtains voter registration forms from other persons at a rate that varies in relation to the number of voter registrations obtained by the person.

that he or she, the circulator, is a qualified elector of this state, or if not a qualified elector of this state, is a U.S. citizen age 18 or older who, if he or she were a resident of this state, would not be disqualified from voting under s. 6.03; that he or she intends to support the candidate
The certification of a qualified circulator under s. 8.15(4)(a) shall be appended to each nomination paper.

Act 126, which was signed into law last month, amends statutes to require a circulator of nomination papers for all offices other than U.S. president to be a “qualified elector of the state.” Previously, circulators could be residents of other states who would have otherwise been eligible to vote in Wisconsin if they lived in the state.

voterFile.cost: $25 base fee per report + $5 per 1,000 voter records (rounded up to the next thousand), capped at $12,500. A full statewide file (~3.5M+ registered voters) reaches/approaches the $12,500 cap. Custom requests add $75 per half-hour of programming. Nomination-paper PDF scans are free.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: General public — no eligibility gate and no political-use limitation; anyone may create a Badger Voters account (via My Wisconsin ID) and purchase the file. Data is provided free only to Wisconsin election officials in their official capacity, law enforcement for law-enforcement purposes, government subunits of another state for official purposes, or ERIC (for list maintenance).
voterFile.useRestrictions: No use restrictions. Per the WEC's Badger Voters FAQ, "Wisconsin law does not place any restrictions on the use of data obtained through this website," and commercial use is expressly permitted. Confidential electors (Wis. Stat. 6.47) and protected judicial officers (Wis. Stat. 757.07) are excluded from all data; no DOB, driver license, or SSN is ever provided.
voterFile.requestProcess: Instant online self-service download. On BadgerVoters.wi.gov a requester creates an account, builds a query (statewide or by district/county/municipality/ward), gets an automated quote (record count + cost), pays by credit/debit card, eCheck, or ACH, and immediately downloads a CSV (large files are zipped). No application approval, written request, or sworn affidavit required.
voterFile.note: Wisconsin's statewide voter file (WisVote) is sold to anyone through the WEC's self-service Badger Voters portal at a $25 base + $5/1,000-record fee capped at $12,500, downloadable instantly after card payment. The standard voter record includes name, residential address, and voter status plus districts and vote history — enough to match petition signers to registered voters.

citations.quote: Voter lists and custom requests have a base fee of $25. Additionally, there is a fee of $5 for every thousand records returned by the request, rounded up to a thousand if less than one thousand, or to the nearest thousand if more. The fee is capped at $12,500. If the request would cost more than that amount, it will instead cost $12,500.
Custom requests incur an additional charge of $75 per half hour of development time required to complete the request.

(4) The charge for reports in electronic format is a $25 base fee per report; plus $5 for the first 1,000 voter registration data records, or up to 1,000 voter registration data records; plus $5 for each additional 1,000 voter registration data records, rounded to the nearest thousand. The maximum charge for an electronic report is $12,500.
Can I use the list for commercial purposes? Wisconsin law does not place any restrictions on the use of data obtained through this website.

There is no provision in law for data to be provided without charge except to Wisconsin election officials in their official capacity, law enforcement agencies for law enforcement purposes, subunits of the state government of another state for official purposes, or the Electronic Registration Information Center, Inc. for the purpose of maintaining the official registration list.

A voter record includes a voter’s name, address, and any contact information they provided with their registration; as well as the electoral districts that voter is in, what elections they voted in, and whether they voted at the polls or absentee back to 2006.

This site allows you to: Create a request for voter data from the Wisconsin Voter Registration System (WisVote) ... Pay for the data using a credit card or through an automated clearing house (ACH) using a checking or savings account ... Download the requested file upon successful payment

(6) The commission shall establish by rule the fee for obtaining a copy of the official registration list, or a portion of the list, including access to the subscription service established under s. 5.05 (14) (b).

circulator.ageRule: A circulator of a presidential/vice-presidential nomination paper must be at least 18 years old. Under Wis. Stat. 8.40(2) as amended by 2025 Wisconsin Act 126, the circulator must be "a qualified elector of this state, or if not a qualified elector of this state, ... a U.S. citizen age 18 or older who, if he or she were a resident of this state, would not be disqualified from voting under s. 6.03.
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNER must be a qualified elector of the jurisdiction; CIRCULATOR of a PRESIDENTIAL petition need only be a U.S. citizen aged 18+ (no WI residency or voter-registration requirement, due to the Act 126 presidential carve-out).
circulator.note: CRITICAL PRESIDENTIAL CARVE-OUT. As of 2026, 2025 Wisconsin Act 126 (Assembly Bill 223; enacted March 27, 2026, effective March 30, 2026) amended Wis. Stat. 8.40(2) to require that circulators of nomination papers be "qualified electors of this state" -- BUT it expressly EXEMPTS the offices of president and vice president of the United States. For a presidential/VP nomination paper, the circulator may be (a) a qualified elector of Wisconsin, OR (b) if not a qualified elector of Wisconsin, "a U.S

citations.quote: except as provided in par. (b), the circulator is a qualified elector of this state, or if not a qualified elector of this state, that the circulator is a U.S. citizen age 18 or older who, if he or she were a resident of this state, would not be disqualified from voting under s. 6.03

citations.field: presidential exception (circulator need not be a qualified WI elector for president/VP)
citations.quote: Under the bill, nomination papers and petitions for the candidacy of candidates for the offices of president and vice president of the United States may be circulated by any person eligible to vote in Wisconsin or by any U.S. citizen aged 18 or older who, if he or she were a Wisconsin resident, would not be disqualified from voting in the state.

citations.field: Act 126 requires WI qualified-elector circulators for all offices OTHER than U.S. president
citations.quote: Act 126 amends statutes to require a circulator of nomination papers for all offices other than U.S. president to be a 'qualified elector of the state.'

citations.field: new residency/qualified-voter requirement and effective date
citations.quote: A new statute requiring circulators of nomination papers to reside in Wisconsin and be qualified to vote in the state. (Enacted March 30, 2026).

citations.field: presidential/VP exception confirmed by enacting legislator's office
citations.quote: a limited exception for presidential and vice-presidential candidates, aligning Wisconsin's policies with federal election practices

citations.field: signer must be elector of the jurisdiction (circulator certification language)
citations.quote: The certification of a qualified circulator stating his or her residence with street and number, if any, shall appear at the bottom of each separate sheet of each petition ... the circulator knows that they are electors of the jurisdiction or district in which the petition is circulated

signaturesHeadline: 3,891 valid sigs (2% of 2022 U.S. House vote); recomputed each cycle
deadlineHeadline: File with SOS no later than 70 days before general (2024: Aug 26, 2024)
riskNote: All 8 fields grounded; statute and SOS form agree. Only open item: circulator qualifications are unstated in primary sources (held conservative). Fee conflict ($200 NASS vs $750) resolved to $750 by W.S. 22-5-208(a)(iii)+22-5-306(b).
sosInquiries: Wyoming independent presidential nominating petition: are there any qualifications for a petition CIRCULATOR (minimum age, Wyoming residency, or voter registration), or may any person circulate, given the form only requires the circulator to certify personal circulation and give a residence address?

fields.reliedValue: 2% of the total number of votes cast in Wyoming for U.S. Representative in the last general election. For the 2024 cycle this equaled 3,891 valid signatures. (Statewide flat figure; recomputed each cycle from the most recent U.S. House vote total.) Statutory basis: W.S. 22-5-304(a).
fields.note: Quotes verified verbatim: SOS form (downloaded+text-extracted, lines 12-16: 'numbering not less than two percent (2%)... 3,891 for the upcoming 2024 General Election (W.S. 22-5-304(a))') and FindLaw W.S. 22-5-304(a). Independently re-derived: 2022 WY U.S. House total = 193,902 votes; 2% = ~3,878, within source-variance of the official 3,891 (corroborates, does not refute). Caveat: statute text reads 'statewide PARTISAN office', but the SOS office-issued independent-presidential form authoritatively applies the same 2% figure and cites 304(a), so this is office-settled, not an open interpretation.

fields.reliedValue: The petition must be filed with the Secretary of State not less than 70 days before the general election. For the 2024 cycle the filing deadline was August 26, 2024. A petition may be circulated for signatures only during the election year (W.S. 22-5-305(a)). Recomputes each cycle (70 days before the November general election date).
fields.note: Verified verbatim: SOS form line 22-24 ('Filing Deadline: August 26, 2024. You must file not less than seventy (70) days prior...') and Ballotpedia ('no later than 70 days prior to the general election'). W.S. 22-5-305(a) circulation-window quote verified verbatim on FindLaw. Date is cycle-specific; the 70-day formula is the durable rule, correctly stated.

fields.reliedValue: None. For president (a statewide office) the requirement is a single statewide total of 2% of the last U.S. House vote; signatures are not allocated per congressional district or county. (W.S. 22-5-304(a) governs statewide office; the per-county/per-district formulas in 22-5-304(b)-(c) apply only to countywide and district offices.)
fields.note: Verified verbatim: W.S. 22-5-304(a) on FindLaw and SOS form lines 37-38. Confirmed adversarially that 304(b) is countywide and 304(c) is district — neither applies to a statewide/presidential petition. No geographic distribution requirement exists. WY has a single at-large U.S. House district, so no district split is even possible.

fields.reliedValue: UNRESOLVED / verify with SOS. Wyoming's petition statutes (W.S. 22-5-304/305/306) and the SOS petition form impose eligibility only on SIGNERS (registered elector, resident in the jurisdiction, eligible to vote for the petitioner). No age, residency, or registration qualification for the CIRCULATOR is stated in any primary source; the form requires only that the circulator certify they personally circulated the petition and give a name and residence address. Treat circulator qualifications as unconfirmed until the SOS verifies; do not assume non-residents may circulate.
fields.note: Citations grounded: SOS form circulator verification (lines 66-67) and W.S. 22-5-305(c) signer requirements, both verbatim. The draft's factual reading (no stated circulator requirement) is correct, BUT absence of a stated requirement is not proof none exists in rule/practice; strictest posture holds it open. Hold + SOS inquiry filed.

fields.reliedValue: Circulator certification (NOT notarized). The petition carries a 'VERIFICATION BY CIRCULATOR' in which the circulator certifies personal circulation and that signatures were made in their presence; there is no notarization requirement on the form. Separately, the presidential and vice-presidential candidates must file sworn statements naming the three presidential electors.
fields.note: Verified verbatim against the extracted SOS form: VERIFICATION BY CIRCULATOR block (lines 65-68) and Sworn Statements paragraph (lines 18-20). Confirmed zero occurrences of 'notary'/'notariz' anywhere in the extracted form text. Circulator attestation, not notarization, is correct.

fields.reliedValue: paper. Signatures must be collected on the printed SOS petition form (which must be approved by the filing office before circulation), and each signer must hand-print name, date of signing, and physical residential address. No online/remote or digitized e-signature system exists for an independent presidential petition in Wyoming; collection and filing are paper-only.
fields.note: Verified verbatim: W.S. 22-5-305(b)-(c) on FindLaw and the SOS form's 'The petition shall be approved by the appropriate filing office prior to circulation' (form lines 5-6). Form requires handwritten signer fields and prohibits P.O. boxes. No statutory or SOS e-signature pathway found; paper-only is correct.

fields.reliedValue: $750 filing fee REQUIRED (in addition to, NOT in lieu of, the signatures). The petition is invalid if not accompanied by the fee. Statutory chain: W.S. 22-5-306(b) ties the independent-petition fee to the same fee a primary candidate pays for the same office, and W.S. 22-5-208(a)(iii) sets $750 for any federal office voted on statewide. The NASS July-2024 summary's $200 figure is incorrect and should be disregarded.
fields.note: Conflict RESOLVED in verification. SOS form ('Filing Fee: $750 – Candidates for federal offices', line 26) and Ballotpedia ($750) verified verbatim; NASS PDF ($200, line 1178 of extract) verified verbatim as the conflicting source. Decisive primary source: W.S. 22-5-208(a)(iii) = '$750.00 for any federal office voted for by electors of the entire state', combined with 22-5-306(b) (fee = same as primary). $750 is statutorily controlling; NASS $200 is simply wrong/stale. Note this is a mandatory fee in addition to signatures, despite the field name 'feeAlternative' — there is no fee-in-lieu-of-signatures path in Wyoming.

compensationModel.verifiedBy: machine (single-pass, candidate-petition); confirm with SoS / counsel before paying real money
compensationModel.operatingStance: PAY PER SIGNATURE — not prohibited; verification is the safeguard; confirm w/ SoS before a large spend
compensationModel.model: unaddressed — no candidate-petition compensation statute; only initiative/referendum payment bans exist, plus a general bribery ban on paying a person "to sign a petition"
compensationModel.note: Wyoming's candidate/independent-presidential petition statutes (W.S. 22-5-301 through 22-5-308) are silent on circulator compensation — they neither permit nor prohibit paying circulators (per signature or otherwise), so candidate-petition comp is UNADDRESSED. The only "payment for signatures" bans in the Election Code (W.S. 22-24-323 initiative; 22-24-420 referendum) are by their own text confined to initiative/referendum petitions and prohibit paying the SIGNER, not paying circulators; they do not reach candidate petitions. However, the general election-bribery felony, W.S. 22-26-109(a)(i), broadly prohibits paying "any money or other valuable thing to a person ... to ... sign or not sign a petition," which covers candidate petitions and makes per-signature pay legally risky. No Wyoming residency or voter-registration requirement is imposed on candidate-petition circulators (only the signers must be Wyoming registered electors); the analogous initiative-circulator rule, W.S. 22-24-306(a), requires only U.S. citizenship and age 18.
compensationModel.sosInquiry: For an independent presidential candidate's nominating petition under W.S. 22-5-301 to 22-5-308, may the campaign pay circulators on a per-signature basis, and does Wyoming impose any residency or voter-registration requirement on those circulators?

citations.quote: 22-5-304. Qualifications and number of signers required. (a) For a statewide partisan office, a petition shall be signed by registered electors, resident in the state and eligible to vote for the petitioner, numbering not less than two percent (2%) of the total number of votes cast for representative in congress in the last general election for the entire state.

22-5-305. When petitions may be circulated; use of copies; requirements. (a) A petition shall be circulated for signatures only during the calendar year in which the election for the office sought is to be held. (b) Copies of the petition may be circulated for signatures, but each separate page shall contain the information required to be contained in the original petition for nomination.

VERIFICATION BY CIRCULATORS  I, ...., do hereby certify that I am a circulator of this petition, and I solely and personally circulated this petition, that all the signatures appearing herein were made in my presence ... and to the best of my knowledge and belief such signatures are those of the persons whose names they purport to be.

22-24-323. Petitions; payment for signatures; misrepresentation of petition; prohibition; penalty. (a) A circulator of an initiative petition or a person who causes the circulation of an initiative petition may not pay or offer to pay any compensation to another person for that person's signature on the initiative petition.

22-26-109. Offering bribe. (a) Offering bribe consists of willfully advancing, paying, offering to pay or causing to be paid, or promising, directly or indirectly, any money or other valuable thing to a person, for any of the following purposes: (i) To induce a person to vote or refrain from voting for or against a candidate or ballot proposition or to sign or not sign a petition;
22-24-306. Sponsors and circulators. (a) ... An individual shall not be qualified to act as a circulator unless he is a citizen of the United States and is at least eighteen (18) years of age.

Wyoming Statutes 22-5-301 through 22-5-308 are controlling legislation. The petition shall be approved by the appropriate filing office prior to circulation. ... A petition for a statewide office shall be signed by registered electors, resident in the jurisdiction and eligible to vote for the petitioner, numbering not less than two percent (2%) of the total number of votes cast in the state for U.S. Representative in the last general election.

voterFile.cost: Statewide File $125; Party $50; County $35; Legislative District $25 (statewide requests, per official fee schedule on the order form). County-level requests: fee set by the local County Clerk.
voterFile.whoCanObtain: Political use only, eligibility-gated per W.S. 22-2-113(a): candidates for political office, candidate campaign committees, political party central committees and officials, elected officials, political action committees, individuals promoting/opposing a ballot issue or candidate, and organizations promoting voter participation. NOT available to the general public or for commercial use.
voterFile.useRestrictions: Political purposes only; commercial use prohibited. Affiant must affirm they will not use the data for commercial purposes and, if redistributing, must notify subsequent parties of the commercial-use prohibition. Violations of W.S. 22-2-113(a) are referred for prosecution as a misdemeanor under W.S. 22-26-112.
voterFile.requestProcess: Application form (Wyoming Voter Registry List Request Form, VoterProductOrderForm.pdf) emailed to elections@wyo.gov or mailed to the Election Division. Requires a signed oath/affidavit under penalty of perjury affirming political-use-only, plus documentary evidence of eligibility (e.g., PAC formation/officers). Payment (cash or check payable to the Wyoming Secretary of State) must be received before data is delivered. For county/municipal officials/candidates/PACs/parties, the request goes to the local County Clerk instead.
voterFile.note: Wyoming's statewide voter registry list is sold by the Secretary of State (Election Division) only to political actors under W.S. 22-2-113(a), at a $125 flat statewide fee, via a paper request form with a sworn political-use oath and prepayment. The standard file includes Voter ID, full name, residence address, county, precinct, registration date, and party — sufficient to match petition signers to registered voters.

citations.quote: Per W.S. 22-2-113(a), “The secretary of state shall furnish at a reasonable price registry lists to any candidate for a political office in the state, candidate's campaign committee, political party central committees and officials thereof, elected officials, political action committees, individuals promoting or opposing a ballot issue or candidate and to organizations which promote voter participation.”
The registry list may be obtained for political purposes only and the requestor must fit a category as outlined in #6 Oath Required on the form.
State Requests: Please reference fees below. Statewide File – $125 Party – $50 County - $35 Legislative District – $25 Payment must be received prior to receiving data.

The VR list data file will only include the following columns: Voter ID, Last Name, First Name, Middle Name, Name Suffix, Residence Address, County, Precinct, Split, Mailing Address, Registration Date, Political Party, and House & Senate District.
(IMPORTANT! This data can ONLY be used for political purposes, not for commercial use.)

I affirm under penalty of perjury that I will use the above voter registration data for political purposes only and that I will not use it for commercial purposes... I understand that violations of W.S. 22-2-113(a) will be referred for prosecution as a misdemeanor under W.S. 22-26-112.
Payment may be submitted via cash or a check made payable to the Wyoming Secretary of State.

circulator.ageRule: No circulator age requirement for candidate nominating petitions. The independent presidential candidate path runs through Wyoming's "nomination by petition" statute (W.S. 22-5-301 et seq., with presidential electors under W.S. 22-19-102(b)). The only thing required of the circulator is the "VERIFICATION BY CIRCULATORS" affidavit in W.S. 22-5-301(a) — certifying they personally circulated the peti
circulator.signerVsCirculator: SIGNERS must be "registered electors, resident in the state and eligible to vote for the petitioner" (W.S. 22-5-304(a), 22-5-305); the presidential ELECTOR nominees must be "qualified electors" (W.S. 22-19-102(b)). The CIRCULATOR faces no such requirement — only the verification affidavit (W.S. 22-5-301(a)) certifying personal circulation and witnessing of signatures, with no age, residency, citiz
circulator.note: Independent presidential candidates qualify in Wyoming by NOMINATION BY PETITION under Chapter 5, Article 3 (W.S. 22-5-301 to 22-5-308), filing presidential-elector nominees under W.S. 22-19-102(b). Controlling statutory text reviewed directly from the official Wyoming Legislature Title 22 compilation (wyoleg.gov). For the circulator, the statute requires ONLY the "VERIFICATION BY CIRCULATORS" affidavit in W.S. 22-5-301(a): the circulator certifies "that I am a circulator of this petition, and I

citations.quote: VERIFICATION BY CIRCULATORS — I, ...., do hereby certify that I am a circulator of this petition, and I solely and personally circulated this petition, that all the signatures appearing herein were made in my presence from .... (month) .... (day), ....(year) through .... (month) .... (day), ....(year), and to the best of my knowledge and belief such signatures are those of the persons whose names they purport to be. (W.S. 22-5-301(a))

22-5-304. Qualifications and number of signers required. (a) For a statewide partisan office, a petition shall be signed by registered electors, resident in the state and eligible to vote for the petitioner, numbering not less than two percent (2%) of the total number of votes cast for representative in congress in the last general election for the entire state.

citations.field: minorCanCirculate / negative inference — citizen+18 requirement exists ONLY for ballot-measure petitions, not candidate petitions
citations.quote: 22-24-306. Sponsors and circulators. (a) ... An individual shall not be qualified to act as a circulator unless he is a citizen of the United States and is at least eighteen (18) years of age. (This requirement appears in Chapter 24 — INITIATIVE AND REFERENDUM; it has no counterpart in the candidate nomination-by-petition statute, W.S. 22-5-301 et seq.)

citations.field: jurisdiction / path for independent presidential candidate
citations.quote: 22-19-102. Nomination. ... (b) Independent candidates for president shall file the candidate's nominees for presidential electors not less than seventy (70) days prior to the general election. The nominees shall be qualified electors.

citations.field: circulation timing / copy rule (no circulator qualification imposed)
